document: Graph neural networks have advanced representation learning for relational data across scientific and industrial domains. Recent interest centers on self-supervised objectives that avoid extensive labeling costs while preserving structural signals. Despite progress, trade-offs remain between robustness to augmentations and fidelity to graph semantics. Furthermore, benchmarks often conflate transductive and inductive regimes, complicating fair assessments. We analyze how pretext choices interact with topology and attribute sparsity in heterogeneous settings. Work on graph contrastive learning includes node-level objectives (Velickovic et al., 2019; You et al., 2020), motif-aware methods (Hassani and Khasahmadi, 2020; Sun et al., 2021), augmentation of edge dropping, feature masking, and diffusion views (Qiu et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2021), and pre-training on large graph corpora (Hu et al., 2020; You et al., 2021). Benchmarks include OGB, Cora, Citeseer, and Pubmed (Hu et al., 2020; Sen et al., 2008), with evaluation F1 and transfer to link prediction (Grover and Leskovec, 2016; Zhang and Chen, 2018). However, reported gains frequently hinge on augmentation strength rather than intrinsic invariance. This observation motivates a closer examination of view design and its statistical side effects. We also revisit evaluation protocols to separate representation quality from classifier capacity. Our study introduces a controlled suite of synthetic and real graphs with calibrated perturbations. The methodology quantifies sensitivity to structural corruption and feature noise under matched budgets. In addition, we assess cross-dataset transfer to test claims of generality. The results inform practical choices for self-supervised training on sparse, noisy graphs. We release code and data splits to facilitate reproducible comparisons.
spans: Work on graph contrastive learning includes node-level objectives (Velickovic et al., 2019; You et al., 2020), motif-aware methods (Hassani and Khasahmadi, 2020; Sun et al., 2021), augmentation of edge dropping, feature masking, and diffusion views (Qiu et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2021), and pre-training on large graph corpora (Hu et al., 2020; You et al., 2021). Benchmarks include OGB, Cora, Citeseer, and Pubmed (Hu et al., 2020; Sen et al., 2008), with evaluation F1 and transfer to link prediction (Grover and Leskovec, 2016; Zhang and Chen, 2018).

document: Multilingual summarization aims to generate concise outputs across and between languages under constrained supervision. Cross-lingual settings amplify challenges in faithfulness, fluency, and content selection due to typological diversity. Pretrained sequence-to-sequence models reduce data requirements yet can memorize domain artifacts. Low-resource languages particularly suffer from coverage gaps in web-scale corpora. Transfer through shared subword vocabularies provides benefits but risks semantic drift across families. Evaluation practices remain inconsistent, especially for morphology-rich targets and mixed-domain inputs. To situate our study, we outline representative methods, datasets, and training practices from prior art. Cross-lingual summarization methods include mBART and mT5 fine-tuning (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021), multilingual denoising and gap-sentence pretraining (Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020), constrained decoding for faithfulness (Chen and Cardie, 2018), and extensions such as multilingual ROUGE (Lin, 4. Datasets frequently used are WikiLingua, XL-Sum, MLSUM, and XSum, with CNN/DailyMail as baselines (Ladhak et al., 2020; Hasan et al., 2021; Scialom et al., 2020; Narayan et al., 2018; Nallapati et al., 2016), and training leverages back-translation and pivoting (Sennrich et al., 2016). These ingredients frame typical pipelines used in research and industry deployments. Nonetheless, gap analyses often blur data scarcity from architectural limitations. We therefore examine where supervision is most effective within limited translation budgets. Our experiments contrast direct fine-tuning with parameter-efficient adaptation under fixed compute. We further explore calibration techniques to reduce hallucination without extensive reference sets. Finally, we discuss implications for practitioners building production-quality multilingual summarizers.
spans: Cross-lingual summarization methods include mBART and mT5 fine-tuning (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021), multilingual denoising and gap-sentence pretraining (Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020), constrained decoding for faithfulness (Chen and Cardie, 2018), and extensions such as multilingual ROUGE (Lin, 2004). Datasets frequently used are WikiLingua, XL-Sum, MLSUM, and XSum, with CNN/DailyMail as baselines (Ladhak et al., 2020; Hasan et al., 2021; Scialom et al., 2020; Narayan et al., 2018; Nallapati et al., 2016), and training leverages back-translation and pivoting (Sennrich et al., 2016).

document: Federated learning enables collaborative model training without centralizing raw data. This paradigm promises privacy benefits but introduces new attack surfaces and statistical challenges. Real-world deployments face device heterogeneity, non-IID distributions, and intermittent connectivity. Communication and privacy mechanisms must balance utility, scalability, and regulatory constraints. Federated learning research encompasses optimization like FedAvg and FedProx (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020), secure aggregation and cryptographic protocols (Bonawitz et al., 2017; So et al., 2022), and differential privacy via DP-SGD and Rényi DP accounting (Abadi et al., 2016; Mironov, 2017). Threat analyses study membership inference, model inversion, and gradient leakage, alongside poisoning attacks (Shokri et al., 2017; Fredrikson et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2019; Bagdasaryan et al., 2020), with evaluations on LEAF datasets such as FEMNIST and StackOverflow (Caldas et al., 2018). These strands shape prevailing assumptions about threats and defenses in distributed learning. Yet many evaluations ignore client sampling effects that mask privacy leakage in practice. We conduct analyses isolating gradient sparsity, clipping, and quantization under realistic participation. The study also measures privacy-utility frontiers using comparable budgets across datasets and models. Our framework harmonizes accounting assumptions and asynchronous update patterns. We report end-to-end costs that consider cryptography overheads and local computation limits. The findings highlight conditions where protections fail under moderate drift. We conclude with guidelines for deployment on resource-constrained devices. Code, configurations, and measurement scripts are publicly released.
spans: Federated learning research encompasses optimization like FedAvg and FedProx (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020), secure aggregation and cryptographic protocols (Bonawitz et al., 2017; So et al., 2022), and differential privacy via DP-SGD and Rényi DP accounting (Abadi et al., 2016; Mironov, 2017). Threat analyses study membership inference, model inversion, and gradient leakage, alongside poisoning attacks (Shokri et al., 2017; Fredrikson et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2019; Bagdasaryan et al., 2020), with evaluations on LEAF datasets such as FEMNIST and StackOverflow (Caldas et al., 2018).

document: Robots operating in unstructured environments must adapt to ambiguous goals and shifting preferences. Learning from humans offers scalable supervision beyond dense reward engineering. However, interfaces for feedback can be noisy, sporadic, and biased by cognitive load. Algorithms therefore need to reconcile sample efficiency with robustness to imperfect guidance. Moreover, benchmarks vary widely in embodiment, task granularity, and reset assumptions. We analyze supervision modalities encompassing comparisons, critiques, and demonstrations. Our focus is on how these signals compose with policy learning and safety constraints. To ground the discussion, we first summarize representative approaches and standard evaluations. Learning from human feedback includes preference-based RL (Christiano et al., 2017; Sadigh et al., 2017), policy shaping via TAMER and COACH (Knox and Stone, 2009; Cederborg et al., 2015), inverse RL formulations (Ng and Russell, 2000; Ziebart et al., 2008), and imitation through DAgger and offline pipelines (Ross et al., 2011; Fu et al., 2020). Benchmarks and datasets include Meta-World, D4RL, and Robomimic, with success rate, normalized score, and preference agreement reported (Yu et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2020; Mandlekar et al., 2021; Ibarz et al., 2018). This overview highlights the diversity of assumptions about teacher availability and expertise. We then study how supervision frequency interacts with exploration under fixed energy budgets. Experiments examine preference drift and latency using controlled human-in-the-loop simulations. Results inform interface design choices for reliable deployment on real manipulators. We provide practical checklists for collecting reproducible human feedback at scale.
spans: Learning from human feedback includes preference-based RL (Christiano et al., 2017; Sadigh et al., 2017), policy shaping via TAMER and COACH (Knox and Stone, 2009; Cederborg et al., 2015), inverse RL formulations (Ng and Russell, 2000; Ziebart et al., 2008), and imitation through DAgger and offline pipelines (Ross et al., 2011; Fu et al., 2020). Benchmarks and datasets include Meta-World, D4RL, and Robomimic, with success rate, normalized score, and preference agreement reported (Yu et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2020; Mandlekar et al., 2021; Ibarz et al., 2018).

document: Many scientific questions hinge on understanding cause and effect in dynamical systems. Observational time series provide partial evidence that must be reconciled with plausible mechanisms. Interventions are often infeasible, which elevates the role of algorithmic discovery from data alone. Yet temporal dependence and hidden confounding complicate identifiability and evaluation. Assumptions about stationarity, sparsity, and noise can yield conflicting conclusions across methods. We review modeling choices that govern learning and testing in this setting. Causal discovery for time series includes Granger causality and VAR tests (Granger, 1969; Lutkepohl, 2005), constraint-based procedures like PCMCI (Runge et al., 2019), score-based formulations such as NOTEARS and DYNOTEARS (Zheng et al., 2018; Pamfil et al., 2020), and neural approaches including TCDF and attention models (Nauta et al., 2019; Tank et al., 2018). Evaluations report Hamming distance, accuracy, and predictive fit on benchmarks like simulated SCMs, Tübingen cause-effect pairs, and datasets from finance and physiology (Mooij et al., 2016; Peters et al., 2017; Shimizu et al., 2011). These ingredients set the stage for assessing strengths and failure modes in realistic pipelines. We then examine how lag selection and filtering influence estimated graphs under resource constraints. A modular benchmarking harness allows swapping causal learners while preserving preprocessing. The analysis emphasizes dataset shift between synthetic processes and field data. We report sensitivity to sampling rate and intervention frequency in semi-synthetic scenarios. Our results clarify the trade-offs between predictive accuracy and structural fidelity. Recommendations are provided to guide practitioners toward robust causal modeling of temporal phenomena.
spans: Causal discovery for time series includes Granger causality and VAR tests (Granger, 1969; Lutkepohl, 2005), constraint-based procedures like PCMCI (Runge et al., 2019), score-based formulations such as NOTEARS and DYNOTEARS (Zheng et al., 2018; Pamfil et al., 2020), and neural approaches including TCDF and attention models (Nauta et al., 2019; Tank et al., 2018). Evaluations report Hamming distance, accuracy, and predictive fit on benchmarks like simulated SCMs, Tübingen cause-effect pairs, and datasets from finance and physiology (Mooij et al., 2016; Peters et al., 2017; Shimizu et al., 2011).

document: Temporal knowledge graphs capture evolving relations among entities observed as timestamped facts. Modeling such dynamics is essential for forecasting future events and reasoning over incomplete histories. Early temporal knowledge graph models emphasize static embeddings extended with time-aware components, including TransE and TransH variants (Bordes et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2014), TTransE (Jiang et al., 2016), HyTE (Dasgupta et al., 2018), and diachronic representations such as DE-SimplE and ATiSE (Goel et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2019), alongside benchmark datasets like ICEWS14, ICEWS05-15, YAGO11k and GDELT. Despite rich resources, predictive accuracy remains sensitive to temporal granularity and event sparsity. Prior work also notes challenges in capturing long-range dependencies and nonstationary patterns across time. In downstream applications, temporal reasoning drives forecasting for finance, recommendation, and cyber-security. However, existing architectures frequently trade expressivity for scalability when deployed at web scale. Self-attention and recurrent modules each incur distinct costs under continuous-time assumptions. This motivates examining representations that share parameters across time while preserving event causality. We study how inductive biases interact with observational gaps under realistic temporal noise. We further analyze calibration, uncertainty estimates, and robustness against distributional shifts. Graph neural approaches introduce temporal message passing or recurrent updates, with TGAT and TGN capturing event streams (Xu et al., 2020; Rossi et al., 2020), DyRep modeling dynamic interactions (Trivedi et al., 2019), Know-Evolve employing temporal point processes (Trivedi et al., 2017), and R-GCN extensions for time granularity (Schlichtkrull et al., 2018; Seo et al., 2019), evaluated on datasets such as ICEWS18, GDELT, WIKI-Math, and Temporal-WN18, coupled with negative sampling and contrastive objectives. These systems provide complementary building blocks yet exhibit heterogeneous assumptions about time. Our analysis clarifies when discrete bins, intervals, or continuous clocks better preserve signal. We also benchmark data-efficient training regimes with limited supervision across periods. Finally, we outline open challenges in evaluation protocols for temporal generalization.
spans: Early temporal knowledge graph models emphasize static embeddings extended with time-aware components, including TransE and TransH variants (Bordes et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2014), TTransE (Jiang et al., 2016), HyTE (Dasgupta et al., 2018), and diachronic representations such as DE-SimplE and ATiSE (Goel et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2019), alongside benchmark datasets like ICEWS14, ICEWS05-15, YAGO11k and GDELT.

Graph neural approaches introduce temporal message passing or recurrent updates, with TGAT and TGN capturing event streams (Xu et al., 2020; Rossi et al., 2020), DyRep modeling dynamic interactions (Trivedi et al., 2019), Know-Evolve employing temporal point processes (Trivedi et al., 2017), and R-GCN extensions for time granularity (Schlichtkrull et al., 2018; Seo et al., 2019), evaluated on datasets such as ICEWS18, GDELT, WIKI-Math, and Temporal-WN18, coupled with negative sampling and contrastive objectives.

document: Sentiment analysis across modalities seeks to infer attitudes from language, prosody, and visual cues. The interplay among modalities raises issues of synchronization, noise, and differing temporal resolutions. Early pipelines relied on handcrafted features but struggled with domain transfer and speaker variability. Foundational multimodal sentiment benchmarks and models include CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI with aligned text, audio, and vision (Zadeh et al., 2016; Zadeh et al., 2018), IEMOCAP for acted emotions (Busso et al., 2008), CH-SIMS for Chinese sentiment (Yu et al., 2020), and early fusion, late fusion, and tensor fusion architectures (Poria et al., 2017; Zadeh et al., 2017; Mai et al., 2020). While these resources support progress, heterogeneity in annotation and alignment complicates comparison. Recent advances leverage deep contextual encoders that better capture semantics and paralinguistic signals. However, robustness to accents, lighting, and occlusions remains limited under real-world conditions. Furthermore, interpretability is a recurring concern for high-capacity multimodal architectures. We focus on consistent training protocols and fair evaluation sets to assess generalization. Our framework isolates modality contributions using controlled masking and perturbations. We also quantify calibration and uncertainty under covariate shift and missing channels. Transformer-based models extend cross-modal attention and representation disentanglement, with MulT introducing modality-specific attentions (Tsai et al., 2019), MISA promoting invariant subspaces (Hazarika et al., 2020), MAG-BERT incorporating acoustic-visual guidance (Rahman et al., 2020), and cross-modal pretraining drawing on CLIP, VisualBERT, and ViLBERT (Radford et al., 2021; Li et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2019), alongside contrastive objectives and pseudo-labeling on unannotated videos and speech as well as curriculum strategies for stable multimodal alignment. Despite these developments, reproducibility barriers persist due to undocumented preprocessing and code drift. We provide standardized splits and scripts to reduce variance across runs and seeds. Additionally, we analyze the trade-offs between alignment precision and compute overhead. Finally, we summarize open questions in scalable, privacy-preserving multimodal sentiment learning.
spans: Foundational multimodal sentiment benchmarks and models include CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI with aligned text, audio, and vision (Zadeh et al., 2016; Zadeh et al., 2018), IEMOCAP for acted emotions (Busso et al., 2008), CH-SIMS for Chinese sentiment (Yu et al., 2020), and early fusion, late fusion, and tensor fusion architectures (Poria et al., 2017; Zadeh et al., 2017; Mai et al., 2020).

Transformer-based models extend cross-modal attention and representation disentanglement, with MulT introducing modality-specific attentions (Tsai et al., 2019), MISA promoting invariant subspaces (Hazarika et al., 2020), MAG-BERT incorporating acoustic-visual guidance (Rahman et al., 2020), and cross-modal pretraining drawing on CLIP, VisualBERT, and ViLBERT (Radford et al., 2021; Li et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2019), alongside contrastive objectives and pseudo-labeling on unannotated videos and speech as well as curriculum strategies for stable multimodal alignment.

document: Federated learning enables collaborative model training without centralizing raw data across devices. This paradigm promises stronger privacy and regulatory compliance while leveraging distributed compute. Yet practical deployments face statistical heterogeneity, systems volatility, and communication bottlenecks. Classical federated optimizers address client heterogeneity through aggregation and variance reduction, including FedAvg and FedProx (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020), SCAFFOLD and FedNova to mitigate drift (Karimireddy et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020), and evaluations on FEMNIST, Shakespeare, CIFAR-10, and StackOverflow benchmarks using non-IID partitions and partial participation protocols with compression, quantization, and adaptive client selection heuristics. Such diversity creates optimization instability and fairness concerns among clients. Communication-efficient methods reduce uplink load but risk objective inconsistency across rounds. Moreover, personalization strategies must balance global utility with local adaptation. We examine how algorithmic choices interact with participation dynamics and resource limits. Our study characterizes convergence, fairness, and robustness under controlled non-IID regimes. We also evaluate energy and latency footprints for edge and mobile devices. Threat models include honest-but-curious servers, malicious clients, and byzantine aggregators. Privacy-preserving training incorporates record-level noise addition and cryptographic protocols, covering DP-SGD with moments or Rényi accountants (Abadi et al., 2016; Mironov, 2017), secure aggregation for encrypted updates (Bonawitz et al., 2017), client-level DP via sampling and clipping (Geyer et al., 2017; Balle et al., 2018), teacher-student frameworks like PATE (Papernot et al., 2017), and analyses of privacy amplification, composition bounds, and utility trade-offs across realistic participation rates under heterogeneity, stragglers, and adversarial behaviors. Despite progress, practitioners lack guidance on safe configurations under tight budgets. We provide principled defaults and diagnostics for auditing privacy-utility frontiers. Finally, we discuss open benchmarks that reflect real-world churn and device diversity. These insights inform deployment choices for privacy-aware distributed learning at scale.
spans: Classical federated optimizers address client heterogeneity through aggregation and variance reduction, including FedAvg and FedProx (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020), SCAFFOLD and FedNova to mitigate drift (Karimireddy et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020), and evaluations on FEMNIST, Shakespeare, CIFAR-10, and StackOverflow benchmarks using non-IID partitions and partial participation protocols with compression, quantization, and adaptive client selection heuristics.

Privacy-preserving training incorporates record-level noise addition and cryptographic protocols, covering DP-SGD with moments or Rényi accountants (Abadi et al., 2016; Mironov, 2017), secure aggregation for encrypted updates (Bonawitz et al., 2017), client-level DP via sampling and clipping (Geyer et al., 2017; Balle et al., 2018), teacher-student frameworks like PATE (Papernot et al., 2017), and analyses of privacy amplification, composition bounds, and utility trade-offs across realistic participation rates under heterogeneity, stragglers, and adversarial behaviors.

document: Program synthesis from natural language promises to accelerate software development and education. However, language ambiguity and underspecified intent pose fundamental challenges for code generation. Systems must align surface descriptions with latent APIs, types, and execution semantics. Data-to-code and NL-to-code research spans sequence transduction and grammar-aware decoding, including Seq2Tree and TranX parsers (Dong and Lapata, 2018; Yin and Neubig, 2018), pre-trained models such as CodeBERT, CodeT5, and PLBART (Feng et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Ahmad et al., 2021), and benchmarks like CoNaLa, CONCODE, Spider, and APPS for varying language and specification styles and diverse execution environments. Despite significant gains, models often memorize snippets rather than infer algorithmic structure. Moreover, practical tasks require integration with libraries, environments, and version constraints. Evaluation therefore benefits from test-driven metrics beyond lexical similarity. We examine how prompt formatting and retrieval affect grounding to available tools. Our study probes generalization across domains, tasks, and resource budgets. We analyze error modes including type misuse, naming drift, and off-by-one loops. We also consider safety risks such as insecure patterns and license leakage. Neural code generation further explores executability-aware training and constrained decoding, with type-driven generation and syntax masks (Raychev et al., 2014; Krishnamurthy et al., 2017), execution-guided decoding for SQL and Python (Wang et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019), large language models evaluated on HumanEval and MBPP (Chen et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021), and unit-test feedback, beam reranking, and repair heuristics for improving functional correctness under limited prompts and tool access constraints. These ingredients illustrate the breadth of approaches without resolving deployment caveats. We consequently assess robustness to environment shifts and package updates. Finally, we discuss interactive revisions that combine intent elicitation with verification. The goal is to clarify priorities for reliable, responsible code generation in practice.
spans: Data-to-code and NL-to-code research spans sequence transduction and grammar-aware decoding, including Seq2Tree and TranX parsers (Dong and Lapata, 2018; Yin and Neubig, 2018), pre-trained models such as CodeBERT, CodeT5, and PLBART (Feng et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Ahmad et al., 2021), and benchmarks like CoNaLa, CONCODE, Spider, and APPS for varying language and specification styles and diverse execution environments.

Neural code generation further explores executability-aware training and constrained decoding, with type-driven generation and syntax masks (Raychev et al., 2014; Krishnamurthy et al., 2017), execution-guided decoding for SQL and Python (Wang et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019), large language models evaluated on HumanEval and MBPP (Chen et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021), and unit-test feedback, beam reranking, and repair heuristics for improving functional correctness under limited prompts and tool access constraints.

document: Domain shifts degrade visual recognition systems when training and deployment distributions diverge. Semantic segmentation is particularly sensitive due to pixel-level supervision and structured outputs. Unsupervised domain adaptation for vision has explored feature alignment and adversarial objectives, including DANN, ADDA, and MCD (Ganin et al., 2016; Tzeng et al., 2017; Saito et al., 2018), correlation alignment via DeepCORAL (Sun and Saenko, 2016), and image-level translation such as CyCADA (Hoffman et al., 2018), commonly evaluated on GTA5→Cityscapes and SYNTHIA→Cityscapes benchmarks with synthetic-to-real shifts and challenging weather variations. Although these strategies reduce discrepancies, strong residual gaps remain in urban driving scenes. Label noise and class imbalance further complicate adaptation across rare categories. Efficiency constraints also limit the applicability of heavy generative components. Moreover, evaluation protocols vary widely across works and hinder fair comparison. We focus on data-efficient adaptation that preserves safety-critical performance. Our analysis disentangles appearance, frequency, and context cues affecting transfer. We measure robustness to sensor degradation and adverse weather encountered in practice. We also examine uncertainty estimation to flag unreliable predictions after shift. More recent work studies source-free and test-time adaptation, employing batch-norm updates, entropy minimization, and pseudo-label self-training, with TENT and CoTTA addressing online shifts (Wang et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2020), SHOT transferring via information maximization and prototypes (Liang et al., 2020), and domain stylization and frequency mixing for robustness (Jackson et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2020), evaluated on Cityscapes-C, ACDC, BDD100K, and WildDash under corruptions and seasonal changes and illumination shifts. Despite advances, deployment conditions exhibit nonstationary and compound shifts over time. We assess continual and on-the-fly updates compatible with real-time constraints. Finally, we study failure cases and calibration to guide risk-aware decisions. The broader aim is practical adaptation strategies for autonomous perception systems.
spans: Unsupervised domain adaptation for vision has explored feature alignment and adversarial objectives, including DANN, ADDA, and MCD (Ganin et al., 2016; Tzeng et al., 2017; Saito et al., 2018), correlation alignment via DeepCORAL (Sun and Saenko, 2016), and image-level translation such as CyCADA (Hoffman et al., 2018), commonly evaluated on GTA5→Cityscapes and SYNTHIA→Cityscapes benchmarks with synthetic-to-real shifts and challenging weather variations.

More recent work studies source-free and test-time adaptation, employing batch-norm updates, entropy minimization, and pseudo-label self-training, with TENT and CoTTA addressing online shifts (Wang et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2020), SHOT transferring via information maximization and prototypes (Liang et al., 2020), and domain stylization and frequency mixing for robustness (Jackson et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2020), evaluated on Cityscapes-C, ACDC, BDD100K, and WildDash under corruptions and seasonal changes and illumination shifts.

document: Multimodal sentiment analysis seeks to infer attitudes by integrating language, vision, and acoustics. The field has progressed from isolated feature engineering to end to end learning with pretrained components. Early studies emphasized alignment between streams to avoid temporal drift. Recent work emphasizes robustness under noisy or missing modalities. Industry interest has grown due to applications in media analytics and conversational agents. Despite progress, modeling dependencies across modalities remains challenging. Prior surveys describe fusion taxonomies that motivate comparative evaluation. Joint training often improves recognition but can amplify dataset biases. Cross modal attention has emerged as a practical inductive bias. However, many architectures still struggle to handle variable length speech segments. Further, deployment constraints require compact models with stable calibration. These considerations frame the scope of this study. Early multimodal sentiment benchmarks such as CMU-MOSI (Zadeh et al., 2016), CMU-MOSEI (Zadeh et al., 2018), and MELD (Poria et al., 2019) provide aligned audio, visual, and text streams. Fusion strategies include early fusion with concatenated features (Ngiam et al., 2011), tensor fusion networks (Zadeh et al., 2017), and graph-based interactions (Zadeh et al., 2018b). Pretrained encoders like BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), Wav2Vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020), and ResNet (He et al., 2016) are commonly used for modality-specific representations. Evaluation commonly reports F1 and MAE on MOSI and MOSEI and accuracy on MELD, with additional robustness checks under missing modality protocols (Liu et al., 2021) and noisy audio settings (Tsai et al., 2019). Benchmarks. While these resources enable standardization, they also encourage narrow optimization on limited domains. Multilingual coverage remains limited for both speech and text streams. Visual frames often capture facial expressions but ignore gestural context from the torso and hands. Recent multimodal transformers improve cross stream reasoning yet increase memory pressure substantially. Distillation and pruning can mitigate cost but risk degrading affective nuance. Handling missing modalities during inference remains an open problem for practitioners. A complementary concern is annotation subjectivity in sentiment labels. Disagreement among annotators reflects genuine ambiguity rather than mere noise. Modeling uncertainty alongside predictions can support better downstream decisions. Calibration metrics help quantify reliability under distribution shift. However, calibration trade offs interact with fusion strategies in nontrivial ways. This interaction motivates careful assessment under realistic failure modes. In addition to accuracy, latency and energy consumption shape acceptable designs. Edge devices impose strict budgets that conflict with large cross modal stacks. Compression tailored to heterogeneous signals may preserve critical cues. Training regimens that mix self supervision with labeled objectives can leverage abundant raw video and audio. Domain adaptation is necessary when transferring from acted corpora to in the wild data. Ethical considerations include privacy, consent, and potential stigmatization. We therefore adopt a comprehensive evaluation that reflects deployment realities. Our comparisons emphasize robustness under ablations of each sensory stream. We also analyze calibration and uncertainty to illuminate confidence allocation. The findings inform principled design choices for multimodal affective systems.
spans: Early multimodal sentiment benchmarks such as CMU-MOSI (Zadeh et al., 2016), CMU-MOSEI (Zadeh et al., 2018), and MELD (Poria et al., 2019) provide aligned audio, visual, and text streams. Fusion strategies include early fusion with concatenated features (Ngiam et al., 2011), tensor fusion networks (Zadeh et al., 2017), and graph-based interactions (Zadeh et al., 2018b). Pretrained encoders like BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), Wav2Vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020), and ResNet (He et al., 2016) are commonly used for modality-specific representations. Evaluation commonly reports F1 and MAE on MOSI and MOSEI and accuracy on MELD, with additional robustness checks under missing modality protocols (Liu et al., 2021) and noisy audio settings (Tsai et al., 2019). Benchmarks.

document: Urban mobility systems require accurate short term forecasts to support routing and control. Data from fixed sensors and probe vehicles provides dense temporal coverage but uneven spatial density. Graph modeling aligns naturally with road topology and flow constraints. Early linear models struggled to capture nonlinear propagation of congestion. Deep spatiotemporal networks partially address this gap with learned dynamics. Nevertheless, stability and interpretability remain central concerns for operators. Exogenous signals like weather and events introduce shifts that stress purely autoregressive models. Incorporating side information can reduce error yet risks leakage in operational pipelines. Robust evaluation must therefore reflect closed loop deployment conditions. Additionally, missing data from faulty sensors is routine rather than exceptional. Imputation tied to topology can mitigate bias in historical aggregates. These considerations guide the methodological design space. Traffic forecasting benchmarks frequently rely on sensor networks such as METR-LA and PEMS-BAY (Li et al., 2018), PeMSD7 (Song et al., 2020), and PeMSD4/8 (Guo et al., 2019). Spatiotemporal architectures include diffusion convolutional recurrent networks DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), spatiotemporal graph convolution STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and attention based ASTGCN (Guo et al., 2019). Later developments explore multivariate time series graph learners like MTGNN (Wu et al., 2020), dynamic graph discovery with GTS (Shang et al., 2021), and transformer encoders with temporal positional biases (Cideron et al., 2022). Evaluation commonly reports MAE, RMSE, and MAPE across horizons 15, 30, and 60 minutes, using fixed train validation test splits and z score normalization protocols. Despite standardization, reported gains often depend on bespoke preprocessing choices. Differences in detrending and scaling can overshadow architectural effects. Reproducibility thus benefits from transparent pipelines and shared code. Memory usage also scales with graph size, limiting city wide deployment without partitioning. Learned graph structures can adapt to latent correlations but may drift under shifts. Detecting such drift early helps maintain predictive fidelity. Uncertainty quantification is increasingly valued by traffic managers. Calibrated intervals allow conservative control when congestion risk spikes. Methods based on ensembles or heteroscedastic objectives provide complementary tradeoffs. However, interval sharpness declines when exogenous shock features are unavailable. Sensor maintenance policies interact with algorithmic robustness in subtle ways. Longitudinal studies are needed to characterize degradation over seasons. Fairness and equity also surface when predictions inform pricing or policing. Biased errors can amplify burdens on already congested neighborhoods. Auditable models with interpretable attributions enhance accountability. Simple baselines remain important anchors for performance claims. Benchmarks that incorporate realistic missingness patterns better reflect operations. Evaluation under closed loop receding horizon control clarifies practical value. Our study emphasizes reproducibility and robustness across diverse urban topologies. We implement a unified data pipeline aligned with operational constraints. The models are assessed under missing data and distribution shift regimes. We analyze calibration alongside cost aware metrics relevant to city stakeholders. The results inform deployment strategies for reliable predictive traffic systems.
spans: Traffic forecasting benchmarks frequently rely on sensor networks such as METR-LA and PEMS-BAY (Li et al., 2018), PeMSD7 (Song et al., 2020), and PeMSD4/8 (Guo et al., 2019). Spatiotemporal architectures include diffusion convolutional recurrent networks DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), spatiotemporal graph convolution STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and attention based ASTGCN (Guo et al., 2019). Later developments explore multivariate time series graph learners like MTGNN (Wu et al., 2020), dynamic graph discovery with GTS (Shang et al., 2021), and transformer encoders with temporal positional biases (Cideron et al., 2022). Evaluation commonly reports MAE, RMSE, and MAPE across horizons 15, 30, and 60 minutes, using fixed train validation test splits and z score normalization protocols.

document: Healthcare institutions increasingly seek collaborative learning without centralizing data. Regulatory and ethical constraints motivate privacy preserving training paradigms. Federated learning allows models to learn across sites while keeping data local. Medical imaging offers a high value testbed due to abundant modalities. However, cross site heterogeneity complicates optimization and evaluation. Imaging protocols, devices, and populations vary significantly across hospitals. Communication efficiency is a practical bottleneck for clinical networks. Limited uplink capacity and intermittent connectivity affect synchronization. Asynchronous updates and compression can mitigate overhead at potential accuracy costs. Moreover, secure aggregation introduces additional latency and computation. Trust models influence what telemetry can be shared beyond gradients. These factors shape the feasible design space for deployments. Federated learning in medical imaging has been studied using datasets such as BraTS for brain tumor segmentation (Menze et al., 2015), ISIC for dermoscopic lesions (Codella et al., 2019), and CheXpert or MIMIC-CXR for chest radiography (Irvin et al., 2019; Johnson et al., 2019). Optimization methods include FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), FedProx (Li et al., 2020), SCAFFOLD (Karimireddy et al., 2020), and adaptive FedOpt variants (Reddi et al., 2021). Personalization approaches explore FedBN (Li et al., 2021), meta learning based pFedMe (Dinh et al., 2020), and local adaptation layers for site specific distribution shifts. Privacy mechanisms span secure aggregation (Bonawitz et al., 2017), differential privacy via DP-SGD (Abadi et al., 2016), and homomorphic encryption based protocols for protected updates deployment. Despite methodological breadth, clinical validation often lags behind technical advances. Prospective studies are rare due to coordination overhead and risk management. Distribution drift across seasons and policy changes can erode performance. Federated monitoring that tracks calibration and fairness can alert operators to issues. Label scarcity in some sites impedes personalization effectiveness. Weak supervision from reports or heuristics can partially close this gap. Another challenge is privacy budgeting under repeated training rounds. Composition of privacy loss must account for hyperparameter sweeps and audits. Hybrid protocols can combine secure aggregation with local differential privacy. Such combinations raise questions about compatibility with personalization. Hardware support for encryption influences feasibility in resource constrained clinics. Transparent governance is needed to align incentives across institutions. Evaluation should encompass utility, privacy, and systems costs. Beyond accuracy, clinicians value interpretability and robustness to artifacts. Reporting calibration gives insight into risk aware decision thresholds. Site wise breakdowns reveal where transfer improves or harms outcomes. Stress tests with out of scanner artifacts verify resilience in practice. Releasing standardized pipelines can accelerate reproducible progress. Our study targets heterogeneity, personalization, and privacy under operational constraints. We compare optimizers and normalization strategies across realistic hospital mixes. We incorporate communication budgets to reflect common clinical networks. The analysis highlights tradeoffs between utility and regulatory compliance. Recommendations are derived to guide procurement and trustworthy deployment.
spans: Federated learning in medical imaging has been studied using datasets such as BraTS for brain tumor segmentation (Menze et al., 2015), ISIC for dermoscopic lesions (Codella et al., 2019), and CheXpert or MIMIC-CXR for chest radiography (Irvin et al., 2019; Johnson et al., 2019). Optimization methods include FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), FedProx (Li et al., 2020), SCAFFOLD (Karimireddy et al., 2020), and adaptive FedOpt variants (Reddi et al., 2021). Personalization approaches explore FedBN (Li et al., 2021), meta learning based pFedMe (Dinh et al., 2020), and local adaptation layers for site specific distribution shifts. Privacy mechanisms span secure aggregation (Bonawitz et al., 2017), differential privacy via DP-SGD (Abadi et al., 2016), and homomorphic encryption based protocols for protected updates deployment.

document: Semi supervised learning seeks to exploit large unlabeled corpora with minimal annotation. In text classification, unlabeled web data is abundant but noisy. Consistency regularization and pseudo labeling are two dominant paradigms. Pretrained language models change the bias variance tradeoff of these schemes. Hyperparameter sensitivity and domain shift remain significant hurdles. This section reviews relevant progress and evaluation practice. Early methods relied on generative assumptions and self training. Modern approaches operationalize cluster and smoothness principles with strong augmentations. Aligning augmentations with linguistic structure is still an open question. Noise rates in pseudo labels interact with temperature scaling and thresholds. Warm starts from supervised checkpoints reduce instability. However, overfitting to small labeled sets can still occur. Semi-supervised text classification commonly evaluates on benchmarks such as AG News, IMDb, Yelp Polarity, and Amazon Reviews (Zhang et al., 2015; Maas et al., 2011; McAuley and Leskovec, 2013). Representative methods include Virtual Adversarial Training VAT (Miyato et al., 2017), Mean Teacher temporal ensembling (Tarvainen and Valpola, 2017), MixMatch and RemixMatch adaptations for NLP (Berthelot et al., 2019; 2020), and FixMatch style pseudo labeling with confidence thresholds (Sohn et al., 2020). Unsupervised data augmentation UDA applies back translation or TF-IDF word replacements to enforce consistency (Xie et al., 2020). Recent approaches leverage pretrained language models with consistency losses, entropy minimization, and confidence based sampling across unlabeled corpora drawn from Wikipedia, OpenWebText, and newswire for categorization. Datasets like RCV1 and Yahoo Answers. While these ingredients recur, empirical outcomes vary widely across domains. Legal and financial text often breaks common augmentation assumptions. Back translation can distort named entities and structured fields. Mixup on token embeddings may blur sentiment cues. Curriculum strategies can stage unlabeled data to control drift. Data filters grounded in perplexity or redundancy help prune irrelevant samples. Evaluation should consider label efficiency and compute efficiency jointly. Reporting calibration clarifies reliability under class imbalance. Open set exposure tests resilience to unseen categories. Reproducible pipelines are essential given sensitivity to random seeds. Strong supervised baselines must accompany semi supervised claims. Cross domain adaptation reveals whether gains persist beyond curated corpora. We examine how augmentations interact with pretrained encoders and thresholds. The analysis spans low label and medium label regimes. We compare task specific token masking with back translation and synonyms. We study ramp up schedules for loss weighting and temperature. We evaluate fairness across demographic attributes when available. We report accuracy, F1, and calibration alongside training cost. Our findings provide guidance on balancing noise, diversity, and compute. We distill practical recipes that travel across domains. We highlight open challenges in augmentation design and evaluation. The discussion outlines priorities for robust semi supervised text learning.
spans: Semi-supervised text classification commonly evaluates on benchmarks such as AG News, IMDb, Yelp Polarity, and Amazon Reviews (Zhang et al., 2015; Maas et al., 2011; McAuley and Leskovec, 2013). Representative methods include Virtual Adversarial Training VAT (Miyato et al., 2017), Mean Teacher temporal ensembling (Tarvainen and Valpola, 2017), MixMatch and RemixMatch adaptations for NLP (Berthelot et al., 2019; 2020), and FixMatch style pseudo labeling with confidence thresholds (Sohn et al., 2020). Unsupervised data augmentation UDA applies back translation or TF-IDF word replacements to enforce consistency (Xie et al., 2020). Recent approaches leverage pretrained language models with consistency losses, entropy minimization, and confidence based sampling across unlabeled corpora drawn from Wikipedia, OpenWebText, and newswire for categorization. Datasets like RCV1 and Yahoo Answers.

document: Recommender systems increasingly optimize long term engagement rather than immediate clicks. Reinforcement learning offers a principled framework for sequential decision making. However, exploration is costly and risky in production platforms. As a result, most research is conducted in the offline setting. Logged bandit data and simulators serve as proxies for interaction. Handling bias and nonstationarity is essential for credible evaluation. Recommendations are often presented as slates rather than single items. Slate level constraints complicate credit assignment across positions. User satisfaction depends on diversity, novelty, and fairness metrics. These objectives conflict with pure click maximization. Policy learning must therefore account for multiple goals and constraints. Robustness under delayed feedback and exposure bias is also crucial. Research on reinforcement learning for recommendation references datasets including MovieLens 1M and 20M (Harper and Konstan, 2016), the Yahoo! R6A news click logs (Li et al., 2011), RecSys Challenge datasets, and KuaiRec or MIND for large scale interactions (Gao et al., 2020). Algorithmic baselines span policy gradient REINFORCE (Williams, 1992), value based DQN variants for slates such as SlateQ (Ie et al., 2019), deterministic actor critic DDPG for continuous features (Lillicrap et al., 2016), and multi objective ranking with constrained bandits (Agarwal et al., 2014). Offline evaluation methods use inverse propensity scoring IPS, self normalized IPS, doubly robust estimators, fitted Q evaluation, and Criteo off policy benchmarks (Dudik et al., 2014; Le et al., 2019; Fu et al., 2021). For validation. Despite progress, offline metrics may not predict online impact reliably. Reward misspecification can mask churn or fatigue effects. Counterfactual estimators degrade under extrapolation and covariate shift. Simulators provide safe exploration but risk model bias. Balancing exploitation and exploration requires principled constraints. Safety layers can veto actions that violate content policies. Personalization granularity affects both value and stability. High capacity user embeddings adapt quickly but may overfit sparse histories. Meta learning can transfer structure across cohorts with similar intents. Contextual modeling of session dynamics captures short term preferences. Temporal discounting moderates the influence of delayed outcomes. Multi objective optimization clarifies tradeoffs among goals. Fairness considerations require constraints on exposure and opportunity. Calibration helps align reported uncertainty with observed outcomes. Diversification reduces redundancy and encourages discovery. Interpretability fosters trust among stakeholders and content providers. Reproducible pipelines help vet improvements before live testing. Public leaderboards should include risk aware metrics and resource costs. Our study examines off policy learning for slates under practical constraints. We analyze how estimators behave under varying propensities and clipping. We compare ranking architectures for stability and sample efficiency. The experiments include ablations on slate size and objectives. We summarize guidance for safe and effective reinforcement learning recommenders.

Urban traffic forecasting under irregular sensor topology remains a central challenge for intelligent transportation systems. Accurate predictions must integrate spatial dependencies among roads and temporal dynamics arising from congestion waves and incidents. Despite progress, current models often trade expressiveness for efficiency, hindering deployment in city-scale networks. Graph neural networks have emerged as a compelling paradigm, yet their receptive fields and dynamic adjacency modeling vary widely across implementations. Moreover, reported gains are sensitive to dataset splits and look-ahead horizons, complicating fair comparison across studies. Prior studies encompass diffusion convolutional networks, attention-based spatiotemporal models, and benchmarks such as METR-LA, PeMS-BAY, PeMSD7, and TaxiBJ, alongside sequence models like STGCN and DCRNN, graph transformers (Ying et al., 2021), temporal convolutional networks, and hybrid pipelines integrating ARIMA baselines and LSTMs (Zhang et al., 2017; Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2019), and additional baselines. However, most evaluations emphasize static graphs and station-level series, leaving robustness to topology shifts and sensor outages underexplored. We focus on this robustness axis and study how uncertainty-aware graph construction mediates performance when sensors fail or new links appear. Our approach integrates probabilistic edge sampling with lightweight diffusion to bound worst-case error at fixed latency. We provide a standardized protocol for rolling-origin validation across multiple horizons and controlled topology perturbations. Experiments across four public datasets demonstrate consistent gains in degraded networks without sacrificing real-time throughput.
spans: Prior studies encompass diffusion convolutional networks, attention-based spatiotemporal models, and benchmarks such as METR-LA, PeMS-BAY, PeMSD7, and TaxiBJ, alongside sequence models like STGCN and DCRNN, graph transformers (Ying et al., 2021), temporal convolutional networks, and hybrid pipelines integrating ARIMA baselines and LSTMs (Zhang et al., 2017; Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2019), and additional baselines.

document: Abstractive summarization aims to condense documents into fluent, faithful digests that preserve salient information. While neural models achieve impressive ROUGE, they can hallucinate unsupported facts and struggle with cross-lingual generalization. Practical deployments demand controllable generation and calibrated confidence to support editorial workflows. Current multilingual systems often rely on English-centric pretraining, limiting coverage for low-resource languages and domain-specific styles. Moreover, standard metrics reward n-gram overlap rather than factual grounding, obscuring model weaknesses in reliability. Prior work spans pointer-generator networks, transformer encoder–decoders such as BART, PEGASUS, T5, and ProphetNet, multilingual variants like mBART-50 and mT5, and evaluations on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, NEWSROOM, Gigaword, and WikiLingua, with training regimens that leverage sentence reordering, gap-sentence objectives, and reinforcement learning from ROUGE (See et al., 2017; Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020), and MASS. These developments have established strong baselines but leave open questions about factual control across languages and domains. We address this gap by coupling entity-grounded planning with multilingual consistency regularization during fine-tuning. The planner predicts cross-lingual entity and relation frames, which guide a constrained decoder to avoid unsupported claims. We further introduce reference-free factuality scoring that correlates with human judgments across languages. Experiments on three news corpora and two low-resource benchmarks show improved faithfulness with minimal loss in fluency.
spans: Prior work spans pointer-generator networks, transformer encoder–decoders such as BART, PEGASUS, T5, and ProphetNet, multilingual variants like mBART-50 and mT5, and evaluations on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, NEWSROOM, Gigaword, and WikiLingua, with training regimens that leverage sentence reordering, gap-sentence objectives, and reinforcement learning from ROUGE (See et al., 2017; Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020), and MASS.

document: Automated segmentation of 3D medical images is crucial for diagnosis, treatment planning, and longitudinal monitoring. Brain tumor delineation, in particular, demands precision across heterogeneous scanners and acquisition protocols. However, models often degrade under distribution shifts driven by site-specific reconstruction and annotation variability. Recent self-supervised and domain generalization methods mitigate some variability but increase training complexity and compute. Furthermore, many pipelines rely on heavy test-time augmentation that limits throughput in clinical workflows. Existing approaches include U-Net and V-Net architectures, residual and attention U-Nets, and strong baselines such as nnU-Net and DeepLabV3+, evaluated on BraTS, ISLES, LiTS, and the Medical Segmentation Decathlon, with augmentations like MixUp, CutMix, and intensity standardization, and training protocols leveraging Dice and focal losses (Ronneberger et al., 2015; Milletari et al., 2016; Isensee et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2017). While these systems deliver high Dice scores, performance can be brittle when contrast changes or small lesions appear. We explore a calibration-first design that prioritizes reliable uncertainty estimates and robust small-structure recovery. Our method couples scale-consistent normalization with topology-preserving losses to stabilize predictions across scanners. We introduce a lightweight adaptation module enabling rapid on-site tuning without full retraining. On multi-center studies, the approach reduces false positives and improves boundary accuracy under severe domain shift.
spans: Existing approaches include U-Net and V-Net architectures, residual and attention U-Nets, and strong baselines such as nnU-Net and DeepLabV3+, evaluated on BraTS, ISLES, LiTS, and the Medical Segmentation Decathlon, with augmentations like MixUp, CutMix, and intensity standardization, and training protocols leveraging Dice and focal losses (Ronneberger et al., 2015; Milletari et al., 2016; Isensee et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2017).

document: Modern recommender systems must personalize sequences of items while respecting feedback delays and exposure constraints. Static ranking models optimize myopic click metrics and can induce filter bubbles that harm long-term utility. Reinforcement learning offers a principled framework to trade short-term reward for user retention and diversity. Yet deploying RL in recommendation is challenging due to confounding, partial observability, and safety constraints. Off-policy evaluation and constrained learning are therefore critical components of any practical pipeline. Research covers contextual bandits and slate-aware RL with DQN, DDQN, DDPG, and policy-gradient methods, sequence recommenders such as GRU4Rec, SASRec, and BERT4Rec, simulators and logs like RecSim, OpenBanditDataset, and RecoGym, and benchmarks including MovieLens, Amazon Reviews, KuaiRec, and RecSys Challenge datasets (Hidasi et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2019; Ie et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019; Takeda and Joachims, 2020). Despite this breadth, reliable evaluation under distribution shift and position bias remains unresolved. We develop a counterfactual learner with pessimistic reward shaping to bound regret under logging mismatch. A structural propensity model corrects for position exposure while preserving variance through shrinkage priors. We further enforce catalog and user-level constraints using Lagrangian relaxation to avoid degenerate policies. Extensive simulations and logged-policy studies indicate improved safety and stable gains over baseline bandits.
spans: Research covers contextual bandits and slate-aware RL with DQN, DDQN, DDPG, and policy-gradient methods, sequence recommenders such as GRU4Rec, SASRec, and BERT4Rec, simulators and logs like RecSim, OpenBanditDataset, and RecoGym, and benchmarks including MovieLens, Amazon Reviews, KuaiRec, and RecSys Challenge datasets (Hidasi et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2019; Ie et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019; Takeda and Joachims, 2020).

document: Detecting anomalies in multivariate sensor streams is critical for reliable operation of cyber-physical systems. Practical detectors must react quickly while maintaining low false-alarm rates under concept drift. Correlations across sensors and variable delays make the problem especially challenging for black-box methods. Moreover, scarce labeled anomalies complicate supervised learning and hyperparameter selection. Recent self-supervised objectives improve representation quality but often ignore temporal alignment uncertainty. Published systems span statistical detectors like STL and Isolation Forest, deep generative models such as LSTM-VAE, DAGMM, Donut, USAD, and OmniAnomaly, correlation-aware architectures including MSCRED and GDN, and benchmarks like NAB, Yahoo S5, UCR archive, SMAP, MSL, SWaT, and WADI, with evaluation protocols based on point- and range-level F1 (Hundman et al., 2018; Su et al., 2019; Lai et al., 2021). Despite solid progress, scoring rules remain brittle when anomalies span variable durations and partially overlap. We address this by modeling anomalies as latent intervals with uncertain boundaries and soft coverage. A differentiable alignment kernel permits end-to-end training that naturally accommodates phase shifts. We introduce an evaluation protocol that rewards early detection while penalizing alarm floods. Experiments on industrial logs demonstrate consistent improvements and favorable trade-offs under drift.
spans: Published systems span statistical detectors like STL and Isolation Forest, deep generative models such as LSTM-VAE, DAGMM, Donut, USAD, and OmniAnomaly, correlation-aware architectures including MSCRED and GDN, and benchmarks like NAB, Yahoo S5, UCR archive, SMAP, MSL, SWaT, and WADI, with evaluation protocols based on point- and range-level F1 (Hundman et al., 2018; Su et al., 2019; Lai et al., 2021).

document: Urban traffic forecasting remains challenging due to dynamic spatial dependencies and irregular sensor layouts. Recent models exploit graph structure and temporal signals to improve predictive accuracy while reducing latency. However, the literature spans diverse assumptions about topology dynamics and exogenous factors. To contextualize our approach, we summarize representative strands in spatiotemporal learning for traffic data. Classical graph convolutional forecasters build fixed road-network operators combined with sequence models, exemplified by DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), and Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), with evaluations on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY. Dynamic-graph methods estimate time-varying affinities or attention over sensors, including AGCRN (Bai et al., 2020), GMAN (Zheng et al., 2020), STGNN (Song et al., 2020), and ASTGCN (Guo et al., 2019), alongside spatiotemporal transformers such as StemGNN (Cao et al., 2020), STTN (Xu et al., 2021), and Autoformer-style decompositions (Wu et al., 2021), frequently benchmarked on PEMS03, PEMS04, and PEMS07. Exogenous-aware pipelines incorporate weather, events, or calendar features via multi-branch encoders or gating (Hoang et al., 2019; Pan et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2021; Cini et al., 2022), while robustness and transfer are explored through distribution shift benchmarks and meta-learning over cities (Oreshkin et al., 2020; Yoon et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2021; Imani and White, 2018), with recent datasets extending to PEMS08, TaxiBJ, and LOOP-Seattle benchmarks. Despite these advances, practical deployment confronts nonstationary topology updates and sensor failures. Real-world agencies also require calibrated uncertainty to support risk-aware control. Existing evaluations seldom stress long-horizon rollouts under structural perturbations. We therefore investigate forecasting under topology drift with explicit treatment of missing nodes and edges. Our formulation unifies temporal imputation, structure inference, and prediction within a coherent training objective. We design a bidirectional diffusion prior over graphs to regularize edge evolution across horizons. A lightweight adapter integrates exogenous covariates without retraining the backbone on every shift. We analyze robustness through targeted attacks, progressive road closures, and random sensor dropouts. Extensive experiments on public traffic benchmarks demonstrate consistent gains in accuracy, calibration, and stability.
spans: Classical graph convolutional forecasters build fixed road-network operators combined with sequence models, exemplified by DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), and Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), with evaluations on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY. Dynamic-graph methods estimate time-varying affinities or attention over sensors, including AGCRN (Bai et al., 2020), GMAN (Zheng et al., 2020), STGNN (Song et al., 2020), and ASTGCN (Guo et al., 2019), alongside spatiotemporal transformers such as StemGNN (Cao et al., 2020), STTN (Xu et al., 2021), and Autoformer-style decompositions (Wu et al., 2021), frequently benchmarked on PEMS03, PEMS04, and PEMS07. Exogenous-aware pipelines incorporate weather, events, or calendar features via multi-branch encoders or gating (Hoang et al., 2019; Pan et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2021; Cini et al., 2022), while robustness and transfer are explored through distribution shift benchmarks and meta-learning over cities (Oreshkin et al., 2020; Yoon et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2021; Imani and White, 2018), with recent datasets extending to PEMS08, TaxiBJ, and LOOP-Seattle benchmarks.

document: Abstractive summarization across languages presents intertwined challenges of style, faithfulness, and resource imbalance. Monolingual English corpora have fueled rapid model scaling, yet most languages lack comparable supervision. Cross-lingual transfer must handle divergent discourse structures and morphology. We position our study within the landscape of multilingual and multi-document summarization. Sequence-to-sequence pretraining has become the dominant paradigm for text compression tasks. However, cross-lingual supervision remains uneven and domain shifts can degrade content selection. Prior work explores multi-task setups and translation-based augmentation with mixed outcomes. Source segmentation and discourse cues are also critical when summaries span multiple articles. We therefore survey key resources and modeling trends. Benchmark corpora include CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015), XSum (Narayan et al., 2018), Multi-News (Fabbri et al., 2019), and GovReport (Huang et al., 2021) for English, with multilingual resources such as WikiLingua (Ladhak et al., 2020), MLSum (Scialom et al., 2020), XL-Sum (Hasan et al., 2021), and MASSIVE-Wiki or GigaWikiSum variants for cross-lingual evaluation. Sequence-to-sequence backbones commonly rely on BART and PEGASUS style objectives (Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020), with multilingual extensions using mBART-50 (Tang et al., 2020), mT5 (Xue et al., 2021), ByT5 (Xue et al., 2022), and ProphetNet variants (Qi et al., 2020), while long-context encoders like LED and LongT5 address multi-document settings (Beltagy et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2022). Data augmentation and filtering strategies span round-trip translation, extractive oracles, and factuality-driven reranking (Feng et al., 2021; Du et al., 2021; Ladhak et al., 2022; Goyal and Durrett, 2021), and evaluation commonly uses ROUGE with emerging factuality metrics like QAGS, FACTCC, and SummaC, plus human judgments of consistency and adequacy. Yet automatic scores often fail to capture cross-lingual stylistic preferences. We revisit faithfulness under constrained computation budgets typical for local newsrooms. Our experiments analyze transfer from high-resource to mid-resource languages using controlled domain slices. The study emphasizes measurable gains in factual precision without sacrificing brevity.
spans: Benchmark corpora include CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015), XSum (Narayan et al., 2018), Multi-News (Fabbri et al., 2019), and GovReport (Huang et al., 2021) for English, with multilingual resources such as WikiLingua (Ladhak et al., 2020), MLSum (Scialom et al., 2020), XL-Sum (Hasan et al., 2021), and MASSIVE-Wiki or GigaWikiSum variants for cross-lingual evaluation. Sequence-to-sequence backbones commonly rely on BART and PEGASUS style objectives (Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020), with multilingual extensions using mBART-50 (Tang et al., 2020), mT5 (Xue et al., 2021), ByT5 (Xue et al., 2022), and ProphetNet variants (Qi et al., 2020), while long-context encoders like LED and LongT5 address multi-document settings (Beltagy et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2022). Data augmentation and filtering strategies span round-trip translation, extractive oracles, and factuality-driven reranking (Feng et al., 2021; Du et al., 2021; Ladhak et al., 2022; Goyal and Durrett, 2021), and evaluation commonly uses ROUGE with emerging factuality metrics like QAGS, FACTCC, and SummaC, plus human judgments of consistency and adequacy.

document: Federated learning enables model training across devices without centralizing raw data. However, privacy guarantees and systems constraints complicate optimization at scale. We outline relevant strands across aggregation, privacy accounting, and communication efficiency. Canonical optimizers aggregate local steps using FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), with extensions such as FedProx (Li et al., 2020), Scaffold (Karimireddy et al., 2020), and FedNova (Wang et al., 2020) to mitigate heterogeneity, typically evaluated on EMNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Shakespeare or StackOverflow. Differential privacy mechanisms adapt DP-SGD and DP-Adam with per-example clipping and calibrated Gaussian noise (Abadi et al., 2016; Bu et al., 2020; Kairouz et al., 2021), and privacy accounting leverages the Moments Accountant, Rényi DP, or privacy amplification by subsampling (Mironov, 2017; Wang et al., 2019; Feldman et al., 2018). Communication efficiency employs secure aggregation, sparsification, and quantization using SecAgg protocols, sketching, and sign-based updates (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Alistarh et al., 2017; Konečný et al., 2016; Bernstein et al., 2018), with lossy codecs aligned to wireless constraints and adaptive client sampling. Federated compression also studies error feedback, top-k sparsity, and dynamic bitrate allocation under latency budgets. Device availability models consider stragglers, dropout, and diurnal patterns during client selection, and participation incentives. Nevertheless, formal guarantees often degrade utility under high heterogeneity and limited participation. Moreover, production workloads must respect memory, power, and intermittent connectivity on edge devices. We study privacy-utility trade-offs under realistic participation patterns and strict computation budgets. Our formulation decouples local training from privacy calibration through adaptive clipping schedules. We introduce a server-side accountant that conditions on observed client volumes per round. The method integrates secure aggregation with lightweight variance reduction. We provide a sensitivity analysis that links gradient norms to privacy loss. Experiments cover vision and language benchmarks with varying participation skew. Results show consistent accuracy improvements under fixed privacy budgets. Ablations highlight robustness to shifts in client sampling and gradient sparsity.
spans: Canonical optimizers aggregate local steps using FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), with extensions such as FedProx (Li et al., 2020), Scaffold (Karimireddy et al., 2020), and FedNova (Wang et al., 2020) to mitigate heterogeneity, typically evaluated on EMNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Shakespeare or StackOverflow. Differential privacy mechanisms adapt DP-SGD and DP-Adam with per-example clipping and calibrated Gaussian noise (Abadi et al., 2016; Bu et al., 2020; Kairouz et al., 2021), and privacy accounting leverages the Moments Accountant, Rényi DP, or privacy amplification by subsampling (Mironov, 2017; Wang et al., 2019; Feldman et al., 2018). Communication efficiency employs secure aggregation, sparsification, and quantization using SecAgg protocols, sketching, and sign-based updates (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Alistarh et al., 2017; Konečný et al., 2016; Bernstein et al., 2018), with lossy codecs aligned to wireless constraints and adaptive client sampling. Federated compression also studies error feedback, top-k sparsity, and dynamic bitrate allocation under latency budgets. Device availability models consider stragglers, dropout, and diurnal patterns during client selection, and participation incentives.

document: Protein structure prediction has undergone rapid progress through geometric learning and large-scale sequence modeling. Advances in multiple sequence alignments and end-to-end differentiable pipelines have shifted accuracy ceilings. Nevertheless, efficiency and interpretability remain core concerns for broader scientific use. Our work examines sequence-only approaches under computational constraints typical of small labs. We begin by outlining representative models, datasets, and evaluation protocols. Sequence embeddings trained at scale have emerged as effective surrogates for evolutionary information. Concurrently, template-based augmentation persists in settings with limited homologs. Benchmarks vary in family diversity and domain boundaries. We distinguish supervised from self-supervised paradigms, with implications for transferability. We also summarize common data filtering practices to reduce leakage. The following references frame the landscape prior to our experiments. End-to-end predictors include AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., 2021), RoseTTAFold (Baek et al., 2021), and trRosetta (Yang et al., 2020), supported by MSA search tools and templates from PDB and PDB70. Protein language models trained on UniRef50, UniRef90, and BFD underpin ESM-1b, ESM-2, and ESMFold (Rives et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2023; Baek et al., 2021), alongside ProtTrans models such as ProtBert and ProtT5 (Elnaggar et al., 2021), and MSA Transformer for alignment-aware representations (Rao et al., 2021), with pretraining objectives ranging from masked token recovery to contact prediction. Evaluation settings span CASP targets, CAMEO weekly assessments, and curated domains from SCOPe and CATH (Kryshtafovych et al., 2021; Haas et al., 2018; Chandonia et al., 2019; Orengo et al., 1997), while side tasks cover secondary structure, contact or distance maps, and binding site annotation using TAPE, FLIP, and ProteinNet (Rao et al., 2019; Berman et al., 2000; Rives et al., 2021), with metrics including lDDT, TM-score, and GDT-TS, for both monomers and complexes across diverse folds. Despite impressive accuracy, sequence-only predictors still struggle on large oligomeric assemblies. We therefore study compute-aware adaptations that prioritize reliability over marginal accuracy on heavy targets.
spans: End-to-end predictors include AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., 2021), RoseTTAFold (Baek et al., 2021), and trRosetta (Yang et al., 2020), supported by MSA search tools and templates from PDB and PDB70. Protein language models trained on UniRef50, UniRef90, and BFD underpin ESM-1b, ESM-2, and ESMFold (Rives et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2023; Baek et al., 2021), alongside ProtTrans models such as ProtBert and ProtT5 (Elnaggar et al., 2021), and MSA Transformer for alignment-aware representations (Rao et al., 2021), with pretraining objectives ranging from masked token recovery to contact prediction. Evaluation settings span CASP targets, CAMEO weekly assessments, and curated domains from SCOPe and CATH (Kryshtafovych et al., 2021; Haas et al., 2018; Chandonia et al., 2019; Orengo et al., 1997), while side tasks cover secondary structure, contact or distance maps, and binding site annotation using TAPE, FLIP, and ProteinNet (Rao et al., 2019; Berman et al., 2000; Rives et al., 2021), with metrics including lDDT, TM-score, and GDT-TS, for both monomers and complexes across diverse folds.

document: Learning dexterous manipulation from interaction promises robust generalization beyond scripted control. Model-free reinforcement learning frequently employs off-policy and on-policy algorithms such as DQN (Mnih et al., 2015), DDPG (Lillicrap et al., 2016), TD3 (Fujimoto et al., 2018), SAC (Haarnoja et al., 2018), and PPO (Schulman et al., 2017), with visual encoders from CPC, BYOL, and R3M (Oord et al., 2018; Grill et al., 2020; Nair et al., 2022). Model-based approaches and world models include PETS (Chua et al., 2018), PlaNet and Dreamer variants (Hafner et al., 2019; Hafner et al., 2020), and MBPO (Janner et al., 2019), while sim-to-real techniques span domain randomization, domain adaptation, and system identification (Tobin et al., 2017; James et al., 2019; Chebotar et al., 2019). Offline and imitation pipelines leverage DAgger and behavior cloning, as well as BCQ, CQL, and IQL (Ross et al., 2011; Fujimoto et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2020; Kostrikov et al., 2021), with evaluations on D4RL, Meta-World, RoboSuite, ManiSkill, RLBench, and BridgeData benchmarks, and language-conditioned policies like CLIPort, RT-1, and RT-2 for instruction following and generalization tasks. Despite notable progress, sample efficiency and deployment reliability remain open issues in robotics. Real systems must handle delays, resets, and safety constraints during exploration. Sparse rewards and long horizons further challenge credit assignment in contact-rich skills. We focus on scalable data reuse under realistic reset and safety limitations. Our approach integrates offline pretraining with cautious online improvement using uncertainty cues. We design a curriculum over task difficulty driven by success-rate estimates. A safety shield enforces action constraints without altering policy gradients. The vision stack uses frozen encoders with lightweight adaptation to new scenes. We evaluate on simulated and real tabletop manipulation with standardized hardware. Training budgets reflect practical lab settings rather than unlimited rollouts. The results demonstrate improved stability and fewer catastrophic failures across seeds. Ablations attribute gains to calibrated exploration and reuse of suboptimal demonstrations.

document: Temporal link prediction on dynamic graphs is central to forecasting interactions in recommendation, finance, and communication networks. Models must encode evolving structure while maintaining scalability and robustness to distribution shift. Prior surveys highlight the trade-offs between snapshot-based and event-driven formulations but leave open questions on memory design and evaluation fidelity. We focus on settings where updates arrive continuously and node attributes are sparse. This context creates challenges for stable training, negative sampling bias, and fair comparison across datasets. Early static graph methods include GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), and GAT (Velickovic et al., 2018), emphasizing neighborhood aggregation and attention on fixed topologies. Temporal extensions explore discrete snapshots and continuous-time events via TGAT (Xu et al., 2020), TGN (Rossi et al., 2020), DySAT (Sankar et al., 2020), and JODIE-style recurrent embeddings (Kumar et al., 2019). Benchmark datasets frequently used are Reddit, Wikipedia, and MOOC for user–item streams, and ICEWS14, ICEWS05-15, and WIKI-Talk for event prediction. Training protocols commonly adopt negative sampling, time-respecting evaluation splits, and metrics such as Hits@k, MRR, and AUC across inductive and transductive settings. Common implementations note memory modules, neighbor caching, mini-batch event processing, and encoders for nodes, edges, and timestamps in codebases and repositories. Despite extensive progress, reported improvements are often confounded by inconsistent temporal splits and varying neighbor sampling budgets. Our study revisits temporal supervision signals and disentangles architectural choices from data ordering effects. We provide a standardized evaluation protocol that aligns message passing depth, memory refresh rates, and batch construction. We analyze latency, peak memory, and throughput to assess practicality in streaming environments. We also examine failure modes under cold-start and partial observability, highlighting where dynamic representations degrade. The resulting insights clarify which components materially drive gains and which ones mainly reflect easier evaluation conditions.
