document: Cross-lingual summarization seeks to condense information while changing the language of expression. This dual objective complicates supervision, alignment, and evaluation under budget-limited annotation. Prior research varies in how it balances translation quality with content selection. We examine transfer regimes that rely on multilingual pre-training and limited target-language signals. Monolingual summarization benchmarks such as CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015), XSum (Narayan et al., 2018), and Gigaword (Rush et al., 2015) provide abstractive training corpora with varying compression ratios. Multilingual or cross-lingual settings commonly draw on WikiLingua (Ladhak et al., 2020), MLSum (Sciaretta et al., 2020), XL-Sum (Hasan et al., 2021), and VoxSum-style collections for broader language coverage. Pre-trained sequence-to-sequence models include mBART (Liu et al., 2020), mT5 (Xue et al., 2021), PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020), and ProphetNet (Qi et al., 2020), often combined with back-translation, pivoting, or pseudo-parallel mining using LASER (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019) or LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020). Evaluation typically reports ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L, and BERTScore, with works incorporating COMET, chrF, or ratings for faithfulness and fluency language directions. Despite the availability of resources, comparability remains hindered by differing tokenizers and normalization choices. Length mismatch between source and target languages further induces metric sensitivity. We therefore systematize preprocessing and report confidence intervals to contextualize scores. Our analysis isolates the contribution of cross-lingual alignment from summarization-specific inductive biases. We also probe factual consistency with language-agnostic entailment models. Error taxonomy distinguishes lexical hallucinations from structural omissions across language pairs. Finally, we discuss data curation practices that reduce topical skew and duplication. These considerations frame our subsequent modeling and evaluation choices.

Multilingual or cross-lingual settings commonly draw on WikiLingua (Ladhak et al., 2020), MLSum (Sciaretta et al., 2020), XL-Sum (Hasan et al., 2021), and VoxSum-style collections for broader language coverage. Pre-trained sequence-to-sequence models include mBART (Liu et al., 2020), mT5 (Xue et al., 2021), PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020), and ProphetNet (Qi et al., 2020), often combined with back-translation, pivoting, or pseudo-parallel mining using LASER (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019) or LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020). Evaluation typically reports ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L, and BERTScore, with works incorporating COMET, chrF, or ratings for faithfulness and fluency language directions.

document: Algorithmic recommendation systems shape access to information, culture, and opportunity. As deployment expands, concerns about disparate impact across user groups intensify. A growing literature examines how ranking and exposure dynamics intersect with sensitive attributes. We study user-level utility and provider-level exposure under operational constraints. We formalize auditing scenarios that reflect platform feedback loops and budgeted exploration. Fairness formulations include demographic parity, equalized odds, equal opportunity, and calibration, with algorithmic treatments spanning pre-processing, in-processing, and post-processing strategies (Kamiran and Calders, 2012; Hardt et al., 2016; Zafar et al., 2017; Narasimhan, 2018). Recommendation backbones frequently use matrix factorization and modern neural variants such as BPR (Rendle et al., 2009), NCF (He et al., 2017), NGCF (Wang et al., 2019), and LightGCN (He et al., 2020). Empirical studies draw on public datasets like MovieLens, LastFM, Yelp, Amazon Books, and KuaiRec, accompanied by sensitive attribute proxies including gender, age, and geography. Evaluation practice typically reports NDCG and Recall at cutoffs, along with fairness measures like exposure disparity, equalized odds gaps, popularity bias indices, and calibration error. Statistical tests include bootstrap intervals and permutation baselines. Yet existing reports vary in how they subsample users and normalize popularity, complicating comparison. We develop a controlled evaluation harness that fixes candidate pools and exposure caps. Our analysis quantifies the trade-off surface between accuracy and exposure regularization. We characterize sensitivity to missing or noisy attributes via synthetic perturbations. We also report cost impacts measured as impressions and latency on real workloads. These results inform practical fairness interventions for large-scale recommenders.

Recommendation backbones frequently use matrix factorization and modern neural variants such as BPR (Rendle et al., 2009), NCF (He et al., 2017), NGCF (Wang et al., 2019), and LightGCN (He et al., 2020). Empirical studies draw on public datasets like MovieLens, LastFM, Yelp, Amazon Books, and KuaiRec, accompanied by sensitive attribute proxies including gender, age, and geography. Statistical tests include bootstrap intervals and permutation baselines.

document: Biomedical relation extraction connects mentions of entities to structured knowledge. Accurate extraction supports curation, search, and hypothesis generation in life sciences. The task is challenging due to long sentences, nested mentions, and ambiguous triggers. Researchers have explored architectures that leverage syntax and domain pre-training. Distant supervision remains attractive yet introduces noisy labels and context drift. Relation extraction has been studied with convolutional and recurrent encoders using position features, including CNN (Zeng et al., 2014), PCNN (Zeng et al., 2015), BiLSTM-ATT (Zhou et al., 2016), and dependency-based Tree-LSTM and GCN variants (Tai et al., 2015; Marcheggiani and Titov, 2017). Pre-trained language models such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), BioBERT (Lee et al., 2020), and SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019) dominate recent benchmarks with task-specific classification heads and span pooling. Common datasets include SemEval 2010 Task 8, TACRED (Zhang et al., 2017), ChemProt (Krallinger et al., 2017), and DDIExtraction (Herrero-Zazo et al., 2013), with distant supervision variants derived from UMLS and MeSH. Evaluation protocols typically report F1, precision, and recall, with splits and ablations over entity masking or lexical cues. Despite strong benchmarks, reproducibility is hampered by tokenization choices and entity normalization differences. Comparisons are further complicated by inconsistent negative sampling and candidate generation steps. We focus on sentence-level extraction with controlled preprocessing and transparent hyperparameters. Our study examines generalization across out-of-corpus datasets and entity overlap regimes. We also quantify the contribution of syntactic pruning versus pre-training scale. Finally, we discuss annotation artifacts and their interaction with model cues. These considerations guide an evaluation protocol for realistic biomedical text mining.

Pre-trained language models such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), BioBERT (Lee et al., 2020), and SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019) dominate recent benchmarks with task-specific classification heads and span pooling. Common datasets include SemEval 2010 Task 8, TACRED (Zhang et al., 2017), ChemProt (Krallinger et al., 2017), and DDIExtraction (Herrero-Zazo et al., 2013), with distant supervision variants derived from UMLS and MeSH. document: Vision–language models enable retrieval, grounding, and captioning by coupling visual and textual representations. Despite remarkable progress, evaluation disparities blur which components truly matter at scale. We revisit pretraining objectives and data curation through a unified lens. Our focus is on image–text retrieval and captioning under constrained compute. We aim to clarify how dataset size, supervision type, and architecture interact in practice. Large-scale vision–language pretraining uses web corpora like Conceptual Captions (Sharma et al., 2018), SBU Captions (Ordonez et al., 2011), YFCC100M (Thomee et al., 2016), and LAION-400M (Schuhmann et al., 2021) to learn aligned representations. Representative models include CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021), OSCAR (Li et al., 2020), VinVL (Zhang et al., 2021), BLIP (Li et al., 2022), and OFA (Wang et al., 2022). Common downstream evaluations cover MSCOCO and Flickr30k for retrieval and captioning, using contrastive fine-tuning, cross-entropy training, and CIDEr optimization with self-critical sequence training (Rennie et al., 2017). Metrics typically include Recall@1, Recall@5, Recall@10 for retrieval and BLEU, METEOR, ROUGE-L, SPICE, and CIDEr for captioning, with standard Karpathy test splits. Encoders span ResNet backbones and Vision Transformers. However, reported gains often conflate pretraining scale with evaluation shortcuts and prompt selection. We standardize tokenization, resolution, and text normalization while holding batch size and schedule fixed. Our experiments measure sensitivity to caption noise, duplicate pairs, and near-duplicate images. We analyze compute–performance trade-offs to guide feasible configurations for practitioners. We also study transfer to domain-shifted retrieval scenarios with limited labels. This setup clarifies which ingredients consistently improve performance across tasks and datasets.
spans: Large-scale vision–language pretraining uses web corpora like Conceptual Captions (Sharma et al., 2018), SBU Captions (Ordonez et al., 2011), YFCC100M (Thomee et al., 2016), and LAION-400M (Schuhmann et al., 2021) to learn aligned representations. Representative models include CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021), OSCAR (Li et al., 2020), VinVL (Zhang et al., 2021), BLIP (Li et al., 2022), and OFA (Wang et al., 2022). Common downstream evaluations cover MSCOCO and Flickr30k for retrieval and captioning, using contrastive fine-tuning, cross-entropy training, and CIDEr optimization with self-critical sequence training (Rennie et al., 2017). Metrics typically include Recall@1, Recall@5, Recall@10 for retrieval and BLEU, METEOR, ROUGE-L, SPICE, and CIDEr for captioning, with standard Karpathy test splits. Encoders span ResNet backbones and Vision Transformers.

document: Instruction-tuned language models are increasingly deployed as domain-specific assistants, yet their evaluation protocols and training mixtures remain unsettled. Recent work on aligning large language models has emphasized instruction tuning corpora, supervised fine-tuning procedures, and preference modeling protocols across open and proprietary systems. Datasets such as Super-NaturalInstructions, FLAN, and T0 assemble heterogeneous task templates and input–output pairs drawn from classification, extraction, and generation tasks. Self-Instruct expands seed prompts into synthetic demonstrations, while Alpaca and Dolly provide open replications of instruction-following behaviors using curated or distilled data. Vicuna, OpenAssistant, and RedPajama incorporate conversational traces, multi-turn dialogues, and web-scale corpora to enrich coverage of user intents. Evaluation suites including MMLU, BIG-bench, and HELM report broad performance indicators, while targeted benchmarks like TruthfulQA, HellaSwag, ARC, and GSM8K probe reasoning, commonsense, factuality, and mathematical competence. Traditional reading comprehension corpora such as SQuAD, CoQA, and QuAC continue to be used for instruction reformulations and few-shot prompting templates. Preference optimization methods span reinforcement learning from human feedback with PPO-style objectives, direct preference optimization, and rejection sampling over pairwise comparisons. Human annotation protocols vary from expert raters to crowd workers, with interface designs controlling rubric clarity, calibration examples, and quality assurance checks across multiple rounds. Open evaluations like Chatbot Arena collect head-to-head votes, whereas controlled audits in the Holistic Evaluation of Language Models framework emphasize standardized prompts, transparent setups, and error taxonomies. Multilingual and cross-domain instruction sets appear in BLOOM, XGLM resources, and FLORES-based evaluations targeting translation coverage and typological diversity. Safety-oriented corpora assemble refusal templates, harmlessness criteria, and toxicity filters that have been deployed in RLHF pipelines and guardrail classifiers. Tool-augmented instruction datasets reflect retrieval augmentation with Wikipedia passages, calculator calls for GSM-style arithmetic, code execution for program synthesis, and plugin invocations for browsing or scheduling. Few-shot collections like MetaICL and Natural Instructions define unified input formats, canonical verbalizers, and demonstration sampling heuristics to stabilize cross-task generalization. Data curation practices cite deduplication, contamination screening against test sets, and mixture weighting to balance summarization, translation, reasoning, and dialogue activities. Public recipes mention tokenization normalizers, instruction header formats, Markdown wrapping of exemplars, and stop-sequence control for safer termination behavior during decoding. Cross-evaluation studies report shifts when models are tuned on FLAN v2 versus mixtures that add CoT expositions, with deliberate reasoning traces drawn from GSM8K, StrategyQA, and OpenBookQA. Prompt formatting libraries adopt YAML-like schemas, task taxonomies, and evaluation harnesses that interface with Hugging Face datasets, OpenAI-compatible APIs, or local deployment stacks. Curated preference datasets include Anthropic HH, Sparrow-style dialogues, Stanford Human Preferences, and OpenAI WebGPT comparisons assembled through browser-augmented question answering. Reporting practices list win rates, Elo ratings, exact match, ROUGE, BLEU, BERTScore, and calibration plots using temperature sweeps and reliability diagrams under varied decoding settings. Open baselines cite LLaMA, T5, and GPT-NeoX checkpoints widely used. Despite the breadth of resources, there remains no consensus on how to isolate domain transfer effects from instruction mixture composition in professional settings. We therefore design controlled ablations and task-centric audits to clarify the specific choices that matter for reliability, fidelity, and maintainability in high-stakes deployments.

Cross-evaluation studies report shifts when models are tuned on FLAN v2 versus mixtures that add CoT expositions, with deliberate reasoning traces drawn from GSM8K, StrategyQA, and OpenBookQA. Prompt formatting libraries adopt YAML-like schemas, task taxonomies, and evaluation harnesses that interface with Hugging Face datasets, OpenAI-compatible APIs, or local deployment stacks. Curated preference datasets include Anthropic HH, Sparrow-style dialogues, Stanford Human Preferences, and OpenAI WebGPT comparisons assembled through browser-augmented question answering. Reporting practices list win rates, Elo ratings, exact match, ROUGE, BLEU, BERTScore, and calibration plots using temperature sweeps and reliability diagrams under varied decoding settings. Open baselines cite LLaMA, T5, and GPT-NeoX checkpoints widely used. Yet the landscape does not separate tool-use competence from general following ability or dataset-specific artifacts. We introduce a disentangled assessment that factors tools, prompts, and feedback to identify which elements predict robust analytical performance.

document: Policy-constrained dialog agents use preference-optimized language models, but the training and evaluation ecosystem remains diffuse and difficult to navigate. Recent work on aligning large language models has emphasized instruction tuning corpora, supervised fine-tuning procedures, and preference modeling protocols across open and proprietary systems. Datasets such as Super-NaturalInstructions, FLAN, and T0 assemble heterogeneous task templates and input–output pairs drawn from classification, extraction, and generation tasks. Self-Instruct expands seed prompts into synthetic demonstrations, while Alpaca and Dolly provide open replications of instruction-following behaviors using curated or distilled data. Vicuna, OpenAssistant, and RedPajama incorporate conversational traces, multi-turn dialogues, and web-scale corpora to enrich coverage of user intents. Evaluation suites including MMLU, BIG-bench, and HELM report broad performance indicators, while targeted benchmarks like TruthfulQA, HellaSwag, ARC, and GSM8K probe reasoning, commonsense, factuality, and mathematical competence. Traditional reading comprehension corpora such as SQuAD, CoQA, and QuAC continue to be used for instruction reformulations and few-shot prompting templates. Preference optimization methods span reinforcement learning from human feedback with PPO-style objectives, direct preference optimization, and rejection sampling over pairwise comparisons. Human annotation protocols vary from expert raters to crowd workers, with interface designs controlling rubric clarity, calibration examples, and quality assurance checks across multiple rounds. Open evaluations like Chatbot Arena collect head-to-head votes, whereas controlled audits in the Holistic Evaluation of Language Models framework emphasize standardized prompts, transparent setups, and error taxonomies. Multilingual and cross-domain instruction sets appear in BLOOM, XGLM resources, and FLORES-based evaluations targeting translation coverage and typological diversity. Safety-oriented corpora assemble refusal templates, harmlessness criteria, and toxicity filters that have been deployed in RLHF pipelines and guardrail classifiers. Tool-augmented instruction datasets reflect retrieval augmentation with Wikipedia passages, calculator calls for GSM-style arithmetic, code execution for program synthesis, and plugin invocations for browsing or scheduling. Few-shot collections like MetaICL and Natural Instructions define unified input formats, canonical verbalizers, and demonstration sampling heuristics to stabilize cross-task generalization. Data curation practices cite deduplication, contamination screening against test sets, and mixture weighting to balance summarization, translation, reasoning, and dialogue activities. Public recipes mention tokenization normalizers, instruction header formats, Markdown wrapping of exemplars, and stop-sequence control for safer termination behavior during decoding. Cross-evaluation studies report shifts when models are tuned on FLAN v2 versus mixtures that add CoT expositions, with deliberate reasoning traces drawn from GSM8K, StrategyQA, and OpenBookQA. Prompt formatting libraries adopt YAML-like schemas, task taxonomies, and evaluation harnesses that interface with Hugging Face datasets, OpenAI-compatible APIs, or local deployment stacks. Curated preference datasets include Anthropic HH, Sparrow-style dialogues, Stanford Human Preferences, and OpenAI WebGPT comparisons assembled through browser-augmented question answering. Critically, the existing resources rarely quantify the tradeoffs between helpfulness, honesty, and harmlessness under distribution shift or deployment feedback. We articulate a measurement framework and a data collection protocol that exposes these tradeoffs under reproducible conditions.

Recent work on aligning large language models has emphasized instruction tuning corpora, supervised fine-tuning procedures, and preference modeling protocols across open and proprietary systems. Multilingual and cross-domain instruction sets appear in BLOOM, XGLM resources, and FLORES-based evaluations targeting translation coverage and typological diversity. Few-shot collections like MetaICL and Natural Instructions define unified input formats, canonical verbalizers, and demonstration sampling heuristics to stabilize cross-task generalization. document: Abstractive summarization has advanced rapidly with large pretrained sequence-to-sequence models. Despite improved fluency, models often fail to generalize when the source domain diverges from supervised training data. This gap is acute in settings where stylistic, topical, or length distributions shift across sources. Prior domain adaptation strategies examine fine-tuning schedules, vocabulary control, and contrastive alignment. Data selection and filtering have also been explored to reduce spurious correlations. However, systematic assessments of robustness across multiple domains remain inconsistent. Transfer methods may overfit high-resource news corpora and degrade on procedural or scientific texts. Evaluation further conflates factuality with surface overlap, obscuring true generalization behavior. Few studies jointly analyze style, content, and compression across heterogeneous domains. A clearer view of datasets and baselines is necessary to contextualize cross-domain performance. Prior studies on abstractive summarization evaluate systems on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, Gigaword, and Multi-News, with baselines such as BART, T5, Pegasus, and ProphetNet. Benchmarks and reports detail setups across these corpora and models (Nallapati et al., 2016; Narayan et al., 2018; Rush, 2015; Fabbri et al., 2019; Koupaee and Wang, 2018; Lewis et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). While these resources enable comparison, they vary in article length, summary style, and annotation policy. Differences in reference abstractive density and reporting bias complicate transfer conclusions. Moreover, pretrained checkpoints differ in tokenization and pretraining corpora, affecting adaptation dynamics. Recent measures of factual consistency indicate that domain shift interacts with hallucination tendencies. We focus on characterizing which adaptation levers matter under controlled distributional changes. Our framework isolates data regime, objective, and evaluation factors for fair comparisons. We introduce stratified domain splits to decouple style from topic. We also calibrate decoding to mitigate length and novelty artifacts. Experiments examine generalization on news, how-to, and multi-document domains under matched budgets. The results reveal trade-offs between stability and plasticity that inform future model selection.

document: Object detection performance has improved dramatically with convolutional and transformer-based architectures. Despite these gains, robustness in real-world conditions such as rain, fog, and nighttime remains a challenge. Distribution shifts in illumination, contrast, and texture often degrade localization and classification. Data augmentation helps, yet can be insufficient without realistic sensor effects. Synthetic-to-real adaptation has emerged as a complementary direction. However, evaluation under adverse weather is fragmented across studies. Models are frequently compared under varying image resolutions and nonuniform test sets. This complicates attribution of robustness to architectural choices or training recipes. Furthermore, per-class sensitivity to weather differs from aggregate mAP trends. A consolidated view of mainstream datasets and detectors provides necessary context. Standardized metrics and protocols further ground claims about robustness. Extensive benchmarks cover general and driving scenes, including PASCAL VOC, MS COCO, Cityscapes, BDD100K, and KITTI, alongside detectors such as Faster R-CNN, RetinaNet, YOLOv3–v5, and DETR. Canonical papers describe standard protocols for these settings (Everingham et al., 2010; Lin et al., 2014; Cordts et al., 2016; Geiger et al., 2012; Ren et al., 2015; Lin et al., 2017; Carion et al., 2020). Yet these benchmarks seldom isolate adverse weather with calibrated sensor models. Public subsets focusing on fog or rain are small and inconsistently annotated. We disentangle robustness sources by fixing data curation and varying augmentation and normalization. Our study quantifies cross-weather transfer while maintaining identical training budgets. We incorporate physics-inspired corruptions to approximate scattering and glare. Additionally, we analyze error taxonomies to pinpoint missed and hallucinated boxes. The protocol reports stratified mAP across weather categories and object scales. This design reduces confounds from resolution and batch-size choices. Findings highlight conditions under which architectural inductive biases confer resilience.
spans: Extensive benchmarks cover general and driving scenes, including PASCAL VOC, MS COCO, Cityscapes, BDD100K, and KITTI, alongside detectors such as Faster R-CNN, RetinaNet, YOLOv3–v5, and DETR. Canonical papers describe standard protocols for these settings (Everingham et al., 2010; Lin et al., 2014; Cordts et al., 2016; Geiger et al., 2012; Ren et al., 2015; Lin et al., 2017; Carion et al., 2020).

document: Offline evaluation of recommender systems suffers from bias induced by historical logging policies. Click and rating data encode exposure mechanisms that confound counterfactual performance estimates. Naïve replay or random holdout therefore overestimates familiar items and popular users. Causal estimators aim to debias evaluation and learning using propensity information. However, their effectiveness depends on accurate logging and overlap conditions. Practical systems also face high variance and covariate shift across time. Recent work studies regularization and representation learning to stabilize estimators. A clear map of benchmark tasks and estimators assists reproducible comparisons. It also clarifies which assumptions drive observed gains. We first summarize prevailing datasets and methods to situate our study. Work on causal recommendation spans inverse propensity scoring, doubly robust estimators, off-policy bandits, and deconfounding approaches, with evaluations on MovieLens, Yahoo! R6A, Coat, and KuaiRec. Foundational and benchmark papers detail methods, evaluation metrics, datasets, and baselines used across these settings (Schnabel et al., 2016; Swaminathan and Joachims, 2015; Dudík et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2019; Liang et al., 2016; Saito, 2020). Despite shared terminology, implementations vary in propensity clipping, renormalization, and bootstrapping. These choices materially affect ranking metrics and confidence intervals. We therefore adopt a unified pipeline that logs policies explicitly and enforces overlap checks. The pipeline separates estimator choice from representation learning to expose trade-offs. We evaluate stability under misspecified propensities and temporal drift. Our analysis emphasizes calibration of exposure models and the role of covariate coverage. We report paired-sample intervals to compare learning objectives fairly. We also study estimator sensitivity to user and item cold starts. Taken together, the results caution against purely variance-based model selection. They motivate benchmarks that foreground identifiability alongside accuracy.
spans: Work on causal recommendation spans inverse propensity scoring, doubly robust estimators, off-policy bandits, and deconfounding approaches, with evaluations on MovieLens, Yahoo! R6A, Coat, and KuaiRec. Foundational and benchmark papers detail methods, evaluation metrics, datasets, and baselines used across these settings (Schnabel et al., 2016; Swaminathan and Joachims, 2015; Dudík et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2019; Liang et al., 2016; Saito, 2020).

document: Short-term traffic forecasting underpins intelligent transportation and smart city planning. Spatiotemporal dependencies among sensors challenge classical time-series baselines. Graph neural networks integrate spatial topology with temporal dynamics for improved accuracy. Yet replication across datasets remains difficult due to inconsistent preprocessing. Reported gains often conflate architecture, horizon, and data curation effects. Robust conclusions require careful control of graph construction and normalization. Furthermore, nonstationary demand can invalidate fixed adjacency assumptions. We examine comparability across popular datasets and architectures to clarify reporting. Traffic forecasting benchmarks use METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, and PEMS03/04/07/08, with representative architectures including DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, ASTGCN, and T-GCN. Dataset and method papers document sensor graphs, split protocols, horizons, and metrics in these settings (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019; Pan et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020). Nevertheless, preprocessing pipelines differ in filtering, imputation, and speed aggregation. This leads to measurable discrepancies even for identical models. We standardize sensor selection and temporal alignment to reduce variance. Our evaluation isolates architectural choices from data handling artifacts. We further examine robustness to missing data and sudden regime changes. To probe stationarity assumptions, we test adaptive adjacency schemes. We report errors stratified by horizon and congestion level to contextualize averages. Computational cost is measured at matched accuracy to reveal Pareto fronts. We release code and standardized splits to encourage consistent reporting. The study highlights which design elements drive gains across horizons. These findings support more reliable adoption of spatiotemporal graph models. They also suggest priorities for future benchmark design.
spans: Traffic forecasting benchmarks use METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, and PEMS03/04/07/08, with representative architectures including DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, ASTGCN, and T-GCN. Dataset and method papers document sensor graphs, split protocols, horizons, and metrics in these settings (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019; Pan et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020).

document: Extracting semantic relations from biomedical text accelerates knowledge base construction. Supervised models rely on annotated corpora that capture entity types and relation schemas. Transformer encoders pretrained on scientific text have improved performance over earlier CNN and RNN systems. Yet domain drift across subfields and publication venues remains a barrier to robustness. Ambiguity in chemical and gene nomenclature further complicates generalization. Standard evaluation often overlooks calibration and uncertainty under limited evidence. Negative sampling strategies can distort precision–recall curves in skewed label spaces. Consistent baselines and datasets are needed to contextualize architectural improvements. In this study, we analyze model behavior under schema and surface shift. We compare terminology coverage, entity masking, and sentence length effects. Before detailing our setup, we briefly review commonly used corpora and baselines. Biomedical relation extraction studies evaluate on ChemProt, DDIExtraction 2013, BioRED, and BioNLP shared tasks, using baselines such as CNN-PCNN, BiLSTM-ATT, BioBERT, SciBERT, and PubMedBERT. Dataset and model papers report schemes, splits, and metrics for these resources and systems (Kringelum et al., 2016; Herrero-Zazo et al., 2013; Kim et al., 2009; Zeng et al., 2015; Lee et al., 2020; Gu et al., 2021). These resources differ substantially in entity definitions and annotation granularity. Relation types range from fine-grained mechanisms to coarse interactions, affecting comparability. We harmonize labels to a shared interface while preserving corpus-specific nuance. Our evaluation emphasizes cross-corpus transfer under consistent hyperparameters. We measure calibration error alongside F1 to reflect practical screening needs. Span masking ablations reveal reliance on entity surface forms. Additional experiments examine long-sentence handling with sliding window attention. All code, models, and preprocessing scripts will be released for reproducibility. The findings identify dataset properties that most influence generalization.

Dataset and model papers report schemes, splits, and metrics for these resources and systems (Kringelum et al., 2016; Herrero-Zazo et al., 2013; Kim et al., 2009; Zeng et al., 2015; Lee et al., 2020; Gu et al., 2021).

document: Cross-lingual entity linking remains a core challenge for multilingual information access across the web. The task requires associating surface mentions in one language with canonical entries in a multilingual knowledge base. Practical deployments must cope with noisy text, limited resources, and shifting ontologies. Recent advances in multilingual pretrained models have changed prevailing design assumptions. However, gaps persist in robustness and transfer to truly low-resource domains. Prior work often assumes abundant anchor supervision or high-coverage alias tables. In contrast, many languages lack reliable mention dictionaries or clean cross-lingual signals. This motivates a reexamination of candidate generation and ranking under sparse supervision. We also consider the role of typological features in cross-lingual generalization. Research on cross-lingual entity linking has drawn from multilingual embeddings, alignment resources, and knowledge base signals. Early approaches relied on translation or pivoting through English (McNamee et al., 2011; Ji et al., 2015), while recent methods exploit multilingual transformers mBERT and XLM-R for mention and candidate encoding (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020; Wu and Dredze, 2019). Benchmarks include TAC-KBP EL and Cold Start (Ji and Grishman, 2011), Wikipedia corpora (Sil and Florian, 2016), multilingual adaptations of AIDA/CoNLL (Hoffart et al., 2011; Upadhyay et al., 2018), and the Mewsli-X and WikiAnn for supervision (Botha et al., 2020; Pan et al., 2017), along with candidate generation techniques from TF-IDF and BM25 to retrieval and alias tables (Robertson and Walker, 1994; Johnson et al., 2017; Gillick et al., 2019). Despite impressive averages on public leaderboards, error patterns cluster by script, morphology, and domain. We study these factors by stratifying evaluation and disentangling candidate generation from mention encoding. Our analysis highlights how transliteration and subword coverage interact with retrieval constraints. We further quantify the impact of cross-lingual homographs on disambiguation. Building on these observations, we introduce a training protocol that regularizes retrieval with typology-aware constraints. The approach is designed to be architecture-agnostic and compatible with standard encoders. It also reduces reliance on noisy anchors by leveraging contextual cues in the target language. We complement intrinsic metrics with ablations that isolate each component. To ensure realistic results, we evaluate on held-out domains and unseen scripts. We further report efficiency statistics because candidate generation often dominates runtime. Finally, we release evaluation templates to encourage reporting beyond global accuracy. Together, these elements aim to clarify which cross-lingual signals matter most for reliable entity linking.

Early approaches relied on translation or pivoting through English (McNamee et al., 2011; Ji et al., 2015), while recent methods exploit multilingual transformers mBERT and XLM-R for mention and candidate encoding (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020; Wu and Dredze, 2019). Benchmarks include TAC-KBP EL and Cold Start (Ji and Grishman, 2011), Wikipedia corpora (Sil and Florian, 2016), multilingual adaptations of AIDA/CoNLL (Hoffart et al., 2011; Upadhyay et al., 2018), and the Mewsli-X and WikiAnn for supervision (Botha et al., 2020; Pan et al., 2017), along with candidate generation techniques from TF-IDF and BM25 to retrieval and alias tables (Robertson and Walker, 1994; Johnson et al., 2017; Gillick et al., 2019).

document: Detecting anomalies in industrial telemetry is critical for safety, cost control, and uptime. Streams are high-dimensional, irregular, and subject to regime shifts. False alarms erode operator trust while misses can be catastrophic. Despite progress, production settings rarely match academic benchmarks. Issues like covariate shift, missingness, and delayed labels complicate deployment. In addition, interpretability is required for root cause analysis and remediation. These constraints motivate a careful reassessment of modeling choices. We focus on methods that remain stable under distribution shift and sparse supervision. We also examine the interface between detection, explanation, and action. Industrial time-series anomaly detection has been explored through statistical baselines, deep representation learning, and hybrid forecasting-error schemes. Classical detectors include ARIMA and STL decomposition with control charts (Box and Jenkins, 1970; Cleveland et al., 1990; Montgomery, 2009), while matrix profile and discord discovery provide subsequence outlier scores (Yeh et al., 2016; Keogh et al., 2005), and spectral density tests capture periodic deviations (Priestley, 1981; Parzen, 1962). Neural approaches use autoencoders, variational models, and networks trained on datasets such as NAB, Yahoo Webscope, Anomaly Archive, and MSL/SMAP from NASA (Lavin and Ahmad, 2015; Laptev et al., 2015; Dau et al., 2019; Hundman et al., 2018), with attention mechanisms, reconstruction thresholds, and residuals as proxies (Malhotra et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2018; Audibert et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2019). Prior evaluations often aggregate across regimes, masking brittle behaviors. We therefore stratify by operational modes and introduce shift-aware validation. Our framework decouples representation learning from threshold selection. We integrate censoring models to handle delayed or partial labels. To improve trust, we pair detectors with counterfactual explanations based on actionable features. The approach reduces spurious alerts while preserving early warnings. We present a recipe for calibrating decisions using limited fault tickets. Extensive ablations isolate the roles of ensembling and covariate normalization. We evaluate on proprietary plants and public datasets under identical protocols. Results highlight consistent benefits under simulated and real shifts. We also report operator feedback from shadow deployments. Collectively, the study maps a path toward dependable anomaly detection in industry.

Classical detectors include ARIMA and STL decomposition with control charts (Box and Jenkins, 1970; Cleveland et al., 1990; Montgomery, 2009), while matrix profile and discord discovery provide subsequence outlier scores (Yeh et al., 2016; Keogh et al., 2005), and spectral density tests capture periodic deviations (Priestley, 1981; Parzen, 1962). Neural approaches use autoencoders, variational models, and networks trained on datasets such as NAB, Yahoo Webscope, Anomaly Archive, and MSL/SMAP from NASA (Lavin and Ahmad, 2015; Laptev et al., 2015; Dau et al., 2019; Hundman et al., 2018), with attention mechanisms, reconstruction thresholds, and residuals as proxies (Malhotra et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2018; Audibert et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2019).

document: Urban congestion imposes high costs on mobility, equity, and the environment. Traffic signals are a principal actuator for shaping network performance. Fixed-time plans struggle under variable demand and incidents. Adaptive control promises responsiveness but is challenging to tune at scale. Reinforcement learning offers a data-driven alternative with minimal modeling assumptions. However, variability in simulators and metrics hampers comparability. There is also tension between local and network-level objectives. We examine how design choices affect transfer from simulation to field. Our study isolates reward definitions, state encodings, and exploration schedules. Research on adaptive traffic signal control spans optimization, control, and reinforcement learning. Classical systems use Webster formulations and max-pressure policies grounded in queueing theory (Webster, 1958; Varaiya, 2013; Wongpiromsarn et al., 2012), while actuated and SCOOT/SCATS deployments rely on heuristic detectors and online tuning (Hunt et al., 1982; Lowrie, 1992). Data-driven approaches apply Q-learning, actor–critic, and graph reinforcement learning with benchmarks such as SUMO, CityFlow, and NGSIM, alongside datasets like PeMS and METR-LA for demand patterns (Watkins and Dayan, 1992; Liang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Krajzewicz et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2019; Li et al., 2018), and incorporate reward designs based on delay, queue length, throughput, and emission surrogates (Wei et al., 2018; Chu et al., 2019; Arel et al., 2010; Zang et al., 2020). Unlike single-intersection studies, we consider corridors with spillback and offsets. We analyze observability limits stemming from partial detection. We further assess robustness to sensor noise and demand shifts. To reduce variance, we adopt consistent random seeds and scenario splits. We incorporate lane-level constraints to prevent unsafe phase sequences. Our agents share parameters across intersections using graph message passing. We calibrate simulators using limited probe trajectory data. Evaluation uses out-of-sample scenarios and peak-period stress tests. We include counterfactual baselines derived from real timing plans. The results emphasize the importance of reward shaping for fairness. We release reproducible scripts and a policy auditing toolkit. These assets aim to foster transparent progress in learning-based traffic control.
spans: Research on adaptive traffic signal control spans optimization, control, and reinforcement learning. Classical systems use Webster formulations and max-pressure policies grounded in queueing theory (Webster, 1958; Varaiya, 2013; Wongpiromsarn et al., 2012), while actuated and SCOOT/SCATS deployments rely on heuristic detectors and online tuning (Hunt et al., 1982; Lowrie, 1992). Data-driven approaches apply Q-learning, actor–critic, and graph reinforcement learning with benchmarks such as SUMO, CityFlow, and NGSIM, alongside datasets like PeMS and METR-LA for demand patterns (Watkins and Dayan, 1992; Liang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Krajzewicz et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2019; Li et al., 2018), and incorporate reward designs based on delay, queue length, throughput, and emission surrogates (Wei et al., 2018; Chu et al., 2019; Arel et al., 2010; Zang et al., 2020).

document: Legal practitioners face information overload as caselaw and regulations rapidly expand. Automated argument mining can support retrieval, drafting, and compliance analysis. However, legal discourse exhibits specialized structure and citation practices. Long documents and heterogeneity across jurisdictions pose additional difficulties. Prior methods often neglect domain shifts between courts and statutes. We target robust extraction of claims, evidence, and links in lengthy documents. Our perspective emphasizes transparency and auditability of outputs. We also consider alignment with practitioner workflows and ethical constraints. The discussion situates our approach relative to established corpora and baselines. Argument mining in legal and regulatory texts has drawn from rhetorical structures, discourse parsing, pretrained encoders, and domain-adapted language models. Prior datasets include the European Court of Human Rights decisions, the IBM Debater corpus, the UK-2019 Statutory Instruments collection, and the CaseHOLD, LEDGAR, and MultiEURLEX resources (Alekseev et al., 2020; Bar-Haim et al., 2014; Chalkidis et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021; Tuggener et al., 2020; Chalkidis et al., 2021b). Methods span sequence labeling, parsers, and graph neural networks over legal citation graphs, with baselines ranging from CRFs and BiLSTMs to Legal-BERT, Longformer, and DeBERTa variants, often trained with weak supervision from headings or citations (Lafferty et al., 2001; Huang et al., 2015; Beltagy et al., 2020; Chalkidis et al., 2020; He et al., 2021; Petroni et al., 2020). Despite broad availability of pretrained legal encoders, annotation scarcity remains acute. We therefore combine limited expert labels with distantly supervised signals. A length-aware encoder preserves cross-reference structure without truncation. A hierarchical graph layer propagates constraints across sections. We incorporate citation functions as auxiliary prediction targets. The training objective balances extraction fidelity and structural coherence. Evaluation follows scenario-based splits across courts and time. We report inter-annotator agreement to contextualize upper bounds. Error analysis highlights failure modes around negation and exceptions. Ablations clarify contributions of graph structure and curriculum learning. We release templates and governance checklists to aid deployment. The goal is dependable, explainable argument mining for real legal practice.
spans: Argument mining in legal and regulatory texts has drawn from rhetorical structures, discourse parsing, pretrained encoders, and domain-adapted language models. Prior datasets include the European Court of Human Rights decisions, the IBM Debater corpus, the UK-2019 Statutory Instruments collection, and the CaseHOLD, LEDGAR, and MultiEURLEX resources (Alekseev et al., 2020; Bar-Haim et al., 2014; Chalkidis et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021; Tuggener et al., 2020; Chalkidis et al., 2021b). Methods span sequence labeling, parsers, and graph neural networks over legal citation graphs, with baselines ranging from CRFs and BiLSTMs to Legal-BERT, Longformer, and DeBERTa variants, often trained with weak supervision from headings or citations (Lafferty et al., 2001; Huang et al., 2015; Beltagy et al., 2020; Chalkidis et al., 2020; He et al., 2021; Petroni et al., 2020).

document: Radiotherapy planning relies on accurate delineation of organs and targets. Manual contouring is time intensive and prone to interobserver variability. Automation promises consistency and reduced workload for clinicians. Yet clinical adoption depends on robustness, speed, and uncertainty quantification. Imaging heterogeneity across scanners and centers complicates generalization. Protocol differences further impact label definitions and boundaries. We examine scalable segmentation under data shift and tight latency constraints. Our study emphasizes evaluation practices aligned with clinical endpoints. We also analyze calibration and failure detection for safe usage. The following overview positions our work among existing systems. Automatic segmentation for radiotherapy has been approached with atlas-based registration, classical machine learning, and deep convolutional architectures. Public datasets include MICCAI Head-Neck Challenge, LiTS for liver and tumors, BraTS for brain gliomas, and MSD tasks covering multiple organs (Raudaschl et al., 2017; Bilic et al., 2019; Menze et al., 2015; Simpson et al., 2019). Architectures span U-Net variants, V-Net, nnU-Net, and transformers for volumetric inputs, trained with losses such as Dice, focal, and boundary losses, using augmentations like elastic deformations and intensity scaling, and evaluated by Dice, HD95, and surface distance metrics (Ronneberger et al., 2015; Milletari et al., 2016; Isensee et al., 2021; Hatamizadeh et al., 2022; Sudre et al., 2017; Karimi et al., 2019; Taha and Hanbury, 2015). Hybrid pipelines integrate organ-at-risk ontologies and statistical shape priors. Despite strong benchmarks, cross-site performance often drops markedly. We mitigate this by combining self-training and style normalization. A lightweight backbone enables deployment on standard workstation GPUs. We attach an evidential head to surface uncertainties in contour proposals. An efficient patching strategy preserves context without exceeding memory limits. We report time-to-plan and edit-distance metrics alongside Dice. Prospective testing uses fixed protocols and blinded clinician review. Qualitative audits identify common errors at tissue interfaces. Release materials include de-identified models and risk management documentation. The results point to practical routes for safe clinical integration.
spans: Automatic segmentation for radiotherapy has been approached with atlas-based registration, classical machine learning, and deep convolutional architectures. Public datasets include MICCAI Head-Neck Challenge, LiTS for liver and tumors, BraTS for brain gliomas, and MSD tasks covering multiple organs (Raudaschl et al., 2017; Bilic et al., 2019; Menze et al., 2015; Simpson et al., 2019). Architectures span U-Net variants, V-Net, nnU-Net, and transformers for volumetric inputs, trained with losses such as Dice, focal, and boundary losses, using augmentations like elastic deformations and intensity scaling, and evaluated by Dice, HD95, and surface distance metrics (Ronneberger et al., 2015; Milletari et al., 2016; Isensee et al., 2021; Hatamizadeh et al., 2022; Sudre et al., 2017; Karimi et al., 2019; Taha and Hanbury, 2015). Hybrid pipelines integrate organ-at-risk ontologies and statistical shape priors.

document: Graph neural networks have become central to computational chemistry for modeling molecules as relational structures. Predicting quantum and biophysical properties from graphs supports drug discovery and materials design at scale. Early formulations emphasized message passing as a way to summarize local neighborhoods efficiently. Despite notable progress, modeling long-range interactions and data scarcity remain open challenges in practice. We focus on the interplay between architectural bias and pretraining for small labeled regimes. Foundational approaches span neural fingerprints and message passing networks across benchmarks such as QM9 and ZINC (Duvenaud et al., 2015; Kearnes et al., 2016; Ramakrishnan et al., 2014; Sterling and Irwin, 2015). Subsequent extensions include interaction networks, graph attention, and pretraining strategies evaluated on MoleculeNet, Tox21, and PCBA (Gilmer et al., 2017; Veličković et al., 2018; Ying et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2017). Datasets such as FreeSolv and ESOL provide evaluation protocols. However, these efforts reveal a tension between expressive power and sample efficiency when supervision is scarce. We revisit this trade-off by coupling long-range conditioning with chemically motivated masking during pretraining. Our formulation targets energy-related properties where conventional node-local encoding underperforms. We also quantify how self-supervised objectives interact with topology augmentation in limited data settings. To ensure fairness, we re-evaluate prior splits and control for scaffold leakage in all comparisons. This perspective clarifies when architectural complexity yields gains beyond data curation and augmentation. The resulting analysis motivates principled choices for graph design and pretraining that generalize across assays.
spans: Foundational approaches span neural fingerprints and message passing networks across benchmarks such as QM9 and ZINC (Duvenaud et al., 2015; Kearnes et al., 2016; Ramakrishnan et al., 2014; Sterling and Irwin, 2015). Subsequent extensions include interaction networks, graph attention, and pretraining strategies evaluated on MoleculeNet, Tox21, and PCBA (Gilmer et al., 2017; Veličković et al., 2018; Ying et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2017). Datasets such as FreeSolv and ESOL provide evaluation protocols.

document: Algorithmic recommendations increasingly mediate access to news, jobs, and entertainment. Fairness concerns arise when exposure and utility are unevenly distributed across users and items. Existing audits emphasize measurement frameworks tied to platform constraints and stakeholder goals. We examine ranking-time interventions that balance utility with constrained fairness budgets. Our formulation accounts for both provider and consumer level objectives. Prior research catalogs fairness definitions and auditing protocols in recommender systems and learning to rank (Burke, 2017; Ekstrand et al., 2018; Diaz et al., 2020). Methodological advances include exposure-aware objectives, constrained optimization for demographic parity, and amortized post-processing of slates (Biega et al., 2018; Singh and Joachims, 2018; Mehrotra et al., 2018). Empirical studies often rely on MovieLens, lastfm-1k, and MSLR-WEB10K to benchmark trade-offs between accuracy and equity (Harper and Konstan, 2015; Celma, 2010; Qin and Liu, 2013). Datasets vary widely. Yet these datasets differ in exposure dynamics and provider granularity, complicating cross-study conclusions. We formalize a budgeted fairness controller that adjusts slate exposure while preserving utility guarantees. The controller integrates seamlessly with differentiable ranking layers used in large-scale systems. We derive generalization bounds that connect budget size to regret in constrained utility. Extensive simulations illustrate robustness to demand shocks and head–tail skew. Finally, we present a policy analysis for stakeholder choice under sensitive attribute uncertainty.
spans: Prior research catalogs fairness definitions and auditing protocols in recommender systems and learning to rank (Burke, 2017; Ekstrand et al., 2018; Diaz et al., 2020). Methodological advances include exposure-aware objectives, constrained optimization for demographic parity, and amortized post-processing of slates (Biega et al., 2018; Singh and Joachims, 2018; Mehrotra et al., 2018). Empirical studies often rely on MovieLens, lastfm-1k, and MSLR-WEB10K to benchmark trade-offs between accuracy and equity (Harper and Konstan, 2015; Celma, 2010; Qin and Liu, 2013). Datasets vary widely.

document: Multimodal learning promises unified representations across medical images and associated text. Radiology report supervision offers rich semantic grounding that complements pixel-level labels. However, modality gaps and limited expert annotations impede generalization across institutions. We investigate cross-modal pretraining that aligns images with structured report cues. Our analysis emphasizes transfer to segmentation and classification under domain shift. Clinical imaging has benefited from convolutional and transformer backbones alongside domain-adaptive toolkits (Ronneberger et al., 2015; He et al., 2016; Dosovitskiy et al., 2021; Cardoso et al., 2022). Large-scale datasets and benchmarks—MIMIC-CXR, CheXpert, and ChestX-ray14—enable standardized evaluation of thoracic findings and representation learning (Johnson et al., 2019; Irvin et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017). Pretraining corpora vary in scale, labeling quality, and de-identification procedures across institutions and released subsets, and common splits support reproducible comparison across tasks with anatomical coverage. Despite dataset breadth, alignment with clinical intent often hinges on subtle lexical choices in reporting. We introduce a cross-modal objective that restricts alignment to medically grounded entities extracted from reports. This constraint mitigates spurious correspondences between stylistic phrases and image artifacts. We evaluate on segmentation transfer from chest radiographs to computed tomography of the thorax. Our ablations quantify contributions from entity filtering and view-normalized augmentations. We analyze robustness under institution shift using held-out hospitals with distinct acquisition protocols. The results inform scalable strategies for multimodal representation learning in clinical imaging workflows.
spans: Clinical imaging has benefited from convolutional and transformer backbones alongside domain-adaptive toolkits (Ronneberger et al., 2015; He et al., 2016; Dosovitskiy et al., 2021; Cardoso et al., 2022). Large-scale datasets and benchmarks—MIMIC-CXR, CheXpert, and ChestX-ray14—enable standardized evaluation of thoracic findings and representation learning (Johnson et al., 2019; Irvin et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017). Pretraining corpora vary in scale, labeling quality, and de-identification procedures across institutions and released subsets, and common splits support reproducible comparison across tasks with anatomical coverage.

document: Generative models of code increasingly serve as assistive tools for software development. They promise accelerated prototyping while raising questions about reliability and test adequacy. The gap between surface fluency and functional correctness remains a persistent challenge. We study synthesis-time constraints that encourage executable and generalizable programs across tasks. Our approach integrates lightweight verification with uncertainty-aware decoding. Recent models demonstrate impressive pass rates on standardized code benchmarks using transformer architectures trained on large repositories (Chen et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Rozière et al., 2023; Allal et al., 2023). Evaluation suites such as HumanEval, MBPP, and CodeContests assess functional correctness and generalization across programming problems (Chen et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022). Common training corpora include filtered snapshots of GitHub, permissively licensed datasets, and mined solutions from online judges and forums archives. Nonetheless, naive decoding often yields brittle programs that overfit to examples or rely on undefined behavior. We impose resource-aware test synthesis during sampling to penalize spurious shortcuts. The decoding policy coordinates unit tests, type checks, and coverage to filter candidates online. We analyze error modes across categories including control flow, data structure usage, and numerics. Our method complements compiler feedback by steering exploration rather than patching after generation. Experiments show consistent improvements under adversarial test augmentation and reduced reliance on reference solutions. These findings illuminate how lightweight verification can shape practical code generation systems.
spans: Recent models demonstrate impressive pass rates on standardized code benchmarks using transformer architectures trained on large repositories (Chen et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Rozière et al., 2023; Allal et al., 2023). Evaluation suites such as HumanEval, MBPP, and CodeContests assess functional correctness and generalization across programming problems (Chen et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022). Common training corpora include filtered snapshots of GitHub, permissively licensed datasets, and mined solutions from online judges and forums archives.

document: Building accurate speech recognizers for low-resource languages remains a pressing problem. Scarcity of transcripts and pronunciation lexicons limits conventional supervised pipelines. Self-supervised pretraining and multilingual transfer offer a path to reduce annotation needs. However, domain mismatch and pronunciation variation degrade naive transfer to new languages. We explore data selection and representation adaptation for cross-lingual ASR in extreme low-resource regimes. Our framework jointly optimizes unsupervised objectives and pronunciation-aware fine-tuning. Recent models leverage contrastive or masked prediction over raw audio to learn robust features before fine-tuning (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Chung et al., 2021; Radford et al., 2023). Benchmark corpora for pretraining and evaluation include Librispeech, Multilingual LibriSpeech, VoxPopuli, and Common Voice, spanning diverse languages and recording conditions (Panayotov et al., 2015; Pratap et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Ardila et al., 2020). Phoneme inventories and grapheme conventions further complicate cross-lingual adaptation for end-to-end systems globally. To address these issues, we prioritize source data that matches target prosody and channel characteristics. A lightweight predictor estimates transferability using acoustic and text features without target labels. We incorporate phonotactic constraints during decoding to reduce implausible sequences in low-data settings. Our experiments span three typologically diverse languages with under one hour of supervision each. The analysis decomposes error by pronunciation class and borrowing patterns within code-switched segments. We report gains that persist across speakers and microphones, underscoring stable low-resource adaptation.
spans: Recent models leverage contrastive or masked prediction over raw audio to learn robust features before fine-tuning (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Chung et al., 2021; Radford et al., 2023). Benchmark corpora for pretraining and evaluation include Librispeech, Multilingual LibriSpeech, VoxPopuli, and Common Voice, spanning diverse languages and recording conditions (Panayotov et al., 2015; Pratap et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Ardila et al., 2020). Phoneme inventories and grapheme conventions further complicate cross-lingual adaptation for end-to-end systems globally.

document: Multimodal misinformation detection has emerged as a critical challenge at the intersection of NLP and computer vision due to the speed of content propagation. Despite steady progress, existing systems often conflate correlation with causation when fusing textual and visual cues, motivating a closer look at modeling choices. Prior surveys emphasize limitations in robustness and generalization under distribution shift, which informs our problem framing. We therefore examine alignment, calibration, and evidence attribution as levers for building reliable detectors. Early multimodal detectors coupled convolutional text embeddings with visual backbones such as VGG-16 and ResNet-50, reporting results on FakeNewsNet and Twitter15/16 while relying on handcrafted features for propagation patterns. Subsequent work adopted attention-based fusion with BiLSTM or Transformer encoders, incorporating image regions from Faster R-CNN and language representations from BERT or RoBERTa, and evaluated on Weibo, GossipCop, and PolitiFact subsets. Memory networks and graph neural networks appeared next, linking posts, users, and images through GCN or GAT layers, sometimes augmented with knowledge bases like ConceptNet and VisualGenome to supply entity and relation grounding. Temporal rumor detection leveraged recurrent units and Hawkes process features, whereas contrastive formulations trained SimCLR variants or CLIP-style objectives on MM-COVID, Fakeddit, and PHEME to align semantics across modalities under weak supervision. Data augmentation strategies included back-translation, MixUp for embeddings, CutMix for images, and entity masking, with reported gains on CoAID, MediaEval Verifying Multimedia Use, and the SemEval-2020 Task 11 propaganda benchmark. Calibration and uncertainty estimation appeared via temperature scaling, MC dropout, and deep ensembles, accompanied by robustness tests using ImageNet-C style corruptions adapted for memes and adversarial text paraphrases generated by TextFooler and BAE. Explanations were reported through Grad-CAM, LIME, and Integrated Gradients, and datasets emphasizing cross-lingual phenomena introduced X-Fakeddit, PAN-X rumor corpora, and multilingual extensions of BuzzFace with aligned visual annotations and temporal shifts. These patterns reveal a fragmentation across datasets, objectives, and evaluation practices that complicates cumulative progress. In particular, evidence attribution rarely aligns with human-identifiable manipulations, leading to spurious correlations under benign covariate shift. We focus on disentangling correlation from causal signals by constraining modality interactions and validating with counterfactual tests. Our analysis targets distribution shift settings curated to reflect realistic platform interventions and content recycling. By aligning modeling choices with diagnostics that measure attribution faithfulness, we aim to clarify what constitutes genuine multimodal verification.

Data augmentation strategies included back-translation, MixUp for embeddings, CutMix for images, and entity masking, with reported gains on CoAID, MediaEval Verifying Multimedia Use, and the SemEval-2020 Task 11 propaganda benchmark. Calibration and uncertainty estimation appeared via temperature scaling, MC dropout, and deep ensembles, accompanied by robustness tests using ImageNet-C style corruptions adapted for memes and adversarial text paraphrases generated by TextFooler and BAE. document: Personalized recommendation has shifted toward graph-based formulations in response to the sparsity and heterogeneity of user-item interactions. However, inductive generalization and debiasing remain difficult when exposure and preference are entangled in historical logs. We frame the problem as learning robust relational signals while accounting for selection and popularity biases. To motivate our design, we review representative models and datasets spanning static, temporal, and counterfactual settings. Early neural recommenders combined matrix factorization with multilayer perceptrons, exemplified by NCF and NeuMF on MovieLens and Pinterest, while pairwise formulations adopted BPR losses to optimize top-k ranking metrics such as Recall and NDCG. Graph-based approaches then emerged, with NGCF propagating embeddings along user–item edges, PinSage scaling random walks for web-scale graphs, and LightGCN simplifying message passing by removing transformations and nonlinearities on Gowalla and Yelp. Inductive settings leveraged GraphSAGE and GAT to embed cold-start nodes from sampled neighborhoods, and temporal dynamics were modeled with TGAT, JODIE, and DySAT using time-stamped interactions from Reddit, LastFM, and Taobao. Self-supervised learning introduced contrastive objectives such as InfoNCE, as in SGL, SimGCL, and NCL, preparing augmented graphs through node dropout, edge perturbation, and random walk views on Amazon-Books and Alibaba iFashion. Knowledge-aware recommenders incorporated side information via KGCN, RippleNet, and MetaKG, enriching propagation with TransE-style relation embeddings and entity paths from Freebase, DBpedia, and ConceptNet. Exposure bias and popularity skew were addressed by IPS and SNIPS reweighting, MACR regularization, and causal formulations like CR and DICE, alongside counterfactual estimators trained on KuaiRec and simulated bandit logs. Evaluation commonly reported HR, NDCG, and MRR under leave-one-out protocols with fixed negative sampling seeds, using public splits from Amazon-CDs, Yelp2018, and Gowalla, and cross-domain transfers between Steam, Book-Crossing, and Douban with reproducible random seed control. Together, these trends indicate a maturation of architectures but persistent vulnerability to confounding exposures in offline evaluation. We address this by separating preference modeling from exposure modeling and by enforcing invariances across perturbations of the interaction graph. Our framework targets inductive cold-start and counterfactual re-ranking scenarios where deployment constraints preclude full retraining. We further standardize evaluation via unified splits and calibrated metrics that account for popularity skew. The resulting analysis clarifies when graph propagation helps beyond capacity scaling and when it merely amplifies bias.
spans: Early neural recommenders combined matrix factorization with multilayer perceptrons, exemplified by NCF and NeuMF on MovieLens and Pinterest, while pairwise formulations adopted BPR losses to optimize top-k ranking metrics such as Recall and NDCG. Graph-based approaches then emerged, with NGCF propagating embeddings along user–item edges, PinSage scaling random walks for web-scale graphs, and LightGCN simplifying message passing by removing transformations and nonlinearities on Gowalla and Yelp. Inductive settings leveraged GraphSAGE and GAT to embed cold-start nodes from sampled neighborhoods, and temporal dynamics were modeled with TGAT, JODIE, and DySAT using time-stamped interactions from Reddit, LastFM, and Taobao. Self-supervised learning introduced contrastive objectives such as InfoNCE, as in SGL, SimGCL, and NCL, preparing augmented graphs through node dropout, edge perturbation, and random walk views on Amazon-Books and Alibaba iFashion. Knowledge-aware recommenders incorporated side information via KGCN, RippleNet, and MetaKG, enriching propagation with TransE-style relation embeddings and entity paths from Freebase, DBpedia, and ConceptNet. document: Low-resource neural machine translation remains challenging due to limited parallel corpora and domain shift between available sources and target usage. While multilingual pretraining has reduced the gap, adaptation strategies often underperform when lexical and syntactic divergence is high. We investigate how data curation and objective design interact to determine transfer success. To ground our approach, we first catalogue representative techniques and benchmarks used for low-resource evaluation. Back-translation and iterative back-translation have been extensively applied using monolingual data from Common Crawl and Wikipedia, generating synthetic pairs for systems based on Transformer-base and Transformer-big across WMT14 and WMT16 tracks. Unsupervised and weakly supervised approaches rely on cross-lingual embeddings from LASER and multilingual language models such as mBART, XLM, and mT5, initializing encoders and decoders for small bilingual fine-tuning on OPUS subsets. Meta-learning variants adapt MAML, Reptile, and Meta-KD to translation tasks, simulating few-shot episodes with IWSLT and TED Talks corpora and transferring to unseen language pairs including Nepali–English and Sinhala–English. Noise-robust objectives include denoising autoencoding, masked span prediction, and round-trip consistency, often paired with dual learning and minimum risk training to stabilize training under limited supervision. Sparse and efficient models incorporate adapters, LoRA, and prefix tuning to reduce parameter footprints, evaluated with BLEU, chrF, and COMET on Europarl, JW300, and Bible corpora. Data selection heuristics employ cross-entropy difference, bilingual lexical similarity, and temperature-based sampling, curating shards from ParaCrawl, CCAligned, and News Commentary to balance domain and quality. Constraint-based decoding uses lexicon injection, class-based soft constraints, and lexically constrained beam search, and domain adaptation leverages instance weighting, vocabulary re-mapping, and term banks from medical and legal glossaries. Evaluation setups frequently include small dev sets, script normalization, byte-level BPE tokenization, and multilingual joint training schedules across families and regions. Despite these innovations, reproducibility varies due to inconsistent preprocessing, data filtering, and evaluation pipelines. We center our study on principled data selection and constrained adaptation under documented domain and grammar shifts. Our objectives disentangle lexical memorization from structural transfer using targeted probes and counterfactual data. We also prioritize cost-aware updates amenable to on-device or nonephemeral deployment. This focus clarifies which ingredients matter for genuine low-resource gains beyond scale and dataset curation artifacts.

document: Industrial IoT deployments rely on timely anomaly detection to prevent cascading failures and safety incidents. Yet practitioners face distribution drift, missing data, and multimodal correlations that complicate reliable monitoring. We approach the problem by combining predictive uncertainty with structure-aware reconstruction signals. To contextualize design choices, we first summarize common datasets, architectures, and evaluation practices in the literature. Classical methods include ARIMA residual tests, STL decomposition with robust z-scores, and spectral diagnostics, often contrasted with one-class SVMs and Isolation Forest on Yahoo S5 and the Numenta Anomaly Benchmark. Deep reconstruction approaches employ denoising autoencoders, variational autoencoders, and contractive autoencoders, with Donut, OmniAnomaly, and USAD reporting F1 and PRAUC on SMAP and MSL from the NASA telemetry corpus. Sequence prediction models utilize LSTM, GRU, and TCN forecasters with error-based detection, and multivariate settings add attention or transformer layers for cross-sensor coupling on SWaT and WADI water treatment logs. Adversarial formulations adopt GAN-based discriminators and sequence critics as in TadGAN and MAD-GAN, balancing reconstruction and discrimination losses to delineate subtle deviations under noisy conditions. Probabilistic techniques introduce normalizing flows, autoregressive likelihoods, and deep state space models, estimating per-timestep densities and tail probabilities for thresholding on Yahoo Webscope and KPI datasets. Graph and relational models represent sensors as nodes connected by topology or learned affinities, using GCN, Graph WaveNet, or Temporal Fusion Transformers to capture long-range structure in power grid and HVAC testbeds. Evaluation protocols standardize point-adjusted scoring, latency-aware metrics, and tolerance windows, with public splits from NAB, UCR anomaly archives, and the SKAB benchmark for pump systems. Labeling practices consider delayed effects, composite alarms, overlapping anomalies, and maintenance interventions recorded in incident tickets and operator shift logs across deployments. Despite this breadth, ground truth ambiguity and post-hoc scoring rules can inflate performance beyond actionable value. We reconcile detection accuracy with operational latency by aligning metrics to intervention costs and change propagation. Our method integrates topology priors and calibrated uncertainty to triage alerts under missing and delayed labels. We further test robustness under simulated sensor dropouts and drift aligned with real maintenance records. This yields a more faithful picture of readiness for sustained industrial monitoring.

document: Federated learning enables on-device training without centralizing raw data, but heterogeneity, privacy, and efficiency remain open challenges. In practice, teams must balance test accuracy with communication, fairness, and formal privacy guarantees across diverse clients. We take a principled view that couples personalization with certified privacy under realistic participation patterns. Before presenting our approach, we survey representative optimization, privacy, and personalization techniques and benchmarks. Canonical optimization uses FedAvg with periodic client aggregation, while FedProx, SCAFFOLD, and FedAdam stabilize updates under non-IID data, and q-FedAvg incorporates fairness-aware objectives for skewed loss distributions. Personalization strategies include pFedMe, Per-FedAvg, Ditto, and FedRep, which decompose global and local parameters or add proximal penalties to accommodate client-specific optima on LEAF FEMNIST and Shakespeare. Clustered and multi-task variants such as IFCA, FedFomo, and MOCHA partition clients by relatedness or jointly solve related objectives, with evaluations on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Stack Overflow next-word prediction. Privacy mechanisms build on DP-SGD with moments accountant or Rényi DP, PATE-style teacher aggregation, and secure aggregation protocols following Bonawitz et al., integrated with compression via quantization, sparsification, and sketching. Communication efficiency employs local steps, gradient accumulation, and error feedback, and compression-friendly optimizers like SignSGD, Top-k SGD, and FedCOMGATE are benchmarked under limited uplink budgets. Threat models consider membership inference, property inference, and gradient inversion attacks such as Deep Leakage from Gradients and iDLG, along with model poisoning and backdoor insertion. Public benchmarks include LEAF, TensorFlow Federated simulations, and FedScale, with client availability traces from Reddit, Google Speech Commands, and OpenLoris to model partial participation and churn. Hardware constraints are represented through edge accelerators and mobile CPUs, and heterogeneity is emulated by Dirichlet partitions and temporal sampling masks across client cohorts in repeated rounds of training. Despite promising results, comparisons are confounded by inconsistent privacy accounting, differing client samplers, and opaque preprocessing. We standardize these factors and examine the trade space between personalization and certified privacy. Our formulation separates population and client-level risks using a bilevel objective with calibrated noise. We further capture real availability by replaying traces during training and evaluation. The resulting insights clarify when private personalization is feasible under realistic participation budgets.
