document: Document-level question answering over visually-rich documents requires reasoning over text, layout, and graphics. Recent systems leverage pre-trained vision-language encoders while grappling with domain shift and limited labeled data. We focus on grounding questions to structure that emerges from layout regularities and lexical cues. Prior approaches vary widely in how they encode spatial relations and textual semantics. Work on visually rich document QA spans pretraining and datasets, including LayoutLM and LayoutLMv2 for text-layout fusion, Donut and TILT as OCR-free or semi-structured models, DocFormer and LiLT for multimodal transformers, and benchmarks such as DocVQA, InfographicVQA, FUNSD, SROIE, ChartQA, and TextCaps, alongside tools like Detectron2-based detectors and Tesseract OCR pipelines widely adopted in evaluations across common leaderboards and shared tasks alike. Despite these advances, disparate evaluation protocols obscure which components truly aid generalization across templates. We therefore establish a controlled benchmark that isolates layout invariance from linguistic paraphrase. Building on this setup, we introduce a modular encoder that disentangles reading order, relative position, and semantic similarity. The architecture enables ablations that attribute gains to specific inductive biases rather than parameter scale. Experiments on zero-shot transfer reveal consistent benefits from compositional layout tokens when fonts and page geometry change. We release code, models, and diagnostic splits to encourage reproducible analysis of grounding behaviors.
spans: Work on visually rich document QA spans pretraining and datasets, including LayoutLM and LayoutLMv2 for text-layout fusion, Donut and TILT as OCR-free or semi-structured models, DocFormer and LiLT for multimodal transformers, and benchmarks such as DocVQA, InfographicVQA, FUNSD, SROIE, ChartQA, and TextCaps, alongside tools like Detectron2-based detectors and Tesseract OCR pipelines widely adopted in evaluations across common leaderboards and shared tasks alike.

document: Detecting anomalies in attributed graphs underpins monitoring for fraud, intrusion, and malfunction. Practical deployments must operate with scarce labels while contending with evolving topology. We study inductive detectors that reconcile local structure and attribute sparsity. Prior research offers disparate training signals, from context prediction to self-supervised reconstructions. However, comparative evidence remains confounded by evaluation shortcuts and leakage. Representative methods span embedding and reconstruction families, including DeepWalk, node2vec, and GraphSAGE for structural context; GAE, VGAE, and DOMINANT for autoencoding; CoLA and AnomalyDAE for contrastive or denoising objectives; and evaluations commonly report on Cora, Citeseer, PubMed, Amazon Computers, and YelpChi, with implementations in PyTorch Geometric and DGL standardizing preprocessing and negative sampling protocols under transductive and inductive graph settings alike. To address these gaps, we formulate a perturbation-invariant objective that calibrates rarity against neighborhood stability. The design permits principled thresholds via conformal risk control on sliding windows. Our analysis decomposes performance by anomaly archetype and graph stationarity. Extensive experiments on temporal benchmarks demonstrate reliable early detection with bounded false alarms. We discuss limitations in highly clustered regimes and avenues for robust adaptation.
spans: Representative methods span embedding and reconstruction families, including DeepWalk, node2vec, and GraphSAGE for structural context; GAE, VGAE, and DOMINANT for autoencoding; CoLA and AnomalyDAE for contrastive or denoising objectives; and evaluations commonly report on Cora, Citeseer, PubMed, Amazon Computers, and YelpChi, with implementations in PyTorch Geometric and DGL standardizing preprocessing and negative sampling protocols under transductive and inductive graph settings alike.

document: Probabilistic time series forecasting supports planning in energy, retail, and transportation. Nonstationarity and covariate shifts complicate training regimes that rely on fixed windows or calendar features. We revisit sequence modeling backbones to separate representation, uncertainty, and seasonality. Recent models range from classical ARIMA and Prophet to neural methods such as DeepAR, N-BEATS, Temporal Fusion Transformers, Informer, Autoformer, FEDformer, PatchTST, and DLinear, evaluated on benchmarks including M4, M5, ETT (ETTh1/ETTm2), Electricity, Traffic, Exchange Rate, Weather, and ILI, with toolkits like GluonTS, PyTorch Forecasting, and darts facilitating training pipelines and standardized metrics across long-horizon tasks and multivariate covariate configurations alike. Despite this variety, reported gains often conflate distributional targets with likelihood choices. We propose a decomposition that unifies scale handling and calendar conditioning across horizons. The approach introduces a calibration layer that normalizes residual dynamics before distributional fitting. Coupled with multi-horizon quantile supervision, it yields interpretable components without sacrificing accuracy. A common loader ensures identical splits, losses, and metrics across baselines. On electricity, traffic, and retail datasets, the method improves MASE and CRPS with fewer parameters. We release templates enabling reproducible ablations across public corpora.
spans: Recent models range from classical ARIMA and Prophet to neural methods such as DeepAR, N-BEATS, Temporal Fusion Transformers, Informer, Autoformer, FEDformer, PatchTST, and DLinear, evaluated on benchmarks including M4, M5, ETT (ETTh1/ETTm2), Electricity, Traffic, Exchange Rate, Weather, and ILI, with toolkits like GluonTS, PyTorch Forecasting, and darts facilitating training pipelines and standardized metrics across long-horizon tasks and multivariate covariate configurations alike.

document: Accurate delineation of lesions in volumetric imaging is crucial for prognosis and treatment planning. Deep networks have improved segmentation but often struggle with inter-scanner variability and limited annotations. We investigate architectural and training choices that stabilize performance under domain shift. Our analysis focuses on decoupling localization sensitivity from boundary precision. We hypothesize that normalization and receptive field control are primary drivers of robustness. Segmentation research comprises canonical architectures and benchmarks, including U-Net, 3D U-Net, V-Net, nnU-Net, Attention U-Net, DeepLabV3+, TransUNet, and Swin-UNet, evaluated on BraTS, ISLES, MSD, KiTS, and ACDC datasets, with pipelines frequently implemented in MONAI and nnU-Net frameworks, and preprocessing relying on SimpleITK spacing normalization, intensity z-scoring, and patch-based tiling strategies for memory-constrained training in three-dimensional acquisitions and cross-vendor clinical cohorts alike. Building on these observations, we introduce a lightweight prior that regularizes feature scales across resolutions. The mechanism complements standard augmentations by constraining intensity statistics without paired adaptation. A calibrated uncertainty head flags slices likely to benefit from clinician review. Experiments across scanners quantify gains under harmonization-free setups. We provide code and cross-site splits to promote transparent comparisons.
spans: Segmentation research comprises canonical architectures and benchmarks, including U-Net, 3D U-Net, V-Net, nnU-Net, Attention U-Net, DeepLabV3+, TransUNet, and Swin-UNet, evaluated on BraTS, ISLES, MSD, KiTS, and ACDC datasets, with pipelines frequently implemented in MONAI and nnU-Net frameworks, and preprocessing relying on SimpleITK spacing normalization, intensity z-scoring, and patch-based tiling strategies for memory-constrained training in three-dimensional acquisitions and cross-vendor clinical cohorts alike.

document: Cross-lingual retrieval enables users to search across languages without parallel queries. The challenge lies in aligning semantic spaces while respecting language-specific morphology and scripts. Recent embedding models reduce translation reliance but face robustness gaps in long-tail domains. We examine retrieval architectures that separate multilingual alignment from document relevance modeling. Key resources and methods include mBERT, XLM-R, LaBSE, and LASER for multilingual representations; ColBERT-X, mColBERT, and SPLADE for late interaction and sparse expansion; datasets such as MIRACL, Mr.TyDi, mMARCO, and XOR-TyDi QA; and infrastructure like FAISS, Anserini, and Pyserini that underpin dense indexing, approximate nearest neighbor search, and cross-encoder reranking in multilingual retrieval studies spanning scripts and resource levels without translation. Prior results are difficult to compare due to inconsistent tokenization and per-language tuning. We introduce a standardized pipeline that controls indexing, query expansion, and reranking across typologically diverse corpora. The design decouples lexical and semantic signals through layered retrieval with calibrated score fusion. Our analyses highlight where alignment errors versus term mismatch dominate failure modes. On MIRACL and Mr.TyDi, we report consistent gains in low-resource languages without external translation. We share training scripts and indexes to support reproducible cross-lingual experiments.
spans: Key resources and methods include mBERT, XLM-R, LaBSE, and LASER for multilingual representations; ColBERT-X, mColBERT, and SPLADE for late interaction and sparse expansion; datasets such as MIRACL, Mr.TyDi, mMARCO, and XOR-TyDi QA; and infrastructure like FAISS, Anserini, and Pyserini that underpin dense indexing, approximate nearest neighbor search, and cross-encoder reranking in multilingual retrieval studies spanning scripts and resource levels without translation.

document:
Introduction

Clinical text mining enables downstream applications such as cohort discovery and pharmacovigilance. Extracting entities like medications, diagnoses, and laboratory findings remains challenging due to idiosyncratic language and privacy constraints. Models trained on one institution often degrade when applied to another with different documentation styles. This distribution shift interacts with label scarcity, since annotations are costly and protected. A growing body of research explores representation learning and transfer strategies to address these issues. However, comparative evidence under strict privacy and governance settings is limited.

Prior work on clinical named entity recognition spans traditional sequence models and modern transformers. Early systems leveraged BiLSTM-CRF decoders (Lample et al., 2016; Ma and Hovy, 2016) with ELMo embeddings (Peters et al., 2018), and were evaluated on CoNLL-2003, i2b2 2010/2012, and BC5CDR (Li et al., 2016). Subsequent studies adopted contextual pretraining, including BioBERT and ClinicalBERT (Lee et al., 2020; Alsentzer et al., 2019) and SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019), often fine-tuned with CRF layers (Lafferty et al., 2001). Domain adaptation and robustness have been explored with adversarial training (Ganin et al., 2016), self-training with labels (Ruder and Plank, 2018), multi-source transfer (Guo et al., 2018), and data augmentation using synonym replacement and back-translation (Wei and Zou, 2019; Sennrich et al., 2016).

Yet little is known about how these design choices behave when institutions cannot share text or identifiers. We investigate settings where only parameter updates or masked statistics may be exchanged across sites. Our study formalizes privacy-constrained transfer and tests practical recipes that respect operational boundaries. We quantify performance, calibration, and drift sensitivity under realistic cross-hospital protocols. The findings aim to guide practitioners seeking robust extraction with minimal data movement. We also outline implications for governance, reproducibility, and compliance in clinical NLP deployments.

Early systems leveraged BiLSTM-CRF decoders (Lample et al., 2016; Ma and Hovy, 2016) with ELMo embeddings (Peters et al., 2018), and were evaluated on CoNLL-2003, i2b2 2010/2012, and BC5CDR (Li et al., 2016). Subsequent studies adopted contextual pretraining, including BioBERT and ClinicalBERT (Lee et al., 2020; Alsentzer et al., 2019) and SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019), often fine-tuned with CRF layers (Lafferty et al., 2001). Domain adaptation and robustness have been explored with adversarial training (Ganin et al., 2016), self-training with labels (Ruder and Plank, 2018), multi-source transfer (Guo et al., 2018), and data augmentation using synonym replacement and back-translation (Wei and Zou, 2019; Sennrich et al., 2016).

document:
Introduction

Personalized recommendation under sparse feedback remains a central challenge in modern marketplaces. Production systems must balance accuracy with privacy, efficiency, and fairness constraints. As user behavior fragments across devices and sessions, sequence modeling becomes increasingly important. Moreover, new items appear continuously, creating cold-start scenarios with limited interaction history. Recent attention has turned to methods that leverage structure, side information, and self-supervision. Yet consistent evaluations for extreme cold-start under short sessions are comparatively rare. To contextualize our study, we summarize representative neural and graph-based approaches.

Session-based and sequential recommendation has progressed through GRU4Rec (Hidasi et al., 2016), SASRec (Kang and McAuley, 2018), NextItNet (Yuan et al., 2019), and BERT4Rec (Sun et al., 2019), often benchmarked on datasets MovieLens-1M, Amazon, Yelp, and Retailrocket. Graph-enhanced models incorporate item and user relations via GCN, PinSage, and LightGCN (Hamilton et al., 2017; Ying et al., 2018; He et al., 2020), while knowledge-aware approaches such as RippleNet and KGAT connect items to external entities (Wang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019). For cold-start personalization, meta-learning and representation transfer have been explored with MAML-style adaptation and MeLU (Finn et al., 2017; Lee et al., 2019), alongside self-supervised pretraining objectives exemplified by S3-Rec and CL4SRec (Zhou et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2020).

Despite these advances, little is known about performance when users provide only a handful of clicks. We study budgeted interaction regimes where models must adapt within five or fewer events. Our experiments compare lightweight adaptation modules under controlled latency budgets. We analyze stability to catalog churn and exposure bias in rapidly evolving inventories. The results highlight failure modes and tradeoffs pertinent to deploying session models in practice. We conclude by discussing implications for evaluation design and responsible recommendation.
spans: Session-based and sequential recommendation has progressed through GRU4Rec (Hidasi et al., 2016), SASRec (Kang and McAuley, 2018), NextItNet (Yuan et al., 2019), and BERT4Rec (Sun et al., 2019), often benchmarked on datasets MovieLens-1M, Amazon, Yelp, and Retailrocket. Graph-enhanced models incorporate item and user relations via GCN, PinSage, and LightGCN (Hamilton et al., 2017; Ying et al., 2018; He et al., 2020), while knowledge-aware approaches such as RippleNet and KGAT connect items to external entities (Wang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019). For cold-start personalization, meta-learning and representation transfer have been explored with MAML-style adaptation and MeLU (Finn et al., 2017; Lee et al., 2019), alongside self-supervised pretraining objectives exemplified by S3-Rec and CL4SRec (Zhou et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2020).

document:
Introduction

Automatic speech recognition has seen dramatic gains with self-supervision and large-scale data. However, many languages remain underserved due to limited resources and domain variability. Mismatches in channel, accent, and code-switching complicate deployment for global users. Robust models must generalize across domains while preserving low latency and small memory. We examine data mixing strategies and decoding constraints for multilingual scenarios.

Large-scale automatic speech recognition has advanced with self-supervised pretraining and robust multilingual finetuning across diverse corpora in low-resource and high-resource settings alike. Foundational datasets include LibriSpeech, TED-LIUM, VoxPopuli, and Common Voice and Fisher Spanish-English and Switchboard (Panayotov et al., 2015; Rousseau et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2021; Ardila et al., 2020). Model architectures span CTC and RNN-T decoders over CNN or Transformer encoders, alongside wav2vec 2.0, HuBERT, and XLSR-style pretraining (Graves et al., 2006; Graves, 2012; Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Conneau et al., 2020). Multilingual and zero-shot transfer have been explored using SpeechStew, MMS, and Whisper training recipes with cross-lingual subword vocabularies and language-agnostic features (Ghodsi et al., 2021; Pratap et al., 2023; Radford et al., 2023).

While such ingredients are powerful, recipe interactions remain opaque for mixed-language utterances. We investigate how sampling, lexicon normalization, and language tags impact code-switching accuracy. Our analysis emphasizes deployment-friendly beam pruning and on-device constraints. We present controlled ablations that vary language coverage and acoustic condition. We then quantify robustness to noisy transcripts and domain drift in streaming contexts. The study yields actionable settings for fitting multilingual models under bounded compute. We outline future work on dynamic language routing and rapid language addition.
spans: Large-scale automatic speech recognition has advanced with self-supervised pretraining and robust multilingual finetuning across diverse corpora in low-resource and high-resource settings alike. Foundational datasets include LibriSpeech, TED-LIUM, VoxPopuli, and Common Voice and Fisher Spanish-English and Switchboard (Panayotov et al., 2015; Rousseau et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2021; Ardila et al., 2020). Model architectures span CTC and RNN-T decoders over CNN or Transformer encoders, alongside wav2vec 2.0, HuBERT, and XLSR-style pretraining (Graves et al., 2006; Graves, 2012; Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Conneau et al., 2020). Multilingual and zero-shot transfer have been explored using SpeechStew, MMS, and Whisper training recipes with cross-lingual subword vocabularies and language-agnostic features (Ghodsi et al., 2021; Pratap et al., 2023; Radford et al., 2023).

document:
Related Work

Estimating causal effects from electronic health records is crucial for decision support. Observational data introduce confounding, missingness, and treatment-selection biases. Practical pipelines must balance identification assumptions with model misspecification and variance. Moreover, healthcare interventions evolve over time, complicating evaluation with time-varying confounders. Researchers have proposed a range of adjustment and learning strategies to address these concerns. We review representative approaches and datasets before detailing our study design.

Estimation of treatment effects from observational data has leveraged a spectrum of statistical and machine learning tools. Classical methods include propensity score matching and inverse probability weighting (Rosenbaum and Rubin, 1983; Robins et al., 2000), augmented inverse probability weighting and targeted maximum likelihood estimation (Bang and Robins, 2005; van der Laan and Rubin, 2006). Representation-learning approaches such as TARNet, Dragonnet, and balancing neural networks aim to reduce covariate shift (Shalit et al., 2017; Shi et al., 2019; Johansson et al., 2016). Heterogeneous effects are modeled with Causal Forests and additive regression trees (Wager and Athey, 2018; Chipman et al., 2010), and evaluations use IHDP, JOBS II, ACIC, and MIMIC-III benchmarks (Hill, 2011; LaLonde, 1986; Dorie et al., 2019; Johnson et al., 2016).

Despite this progress, uncertainty quantification and transportability across hospitals remain underexplored. We investigate estimators that deliver interval estimates while maintaining competitive bias under shift. Our evaluation isolates overlap violations and sensitivity to unobserved confounding proxies. We design semi-synthetic tasks anchored in real covariate distributions to stress-test methods. The results clarify tradeoffs between efficiency, calibration, and robustness for clinical use. We conclude with guidance for linking causal estimands to operational decisions in care pathways.
spans: Estimation of treatment effects from observational data has leveraged a spectrum of statistical and machine learning tools. Classical methods include propensity score matching and inverse probability weighting (Rosenbaum and Rubin, 1983; Robins et al., 2000), augmented inverse probability weighting and targeted maximum likelihood estimation (Bang and Robins, 2005; van der Laan and Rubin, 2006). Representation-learning approaches such as TARNet, Dragonnet, and balancing neural networks aim to reduce covariate shift (Shalit et al., 2017; Shi et al., 2019; Johansson et al., 2016). Heterogeneous effects are modeled with Causal Forests and additive regression trees (Wager and Athey, 2018; Chipman et al., 2010), and evaluations use IHDP, JOBS II, ACIC, and MIMIC-III benchmarks (Hill, 2011; LaLonde, 1986; Dorie et al., 2019; Johnson et al., 2016).

document:
Introduction

Predicting molecular properties underpins discovery in materials science and drug design. Graph-based learning offers a natural interface to encode chemical structure and constraints. However, generalization across scaffolds and assay domains remains a persistent challenge. To situate our contribution, we summarize representative neural architectures and benchmarks.

Molecular property prediction with learned graph representations builds on message passing mechanisms across atom-bond graphs. Foundational architectures include GCN, GAT, and MPNN variants (Kipf and Welling, 2017; Veličković et al., 2018; Gilmer et al., 2017), along with physics-aware models such as SchNet and DimeNet (Schütt et al., 2017; Klicpera et al., 2020). Recent transformer-style encoders, including Graphormer and GROVER, extend fields and incorporate priors (Ying et al., 2021; Rong et al., 2020). Standard benchmarks comprise MoleculeNet tasks over QM9, ZINC, PCBA, and FreeSolv with scaffold splits and ROC-AUC or MAE metrics (Wu et al., 2018). Pretraining and transfer leverage masked atom modeling and contrastive objectives in ChemBERTa, MolCLR, and GraphCL (Chithrananda et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; You et al., 2020).

Yet systematic evaluations of data efficiency and split robustness across assays are limited. We examine low-data regimes with strict scaffold splits and realistic noise injection. Our framework assesses models under controlled augmentation and constrained hyperparameter budgets. We quantify uncertainty and calibration to inform risk-aware screening decisions. The study also reports ablations isolating message depth, positional encodings, and readout choices. Our results expose stability gaps that matter for high-throughput candidate triage. We discuss implications for pretraining strategies and benchmark design in cheminformatics.
spans: Molecular property prediction with learned graph representations builds on message passing mechanisms across atom-bond graphs. Foundational architectures include GCN, GAT, and MPNN variants (Kipf and Welling, 2017; Veličković et al., 2018; Gilmer et al., 2017), along with physics-aware models such as SchNet and DimeNet (Schütt et al., 2017; Klicpera et al., 2020). Recent transformer-style encoders, including Graphormer and GROVER, extend fields and incorporate priors (Ying et al., 2021; Rong et al., 2020). Standard benchmarks comprise MoleculeNet tasks over QM9, ZINC, PCBA, and FreeSolv with scaffold splits and ROC-AUC or MAE metrics (Wu et al., 2018). Pretraining and transfer leverage masked atom modeling and contrastive objectives in ChemBERTa, MolCLR, and GraphCL (Chithrananda et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; You et al., 2020).

document: Multimodal sentiment analysis seeks to infer user attitudes by integrating text, images, and audio from social platforms. Its promise lies in capturing affective cues that are lost when modalities are processed in isolation. However, practical settings present noisy inputs, colloquialisms, and rapidly drifting visual memes. This section reviews progress while identifying limits that inform our study. Early work fused handcrafted visual features with bag of words representations to predict polarity labels. With deep learning, joint encoders emerged that align representations across modalities. Temporal models further captured conversational context and user specific baselines. Despite gains, models still struggle with distribution shift and spurious correlations between modality specific artifacts and labels. Benchmarks commonly emphasize in domain accuracy rather than stable generalization. Moreover, annotation guidelines rarely calibrate frames like sarcasm or multimodal incongruity. These gaps motivate architectures that disentangle modality evidence and expose uncertainty. They also call for evaluation that stresses robustness and accountability. We therefore revisit cross modal attention and causal regularization under a unified training schedule. Multimodal sentiment modeling has been explored with Tensor Fusion Network, Low-rank Multimodal Fusion, and MulT, alongside graph-based memory approaches and multimodal transformers (Zadeh et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2018; Tsai et al., 2019; Hazarika et al., 2020). Common datasets include CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, MVSA-Single, MVSA-Multiple, and Twitter15 or Twitter17 for image-text polarity (Zadeh et al., 2016; Niu et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018). Pretraining strategies span image-text contrastive learning, visual entailment pretraining, and masked language modeling with visual tags (Radford et al., 2021; Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019). Evaluation typically reports accuracy and correlation metrics. While these resources catalyzed rapid progress, they expose biases toward Western, English speaking users. Visual memes and filters also change faster than benchmark updates. Cross modality misalignment confounds attention mechanisms that conflate salience with sentiment. Inconsistent annotation guidelines further entrench dataset specific shortcuts. Several studies explore debiasing, but many rely on metadata that is unavailable at test time. Others introduce adversarial objectives that destabilize training and sacrifice calibration. There remains a need to quantify how much each modality actually contributes to final predictions. We address this by structuring attention as a set of causal queries over modality specific interventions. The design separates content evidence from stylistic confounds using counterfactual masks. It also encourages sparse attributions that are traceable to tokens and regions. To probe robustness, we create stress tests that perturb incongruent channels. We further assess whether models maintain sign consistency under controlled flips of individual modalities. Our experiments cover both sentence level and utterance level settings. We evaluate out of distribution performance through temporal slices and creator splits. We report predictive accuracy, calibration error, and stability under controlled corruption. The results indicate strong gains on robustness without sacrificing in domain accuracy. We conclude by discussing implications for fair moderation and human centered deployment.
spans: Multimodal sentiment modeling has been explored with Tensor Fusion Network, Low-rank Multimodal Fusion, and MulT, alongside graph-based memory approaches and multimodal transformers (Zadeh et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2018; Tsai et al., 2019; Hazarika et al., 2020). Common datasets include CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, MVSA-Single, MVSA-Multiple, and Twitter15 or Twitter17 for image-text polarity (Zadeh et al., 2016; Niu et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018). Pretraining strategies span image-text contrastive learning, visual entailment pretraining, and masked language modeling with visual tags (Radford et al., 2021; Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019). Evaluation typically reports accuracy and correlation metrics.

document: Traffic forecasting underpins routing, congestion mitigation, and proactive control in urban networks. Spatiotemporal dependencies and irregular sensor layouts make the task challenging. Graph neural networks provide a natural inductive bias for modeling road topology. Nevertheless, temporal heterogeneity and missing data cause brittle predictions during incidents. This section surveys advances and frames the limitations addressed in our approach. Early pipelines decomposed flow into seasonal and residual components with ARIMA variants. Neural models later replaced manual seasonality with gated recurrence on lagged signals. Convolutional architectures projected sensors onto grids to reuse image operators. Graph convolutions improved locality by diffusing information along directed edges. Attention mechanisms further captured long range dependencies across corridors. Yet most models assume fixed graphs that ignore dynamic control and lane closures. Transfer across cities also remains difficult due to scale and sensor heterogeneity. Robust forecasting requires principled treatment of topology changes and covariate shift. We pursue adaptive graphs and causal regularizers within a unified training routine. We also revisit imputation strategies that respect conservation laws. Recent graph-based predictors include DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, GMAN, and AGCRN, which variously employ diffusion convolution, temporal convolutions, spatiotemporal attention, and adaptive adjacency learning (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Zheng et al., 2020; Bai et al., 2020). Benchmarks commonly use METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03, PEMS04, PEMS07, and PEMS08 with MAE, RMSE, and MAPE metrics (Li et al., 2018; Song et al., 2020). Data augmentation strategies include noise injection, time warping, and mixup for sequences (Um et al., 2017; DeVries and Taylor, 2017; Zhang et al., 2018). External features include weather, events, and holidays. Despite strong scores, most evaluations consider routine conditions with limited incident coverage. Predictors frequently extrapolate poorly when topology or demand patterns shift. Static graphs cannot reflect closures, reversible lanes, or signal retiming. Similarly, missing sensors trigger propagation errors that cascade across horizons. The interaction of control policies with learned dynamics remains understudied. We target these limitations with a model that adapts edges using context. The approach injects control signals as priors over connectivity. Temporal modules separate recurrent dynamics from event triggered changes. To counter missing data, we leverage physics informed constraints on flows. We also introduce invariance penalties that stabilize predictions across day types. A curriculum schedules disturbances to avoid collapse during early training. We evaluate transfer across cities using aligned subnetworks and covariate matching. Our ablations examine the tradeoffs between adaptivity and stability. Results show improved robustness without degrading peak hour accuracy. The analysis highlights implications for resilient urban operations.
spans: Recent graph-based predictors include DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, GMAN, and AGCRN, which variously employ diffusion convolution, temporal convolutions, spatiotemporal attention, and adaptive adjacency learning (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Zheng et al., 2020; Bai et al., 2020). Benchmarks commonly use METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03, PEMS04, PEMS07, and PEMS08 with MAE, RMSE, and MAPE metrics (Li et al., 2018; Song et al., 2020). Data augmentation strategies include noise injection, time warping, and mixup for sequences (Um et al., 2017; DeVries and Taylor, 2017; Zhang et al., 2018). External features include weather, events, and holidays.

document: Federated learning enables on device training without centralizing raw user data. Mobile text entry is a canonical application with strong privacy constraints. Personalization is vital because typing styles, dictionaries, and contexts vary widely. However, heterogeneity across users and devices complicates optimization and evaluation. Communication limits and partial participation further stress training dynamics. This section reviews related methods and situates our contributions. Early federated algorithms focused on weighted averaging under non IID data. Successive work introduced proximal regularization to control client drift. Others explored clustered aggregation to partition users into latent communities. Personalization layers and multi task formulations became popular for keyboards. Yet a principled decomposition between shared knowledge and private traits remains elusive. Moreover, existing metrics seldom reflect downstream user experience beyond accuracy. Approaches include FedAvg, FedProx, Scaffold, Per-FedAvg, and pFedMe, alongside meta-learning and multi-task formulations for personalization (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020; Fallah et al., 2020; Dinh et al., 2020). Public datasets for language modeling and typing research often reference WikiText-103, Penn Treebank, Reddit comments, and the StackOverflow federated corpus (Merity et al., 2016; Marcus et al., 1993; Baumgartner et al., 2020; TensorFlow Federated, 2019). Privacy accounting typically relies on Rényi differential privacy and subsampled Gaussian mechanisms (Mironov, 2017; Abadi et al., 2016). Evaluation emphasizes perplexity, top-k accuracy, communication rounds, and client participation rates. While these ingredients provide a toolbox, real keyboards display skewed vocabularies and rapid drift. Domain shifts arise from new slang, trending names, and multilingual mixing. Heavy tail phenomena complicate averaging because rare words dominate perceived quality. Strict privacy budgets restrict gradient signal and slow convergence. Communication budgets further limit the number of effective updates per user. These realities demand mechanisms that adapt quickly yet remain stable under constraints. We introduce a decomposition that isolates transferable patterns from idiosyncratic habits. The server maintains a compact shared representation learned across tasks. Clients attach lightweight adapters that steer predictions toward personal distributions. Confidence calibrated routing decides when to trust private or shared paths. We integrate privacy accounting into the routing to bound information leakage. Our curriculum targets frequent tokens first and gradually expands coverage. We measure user centric benefits through completion rate and correction rate. We further track latency, energy, and stall events during interactive typing. Experiments simulate participation skew and overnight vocabulary shifts. We compare against strong personalized baselines under matched privacy budgets. The approach reduces corrections while respecting strict privacy and communication limits. We discuss deployment considerations for production keyboard systems.
spans: Approaches include FedAvg, FedProx, Scaffold, Per-FedAvg, and pFedMe, alongside meta-learning and multi-task formulations for personalization (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020; Fallah et al., 2020; Dinh et al., 2020). Public datasets for language modeling and typing research often reference WikiText-103, Penn Treebank, Reddit comments, and the StackOverflow federated corpus (Merity et al., 2016; Marcus et al., 1993; Baumgartner et al., 2020; TensorFlow Federated, 2019). Privacy accounting typically relies on Rényi differential privacy and subsampled Gaussian mechanisms (Mironov, 2017; Abadi et al., 2016). Evaluation emphasizes perplexity, top-k accuracy, communication rounds, and client participation rates.

document: Automatic speech recognition has advanced rapidly with self supervised pretraining and large transformers. Yet many languages lack labeled audio and present severe domain shift. Phonetic diversity and code switching further complicate training. This section reviews prior work and motivates our method for low resource settings. Early systems relied on HMM GMM pipelines with pronunciation lexicons. End to end models later replaced components with connectionist temporal classification and attention. Self supervised encoders now learn from unlabeled audio at scale. Despite gains, adaptation to new domains still incurs high sample complexity. Long tail speakers and accents remain poorly covered in common corpora. Evaluation practice often ignores robustness to background noise and channel variation. We focus on sample efficient adaptation with explicit control over domain mismatch. Our approach builds on hierarchical adapters for speech encoders. We also align learned units with articulatory priors to improve transfer. To contextualize our design, we summarize notable resources and methods. Prominent self-supervised encoders include wav2vec 2.0, HuBERT, WavLM, and BEST-RQ, trained on large corpora like Libri-Light, VoxPopuli, and GigaSpeech with contrastive or masked prediction objectives (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2022; Kahn et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021). Low-resource benchmarks use Common Voice, BABEL, MLS low-resource splits, and TED-LIUM protocols (Ardila et al., 2020; Gales et al., 2014; Pratap et al., 2020; Rousseau et al., 2012). Adaptation techniques span layer-wise finetuning, adapters, multi-task learning, and shallow fusion with language models (Houlsby et al., 2019; Sennrich et al., 2016; Gulati et al., 2020). Despite broad coverage, these resources do not reflect conversational speech in many communities. Domain shift appears in background noise, telephony channels, and code switching. Annotation scarcity further limits effective lexicon and language model adaptation. Unlabeled audio from the target domain is often available at scale. We therefore seek methods that exploit such audio while preserving privacy. Our model introduces adapters that gate pretrained features by domain attributes. The gating reduces interference between mismatched phonotactics and prosody. We incorporate lightly supervised articulatory priors as auxiliary targets. A curriculum schedules adapter capacity based on validation stability. We add a contrastive objective that separates nuisance factors from content. To quantify robustness, we vary channel conditions and noise mixtures. We also examine transfer under rapid speaker turnover and code switching. Evaluation uses word error rate alongside accent and domain stratification. We compare to strong baselines with matched computational budgets. Results reveal faster adaptation and better stability under scarce labels. Ablations confirm the role of domain gates and auxiliary targets. We conclude with implications for inclusive speech technologies.
spans: Prominent self-supervised encoders include wav2vec 2.0, HuBERT, WavLM, and BEST-RQ, trained on large corpora like Libri-Light, VoxPopuli, and GigaSpeech with contrastive or masked prediction objectives (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2022; Kahn et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021). Low-resource benchmarks use Common Voice, BABEL, MLS low-resource splits, and TED-LIUM protocols (Ardila et al., 2020; Gales et al., 2014; Pratap et al., 2020; Rousseau et al., 2012). Adaptation techniques span layer-wise finetuning, adapters, multi-task learning, and shallow fusion with language models (Houlsby et al., 2019; Sennrich et al., 2016; Gulati et al., 2020).

document: Distant supervision aligns knowledge base facts with text to create training data for relation extraction. The approach scales labeling but introduces substantial noise from sentence mismatches. Multi instance learning and noise modeling aim to mitigate these issues. Recent pretraining has further improved sentence encoders for long contexts. Despite progress, robustness and factual consistency remain problematic in open domains. This section discusses key strands and clarifies the space our method targets. Early models pooled sentences per entity pair with attention to highlight informative mentions. Subsequent work introduced bag level classifiers with relaxed selection assumptions. Recent approaches model sentence truth via latent variables or reinforcement learning. Evidence aggregation across documents has also become an active topic. Nevertheless, noise persists when bags contain only non expressing sentences. Representative models include PCNN with selective attention, MultiR, Mintz labeling, and the CONVATT family, and reinforcement learning instance selectors (Zeng et al., 2015; Hoffmann et al., 2011; Mintz et al., 2009; Lin et al., 2016; Feng et al., 2018). Datasets derive from NYT-Freebase alignments, TACRED, SemEval Task 8, and DocRED for document relations (Riedel et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2017; Hendrickx et al., 2010; Yao et al., 2019). Pretrained encoders often include BERT, RoBERTa, SpanBERT, and LUKE, paired with distant supervision losses (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2020; Yamada et al., 2020). Despite broad adoption, these datasets exhibit reporting bias and limited entity diversity. Heuristics like at least one assumption break down for sparse relations. Models often overfit to surface patterns that correlate weakly with facts. Bag construction also obscures mention level uncertainty within documents. Document level aggregation can amplify label noise when evidence is ambiguous. Prior calibration methods rarely estimate instance probabilities in a principled manner. We pursue a generative labeling model that disentangles bag and mention truth. The model introduces a prior over mention expressiveness grounded in discourse cues. It then marginalizes mention assignments to obtain bag likelihoods for training. Structured variational inference yields tractable updates compatible with pretrained encoders. We incorporate counterfactual data augmentation to test sensitivity to spurious triggers. A new evaluation protocol isolates partial evidence scenarios with negative control mentions. We report precision, recall, and calibration metrics at the mention and bag levels. Comparative baselines include robust MIL, RL selectors, and confidence regularization. Our approach improves extraction accuracy while maintaining calibrated probabilities. It also reduces contradictions with external knowledge base constraints. Case studies illustrate finer granularity error analysis for ambiguous sentences. Ablations reveal contributions of the prior, variational family, and augmentations. We provide code and data preprocessing scripts for reproducibility. Broader impacts concern responsible deployment on noisy open web corpora.
spans: Representative models include PCNN with selective attention, MultiR, Mintz labeling, and the CONVATT family, and reinforcement learning instance selectors (Zeng et al., 2015; Hoffmann et al., 2011; Mintz et al., 2009; Lin et al., 2016; Feng et al., 2018). Datasets derive from NYT-Freebase alignments, TACRED, SemEval Task 8, and DocRED for document relations (Riedel et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2017; Hendrickx et al., 2010; Yao et al., 2019). Pretrained encoders often include BERT, RoBERTa, SpanBERT, and LUKE, paired with distant supervision losses (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2020; Yamada et al., 2020).

document: Molecular property prediction is central to virtual screening, toxicity analysis, and materials discovery. Recent years have seen the maturation of deep graph learning as a dominant paradigm for learning from chemical structures. Unlike fixed fingerprints, learned representations can adapt to task-specific inductive biases and multi-task objectives. Nonetheless, dataset size, label noise, and scaffold distribution shift still challenge robust generalization. Moreover, practical workflows require calibrated uncertainty and principled molecular splits to avoid overestimation. To contextualize our approach, we summarize representative lines of work and commonly used benchmarks. Early graph neural models emphasize message passing on fixed graphs, including GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), and GAT (Veličković et al., 2018), while molecular benchmarks such as QM9 (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014) and MoleculeNet (Wu et al., 2018) standardize evaluation. Subsequent architectures integrate edge updates and directed messages, D-MPNN (Yang et al., 2019) and AttentiveFP (Xiong et al., 2020). Pretraining on chemical corpora appears in GROVER (Rong et al., 2020) and ChemBERTa. Property prediction reports commonly cover ESOL, FreeSolv, and Tox21 with scaffold splits and ROC-AUC metrics reported. Despite the diversity of architectures, comparisons are often confounded by inconsistent preprocessing and hyperparameter budgets. Furthermore, improvements on small molecular datasets can stem from ensembling and data leakage rather than genuine inductive advances. In safety-critical applications, the ability to abstain under uncertainty can be as important as raw accuracy. We therefore adopt rigorous scaffold-balanced splits and assess calibration alongside predictive metrics. We also analyze representation transfer from pretraining regimes under controlled data ablations. Our study aims to clarify performance drivers and provide reproducible guidance for robust molecular modeling.
spans: Early graph neural models emphasize message passing on fixed graphs, including GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), and GAT (Veličković et al., 2018), while molecular benchmarks such as QM9 (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014) and MoleculeNet (Wu et al., 2018) standardize evaluation. Subsequent architectures integrate edge updates and directed messages, D-MPNN (Yang et al., 2019) and AttentiveFP (Xiong et al., 2020). Pretraining on chemical corpora appears in GROVER (Rong et al., 2020) and ChemBERTa. Property prediction reports commonly cover ESOL, FreeSolv, and Tox21 with scaffold splits and ROC-AUC metrics reported.

document: Neural code generation has progressed rapidly with the advent of large language models trained on vast software corpora. The task entails producing compilable, functional programs that meet a specified natural language intent. Practical systems must negotiate syntax, semantics, external libraries, and problem constraints under limited context windows. At the same time, evaluation practices and data curation strongly influence the perceived capabilities of these models. Prior work has also examined the impact of execution feedback and unit tests on search and decoding. We first recap representative models, datasets, and commonly used techniques. Transformer-based code models span general-purpose LLMs and specialized encoders, including GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020), Codex (Chen et al., 2021), CodeT5 (Wang et al., 2021), and AlphaCode (Li et al., 2022), trained on web-scale repositories. Benchmarking commonly uses HumanEval (Chen et al., 2021), MBPP (Austin et al., 2021), APPS (Hendrycks et al., 2021), and CodeXGLUE (Lu et al., 2021), with pass@k or exact-match metrics. Techniques such as prompt engineering, few-shot learning, and self-consistency (Wang et al., 2022) appear alongside unit-test filtering and execution-based reranking in practical pipelines for competitive coding tasks and benchmarks. These ingredients form the backdrop for our examination of context management and tool integration. We focus on retrieval-augmented generation to inject relevant API usage and project-specific conventions. Our analysis highlights brittleness under small prompt perturbations and unstable estimates of pass@k across seeds. To mitigate overfitting to unit tests, we decouple solution synthesis from verification and compare constrained decoders. We also quantify the tradeoff between temperature-based sampling and execution-guided pruning. Finally, we release standardized prompts, seeds, and scripts to enable apples-to-apples evaluation. This framework clarifies where performance originates and where it remains fragile.
spans: Transformer-based code models span general-purpose LLMs and specialized encoders, including GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020), Codex (Chen et al., 2021), CodeT5 (Wang et al., 2021), and AlphaCode (Li et al., 2022), trained on web-scale repositories. Benchmarking commonly uses HumanEval (Chen et al., 2021), MBPP (Austin et al., 2021), APPS (Hendrycks et al., 2021), and CodeXGLUE (Lu et al., 2021), with pass@k or exact-match metrics. Techniques such as prompt engineering, few-shot learning, and self-consistency (Wang et al., 2022) appear alongside unit-test filtering and execution-based reranking in practical pipelines for competitive coding tasks and benchmarks.

document: Recommender systems increasingly mediate access to goods, information, and cultural opportunities. As such, concerns about fairness, accountability, and transparency have moved to the foreground. In ranking scenarios, exposure and calibration interact with utility in complex, feedback-driven ways. Regulation and platform policy create additional constraints that shape algorithm design and evaluation. We scope our discussion to fairness in top-k recommendation with implicit feedback. Before detailing our modeling strategy, we review common metrics, datasets, and algorithmic baselines. Work on fairness-aware recommendation surveys metrics like demographic parity (Calders and Verwer, 2010), equal opportunity (Hardt et al., 2016), and exposure disparity (Singh and Joachims, 2018), with re-ranking methods such as xQuAD (Santos et al., 2010) and FA*IR (Zehlike et al., 2017). Datasets frequently include MovieLens-1M (Harper and Konstan, 2016), Last.fm (Cantador et al., 2011), and RecSys Challenge collections, and evaluation emphasizes NDCG, MAP, and calibrated exposure. Policy-gradient schemes based on REINFORCE (Williams, 1992) and constrained optimization via dual ascent appear as implementation choices across studies in offline and online settings and competitions. While these ingredients are widely used, there remains ambiguity about appropriate risk budgets for group-level constraints. Our interest lies in the stability of exposure constraints under distribution shift induced by user adaptation. We characterize this shift using counterfactual propensity estimators derived from randomized interventions. We then instantiate constrained learners with identical fairness targets but varied surrogate losses. The resulting comparisons illuminate sensitivity to propensity misspecification and surrogate curvature. We complement offline evaluation with a pre-registered A/B test measuring long-term exposure balance. Together, the study clarifies practical levers for robustly satisfying fairness targets in recommender systems.
spans: Work on fairness-aware recommendation surveys metrics like demographic parity (Calders and Verwer, 2010), equal opportunity (Hardt et al., 2016), and exposure disparity (Singh and Joachims, 2018), with re-ranking methods such as xQuAD (Santos et al., 2010) and FA*IR (Zehlike et al., 2017). Datasets frequently include MovieLens-1M (Harper and Konstan, 2016), Last.fm (Cantador et al., 2011), and RecSys Challenge collections, and evaluation emphasizes NDCG, MAP, and calibrated exposure. Policy-gradient schemes based on REINFORCE (Williams, 1992) and constrained optimization via dual ascent appear as implementation choices across studies in offline and online settings and competitions.

document: Multimodal sentiment analysis seeks to infer opinions or affect from synchronized language, vision, and audio streams. The field has benefited from standardized datasets and the availability of reliable feature extractors. Nonetheless, cross-domain robustness and speaker generalization remain persistent challenges. Additionally, label noise and annotation subjectivity complicate model comparison across benchmarks. The interaction among modalities often exhibits redundancy and conflict that naïve fusion cannot resolve. We outline prominent architectures, datasets, and features that shape current practice. Multimodal sentiment and emotion research spans early concatenation baselines and structured fusion, including TFN (Zadeh et al., 2017), MFN (Zadeh et al., 2018), and MAG-BERT (Rahman et al., 2020), alongside transformers like LXMERT (Tan and Bansal, 2019) and ViLBERT (Lu et al., 2019). Evaluation uses CMU-MOSI (Zadeh et al., 2016), CMU-MOSEI (Zadeh et al., 2018), and MELD (Poria et al., 2019), reporting accuracy, F1, and mean absolute error. Additional modalities and cues appear through acoustic features, facial action units, with toolkits like OpenFace (Baltrušaitis et al., 2018) and librosa (McFee et al., 2015). Despite these resources, disagreement in label granularity and segment alignment hinders cumulative progress. Moreover, many reported gains are fragile to speaker split protocols and leakage through pretraining. We therefore prioritize controlled ablations that quantify complementarity rather than aggregate accuracy alone. Our modeling emphasizes causal probes that isolate pathways of cross-modal influence. We design interventions that suppress modality-specific evidence to test reliance and redundancy. Finally, we standardize training seeds and provide scripts for faithful replication across toolchains. The resulting analysis clarifies when and how multimodal fusion delivers reliable improvements.
spans: Multimodal sentiment and emotion research spans early concatenation baselines and structured fusion, including TFN (Zadeh et al., 2017), MFN (Zadeh et al., 2018), and MAG-BERT (Rahman et al., 2020), alongside transformers like LXMERT (Tan and Bansal, 2019) and ViLBERT (Lu et al., 2019). Evaluation uses CMU-MOSI (Zadeh et al., 2016), CMU-MOSEI (Zadeh et al., 2018), and MELD (Poria et al., 2019), reporting accuracy, F1, and mean absolute error. Additional modalities and cues appear through acoustic features, facial action units, with toolkits like OpenFace (Baltrušaitis et al., 2018) and librosa (McFee et al., 2015).

document: Named entity recognition in low-resource settings suffers from limited annotations and domain shift. Data augmentation offers a practical path to increase sample diversity without costly labeling. However, naive perturbations can distort entity boundaries and degrade calibration. The multilingual regime adds tokenization and morphological variability that complicate reuse of monolingual recipes. We focus on augmentation schemes that preserve span structure while encouraging lexical variety. To situate our study, we briefly review widely used techniques and benchmarks for NER augmentation. Augmentation for sequence labeling includes synonym substitution and word deletion heuristics like EDA (Wei and Zou, 2019), contextual token replacement (Kobayashi, 2018), and contextualized masking with CBERT (Wu et al., 2019). Paraphrastic generation appears through back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016) and round-trip translation pipelines, while mixup-style interpolation (Zhang et al., 2018) has been adapted to token spans. Evaluations frequently report CoNLL-2003 (Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003), WikiAnn (Pan et al., 2017), and OntoNotes 5.0 (Pradhan et al., 2013), with span-level F1 and entity-wise calibration metrics under low-resource transfer and cross-lingual settings. While these ingredients are popular, few studies quantify the interaction between augmentation and constrained decoding. We compare span-aware perturbations with entity-consistent sampling under identical budgets and seeds. Our experiments examine robustness to annotation noise by simulating boundary flips and label swaps. We further analyze calibration and boundary uncertainty using abstention curves and ECE metrics. The results highlight the importance of preserving morphological agreement in multilingual contexts. We release recipes that integrate with existing training loops and pretokenizers for reproducible adoption. This evaluation delineates effective augmentation choices for realistic low-resource NER.
spans: Augmentation for sequence labeling includes synonym substitution and word deletion heuristics like EDA (Wei and Zou, 2019), contextual token replacement (Kobayashi, 2018), and contextualized masking with CBERT (Wu et al., 2019). Paraphrastic generation appears through back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016) and round-trip translation pipelines, while mixup-style interpolation (Zhang et al., 2018) has been adapted to token spans. Evaluations frequently report CoNLL-2003 (Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003), WikiAnn (Pan et al., 2017), and OntoNotes 5.0 (Pradhan et al., 2013), with span-level F1 and entity-wise calibration metrics under low-resource transfer and cross-lingual settings.

document: Recent advances in foundation models have prompted renewed interest in grounded knowledge access across modalities. Applications include open-domain question answering, caption-conditioned retrieval, and interactive agents in embodied environments. Despite progress, practical systems still face issues of data coverage, latency, and robustness under distribution shift. The literature encompasses pretraining paradigms, retrieval augmentation, and multimodal alignment objectives. Foundational studies on pretrained language models and transfer learning survey a wide range of techniques across tasks and domains, including contextual encoders and retrieval components. Early transformer architectures are represented by BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019), while subsequent works emphasize scaling and prompting in GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) and instruction tuning (Sanh et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022). For sentence representation and retrieval, DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), and ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) are widely used. Benchmarks frequently referenced include GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), and MS MARCO (Nguyen et al., 2016). Other datasets are also commonly used. In language-centric settings, scalable pretraining combines masked prediction with contrastive or generative losses to improve transfer. Retrieval components are increasingly coupled to parametric models to provide verifiable evidence at inference time. Index construction, negative sampling, and late interaction architectures play important roles in system trade-offs. Multimodal alignment extends these ideas by associating text with images, videos, and structured signals. Compatibility functions must balance locality, efficiency, and cross-domain generality for downstream tasks. Resource constraints motivate parameter-efficient adaptation and decoupled serving pipelines. Recent evaluations emphasize faithfulness, calibration, and robustness under adversarial prompting. Open issues remain around privacy, copyright, and the governance of large-scale training data. Research on object detection and vision backbones spans region-based detectors, one-stage models, and transformer-based designs. Canonical detectors include Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015), RetinaNet (Lin et al., 2017), and YOLOv3/v5 (Redmon and Farhadi, 2018; Jocher et al., 2020), while DETR (Carion et al., 2020) introduces set prediction with bipartite matching. Backbones frequently considered are ResNet (He et al., 2016), EfficientNet (Tan and Le, 2019), and Swin Transformer (Liu et al., 2021). Common evaluation corpora include COCO (Lin et al., 2014), PASCAL VOC (Everingham et al., 2010), and OpenImages (Kuznetsova et al., 2020). Training heuristics reference focal loss, assignment, multi-scale augmentation, and moving averages. Multimodal learning builds on these detection and backbone advances to support dense grounding for captioning and VQA. Region proposals, tokenization of visual patches, and pooled features provide interfaces to language decoders. Indexable image and video stores enable retrieval-augmented generation for complex, time-sensitive queries. Pretraining corpora with aligned alt text and metadata present both opportunities and biases for deployment. Data governance frameworks are needed to document provenance and intended use at scale. Benchmarks that mix perception, reasoning, and interaction can reduce metric gaming and overfitting. Efficient inference requires careful batching, caching, and dynamic routing across retrieval and generation modules. These considerations motivate a comprehensive study of grounded, efficient, and reliable multimodal systems.

document: Scientific question answering requires precise retrieval and trustworthy reasoning over heterogeneous sources. Systems must integrate domain ontologies, citation graphs, and evolving corpora with transparent explanations. Prior work compares generative, extractive, and hybrid approaches under varying supervision regimes. This section reviews strands of research relevant to retrieval, modeling, and evaluation for scientific tasks. Foundational studies on pretrained language models and transfer learning survey a wide range of techniques across tasks and domains, including contextual encoders and retrieval components. Early transformer architectures are represented by BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019), while subsequent works emphasize scaling and prompting in GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) and instruction tuning (Sanh et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022). For sentence representation and retrieval, DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), and ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) are widely used. Benchmarks frequently referenced include GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), and MS MARCO (Nguyen et al., 2016). Other datasets are also commonly used. Retrieval-augmented generation pipelines typically separate retriever and reader to control latency and reuse caches. Calibration studies show that confidence estimates improve when retrieved passages support the final output. Query reformulation can expand evidence sets without increasing the risk of topic drift. Iterative retrieval enables multi-hop reasoning with limited compute budgets. Entity linking and citation-aware heuristics provide anchors for disambiguation and deduplication. Document reranking helps filter boilerplate and prioritize methods and results sections. Answer finalization often includes evidence attribution and formatting for assistive user interfaces. These components interact in ways that affect scientific utility and user trust. Research on object detection and vision backbones spans region-based detectors, one-stage models, and transformer-based designs. Canonical detectors include Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015), RetinaNet (Lin et al., 2017), and YOLOv3/v5 (Redmon and Farhadi, 2018; Jocher et al., 2020), while DETR (Carion et al., 2020) introduces set prediction with bipartite matching. Backbones frequently considered are ResNet (He et al., 2016), EfficientNet (Tan and Le, 2019), and Swin Transformer (Liu et al., 2021). Common evaluation corpora include COCO (Lin et al., 2014), PASCAL VOC (Everingham et al., 2010), and OpenImages (Kuznetsova et al., 2020). Training heuristics reference focal loss, assignment, multi-scale augmentation, and moving averages. For scientific domains, visual tables and plots require specialized parsing to extract quantitative evidence. Multimodal encoders can fuse captions with figure regions to improve grounding. Structured prompts guide models to report units, uncertainty, and data provenance. Robustness testing should cover temporal drift as new preprints and revisions accumulate. Human factors research highlights the importance of controllability and consent in expert workflows. Dataset curation practices determine generalization across subfields and venues. Shared tasks can accelerate reproducibility by standardizing protocols for data release and annotation. The reviewed directions inform the design of scientific assistants that are verifiable and adaptive.

document: Evaluating multimodal systems requires coordinated metrics for perception, grounding, and language quality. Simple accuracy is insufficient when tasks demand spatial reasoning or temporal consistency. Recent work emphasizes auditability and interpretability alongside raw performance numbers. We outline bodies of work that inform the construction of comprehensive evaluation suites. Foundational studies on pretrained language models and transfer learning survey a wide range of techniques across tasks and domains, including contextual encoders and retrieval components. Early transformer architectures are represented by BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019), while subsequent works emphasize scaling and prompting in GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) and instruction tuning (Sanh et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022). For sentence representation and retrieval, DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), and ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) are widely used. Benchmarks frequently referenced include GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), and MS MARCO (Nguyen et al., 2016). Other datasets are also commonly used. Text metrics such as exact match and BLEU are complemented by entity-level and relation-level scores. For images, detection and segmentation quality influence downstream captioning and referring expressions. Human studies can assess preference and faithfulness but introduce cost and variance. Diagnostic datasets target contrastive phenomena to reduce shortcut learning. Perturbation tests probe stability under paraphrasing and minor visual changes. Cost-aware evaluation tracks compute, memory, and energy to reflect deployment constraints. Multi-language settings expose hidden biases in tokenization and normalization. Open-source leaderboards encourage competition but risk overfitting to public test sets. Research on object detection and vision backbones spans region-based detectors, one-stage models, and transformer-based designs. Canonical detectors include Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015), RetinaNet (Lin et al., 2017), and YOLOv3/v5 (Redmon and Farhadi, 2018; Jocher et al., 2020), while DETR (Carion et al., 2020) introduces set prediction with bipartite matching. Backbones frequently considered are ResNet (He et al., 2016), EfficientNet (Tan and Le, 2019), and Swin Transformer (Liu et al., 2021). Common evaluation corpora include COCO (Lin et al., 2014), PASCAL VOC (Everingham et al., 2010), and OpenImages (Kuznetsova et al., 2020). Training heuristics reference focal loss, assignment, multi-scale augmentation, and moving averages. Cross-modal retrieval tasks serve as proxies for alignment but may not reflect reasoning depth. Task graphs can compose atomic skills into end-to-end benchmarks. Logging standardized traces supports reproducibility and failure analysis. Secure sandboxes are valuable for privacy-sensitive assessments with proprietary assets. Weighted aggregations reduce dominance of any single category in composite scores. Calibration and abstention metrics inform safe fallback mechanisms. Agreement between automatic metrics and human judgments remains a moving target. The synthesis of these aspects guides principled evaluation of grounded models.

document: Deploying multimodal assistants in production demands low latency and predictable resource usage. Models must route queries to appropriate retrieval and generation stages under tight budgets. Observability and control loops are essential for safety and quality at scale. We summarize technical foundations that underpin efficient serving for vision and language. Foundational studies on pretrained language models and transfer learning survey a wide range of techniques across tasks and domains, including contextual encoders and retrieval components. Early transformer architectures are represented by BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019), while subsequent works emphasize scaling and prompting in GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) and instruction tuning (Sanh et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022). For sentence representation and retrieval, DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), and ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) are widely used. Benchmarks frequently referenced include GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), and MS MARCO (Nguyen et al., 2016). Other datasets are also commonly used. Sharded vector stores reduce memory pressure without sacrificing recall for popular domains. Gating policies can choose small or large models based on estimated difficulty. Distillation and caching limit cold-start penalties for heavy decoders. Streaming tokenization and incremental decoding improve responsiveness on long inputs. Mixed precision and operator fusion enhance throughput on commodity accelerators. Adaptive batching trades off latency and utilization in multi-tenant clusters. Canarying and rollback procedures protect user experience during rapid iterations. Rate limiting and quotas enforce fairness across tenants and workloads. Research on object detection and vision backbones spans region-based detectors, one-stage models, and transformer-based designs. Canonical detectors include Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015), RetinaNet (Lin et al., 2017), and YOLOv3/v5 (Redmon and Farhadi, 2018; Jocher et al., 2020), while DETR (Carion et al., 2020) introduces set prediction with bipartite matching. Backbones frequently considered are ResNet (He et al., 2016), EfficientNet (Tan and Le, 2019), and Swin Transformer (Liu et al., 2021). Common evaluation corpora include COCO (Lin et al., 2014), PASCAL VOC (Everingham et al., 2010), and OpenImages (Kuznetsova et al., 2020). Training heuristics reference focal loss, assignment, multi-scale augmentation, and moving averages. Visual encoders precompute image features to avoid redundant GPU cycles at inference time. Lightweight detectors gate expensive grounding only when necessary. Serverless abstractions can elastically provision retrieval, ranking, and generation microservices. Parameter-efficient adapters enable per-tenant customization without full model forks. Cost telemetry informs prioritization of optimization efforts and capacity planning. Synthetic load tests approximate real-world mixes of multimodal queries. Privacy policies should cover retention and redaction of images and derived embeddings. Together, these practices support reliable and economical multimodal applications.

document: Data governance shapes the capabilities and risks of large-scale multimodal systems. Licenses, consent, and documentation define permissible use and redistribution of datasets. Research communities explore standards for auditing, redaction, and rights requests. This section situates governance within technical foundations of model training and evaluation. Foundational studies on pretrained language models and transfer learning survey a wide range of techniques across tasks and domains, including contextual encoders and retrieval components. Early transformer architectures are represented by BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019), while subsequent works emphasize scaling and prompting in GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) and instruction tuning (Sanh et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022). For sentence representation and retrieval, DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), and ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) are widely used. Benchmarks frequently referenced include GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), and MS MARCO (Nguyen et al., 2016). Other datasets are also commonly used. Dataset statements provide context on collection, labeling, and intended use. Model cards communicate training data, limitations, and ethical considerations. Retrieval-augmented workflows complicate provenance by mixing private and public sources. Deduplication and filtering impact memorization and leakage in scaled training runs. Differential privacy and secure enclaves offer programmatic controls over sensitive content. Watermarking and content provenance standards can help trace generative outputs. Auditing pipelines benefit from standardized hashes and immutable logs. Community governance models range from open consortia to stewarded access agreements. Research on object detection and vision backbones spans region-based detectors, one-stage models, and transformer-based designs. Canonical detectors include Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015), RetinaNet (Lin et al., 2017), and YOLOv3/v5 (Redmon and Farhadi, 2018; Jocher et al., 2020), while DETR (Carion et al., 2020) introduces set prediction with bipartite matching. Backbones frequently considered are ResNet (He et al., 2016), EfficientNet (Tan and Le, 2019), and Swin Transformer (Liu et al., 2021). Common evaluation corpora include COCO (Lin et al., 2014), PASCAL VOC (Everingham et al., 2010), and OpenImages (Kuznetsova et al., 2020). Training heuristics reference focal loss, assignment, multi-scale augmentation, and moving averages. Vision datasets often include annotations for people, vehicles, and scenes that require careful policy handling. Geographic and temporal skews in imagery can entrench structural biases. Participatory data practices invite stakeholders to shape collection and labeling. Legal frameworks continue to evolve in response to emerging generative capabilities. Risk assessments should consider aggregation harms beyond single data points. Tooling that surfaces data lineage can aid incident response and redress. Transparent benchmarks can align incentives toward safety and accountability. These considerations motivate principled standards for responsible model development.

document: Introduction Multimodal abuse and hate speech on social platforms presents a persistent challenge for automated moderation. Images, text, and layout often interact to convey meaning that is not apparent from any single channel. Advances in deep representation learning have created opportunities to detect harmful content at scale. Yet, the multimodal setting raises issues of alignment, robustness, and cultural context. Governance requirements further demand transparency about model behavior under distribution shift. These pressures motivate a careful examination of what is known about multimodal detection pipelines. This section reviews prior findings and situates the scope of our study. Text encoders alone can misclassify benign terms that acquire derogatory meaning through imagery. Visual features alone can miss targeted slurs embedded in memes and screenshots. Multimodal models therefore require mechanisms to focus on salient cross-channel evidence. Training such models is complicated by noisy annotations and adversarial editing of posts. Researchers have explored robustness against text overlays, occlusions, and stylistic perturbations. Evaluation practices must consider subgroup performance and temporal drift across platform communities. Together these considerations shape the design space for responsible classifiers. Research on multimodal hate speech detection draws on image-text fusion architectures and annotated corpora created across platforms such as Twitter and Instagram. Early convolutional encoders combine visual region features with word embeddings for binary labels (Gomez et al., 2019; Kiela et al., 2020). Transformer-based models leverage cross-attention and contrastive pretraining to align memes and captions (Hossain et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021; Prasad et al., 2022). Public datasets include MMHS150K, Hateful Memes, and FB-Hateful-Meme, with accompanying baseline implementations (Kiela et al., 2020; Fersini et al., 2022; Mandl et al., 2021). Auxiliary signals such as OCR tokens, tags, attributes, and features have been incorporated through late fusion or adapter layers (Velioglu et al., 2020; Sabat et al., 2019; Das et al., 2021; Muennighoff, 2022). Despite these resources, key uncertainties persist around the role of pretraining objectives for rare multimodal slurs. Prior reports suggest that noisy OCR can either help disambiguate sarcasm or amplify false positives. Cross-lingual settings introduce additional drift when visual metaphors differ across languages. There is also limited clarity about data curation choices that balance fairness and efficacy. To address these issues, we examine architectures under controlled interventions on tokenization, region proposals, and matching strategies. Our analysis distinguishes lexical leakage from genuine cross-modal grounding. We further quantify calibration under content remix patterns that mirror realistic platform behavior. The resulting findings are intended to inform dataset updates and feature prioritization. We provide stratified evaluations that expose brittle correlations in common benchmarks. A focus on shift-resilient evidence aggregation yields practical implications for deployment policies. We also discuss annotation guidelines that reduce ambiguity for borderline cases. Finally, we outline open problems in multimodal moderation that require community coordination. These include shared testbeds for adversarial editing and standardized reporting on subgroup harms. The remainder of the paper details methodology, experiments, and limitations.
