document: Introduction Reliable energy forecasts underpin grid stability and market operations. The increasing penetration of renewables heightens variability across spatial and temporal scales. Forecasting systems must balance accuracy, interpretability, and computational budget. Domain knowledge about weather and demand patterns remains central to modeling choices. Probabilistic outputs are necessary for risk aware planning and reserve sizing. However, methodological proliferation complicates fair comparison among approaches. We structure the discussion around modeling paradigms, data regimes, and evaluation criteria. Short term horizons demand high resolution exogenous inputs and careful feature engineering. Medium term horizons benefit from hierarchical reconciliation across regions and customer classes. Long term horizons encounter structural changes that violate stationarity assumptions. Forecast errors can cascade into operational constraints with nonlinear costs. Institutions also require transparent audits of models under extreme weather scenarios. Benchmarks must reflect real world constraints like data latency and sensor outages. These themes motivate a closer look at representative modeling strategies. Forecasting baseload and renewable generation has drawn on autoregressive models, recurrent neural networks, and probabilistic decomposition. Classical ARIMA and SARIMA provide baselines for hourly series (Box and Jenkins, 1976; Hyndman and Athanasopoulos, 2018). Sequence models capture nonlinear dynamics through LSTM and GRU encoders with exogenous covariates such as temperature and calendar effects (Smyl, 2020; Salinas et al., 2020). Global forecasting strategies train across many meters using DeepAR, N-BEATS, Temporal Fusion Transformer, and Informer variants with quantile losses (Oreshkin et al., 2019; Lim et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2021). Public benchmarks include M4, M5, GEFCom2014, and ETT, which support deterministic and probabilistic evaluation protocols (Makridakis et al., 2020; Makridakis et al., 2022; Hong et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2021). Despite diverse techniques, the community lacks clarity about the determinants of calibrated uncertainty. Choices about target normalization and seasonal decomposition can drive unexpected biases. Exogenous features introduce leakage risks when derived from future weather assimilation. We systematically vary covariate availability to quantify sensitivity across models. Our analysis separates the benefits of global training from those of architecture capacity. We also examine robustness to holidays, regime shifts, and data gaps. Results inform guidelines for probabilistic scoring and production deployment. We release reproducible pipelines for common datasets with standardized backtesting. The study highlights interactions between loss functions and temporal aggregation. It further examines the role of cross learning between load and generation series. We include ablations that probe feature importance under distribution shift. Remaining challenges include rare event modeling and equity in model performance. Future work can explore multitask formulations that unite dispatch and forecasting. The following sections detail methodology, results, and implications.

Classical ARIMA and SARIMA provide baselines for hourly series (Box and Jenkins, 1976; Hyndman and Athanasopoulos, 2018). Sequence models capture nonlinear dynamics through LSTM and GRU encoders with exogenous covariates such as temperature and calendar effects (Smyl, 2020; Salinas et al., 2020). Global forecasting strategies train across many meters using DeepAR, N-BEATS, Temporal Fusion Transformer, and Informer variants with quantile losses (Oreshkin et al., 2019; Lim et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2021). Public benchmarks include M4, M5, GEFCom2014, and ETT, which support deterministic and probabilistic evaluation protocols (Makridakis et al., 2020; Makridakis et al., 2022; Hong et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2021).

document: Introduction Extracting biomedical relations from the literature supports hypothesis generation and knowledge base curation. The volume of new articles outpaces the capacity of manual annotation. Automated systems must resolve long context, synonymy, and nested entities. Errors can propagate into downstream discovery and clinical decision support. Domain specific pretraining and structured resources offer complementary advantages. Evaluation protocols remain heterogeneous across tasks and datasets. We outline the methodological landscape relevant to relation extraction. Sentence level extraction can miss cross sentence evidence that appears in abstracts and full text. Document level models introduce additional resolution for co referenced entities across sections. Distant supervision scales labeling at the cost of noisy alignments to knowledge bases. Bootstrapping strategies attempt to control confirmation bias through pattern diversification. Span boundary errors complicate downstream normalization to standardized vocabularies. Annotation interfaces increasingly capture rationales in addition to labels. These trends frame the context for reviewing representative techniques. Biomedical relation extraction has utilized dependency parsing, supervision, and contextualized encoders alongside specialized corpora. Early pipelines relied on feature engineering with parse paths and kernels for protein–protein and drug–drug interactions (Bunescu and Mooney, 2005; Tikk et al., 2010). Neural methods adopt CNNs and BiLSTMs over sentence and document scopes (Zeng et al., 2014; Li et al., 2017; Verga et al., 2018). Pretrained language models fine-tuned on BioBERT, SciBERT, and PubMedBERT report gains across benchmarks such as DDI, BC5CDR, and DocRED (Beltagy et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020; Gu et al., 2021; Yao et al., 2019). Additional supervision arises from knowledge bases CTD, UMLS, and Hetionet and from silver labels derived via co-mention heuristics (Davis et al., 2019; Bodenreider, 2004; Himmelstein et al., 2017). Despite broad progress, ambiguity persists about how context windows influence precision at high recall. The interaction between entity disambiguation and relation cues remains under quantified. We analyze the role of distant supervision filtering and curriculum schedules. Experiments compare sentence, paragraph, and document scopes under matched training budgets. Our study measures calibration, evidence localization, and robustness to spurious lexical triggers. We additionally probe negative sampling strategies against overlapping entity pairs. Findings inform practical guidance for building reliable extraction pipelines. We introduce data splits that limit article leakage across train and test. The benchmark emphasizes clinically salient relations aligned to curated ontologies. We also include ablations on entity masking and marker schemes. Error analysis underscores coreference resolution and discourse cues as open challenges. The work concludes with recommendations for evaluation and reporting standards. Future extensions could integrate structured prompting and interactive labeling. The next sections describe datasets, models, and analyses.

Pretrained language models fine-tuned on BioBERT, SciBERT, and PubMedBERT report gains across benchmarks such as DDI, BC5CDR, and DocRED (Beltagy et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020; Gu et al., 2021; Yao et al., 2019). Additional supervision arises from knowledge bases CTD, UMLS, and Hetionet and from silver labels derived via co-mention heuristics (Davis et al., 2019; Bodenreider, 2004; Himmelstein et al., 2017).

document: Related Work Navigation among humans is a hallmark challenge for autonomous robots operating in public spaces. Agents must respect social norms while ensuring safety and task efficiency. Perception latency and actuation limits further complicate interaction under uncertainty. Modeling multiple agents introduces combinatorial complexity in prediction and planning. Data driven policies can exploit structure but risk overfitting to specific environments. Design choices must balance interpretability, performance, and deployment constraints. We overview prior methods and empirical settings that anchor this literature. Motion forecasting accuracy is tightly coupled to short horizon control feasibility. Crowd density distributions vary substantially across campuses, parks, and sidewalks. Scene geometry and bottlenecks introduce occlusions that degrade tracking quality. Evaluation must encompass collision rate, smoothness, and human comfort. Simulators offer repeatability but can poorly approximate real world multi agent dynamics. Transfer from synthetic to embodied platforms remains a persistent source of brittleness. These considerations inform how we interpret algorithmic benchmarks. Navigation in crowds has been studied with social force models, obstacle formulations, reinforcement learning, and imitation from demonstrations. Classical methods encode interaction through ORCA to generate collision-free trajectories under kinematic constraints (Helbing and Molnár, 1995; Van den Berg et al., 2011). Learning-based approaches include deep RL with LSTM policies and model predictive control hybrids trained in simulators (Chen et al., 2017; Everett et al., 2018; Fan et al., 2020). Benchmarks span ETH, UCY, and Stanford Drone with standardized metrics for collision rate (Pellegrini et al., 2009; Lerner et al., 2007; Robicquet et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2019). Sensing modalities integrate RGB-D, LiDAR, and tracking, while priors derive from SLAM datasets such as KITTI and TUM (Geiger et al., 2013; Sturm et al., 2012). Despite steady progress, questions remain about generalization across densities and camera placements. Policy smoothness can trade off with responsiveness in crowded scenarios. We analyze control horizons, perception rates, and social context features under matched budgets. Experiments compare training on synthetic scenes to fine tuning with real trajectories. Our metrics extend beyond collision counts to capture comfort proxies. We conduct ablations on prediction uncertainty and risk sensitive objectives. The study delineates failure modes tied to sensing and forecasting interactions. We summarize implementation details to facilitate reproducibility in future evaluations. The dataset splits avoid spatial and temporal leakage across environments. Results highlight limits of purely reactive policies in dense flows. Hybrid planners show promise when aligned with calibrated forecasts. Safety validation requires scenario coverage and conservative fallback strategies. We close with open problems in sim to real transfer and human factors. Subsequent sections describe models, data, and analyses.

Learning-based approaches include deep RL with LSTM policies and model predictive control hybrids trained in simulators (Chen et al., 2017; Everett et al., 2018; Fan et al., 2020). Benchmarks span ETH, UCY, and Stanford Drone with standardized metrics for collision rate (Pellegrini et al., 2009; Lerner et al., 2007; Robicquet et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2019). Sensing modalities integrate RGB-D, LiDAR, and tracking, while priors derive from SLAM datasets such as KITTI and TUM (Geiger et al., 2013; Sturm et al., 2012).

document: Introduction Program synthesis with neural models aims to translate intent into executable code. The task spans natural language to code, code to code, and multimodal conditioning. Practical systems must handle library usage, types, and runtime constraints. Safety concerns arise from insecure patterns and data exfiltration risks. Benchmarks increasingly emphasize robustness and generalization across languages. Execution feedback provides a unique learning signal unavailable in ordinary text generation. We survey representative approaches and datasets supporting this area. Tokenization choices influence copy behavior and identifier handling. Decoding strategies trade off diversity and exactness in function signatures. Retrieval can bring context that narrows search over valid solutions. Multilingual models must reconcile syntax and idioms across ecosystems. Data contamination complicates attribution of performance to model capacity. Evaluation protocols vary in test coverage and determinism. These considerations frame how we interpret empirical results. Neural code generation has advanced through large language models and execution-guided decoding across benchmarks. Encoders and decoders fine-tuned on CodeSearchNet and CodeXGLUE corpora support translation and summarization tasks (Husain et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021; Panthaplackel et al., 2020). Evaluation uses HumanEval, MBPP, and APPS with metrics and unit-test feedback (Chen et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021; Hendrycks et al., 2021; Cassano et al., 2022). Prominent models include Codex, CodeT5, and AlphaCode, often employing copy or deobfuscation objectives (Chen et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Fried et al., 2022; Li et al., 2022). Auxiliary techniques leverage retrieval of code snippets and documentation, as well as test-time self-consistency and filtering (Parvez et al., 2021; Shi et al., 2022; Le et al., 2023). Despite rapid progress, fundamental questions remain about semantic understanding versus pattern matching. Execution signals can create reward hacking if tests are weak or unrepresentative. We probe sensitivity to prompt phrasing, input-output formatting, and canonicalization. Experiments compare constrained decoding with repair loops that invoke static analyzers. Our study measures pass rates, runtime, and failure taxonomy under ablations. We also quantify contamination by cross checking overlaps with training corpora. Findings characterize where retrieval and execution feedback most reduce search. We release scripts for deterministic evaluation and reproducibility. The corpus is partitioned to prevent scaffold leakage between train and test. Results motivate more challenging tests emphasizing semantics over templates. We propose guidelines for prompt formatting that reduce ambiguity. Remaining challenges include tooling integration and safe code policies. Future research can unify synthesis with refactoring and repair in loops. The following sections describe setup, models, and analyses.

Encoders and decoders fine-tuned on CodeSearchNet and CodeXGLUE corpora support translation and summarization tasks (Husain et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021; Panthaplackel et al., 2020). Evaluation uses HumanEval, MBPP, and APPS with metrics and unit-test feedback (Chen et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021; Hendrycks et al., 2021; Cassano et al., 2022). Prominent models include Codex, CodeT5, and AlphaCode, often employing copy or deobfuscation objectives (Chen et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Fried et al., 2022; Li et al., 2022). Auxiliary techniques leverage retrieval of code snippets and documentation, as well as test-time self-consistency and filtering (Parvez et al., 2021; Shi et al., 2022; Le et al., 2023).

document: Automatic segmentation under domain shift remains a central obstacle for deploying clinical deep learning across sites and scanners. Variations in acquisition protocols and patient populations produce distributional discrepancies that degrade generalization, particularly for rare pathologies. Prior work highlights the need for methods that adapt without extensive target annotation while preserving anatomical fidelity. We focus on cross-modality and cross-center transfer for volumetric neuroimaging where alignment errors have high clinical cost. Unsupervised domain adaptation for medical image segmentation has been studied through adversarial alignment, style transfer, and self-training across diverse datasets, including BraTS, MSD, and LiTS. Adversarial feature alignment methods employ discriminators to reduce distribution shift between source and target features (Tzeng et al., 2017; Hoffman et al., 2018), while image-to-image translation approaches leverage mappings to match appearances before segmentation (Zhu et al., 2017; Huo et al., 2018). Pseudo-labeling and entropy minimization techniques refine target predictions to stabilize training (French et al., 2018; Vu et al., 2019), and consistency regularization encourages invariant predictions under perturbations (Laine and Aila, 2017; Tarvainen and Valpola, 2017). Architectural backbones commonly include U-Net and its variants (Ronneberger et al., 2015; Isensee et al., 2021) and attention-based decoders (Oktay et al., 2018) for volumetric scans and cross-plane consistency. Evaluation protocols frequently report Dice and Hausdorff metrics on multi-center cohorts, with intensity normalization, bias field correction, histogram matching, and test-time augmentation as routine preprocessing steps (Nyúl and Udupa, 1999; Shinohara et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2021). Despite progress on alignment, reported improvements often conflate gains from preprocessing with representation learning, leaving the relative contribution of each component unclear. Moreover, robustness to intensity outliers and thin structures is rarely stress-tested under realistic perturbations, masking failure modes that matter for triage. We therefore disentangle augmentation, normalization, and adaptation by designing controlled interventions that isolate their effects on boundary precision and small-lesion recall. Our study consolidates evaluation across harmonized splits and ablation protocols to expose trade-offs between stability and sensitivity under shift. The resulting analysis surfaces principled design choices for resource-constrained deployment and establishes a transparent baseline for future adaptive segmentation research.

document: Recent deployments of code assistants expose both productivity gains and fragility when models confront unseen libraries, ambiguous specifications, or security-sensitive contexts. Teams increasingly expect tools that can transform natural language intent into safe, maintainable code with verifiable behavior. However, brittle prompt handling and limited grounding in project context often lead to syntactic fixes that miss semantic requirements. To clarify where current systems succeed and fail, we examine the interaction between evaluation design and controllability mechanisms. Our analysis centers on generalization under constrained context windows and the reliability of self-verification. Recent progress in neural code generation and repair has been driven by large-scale pretraining on source repositories and curated benchmarks such as CodeSearchNet, HumanEval, MBPP, and APPS. Autoregressive transformers adapted for programming languages learn next-token distributions over code tokens and docstrings (Radford et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021), while encoder-decoder models leverage span infilling and denoising objectives to synthesize functions and patches (Raffel et al., 2020; Luo et al., 2022). Retrieval-augmented generators condition decoding on nearest neighbor snippets or API exemplars to improve adherence to library idioms (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Borgeaud et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2023), and prompt-engineering strategies tailor task specifications with few-shot demonstrations, docstring templates, or unit tests (Liu et al., 2021; Reynolds and McDonell, 2021; Ye et al., 2022). Execution-based evaluation using pass@k on HumanEval and MBPP, static analysis to check type or linter conformity, and compiler success rates widely serve as primary metrics across studies (Chen et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022). While benchmarks emphasize functional correctness, industrial adoption hinges on predictability under minor edits and adherence to organizational style guides. Prior studies suggest that post-hoc testing can recover some failures, yet it remains unclear how verification signals should be injected to steer generation without mode collapse. We therefore compare lightweight retrieval, structured prompts, and iterative repair under a unified protocol that decouples search, synthesis, and checking. By instrumenting traces with fine-grained failure taxonomies, we reveal dependence on brittle lexical cues and inconsistent use of API preconditions. The results motivate interface designs that surface uncertainty, encourage specification refinement, and support safe fallback behaviors in continuous integration.

document: Temporal reasoning over knowledge graphs is essential for forecasting events, tracing entity evolution, and answering time-sensitive queries. Unlike static link prediction, temporal settings must contend with distribution drift and nonstationary sparsity. Further, realistic deployments require extrapolation beyond the observed time horizon with calibrated uncertainty. Temporal knowledge graph reasoning has been explored with extensions of static embedding models and recurrent or attention-based dynamics across benchmarks including ICEWS14, ICEWS05-15, GDELT, and WIKIDATA12k. Translation-style approaches incorporate timestamp parameters or decay functions to modulate entity-relation interactions over time (TransE: Bordes et al., 2013; RotatE: Sun et al., 2019; DE-SimplE: Goel et al., 2020), while complex-valued and tensor factorization models capture periodicity and phase shifts (TComplEx: Lacroix et al., 2018; TNTComplEx: Xu and Li, 2019). Sequence encoders introduce temporal context with GRUs or self-attention to model evolving neighborhoods and event intensities (Trivedi et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2020; Han et al., 2021). Training regimes commonly rely on time-aware negative sampling, contrastive objectives, and regularization over temporal smoothness or sparsity (Ruffinelli et al., 2020; Sutskever et al., 2013; Li et al., 2021). Evaluation typically reports filtered MRR and Hits@k with interpolation and extrapolation protocols defined per dataset split, alongside ablations on window size and history length (Zhu et al., 2021; Jiang et al., 2016; García-Durán et al., 2018). Despite broad algorithmic coverage, experimental setups often conflate temporal granularity with supervision density, making cross-benchmark comparison tenuous. Many models also assume globally shared dynamics, which conflicts with domain-specific recurrence patterns present in political and biomedical streams. We revisit representation choices by decoupling structural encoding from temporal parameterization while holding sampling strategies fixed. Our framework tests whether inductive biases favor interpolation at the expense of forecasting rare events under long gaps. By integrating unified preprocessing and a consistent validation protocol, we clarify when temporal history truly helps and when it introduces leakage. The study provides guidance for selecting model classes under constraints on memory, latency, and update frequency.

document: Personalizing models directly on devices promises better user experience without centralizing sensitive data. Yet heterogeneous hardware, connectivity constraints, and skewed usage patterns complicate both optimization and evaluation. Production systems must trade off accuracy, fairness, and privacy budgets while meeting latency targets. We study these tensions through the lens of cross-device federated training for text and speech inputs. Federated learning on edge devices has adopted communication-efficient optimization, personalization, and privacy mechanisms across standard benchmarks such as EMNIST, Shakespeare, StackOverflow, and SpeechCommands. Baseline aggregation relies on local SGD with weighted averaging of client updates (FedAvg: McMahan et al., 2017), with modifications to address client drift and heterogeneity including proximal regularization (FedProx: Li et al., 2020), control variates (SCAFFOLD: Karimireddy et al., 2020), and normalization of update directions (FedNova: Wang et al., 2020). Adaptive server optimizers and momentum schemes further accelerate convergence under partial participation (FedOPT: Reddi et al., 2021; FedAdam; FedYogi) for large, nonconvex objectives. Personalization layers and mixture strategies enable client-specific adapters or clustered models to capture non-iid behavior (Arivazhagan et al., 2019; Dinh et al., 2020; Mansour et al., 2020). Privacy tooling spans secure aggregation for transport-layer confidentiality (Bonawitz et al., 2017), record-level differential privacy using gradient clipping and noise (Abadi et al., 2016; Kairouz et al., 2021), and audits of memorization or membership inference on held-out users (Shokri et al., 2017; Carlini et al., 2019). Despite an abundance of algorithms, reported efficiency often mixes parallelism gains with algorithmic progress, obscuring practical choices. Furthermore, privacy accounting is inconsistently tied to participation rates, making comparisons across workloads unreliable. We therefore factor computation, communication, and privacy cost into a unified budget that reflects real deployment limits. Our experiments contrast aggregation and personalization options under controlled straggler models and quota constraints. The findings inform principled defaults for sustainable, private personalization in mobile applications.

document: Robust control in nonstationary environments depends on understanding which aspects of observations change under intervention and which remain stable. Standard deep reinforcement learning often entangles nuisance variation with causal factors, leading to brittle policies. When rewards, dynamics, or sensor viewpoints shift, agents frequently overfit to superficial statistics rather than actionable structure. We examine representation choices that enable intervention-aware planning without sacrificing sample efficiency. Causal representation learning for decision-making intersects discovery, invariance, and model-based planning across environments such as Atari, Procgen, DeepMind Control Suite, and grid-world simulators with controllable interventions and confounders across tasks. Structure learning approaches estimate directed graphs from observations using constraint-based tests, score search, or differentiable relaxations (PC: Spirtes et al., 2000; GES: Chickering, 2002; NOTEARS: Zheng et al., 2018), while latent-variable models aim to disentangle factors aligned with intervention targets (Locatello et al., 2019; Khemakhem et al., 2020). Invariance-based objectives reduce spurious correlations through risk extrapolation and group robustness criteria (Peters et al., 2016; Arjovsky et al., 2019; Rosenfeld et al., 2021). Model-based reinforcement learning integrates causal structure into world models or transition predictors to support counterfactual rollouts and policy evaluation (Ha and Schmidhuber, 2018; Levine, 2020; Guez et al., 2020). Evaluation relies on intervention generalization, counterfactual policy regret, and recovery of ground-truth graphs on synthetic generators, with additional metrics tracking sample efficiency and OOD performance over held-out domains (Pearl, 2009; Bareinboim and Pearl, 2016; Schölkopf et al., 2021). Despite wide interest, many evaluations reuse visual domains with limited intervention diversity, conflating robustness with overfitting to augmentation. Moreover, alignment between discovered components and actionable variables is seldom verified beyond qualitative plots. We formalize test-time interventions and confounding processes to separate invariance from mere invariability. Our study compares discovery and planning interfaces under fixed compute and rollout budgets, emphasizing policy stability under shift. The findings clarify which objectives transfer across domains and which rely on dataset-specific shortcuts.

Evaluation relies on intervention generalization, counterfactual policy regret, and recovery of ground-truth graphs on synthetic generators, with additional metrics tracking sample efficiency and OOD performance over held-out domains (Pearl, 2009; Bareinboim and Pearl, 2016; Schölkopf et al., 2021).

document: Introduction Multimodal affect analysis integrates acoustic, visual, and textual cues to infer attitudes and emotions from human communication. Despite steady progress, the field continues to grapple with inconsistent generalization across speakers, languages, and recording conditions. Multimodal sentiment research reports extensive benchmarks on CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, IEMOCAP, and MELD, alongside method families such as Tensor Fusion Network (Zadeh et al., 2017), Low-rank Multimodal Fusion (Liu et al., 2018), Multimodal Transformer (Tsai et al., 2019), and MulT variants (Tsai et al., 2019). Further developments include memory-augmented fusion (Hazarika et al., 2018), graph-based affect reasoning (Ghosal et al., 2019), cross-modal distillation (Hazarika et al., 2020), and pretrained audiovisual encoders like AV-HuBERT, wav2vec 2.0, and CLIP used in robust multimodal pipelines (Baevski et al., 2020; Shamma et al., 2021; Radford et al., 2021). These trends illustrate the breadth of modeling choices and the centrality of standardized corpora in reporting progress. Yet reported gains can hinge on pipeline details, raising questions about stability under domain shift. Prior evaluations often emphasize average scores, leaving practical reliability underexplored. Annotation protocol differences further complicate comparisons across datasets and tasks. Modeling strategies also vary in how they encode cross-modal timing and missing modality scenarios. Recent work on robustness highlights augmentation, calibration, and causal views of confounding factors. In multimodal alignment, datasets such as LRS3, LRS2, GRID, CREMA-D, and RAVDESS are paired with techniques including CTC-based synchronization, dynamic time warping, AVSR, and contrastive alignment losses reported by Afouras et al., Chung et al., and Ma et al. These alignment resources and techniques are commonly repurposed to enhance token-level synchronization for downstream recognition and understanding. However, systematic studies on error propagation from alignment into affect prediction remain scarce. We outline an evaluation setting that stresses cross-corpus shift, noisy modalities, and label imbalance. The setup emphasizes reproducibility and transparent ablations over bespoke feature engineering. By foregrounding robustness, the discussion aims to contextualize accuracy gains alongside failure modes.

Further developments include memory-augmented fusion (Hazarika et al., 2018), graph-based affect reasoning (Ghosal et al., 2019), cross-modal distillation (Hazarika et al., 2020), and pretrained audiovisual encoders like AV-HuBERT, wav2vec 2.0, and CLIP used in robust multimodal pipelines (Baevski et al., 2020; Shamma et al., 2021; Radford et al., 2021).

In multimodal alignment, datasets such as LRS3, LRS2, GRID, CREMA-D, and RAVDESS are paired with techniques including CTC-based synchronization, dynamic time warping, AVSR, and contrastive alignment losses reported by Afouras et al., Chung et al., and Ma et al.

document: Related Work Urban mobility systems depend on accurate short-term forecasting to manage congestion and safety. Learning on sensor networks must integrate spatial structure with temporal dynamics under limited latency. Road traffic forecasting has accumulated benchmarks on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, and PEMS08, with widely used spatiotemporal models including DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and AGCRN (Bai et al., 2020). Subsequent advances explore adaptive adjacency learning (Guo et al., 2019), attention-based diffusion (Zhang et al., 2020), multi-resolution temporal kernels (Cini et al., 2022), neural controlled differential equations for streams (Kidger et al., 2020), and topology-aware dynamic graphs such as MTGNN and STG-NCDE for improved sequence modeling under complex road networks and cities globally. These models illustrate the evolution from fixed graphs toward adaptive, data-driven topologies. Yet training stability and interpretability remain open concerns in high-variance traffic regimes. Data irregularity, missing streams, and sensor faults complicate routine deployment. Demand-side signals introduce feedback that undermines naive extrapolation in peak periods. Evaluation practices differ in how they partition nodes and time to avoid leakage. Incorporating uncertainty estimates can help operators quantify operational risk. Exogenous signals appear in studies combining weather from NOAA, event schedules, and holiday calendars with baselines like ARIMA and Prophet and graph models including DCRNN, Graph WaveNet, and GMAN, additionally as gradient boosting ensembles such as XGBoost and LightGBM. Public datasets increasingly include weather and incident logs, inviting multimodal studies of causality. However, real-time constraints require careful distillation of auxiliary features for inference budgets. We consider practical settings emphasizing sparse sensors and evolving routes. Our setup stresses cold-start intersections and topology expansion events. The objective is to frame forecasting quality jointly with reliability under operational shifts.
spans: Road traffic forecasting has accumulated benchmarks on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, and PEMS08, with widely used spatiotemporal models including DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and AGCRN (Bai et al., 2020). Subsequent advances explore adaptive adjacency learning (Guo et al., 2019), attention-based diffusion (Zhang et al., 2020), multi-resolution temporal kernels (Cini et al., 2022), neural controlled differential equations for streams (Kidger et al., 2020), and topology-aware dynamic graphs such as MTGNN and STG-NCDE for improved sequence modeling under complex road networks and cities globally.

Exogenous signals appear in studies combining weather from NOAA, event schedules, and holiday calendars with baselines like ARIMA and Prophet and graph models including DCRNN, Graph WaveNet, and GMAN, additionally as gradient boosting ensembles such as XGBoost and LightGBM.

document: Introduction Low-resource named entity recognition challenges conventional assumptions about labeled data and domain stability. Sequence models must handle noisy capitalization, code-switching, and morphological variability across languages. Named entity recognition research surveys augmentations on CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes, WNUT17, and WikiAnn, with widely adopted techniques including Easy Data Augmentation (Wei and Zou, 2019), back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), span substitution with dictionaries (Zhang et al., 2020), and contextual synonym replacement (Kumar et al., 2019). Recent studies further leverage mBERT and XLM-R encoders (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020), MixUp for spans (Guo et al., 2020), paraphrase generators with PEGASUS and BART (Zhang et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020), and lexicon-driven slot sampling for entity diversity (Fang et al., 2021). These augmentation strategies reflect diverse attempts to synthesize realistic variability from limited corpora. However, the interaction between augmentation and entity boundary detection is still poorly documented. Label noise and schema drift often surface during cross-dataset transfer. Evaluation protocols vary in tokenization alignment, which affects span-level metrics. Multilingual settings add language identification and script normalization complexities. Resource catalogs and annotation guidelines differ in coverage for nested and discontinuous entities. Cross-lingual transfer lines report results on WikiAnn, MasakhaNER, PAN-X, and LORELEI using mBERT, XLM-R, infoXLM, and mT5, alongside translation-based bootstrapping, multilingual dictionaries, and label projection from corpora documented by Rahimi et al., Pan et al., and Adelani et al. Such resources underpin reproducible comparisons but also magnify domain gaps in open-world entities. Practical pipelines must also account for privacy constraints in data acquisition. We outline an approach that prioritizes stability under heterogeneous label sets. The experiments target shift-resistant training signals and measurable generalization. The resulting discussion centers on data processes as much as architectures.

Recent studies further leverage mBERT and XLM-R encoders (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020), MixUp for spans (Guo et al., 2020), paraphrase generators with PEGASUS and BART (Zhang et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020), and lexicon-driven slot sampling for entity diversity (Fang et al., 2021).

Cross-lingual transfer lines report results on WikiAnn, MasakhaNER, PAN-X, and LORELEI using mBERT, XLM-R, infoXLM, and mT5, alongside translation-based bootstrapping, multilingual dictionaries, and label projection from corpora documented by Rahimi et al., Pan et al., and Adelani et al.

document: Related Work Health systems accumulate data across hospitals, devices, and vendors, complicating centralized learning. Federated learning offers a mechanism to train models while retaining data at the source. Federated learning for healthcare frequently references institutional cohorts such as MIMIC-III, eICU, CheXpert, and UK Biobank, and adopts optimization schemes including FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), FedProx (Li et al., 2020), SCAFFOLD (Karimireddy et al., 2020), and adaptive regularization via FedDyn (Acar et al., 2021). Privacy and security components appear alongside differential privacy with DP-SGD (Abadi et al., 2016), secure aggregation and auditing protocols (Bonawitz et al., 2017), attack-robust aggregation like Krum (Blanchard et al., 2017), and personalization through meta-learning or clustering across non-iid clients (Fallah et al., 2020; Sattler et al., 2020). These ingredients frame a toolkit for cross-site collaboration under regulatory constraints. Nevertheless, heterogeneity in prevalence and measurement strongly impacts convergence. Communication budgets and stragglers introduce nontrivial engineering trade-offs. Evaluation often focuses on mean accuracy without surfacing subgroup disparities. Clinical deployment requires calibration, drift monitoring, and governance procedures. Auditability and reproducibility are essential for tracing data and model lineage. Implementations frequently rely on frameworks such as TensorFlow Federated, PySyft, Flower, and FATE, and report results on BraTS, ISIC, CheXpert, and HAM10000 with architectures including U-Net, DenseNet, and ResNet documented in applied studies across radiology and dermatology and oncology. These implementations demonstrate end-to-end feasibility on common imaging and waveform tasks. Yet the alignment between privacy guarantees and clinical utility warrants closer examination. We articulate settings that foreground fairness and robustness under site drift. The experimental design emphasizes interpretable metrics and clinically meaningful error partitions. The discussion situates learning dynamics within real-world operational constraints.

Privacy and security components appear alongside differential privacy with DP-SGD (Abadi et al., 2016), secure aggregation and auditing protocols (Bonawitz et al., 2017), attack-robust aggregation like Krum (Blanchard et al., 2017), and personalization through meta-learning or clustering across non-iid clients (Fallah et al., 2020; Sattler et al., 2020).

Implementations frequently rely on frameworks such as TensorFlow Federated, PySyft, Flower, and FATE, and report results on BraTS, ISIC, CheXpert, and HAM10000 with architectures including U-Net, DenseNet, and ResNet documented in applied studies across radiology and dermatology and oncology.

document: Introduction Smart contracts introduce immutable logic on distributed ledgers with strong economic incentives and adversarial environments. Static and dynamic analyses offer complementary strengths for vulnerability discovery and regression testing. Smart contract analysis literature commonly evaluates on curated corpora such as SmartBugs, SB Curated, and Etherscan-labeled contracts, and compares static and symbolic analyzers including Oyente (Luu et al., 2016), Mythril (Consensys, 2018), Securify (Tsankov et al., 2018), Slither (Feist et al., 2019), and Manticore (Mueller et al., 2018). Dynamic testing and fuzzing work additionally references Echidna (Trail of Bits, 2019), ILF (Huang et al., 2019), ContractFuzzer (Jiang et al., 2018), and toolchains for reentrancy, integer overflows, timestamp dependence, and unchecked calls, with real-world benchmarks drawn from SWC Registry cases and known incident reports. Despite the breadth of tooling, empirical comparisons often rely on overlapping datasets and inconsistent labeling. Tooling heterogeneity complicates attribution of findings to algorithmic choices versus implementation details. Execution semantics in the EVM expose corner cases that defeat naive abstract interpretations. Gas costs and environmental constraints further shape exploit feasibility in deployed systems. Benchmarking practices differ in how they handle compiler versions and library-linked code. Real deployments add upgradeability patterns, proxies, and governance modules that affect flow analysis. Formal verification lines mention KEVM, Isabelle/HOL, Why3, Certora Prover, VerX, and SMT-based encodings for Solidity and Vyper, together with datasets from VerX benchmarks and Ethereum Foundation bug lists, as well as lifecycles captured in Etherscan traces and Tenderly recordings. These resources and encodings provide starting points for reasoning about safety properties at scale. However, community protocols for de-duplication and disclosure timelines remain uneven. We therefore focus on evaluation axes that foreground coverage and false positive control. The study design emphasizes transparent configurations and precise defect taxonomies. The goal is to clarify benchmarking artifacts while maintaining practical relevance for auditors.

Dynamic testing and fuzzing work additionally references Echidna (Trail of Bits, 2019), ILF (Huang et al., 2019), ContractFuzzer (Jiang et al., 2018), and toolchains for reentrancy, integer overflows, timestamp dependence, and unchecked calls, with real-world benchmarks drawn from SWC Registry cases and known incident reports.

Formal verification lines mention KEVM, Isabelle/HOL, Why3, Certora Prover, VerX, and SMT-based encodings for Solidity and Vyper, together with datasets from VerX benchmarks and Ethereum Foundation bug lists, as well as lifecycles captured in Etherscan traces and Tenderly recordings.

document: Large language models exhibit emergent capabilities when trained with diverse objectives and data. Instruction tuning provides a mechanism to align pretrained models with user goals through curated tasks. Multilinguality adds challenges due to cross-lingual transfer, tokenization variance, and evaluation scarcity. Prior research has demonstrated that instruction mixtures can improve generalization beyond seen tasks. However, differences across languages and scripts complicate the design of robust instruction sets. In this work we study the structure of instruction granularity and coverage for cross-lingual transfer. We examine measurement protocols that disentangle instruction following from underlying knowledge. Our analysis emphasizes consistent scoring and prompt formatting across languages. We also consider the role of small adapters for controlling per-language behaviors. This section reviews developments that form the backdrop for our study. Early sequence-to-sequence models established that textual instructions can condition model behavior. Subsequent studies extended the approach to large decoder-only models and mixed-task training. Multilingual extensions explored translation-based augmentation and shared subword vocabularies. Large-scale instruction tuning has been explored across models and benchmarks, including T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), GPT-3 with prompts (Brown et al., 2020), FLAN (Wei et al., 2022), and InstructGPT (Ouyang et al., 2022). Datasets such as SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), Natural Instructions (Mishra et al., 2022), and BIG-bench (Srivastava et al., 2022) are commonly used, alongside evaluation on SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016) and MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2021). Methods like parameter-efficient tuning (Houlsby et al., 2019; Lester et al., 2021) and self-instruction (Wang et al., 2023) are frequently reported too. These efforts collectively motivate a closer look at how instruction composition interacts with multilingual transfer. Unlike prior surveys, we focus on the coupling between prompt templates and evaluation artifacts. We also track the effect of tokenization schemes on per-language calibration. We define a three-part framework covering data creation, parameterization, and measurement. The data component isolates instruction phrasing, exemplars, and negative constraints. The parameterization component compares full fine-tuning, adapters, and prompt-tuning under compute limits. The measurement component separates automatic metrics from human judgments with bilingual adjudication. We adopt resource bands to reflect realistic deployment scenarios in multilingual settings. Our experiments analyze stability, sample efficiency, and sensitivity to instruction perturbations. We further investigate error modes unique to low-resource scripts and mixed-direction scripts. The findings highlight the necessity of consistent evaluation across heterogeneous task families. We conclude with recommendations for reproducible multilingual instruction curation. Limitations include reliance on public corpora and moderate-scale models. Ethical considerations address annotation equity and potential harms from culturally insensitive prompts.
spans: Large-scale instruction tuning has been explored across models and benchmarks, including T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), GPT-3 with prompts (Brown et al., 2020), FLAN (Wei et al., 2022), and InstructGPT (Ouyang et al., 2022). Datasets such as SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), Natural Instructions (Mishra et al., 2022), and BIG-bench (Srivastava et al., 2022) are commonly used, alongside evaluation on SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016) and MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2021). Methods like parameter-efficient tuning (Houlsby et al., 2019; Lester et al., 2021) and self-instruction (Wang et al., 2023) are frequently reported too.

document: Detecting anomalies in multivariate sensor streams is essential for reliability in industrial systems. Models must localize faults quickly while maintaining low false alarm rates. Real deployments face nonstationarity, missing data, and asynchronous sensors. Reconstruction-based detectors dominate, but they often overfit regular variations. Probabilistic formulations improve calibration but can be brittle under distribution shift. We study a representation that balances temporal context with cross-channel structure. Our analysis isolates the roles of lag window, seasonality, and dependency priors. We also formalize an evaluation protocol that penalizes fragmented detections. This section summarizes prior work and datasets relevant to our study. Early methods relied on autoregressive residual thresholds under Gaussian assumptions. Neural forecasters extended this idea by learning nonlinear predictors for normal patterns. Graph-based models exploit process topology to share statistical strength across sensors. Multivariate time series anomaly detection has been widely studied with approaches such as LSTM-AD (Malhotra et al., 2015), DAGMM (Zong et al., 2018), MSCRED (Zhang et al., 2019), OmniAnomaly (Su et al., 2019), MAD-GAN (Li et al., 2019), and USAD (Audibert et al., 2020). Benchmark datasets frequently include NAB (Lavin and Ahmad, 2015), Yahoo S5 (Laptev et al., 2015), SWaT (Mathur and Tippenhauer, 2016), WADI (Ahmed et al., 2017), and NASA SMAP/MSL (Hundman et al., 2018). Recent evaluations also reference KDD Cup 2018 EEG, SMD (Su et al., 2019), and the UCR archive. These references highlight both the diversity of architectures and the variety of benchmarks. In contrast with existing evaluations, we emphasize latency-aware scoring and root-cause hints. We instantiate a family of detectors with shared embeddings and lightweight decoders. The approach encourages sparse saliency that maps to actionable channels. We design ablations to separate forecasting accuracy from detection robustness. We also investigate how densification of labels affects training dynamics. Our metrics report event-level precision and recall alongside pointwise indicators. We quantify stability using bootstrap confidence intervals over sliding windows. We examine the sensitivity of thresholds to class imbalance and drift. We compare hyperparameter heuristics that control lag and dilation rates. Complexity is assessed by wall-clock latency and memory per time step. Results indicate gains under synthetic and real distribution shifts. Case studies on industrial logs demonstrate interpretable failures and earlier alarms. Limitations include reliance on public topology proxies and moderate horizon lengths. We discuss ethical risks from surveillance misuse and propose mitigation guidelines.
spans: Multivariate time series anomaly detection has been widely studied with approaches such as LSTM-AD (Malhotra et al., 2015), DAGMM (Zong et al., 2018), MSCRED (Zhang et al., 2019), OmniAnomaly (Su et al., 2019), MAD-GAN (Li et al., 2019), and USAD (Audibert et al., 2020). Benchmark datasets frequently include NAB (Lavin and Ahmad, 2015), Yahoo S5 (Laptev et al., 2015), SWaT (Mathur and Tippenhauer, 2016), WADI (Ahmed et al., 2017), and NASA SMAP/MSL (Hundman et al., 2018). Recent evaluations also reference KDD Cup 2018 EEG, SMD (Su et al., 2019), and the UCR archive.

document: Molecular property prediction accelerates discovery by screening candidates in silico. Graph neural networks provide a natural representation of atoms and bonds. Physical priors and data efficiency remain central challenges for generalization. Expressive message functions must balance accuracy with computational cost. Benchmarking further complicates progress due to data leakage risks. We examine how architectural choices interact with featurization and constraints. Our perspective emphasizes evaluation fidelity under scaffold splits. We separate thermodynamic and biological targets to avoid conflating signals. We also analyze uncertainty calibration for active learning scenarios. This section summarizes representative architectures and datasets in the field. Early models aggregated local neighborhoods with shallow updates. Later designs introduced directional information and higher-order interactions. Graph-based molecular learning commonly builds on architectures such as MPNN (Gilmer et al., 2017), GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GAT (Veličković et al., 2018), SchNet (Schütt et al., 2017), DimeNet (Klicpera et al., 2020), and GROVER (Rong et al., 2020). Standard benchmarks include MoleculeNet collections (Wu et al., 2018) with Tox21, ToxCast, and FreeSolv, alongside QM9 (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014), ZINC, and OGB-MOLHIV (Hu et al., 2020). Contrastive and self-supervised methods such as InfoGraph (Sun et al., 2020) and GraphCL (You et al., 2020) are also widely reported in recent molecular prediction studies. These resources frame the landscape within which our study operates. In contrast to prior combinations, we tease apart geometry and chemistry contributions. We define a modular stack that isolates encoders, edge kernels, and readouts. The stack allows controlled ablations under matched parameters. We adopt descriptors that respect invariances while remaining computationally light. Training regimes compare contrastive warm starts and supervised baselines. We quantify performance under realistic class imbalance through stratified splits. Active learning loops evaluate acquisition under cost-aware constraints. We assess calibration with proper scoring rules and conformal coverage. Interpretability is examined through atom masking and counterfactual edits. Results indicate gains from targeted geometric priors over blanket complexity. Improvements persist across drug-like and small inorganic subsets. Limitations include the scale of 3D labels and approximated conformers. Reproducibility practices include deterministic seeding and released splits. Ethical considerations discuss dual-use risks in toxicity and synthesis planning.
spans: Graph-based molecular learning commonly builds on architectures such as MPNN (Gilmer et al., 2017), GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GAT (Veličković et al., 2018), SchNet (Schütt et al., 2017), DimeNet (Klicpera et al., 2020), and GROVER (Rong et al., 2020). Standard benchmarks include MoleculeNet collections (Wu et al., 2018) with Tox21, ToxCast, and FreeSolv, alongside QM9 (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014), ZINC, and OGB-MOLHIV (Hu et al., 2020). Contrastive and self-supervised methods such as InfoGraph (Sun et al., 2020) and GraphCL (You et al., 2020) are also widely reported in recent molecular prediction studies.

document: Recommender systems influence what users see and what providers earn. Fairness concerns arise when exposure and relevance interact under constraints. Stakeholders include consumers, producers, and the platform mediating trade-offs. Traditional accuracy objectives can exacerbate historical biases. Popularity feedback loops further entrench disparities. We study fairness-aware ranking under limited feedback and budgeted interventions. Our framework models supply constraints and position bias jointly. We separate allocative fairness from predictive parity to clarify goals. We also measure opportunity costs for affected providers. This section reviews empirical settings and algorithmic tools relevant to our study. Prior work spans user- and item-side fairness with varying definitions and proxies. Fairness in recommendation has been studied using datasets such as MovieLens-1M (Harper and Konstan, 2015), Yelp (Asghar et al., 2016), and Amazon Books (McAuley et al., 2015), with additional logs from Last.fm and KuaiRec. Common metrics include demographic parity, equal opportunity (Hardt et al., 2016), calibration (Steck, 2018), and exposure-based measures (Singh and Joachims, 2018). Methods span re-ranking like FA*IR (Zehlike et al., 2017), diversity-inspired xQuAD (Santos et al., 2010), pairwise regularization for equity (Beutel et al., 2019), and provider-aware objectives for multi-stakeholder settings (Abdollahpouri et al., 2019) across offline and online evaluations. These studies define the landscape of metrics and interventions considered in practice. Our contribution is a policy-aware objective that internalizes supplier constraints. We derive a convex surrogate for exposure allocation with guarantees. The derivation incorporates estimate uncertainty via distributional robustness. We provide a plug-in estimator for position-dependent click propensities. Calibration is enforced through a constrained optimization that respects coverage. We propose a stochastic re-ranking algorithm compatible with large catalogs. The method adapts to budget shifts without retraining predictors. We analyze regret and constraint violation bounds under partial feedback. Simulations illustrate trade-offs between welfare, fairness, and efficiency. Large-scale experiments use counterfactual evaluation with learned propensities. We report both item-level and group-level metrics with confidence intervals. Results indicate improved provider equity at modest accuracy cost. Limitations include reliance on logged policy support and group labels. We discuss transparency and consent considerations for affected creators. We release code to facilitate reproducible assessment of fairness policies.
spans: Fairness in recommendation has been studied using datasets such as MovieLens-1M (Harper and Konstan, 2015), Yelp (Asghar et al., 2016), and Amazon Books (McAuley et al., 2015), with additional logs from Last.fm and KuaiRec. Common metrics include demographic parity, equal opportunity (Hardt et al., 2016), calibration (Steck, 2018), and exposure-based measures (Singh and Joachims, 2018). Methods span re-ranking like FA*IR (Zehlike et al., 2017), diversity-inspired xQuAD (Santos et al., 2010), pairwise regularization for equity (Beutel et al., 2019), and provider-aware objectives for multi-stakeholder settings (Abdollahpouri et al., 2019) across offline and online evaluations.

document: Cross-modal retrieval enables users to search images with text and vice versa. The task relies on aligning vision and language representations. Scaling data and models has led to striking improvements. However, downstream constraints require careful evaluation beyond recall. Hard negatives and domain shifts expose weaknesses in embedding space structure. We study retrieval under budgeted compute and limited labels. Our analysis decomposes performance by caption style and visual diversity. We also consider cross-lingual captions and noisy alt-text. This section reviews benchmarks, models, and training corpora. Early systems relied on CCA projections of hand-crafted features. Deep encoders replaced features with learned representations and shared spaces. Joint training introduced in-batch contrastive losses for efficiency. Vision–language retrieval has been benchmarked on Flickr30k (Plummer et al., 2015), MS COCO (Lin et al., 2014), Conceptual Captions (Sharma et al., 2018), and Visual Genome (Krishna et al., 2017). Representative models include dual encoders such as CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) and ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021), and multi-stream or fusion models like ViLBERT (Lu et al., 2019), LXMERT (Tan and Bansal, 2019), UNITER (Chen et al., 2020), ALBEF (Li et al., 2021), and BLIP (Li et al., 2022). Training corpora aggregate WebImageText, LAION-400M, and image–text pairs from news and social media. These resources set the context for our experimental design. Unlike prior work, we emphasize calibration and abstention under retrieval. We design a temperature-aware scoring rule for long-tail queries. The rule adjusts similarity thresholds by density estimates. A compact encoder distills knowledge from a larger teacher. Distillation matches neighborhood structure while preserving compactness. We introduce perturbation tests for robustness to caption paraphrases. The tests include synonym swaps and word order changes. We quantify sensitivity using retrieval consistency across augmentations. Evaluation considers memory, latency, and energy at inference. We report metrics under closed and open vocabulary conditions. Results show improved robustness with minimal compute overhead. Failure analysis reveals gaps on compositional and counting queries. Limitations include lack of video and multilingual pretraining. We discuss dataset licensing and potential bias amplification.
spans: Vision–language retrieval has been benchmarked on Flickr30k (Plummer et al., 2015), MS COCO (Lin et al., 2014), Conceptual Captions (Sharma et al., 2018), and Visual Genome (Krishna et al., 2017). Representative models include dual encoders such as CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) and ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021), and multi-stream or fusion models like ViLBERT (Lu et al., 2019), LXMERT (Tan and Bansal, 2019), UNITER (Chen et al., 2020), ALBEF (Li et al., 2021), and BLIP (Li et al., 2022). Training corpora aggregate WebImageText, LAION-400M, and image–text pairs from news and social media.

document: Sentiment analysis in client feedback remains difficult when labels are scarce and domains shift rapidly. Prior work shows that cross-domain transfer is feasible but brittle under sparse supervision. We focus on aligning lexical cues and discourse markers across product categories using unlabeled data. Our approach augments representation learning with geometry-aware consistency constraints. We evaluate across e-commerce and app reviews with controlled annotation budgets. To situate our study, we briefly outline representative datasets and methods that shape the space. Research on cross-domain sentiment spans datasets such as the Amazon Reviews corpus (Blitzer et al., 2007), the Multi-Domain Sentiment Dataset (Pang and Lee, 2004), Yelp polarity reviews (Zhang et al., 2015), and IMDB movie reviews (Maas et al., 2011), alongside Twitter sentiment collections and the Stanford Sentiment Treebank (Socher et al., 2013) used for auxiliary supervision and vocabulary coverage. Methodologically, studies apply domain-adversarial training (Ganin et al., 2016), correlation alignment (Sun and Saenko, 2016), virtual adversarial training (Miyato et al., 2017), pseudo-labeling (Lee, 2013), and fine-tuning (Devlin et al., 2019). Despite these advances, existing protocols understate domain drift in review style and topic shifts. We therefore design splits that delay label availability and stress semantic sparsity. Our modeling choices emphasize stability under covariate and label shift simultaneously. We analyze calibration and error localization to explain transfer failures. Experiments compare against strong adversarial and contrastive baselines. We report metrics beyond accuracy, including expected calibration error and abstention utility. Ablations quantify sensitivity to pivot selection and augmentation noise. Finally, we release code and splits to facilitate robust, repeatable benchmarking.
spans: Research on cross-domain sentiment spans datasets such as the Amazon Reviews corpus (Blitzer et al., 2007), the Multi-Domain Sentiment Dataset (Pang and Lee, 2004), Yelp polarity reviews (Zhang et al., 2015), and IMDB movie reviews (Maas et al., 2011), alongside Twitter sentiment collections and the Stanford Sentiment Treebank (Socher et al., 2013) used for auxiliary supervision and vocabulary coverage. Methodologically, studies apply domain-adversarial training (Ganin et al., 2016), correlation alignment (Sun and Saenko, 2016), virtual adversarial training (Miyato et al., 2017), pseudo-labeling (Lee, 2013), and fine-tuning (Devlin et al., 2019).

document: Predicting molecular properties from structure is central to virtual screening and lead optimization. Data scarcity and distribution shift across chemical series hinder generalization in practical settings. We study how inductive biases over local functional groups interact with scaffold splits. Our method integrates substructure pooling with uncertainty-aware training. We aim to improve out-of-domain performance under realistic medicinal chemistry constraints. Before detailing our approach, we review representative datasets and architectures that dominate benchmarks. Benchmark datasets used for molecular learning include MoleculeNet tasks such as ESOL, FreeSolv, and BACE, the QM9 quantum chemistry corpus, ZINC subsets, and Tox21 toxicity panels (Wu et al., 2018; Ramakrishnan et al., 2014; Irwin and Shoichet, 2005; Huang et al., 2016). Representative models encompass graph convolutional networks (Kipf and Welling, 2017), message passing neural networks (Gilmer et al., 2017), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), graph isomorphism networks (Xu et al., 2019), SchNet (Schütt et al., 2017), and AttentiveFP (Xiong et al., 2020), plus graph transformers (Ying et al., 2021). These resources standardize tasks yet may obscure scaffold-level disparities relevant to deployment. Accordingly, we prioritize evaluation on stringent scaffold and temporal splits drawn from proprietary assays. Our formulation decomposes predictions into additive group contributions with learned interaction terms. This encourages interpretability aligned with chemists’ intuition about substituent effects. We calibrate uncertainties to flag unreliable extrapolations for active learning. Comparative experiments show gains on solubility, toxicity, and binding affinity tasks. Ablations analyze contributions from pooling granularity and calibration choices. We also discuss implications for data acquisition in early discovery cycles.
spans: Benchmark datasets used for molecular learning include MoleculeNet tasks such as ESOL, FreeSolv, and BACE, the QM9 quantum chemistry corpus, ZINC subsets, and Tox21 toxicity panels (Wu et al., 2018; Ramakrishnan et al., 2014; Irwin and Shoichet, 2005; Huang et al., 2016). Representative models encompass graph convolutional networks (Kipf and Welling, 2017), message passing neural networks (Gilmer et al., 2017), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), graph isomorphism networks (Xu et al., 2019), SchNet (Schütt et al., 2017), and AttentiveFP (Xiong et al., 2020), plus graph transformers (Ying et al., 2021).

document: Accurate segmentation of medical imagery remains challenging in regimes with limited labels. Scanner variability and pathology heterogeneity further complicate supervised training. Self-supervised pretraining offers a promising way to leverage abundant unlabeled scans. However, most studies tune objectives without considering clinical deployment constraints. We investigate representation learning that preserves anatomical topology under sparse annotations. Our framework introduces structure-aware pretext tasks tied to organ geometry. We couple these with uncertainty regularization to reduce overconfident errors. Before detailing our design, we summarize prevalent datasets and pretraining methods used in the field. Common benchmarks span the Multimodal Brain Tumor Segmentation Challenge, the Medical Segmentation Decathlon, LUNA16 for pulmonary nodules, ACDC for cardiac MRI, ISLES for stroke lesions, and KiTS for renal tumors (Menze et al., 2015; Antonelli et al., 2022; Setio et al., 2017; Bernard et al., 2018; Maier et al., 2017; Heller et al., 2019). Pretraining approaches include self-supervised SimCLR (Chen et al., 2020), MoCo (He et al., 2020), SwAV (Caron et al., 2020), masked autoencoders (He et al., 2022), DINO (Caron et al., 2021), and nnU-Net (Isensee et al., 2021). These resources enable controlled comparisons but often neglect cross-site generalization and label noise. We therefore curate federated splits reflecting hospital-specific protocols and demographics. Our loss enforces consistency across views aligned by deformable registration. We pair this with a lightweight decoder compatible with standard clinical GPUs. Experiments demonstrate improvements on tumor and organ delineation with fewer labels. We release scripts and federation guidelines to support reproducible assessment.
spans: Common benchmarks span the Multimodal Brain Tumor Segmentation Challenge, the Medical Segmentation Decathlon, LUNA16 for pulmonary nodules, ACDC for cardiac MRI, ISLES for stroke lesions, and KiTS for renal tumors (Menze et al., 2015; Antonelli et al., 2022; Setio et al., 2017; Bernard et al., 2018; Maier et al., 2017; Heller et al., 2019). Pretraining approaches include self-supervised SimCLR (Chen et al., 2020), MoCo (He et al., 2020), SwAV (Caron et al., 2020), masked autoencoders (He et al., 2022), DINO (Caron et al., 2021), and nnU-Net (Isensee et al., 2021).

document: Forecasting nonstationary time series is vital for planning under uncertainty. Real deployments demand calibrated distributions rather than point predictions. Moreover, covariate shifts and sparse events complicate training and evaluation. We target probabilistic forecasting with mechanisms that remain robust to regime changes. Our method builds hierarchical priors over seasonal and intermittent components. We also integrate conformal calibration to bound misspecification risk. Before turning to the model, we summarize influential datasets and architectures frequently used in evaluation. Standard benchmarks include the M4 and M5 competitions, the Electricity and Traffic datasets, Exchange rate series, hourly Weather, and the ETT collections for long-horizon forecasting (Makridakis et al., 2018; Makridakis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2017; Lai et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2021). Representative models include Prophet (Taylor and Letham, 2018), DeepAR (Salinas et al., 2020), N-BEATS (Oreshkin et al., 2020), the Temporal Fusion Transformer (Lim et al., 2021), Informer (Zhou et al., 2021), FEDformer (Zhou et al., 2022), and recent PatchTST for series-as-patches (Nie et al., 2023). While these benchmarks are popular, they often miss regime breaks typical in operations. Therefore we construct stress tests with simulated shocks and altered covariate availability. Our inference scheme couples likelihood mixtures with shape constraints for stability. This yields sharper predictive intervals without sacrificing coverage at long horizons. Comparisons include both classical and deep baselines under identical splits. We report accuracy, calibration, and decision-centric cost metrics. The analysis highlights when distributional modeling most benefits downstream control.

Representative models include Prophet (Taylor and Letham, 2018), DeepAR (Salinas et al., 2020), N-BEATS (Oreshkin et al., 2020), the Temporal Fusion Transformer (Lim et al., 2021), Informer (Zhou et al., 2021), FEDformer (Zhou et al., 2022), and recent PatchTST for series-as-patches (Nie et al., 2023).

document: Personalized recommendation can inadvertently magnify existing societal and market biases. Platforms must balance utility with fair exposure across items and users. Yet fairness objectives can conflict with accuracy and business constraints. We formulate a framework that reconciles exposure guarantees with user satisfaction. Our perspective treats fairness as a constrained optimization over ranking policies. We validate with user studies and large-scale logs. Before presenting the framework, we survey datasets and algorithmic strands commonly referenced in fairness-aware recommendation. Public corpora often used in fairness studies include MovieLens 1M and 20M, Amazon product reviews, Last.fm listening logs, and RecSys Challenge datasets from Yelp and Trivago (Harper and Konstan, 2015; McAuley et al., 2015; Celma, 2010; Quadrana et al., 2018). Methodological directions feature FA*IR for fair ranking (Zehlike et al., 2017), exposure-aware re-ranking (Singh and Joachims, 2018), calibrated recommendation (Steck, 2018), adversarial debiasing (Beutel et al., 2019), popularity reweighting for long-tail balance (Abdollahpouri et al., 2019), fairness regularization (Yao and Huang, 2017), and fairness-aware online bandits (Patro et al., 2020). These strands establish useful baselines but rarely address multi-stakeholder trade-offs dynamically. Accordingly, we model feedback loops that couple provider exposure and user engagement. Our method integrates counterfactual estimators with constrained policy gradients. We derive bounds that connect fairness constraints to long-term value variance. Experiments compare against re-ranking, adversarial, and calibration baselines under identical budgets. We analyze impacts on minority providers and cold-start users. Findings inform practical, monitorable targets for platform governance.

Methodological directions feature FA*IR for fair ranking (Zehlike et al., 2017), exposure-aware re-ranking (Singh and Joachims, 2018), calibrated recommendation (Steck, 2018), adversarial debiasing (Beutel et al., 2019), popularity reweighting for long-tail balance (Abdollahpouri et al., 2019), fairness regularization (Yao and Huang, 2017), and fairness-aware online bandits (Patro et al., 2020).

document: Abstractive summarization has become a cornerstone for condensing long texts into concise narratives across news, scientific literature, and policy documents. Despite rapid progress, practitioners continue to report tensions between informativeness, faithfulness, and controllability in real deployments. Models that succeed on curated benchmarks often face robustness issues under distribution shift and domain diversity. Deployment constraints add additional pressures around latency, memory, and privacy, particularly for edge and on-premise scenarios. Curating appropriate training data remains difficult, with annotation cost, copyright, and domain mismatch complicating scale. These practical challenges motivate a careful review of modeling trends and evaluation settings that dominate the literature. A systematic view also clarifies how datasets, metrics, and architectures interact to shape reported progress. We therefore contextualize recent advances and common practices that frame the current state of the field. Early neural architectures for document summarization emphasized hierarchical encoders and pointer mechanisms, with representative systems including the pointer-generator network of See et al. (2017), the reinforcement learning objective of Paulus et al. (2018), and the coverage penalty explored by Tu et al. (2016). Abstractive sequence-to-sequence models subsequently incorporated copy and coverage refinements, as in Gehrmann et al. (2018), while extractive formulations adopted neural ranking over sentence candidates using contextual encoders similar to Nallapati et al. (2017) and Narayan et al. (2018). Large-scale pretraining shifted performance trends, with BERT-based summarizers leveraging encoder-only objectives for sentence scoring (Liu, 2019; Zhong et al., 2020) and encoder-decoder T5 or BART variants producing fluent abstracts from news datasets such as CNN/DailyMail and XSum (Raffel et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020). Hybrid approaches further integrated content planning and discourse ordering using entity grids, content selectors, or latent plan decoders, as shown in Puduppully et al. (2019), Narayan et al. (2021), Fan et al. (2019), and Dou et al. (2021). Evaluation practices relied on automatic metrics such as ROUGE, BLEU, and METEOR (Lin, 2004; Papineni et al., 2002; Banerjee and Lavie, 2005), with learned metrics like BERTScore and MoverScore later applied to mitigate lexical mismatch penalties (Zhang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2019). Common corpora include CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015), XSum (Narayan et al., 2018), Newsroom (Grusky et al., 2018), Gigaword (Graff et al., 2003), Multi-News (Fabbri et al., 2019), and GovReport (Huang et al., 2021), alongside domain-specific collections such as arXiv, PubMed, and BigPatent (Cohan et al., 2018; Sharma et al., 2019). Control mechanisms for length and style have been explored through constrained decoding, prefix tuning, and prompt-based steering (Fan et al., 2018; Keskar et al., 2019; Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021), while faithfulness concerns motivated factuality-oriented training using question-answering critics, entailment objectives, and entity-level constraints (Eyal et al., 2019; Kryscinski et al., 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2021). Multi-document summarization adopted graph-based aggregation and retrieval-augmented generation, combining centrality measures, cluster attention, and evidence reranking across Multi-News, WikiSum, and WCEP (Yasunaga et al., 2017; Liu and Lapata, 2019; Fabbri et al., 2019; Perez-Beltrachini et al., 2019). Cross-lingual and multilingual settings leveraged mBART and mT5 with sentencepiece vocabularies over CC-100 and mC4, utilized bilingual constraints or alignment losses to preserve content across translation, and evaluated on MLSum, XL-Sum, and WikiLingua benchmarks (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; Scialom et al., 2020; Hasan et al., 2021; Ladhak et al., 2020). Recent compression-oriented studies consider knowledge distillation, layer pruning, and quantization-aware training for summarizers on-device, with evaluations on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, and Reddit TIFU reporting latency and memory reductions under tight budgets. The breadth of techniques reveals numerous design choices that interact with task assumptions and resource availability. However, persistent discrepancies between automatic scores and human judgments highlight limitations in conventional evaluation regimens. Domain-specific requirements, such as citation consistency in scientific briefs or sensitivity controls in clinical notes, further complicate model selection. Practical workflows increasingly favor modular toolchains that combine retrieval, content planning, and constrained decoding with lightweight oversight. Emerging governance guidelines emphasize transparency around training data provenance and risk assessments for factuality-sensitive applications. Opportunities arise in bridging dataset biases with targeted objectives that better reflect user-facing criteria. Scalable methods that maintain faithfulness under tight budgets are particularly pressing for organizations operating outside large compute centers. Against this backdrop, we outline the problem setting and empirical strategy adopted in the remainder of the paper.

document: Scientific summarization demands compression of lengthy, technical documents while preserving key claims, evidence, and limitations. Recent models handle thousands of tokens but still struggle with multi-section discourse and cross-referencing of figures or tables. Readers expect consistent terminology and accurate citation attribution, which complicates standard paraphrase-heavy generation. Benchmark heterogeneity also obscures which advances translate to observable gains in expert workflows. Laboratory notes, preprints, and peer-reviewed articles each exhibit different rhetorical moves and citation densities. These realities necessitate a broad scan of modeling and evaluation practices that shape current reporting. In particular, trends in pretraining, control, and factuality are central to understanding scientific use cases. We summarize widely adopted approaches, datasets, and metrics studied to date. Early neural architectures for document summarization emphasized hierarchical encoders and pointer mechanisms, with representative systems including the pointer-generator network of See et al. (2017), the reinforcement learning objective of Paulus et al. (2018), and the coverage penalty explored by Tu et al. (2016). Abstractive sequence-to-sequence models subsequently incorporated copy and coverage refinements, as in Gehrmann et al. (2018), while extractive formulations adopted neural ranking over sentence candidates using contextual encoders similar to Nallapati et al. (2017) and Narayan et al. (2018). Large-scale pretraining shifted performance trends, with BERT-based summarizers leveraging encoder-only objectives for sentence scoring (Liu, 2019; Zhong et al., 2020) and encoder-decoder T5 or BART variants producing fluent abstracts from news datasets such as CNN/DailyMail and XSum (Raffel et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020). (2021), Fan et al. (2019), and Dou et al. (2021).
