Cross-lingual and multilingual settings leveraged mBART and mT5 with sentencepiece vocabularies over CC-100 and mC4, utilized bilingual constraints or alignment losses to preserve content across translation, and evaluated on MLSum, XL-Sum, and WikiLingua benchmarks (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; Scialom et al., 2020; Hasan et al., 2021; Ladhak et al., 2020). Recent compression-oriented studies consider knowledge distillation, layer pruning, and quantization-aware training for summarizers on-device, with evaluations on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, and Reddit TIFU reporting latency and memory reductions under tight budgets. For technical articles, hierarchical content selection anchored to discourse roles remains especially relevant. Evaluation protocols benefit from task-specific dimensions, including claim verifiability and method fidelity. Human-in-the-loop curation, where models draft and experts revise, reframes objectives around edit efficiency. Document structure priors, such as section headings and citation graphs, can complement token-level modeling. Open licensing and de-identification continue to influence dataset design in biomedical and legal domains. As sequence lengths grow, compute-aware strategies for training and inference become necessary to sustain iteration speed. Cross-venue reproducibility checks are critical for understanding sensitivity to pretraining data and seed variance. The ensuing sections detail the task definition, resources, and evaluation plan for our study.

document: Meeting summarization focuses on conversational data with overlapping speech, disfluencies, and long-range dependencies. Stakeholders prioritize action items, decisions, and responsibilities rather than purely extractive highlights. Speaker attribution and temporal grounding interact with summarization quality in subtle ways. Noisy transcripts from diarization and ASR introduce errors that cascade through downstream processing. Multimodal cues such as slides or whiteboard content are often unavailable or misaligned with transcripts. These conditions motivate scrutiny of modeling baselines and evaluation artifacts derived from news-centric corpora. It is helpful to catalog methodology patterns that recur across abstractive and extractive systems. The following overview assembles widely referenced components and datasets. Early neural architectures for document summarization emphasized hierarchical encoders and pointer mechanisms, with representative systems including the pointer-generator network of See et al. (2017), the reinforcement learning objective of Paulus et al. (2018), and the coverage penalty explored by Tu et al. (2016). Abstractive sequence-to-sequence models subsequently incorporated copy and coverage refinements, as in Gehrmann et al. (2018), while extractive formulations adopted neural ranking over sentence candidates using contextual encoders similar to Nallapati et al. (2017) and Narayan et al. (2018). Large-scale pretraining shifted performance trends, with BERT-based summarizers leveraging encoder-only objectives for sentence scoring (Liu, 2019; Zhong et al., 2020) and encoder-decoder T5 or BART variants producing fluent abstracts from news datasets such as CNN/DailyMail and XSum (Raffel et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020). (2021), Fan et al. (2019), and Dou et al. (2021). Multi-document summarization adopted graph-based aggregation and retrieval-augmented generation, combining centrality measures, cluster attention, and evidence reranking across Multi-News, WikiSum, and WCEP (Yasunaga et al., 2017; Liu and Lapata, 2019; Fabbri et al., 2019; Perez-Beltrachini et al., 2019). Cross-lingual and multilingual settings leveraged mBART and mT5 with sentencepiece vocabularies over CC-100 and mC4, utilized bilingual constraints or alignment losses to preserve content across translation, and evaluated on MLSum, XL-Sum, and WikiLingua benchmarks (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; Scialom et al., 2020; Hasan et al., 2021; Ladhak et al., 2020). Recent compression-oriented studies consider knowledge distillation, layer pruning, and quantization-aware training for summarizers on-device, with evaluations on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, and Reddit TIFU reporting latency and memory reductions under tight budgets. Conversational settings additionally require mechanisms for disentangling threads and tracking commitments across turns. Role-aware encoders and speaker-conditioned decoders can address attribution but increase parameterization. ASR error robustness often benefits from subword and confusion-aware training regimes. Evaluation beyond ROUGE, including pyramid methods and structured slot accuracy, aligns better with user needs. Privacy concerns around corporate meetings limit dataset sharing and complicate reproducibility. Domain adaptation from public podcasts to private enterprise meetings remains an open practical challenge. On-device summarization is attractive in regulated industries with strict data residency rules. Subsequent sections specify our operational requirements and experimental constraints.

document: Multilingual policy summarization supports stakeholders who must navigate legislation and regulations across jurisdictions. Terminological variation and legalese complicate generalization from English-centric corpora. Low-resource languages frequently lack aligned summaries or parallel legal corpora of sufficient quality. Machine translation can propagate ambiguities that distort obligations and exemptions. Stakeholders require faithful, conservative summaries with calibrated uncertainty around statutory interpretation. A review of mainstream approaches clarifies baseline capabilities transferable to public-sector contexts. It also situates training data and evaluation norms that dominate recent publications. We compile representative methods, resources, and metrics commonly cited in the literature. Early neural architectures for document summarization emphasized hierarchical encoders and pointer mechanisms, with representative systems including the pointer-generator network of See et al. (2017), the reinforcement learning objective of Paulus et al. (2018), and the coverage penalty explored by Tu et al. (2016). (2018), while extractive formulations adopted neural ranking over sentence candidates using contextual encoders similar to Nallapati et al. (2017) and Narayan et al. (2018). Recent compression-oriented studies consider knowledge distillation, layer pruning, and quantization-aware training for summarizers on-device, with evaluations on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, and Reddit TIFU reporting latency and memory reductions under tight budgets. Legal applications further emphasize citation fidelity and preservation of modal verbs expressing obligations. Jurisdiction-specific ontologies provide structure that can guide content selection and compression. Human review loops are essential given potential harms from misinterpretation of binding text. Benchmarks would benefit from multilingual, domain-grounded tasks with expert-validated references. Resource constraints in government IT call for models that are compact, auditable, and robust. Transparent reporting of pretraining data sources supports procurement and risk assessment. Continual learning is relevant as statutes evolve and administrative guidance is updated. We next define the problem scope and operational evaluation used in our study.

(2021), Fan et al. (2019), and Dou et al. (2021). Evaluation practices relied on automatic metrics such as ROUGE, BLEU, and METEOR (Lin, 2004; Papineni et al., 2002; Banerjee and Lavie, 2005), with learned metrics like BERTScore and MoverScore later applied to mitigate lexical mismatch penalties (Zhang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2019). Common corpora include CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015), XSum (Narayan et al., 2018), Newsroom (Grusky et al., 2018), Gigaword (Graff et al., 2003), Multi-News (Fabbri et al., 2019), and GovReport (Huang et al., 2021), alongside domain-specific collections such as arXiv, PubMed, and BigPatent (Cohan et al., 2018; Sharma et al., 2019). Control mechanisms for length and style have been explored through constrained decoding, prefix tuning, and prompt-based steering (Fan et al., 2018; Keskar et al., 2019; Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021), while faithfulness concerns motivated factuality-oriented training using question-answering critics, entailment objectives, and entity-level constraints (Eyal et al., 2019; Kryscinski et al., 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2021). Multi-document summarization adopted graph-based aggregation and retrieval-augmented generation, combining centrality measures, cluster attention, and evidence reranking across Multi-News, WikiSum, and WCEP (Yasunaga et al., 2017; Liu and Lapata, 2019; Fabbri et al., 2019; Perez-Beltrachini et al., 2019). Cross-lingual and multilingual settings leveraged mBART and mT5 with sentencepiece vocabularies over CC-100 and mC4, utilized bilingual constraints or alignment losses to preserve content across translation, and evaluated on MLSum, XL-Sum, and WikiLingua benchmarks (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; Scialom et al., 2020; Hasan et al., 2021; Ladhak et al., 2020). Recent compression-oriented studies consider knowledge distillation, layer pruning, and quantization-aware training for summarizers on-device, with evaluations on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, and Reddit TIFU reporting latency and memory reductions under tight budgets.

document: Educational summarization aims to condense textbooks, lectures, and lesson plans for adaptive learning. Pedagogical quality depends on curricular alignment, prerequisite pacing, and misconceptions addressed. Summaries must preserve definitions, theorems, and worked examples without introducing conceptual drift. Student-facing constraints require controllable length, vocabulary level, and modality. Datasets in education remain small relative to news and scientific domains, making transfer learning common. A survey of general summarization research provides a foundation for educational applications. Key axes include backbone architectures, data resources, and quality assessment traditions. Below we compile representative models, datasets, and metrics from the broader literature. Early neural architectures for document summarization emphasized hierarchical encoders and pointer mechanisms, with representative systems including the pointer-generator network of See et al. (2017), the reinforcement learning objective of Paulus et al. (2018), and the coverage penalty explored by Tu et al. (2016). Abstractive sequence-to-sequence models subsequently incorporated copy and coverage refinements, as in Gehrmann et al. (2018), while extractive formulations adopted neural ranking over sentence candidates using contextual encoders similar to Nallapati et al. (2017) and Narayan et al. (2018). Large-scale pretraining shifted performance trends, with BERT-based summarizers leveraging encoder-only objectives for sentence scoring (Liu, 2019; Zhong et al., 2020) and encoder-decoder T5 or BART variants producing fluent abstracts from news datasets such as CNN/DailyMail and XSum (Raffel et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020). Hybrid approaches further integrated content planning and discourse ordering using entity grids, content selectors, or latent plan decoders, as shown in Puduppully et al. (2019), Narayan et al. (2021), Fan et al. (2019), and Dou et al. (2021). Evaluation practices relied on automatic metrics such as ROUGE, BLEU, and METEOR (Lin, 2004; Papineni et al., 2002; Banerjee and Lavie, 2005), with learned metrics like BERTScore and MoverScore later applied to mitigate lexical mismatch penalties (Zhang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2019). Control mechanisms for length and style have been explored through constrained decoding, prefix tuning, and prompt-based steering (Fan et al., 2018; Keskar et al., 2019; Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021), while faithfulness concerns motivated factuality-oriented training using question-answering critics, entailment objectives, and entity-level constraints (Eyal et al., 2019; Kryscinski et al., 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2021). Multi-document summarization adopted graph-based aggregation and retrieval-augmented generation, combining centrality measures, cluster attention, and evidence reranking across Multi-News, WikiSum, and WCEP (Yasunaga et al., 2017; Liu and Lapata, 2019; Fabbri et al., 2019; Perez-Beltrachini et al., 2019). Cross-lingual and multilingual settings leveraged mBART and mT5 with sentencepiece vocabularies over CC-100 and mC4, utilized bilingual constraints or alignment losses to preserve content across translation, and evaluated on MLSum, XL-Sum, and WikiLingua benchmarks (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; Scialom et al., 2020; Hasan et al., 2021; Ladhak et al., 2020). Recent compression-oriented studies consider knowledge distillation, layer pruning, and quantization-aware training for summarizers on-device, with evaluations on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, and Reddit TIFU reporting latency and memory reductions under tight budgets. Educational settings additionally need difficulty calibration and alignment with learning objectives. Curricular metadata and knowledge graphs can inform selection and ordering of content. Human evaluation should incorporate rubric-based ratings by instructors and learning scientists. Safety requirements span age-appropriate content and amplification of biases in historical materials. On-device or offline operation enables deployment in bandwidth-constrained classrooms. Authoring tools that display provenance and editable rationales may increase teacher trust. Long-term outcomes should consider learning gains and retention, not only text similarity. The remainder details our task setup, resources, and evaluation plan.

document: Multilingual policy summarization supports stakeholders who must navigate legislation and regulations across jurisdictions. Terminological variation and legalese complicate generalization from English-centric corpora. Low-resource languages frequently lack aligned summaries or parallel legal corpora of sufficient quality. Machine translation can propagate ambiguities that distort obligations and exemptions. Stakeholders require faithful, conservative summaries with calibrated uncertainty around statutory interpretation. A review of mainstream approaches clarifies baseline capabilities transferable to public-sector contexts. It also situates training data and evaluation norms that dominate recent publications. We compile representative methods, resources, and metrics commonly cited in the literature. Early neural architectures for document summarization emphasized hierarchical encoders and pointer mechanisms, with representative systems including the pointer-generator network of See et al. (2017), the reinforcement learning objective of Paulus et al. (2018), and the coverage penalty explored by Tu et al. (2016). (2018), while extractive formulations adopted neural ranking over sentence candidates using contextual encoders similar to Nallapati et al. (2017) and Narayan et al. (2018). Recent compression-oriented studies consider knowledge distillation, layer pruning, and quantization-aware training for summarizers on-device, with evaluations on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, and Reddit TIFU reporting latency and memory reductions under tight budgets. Legal applications further emphasize citation fidelity and preservation of modal verbs expressing obligations. Jurisdiction-specific ontologies provide structure that can guide content selection and compression. Human review loops are essential given potential harms from misinterpretation of binding text. Benchmarks would benefit from multilingual, domain-grounded tasks with expert-validated references. Resource constraints in government IT call for models that are compact, auditable, and robust. Transparent reporting of pretraining data sources supports procurement and risk assessment. Continual learning is relevant as statutes evolve and administrative guidance is updated. We next define the problem scope and operational evaluation used in our study.

document: Urban traffic prediction is a core problem for intelligent transportation and mobility planning. Prior methods include statistical baselines and deep sequence models, but spatial correlations challenge simple architectures. Graph neural networks provide a natural inductive bias to capture road topology and dynamic flows. Nevertheless, how to integrate temporal heterogeneity and external signals remains open. We frame the task as multi-step forecasting on sensor graphs under realistic missing data. Early graph-based forecasters adopt diffusion or spectral operators to encode road networks, with DCRNN using diffusion convolution on directed graphs and STGCN applying Chebyshev filters and temporal gating. Graph WaveNet introduces an adaptive adjacency alongside dilated convolutions for long-range dependencies, while ASTGCN integrates spatial and temporal attention within stacked convolutional blocks. Subsequent transformer-flavored architectures such as GMAN, STTN, and ST-Transformer emphasize multi-head attention across nodes and timesteps and often embed positional encodings tied to periodicity. Evaluation commonly relies on the METR-LA and PEMS-BAY sensor datasets, with additional benchmarks including PEMS03, PEMS04, and PEMS08 for California freeways, and TaxiBJ for citywide flows. Training protocols frequently adopt horizon-specific losses, masked MAE for handling missing sensors, and curriculum learning to stabilize rollout length during multi-step prediction. External signals include weather, holidays, and incidents, added via embeddings, concatenation, auxiliary encoders, and temporal markers and calendars. Despite progress, performance drops sharply under distribution shift caused by accidents, construction, or extreme weather. Existing adaptive adjacency schemes often overfit to stationary correlations and ignore causal interventions. Furthermore, error compounding across horizons leads to instability, especially for sparsely observed nodes. We address these challenges by distinguishing structural persistence from episodic disruptions within a probabilistic spatio-temporal model. The approach decouples long-term topology from event-conditioned dynamics through hierarchical latent factors. This design targets calibrated forecasts when sensor coverage is intermittent or when covariates are partially missing. Our learning objective aligns one-step and multi-step risks using a consistency regularizer that preserves uncertainty propagation. We complement standard sensor benchmarks with targeted shift suites that simulate lane closures and rerouting. The evaluation protocol stresses robustness by reporting tail-aware metrics and coverage under conformal predictors. Ablations quantify the contribution of hierarchical factors, covariate gates, and adaptive message passing. We report improvements across horizons with reduced negative log-likelihood and better calibration slopes. Qualitative analyses illustrate resilient behavior during congestion waves and incident clearance phases. Code, data splits, and configuration files will be released to support reproducibility and follow-up research.

document: Recommender systems mediate access to information and opportunities across digital platforms. Concerns about fairness arise when exposure is systematically skewed toward already advantaged users or items. Ranking positions translate into economic value, so disparities can amplify feedback loops. Platforms also contend with selection bias because observed clicks reflect previous ranking policies. These dynamics complicate evaluation and learning from logged data. Prior work proposes algorithmic interventions to mitigate documented harms, yet evidence remains fragmented across tasks and datasets. We review approaches that address bias in both model training and result presentation. Bias in implicit-feedback recommenders has been addressed with inverse propensity scoring, where propensity-weighted estimators correct exposure imbalance in datasets such as MovieLens-1M and Yahoo! R3. Pairwise debiasing methods like CausE and SVD-IPS modify the training objective to discount popularity, while user-stratified negative sampling schemes aim to equilibrate click propensities. Exposure-aware re-ranking approaches, including xQuAD, FA*IR, and DELTR, allocate positions under constraints on demographic parity or minimum exposure for protected item groups. Fairness metrics commonly report exposure disparity, calibration within groups, equal opportunity at fixed recall, and user-level risk differences across Amazon, Yelp, and Last.fm benchmarks. Causal graphical analyses model selection bias with logged bandit feedback, using techniques such as doubly robust estimation, self-normalized IPS, and marginal sensitivity models. Recent datasets, including Bias-in-Rec, Kiva microloans, and BookCrossing demographics, support evaluations that separate provider fairness, consumer fairness, and popularity confounding in offline simulators. Despite the breadth of methods, few studies analyze trade-offs between provider exposure and user relevance under consistent protocols. Moreover, interventions optimized for demographic parity can unintentionally degrade minority consumer utility if calibration is ignored. We formalize these tensions through a bi-criteria objective that internalizes exposure constraints during training. The formulation links counterfactual exposures with risk-sensitive ranking losses using a shared set of sufficient statistics. This perspective clarifies when re-ranking alone suffices and when representation learning must absorb the constraint. Our experiments leverage logged bandit feedback while maintaining unbiased estimators through careful propensity clipping. We adopt a uniform evaluation suite with consistent splits and cross-dataset demographic mappings. Analyses examine Pareto frontiers and fairness regret to quantify policy choices. We release diagnostic tools that report both exposure and welfare decomposition at user and item subgroup levels. Case studies on job recommendations and music discovery illustrate context-specific implications for stakeholders. The framework aims to guide principled deployment by separating normative choices from statistical artifacts.

document: Multimodal machine translation investigates whether visual or acoustic context can resolve linguistic ambiguities in text-only translation. While neural translation models have advanced rapidly, improvements from auxiliary modalities remain inconsistent. A key challenge is aligning heterogeneous representations without drowning the signal in strong language models. Another difficulty lies in evaluation, where standard metrics may ignore visually grounded adequacy. This section summarizes benchmarks and architectures relevant to grounded translation. Prior surveys emphasize the tension between end-to-end learning and feature-level fusion strategies. We group related work by resource type and integration mechanism. We then describe recent findings on robustness to occlusion and domain shift. Our review informs a design that tests modality contributions under controlled ablations. Early multimodal studies used the Multi30K dataset, extending Flickr30K with German and French captions, and explored attention over global CNN features extracted by VGG or ResNet encoders. The WMT16 and WMT17 shared tasks spurred text-plus-image systems such as Imagination, Doubly-Attentive NMT, and Fusion-Conv, alongside retrieval-based rerankers that exploit visual neighborhood similarity. Speech-to-text resources including How2 and MuST-C enabled audio-conditioned translation, where models consume log-mel spectrograms with convolutional front-ends or pre-extracted wav2vec features. Recent transformer baselines integrate visual tokens from Faster R-CNN region proposals or CLIP embeddings, feeding them through cross-modal attention layers or gated fusion modules. Data augmentation techniques include back-translation with synthetic images, masked region modeling, and contrastive alignment that ties source phrases to detected objects. Benchmarks report BLEU and METEOR on Multi30K, Ambiguous Captions, and IKEA assembly instructions, with human assessments targeting pronoun resolution and visually dependent lexical choices. Yet gains from images or audio often vanish when text-only encoders are scaled or pretrained on massive corpora. This pattern suggests a need for protocols that hold language strength constant while varying auxiliary inputs. We therefore evaluate controlled capacity budgets and ablate fusion points across comparable parameter counts. The study also considers annotation noise in object detectors and automatic speech transcripts. We analyze when modality signals act as regularizers versus primary information sources. A causal framing separates associative cues from truly necessary sensory evidence. Experiments quantify improvements on ambiguity-targeted subsets rather than overall averages alone. Error typologies reveal whether benefits concentrate on deixis, gender, or spatial relations. Together these analyses clarify the realistic scope of multimodal assistance in contemporary translation pipelines.

document: Detecting anomalies in multivariate sensor streams is essential for reliable operation of cyber-physical systems. Industrial environments impose latency and false-alarm constraints that strain generic outlier methods. Correlations across channels and nonstationary regimes complicate density estimation. Model misspecification can yield brittle thresholds and costly interventions. Recent deep generative approaches promise richer uncertainty modeling but suffer from distribution drift. This section reviews canonical datasets and architectures for temporal anomaly detection. We focus on benchmarks that reflect realistic feedback and delayed labels. Classical baselines include ARIMA residual monitoring, Hotelling’s T-squared on PCA scores, and seasonal-trend decomposition with robust z-scores. Deep learning methods feature reconstruction and probabilistic objectives, such as LSTM-VAE, Donut, DAGMM, and OmniAnomaly modeling latent dynamics with stochastic recurrent layers. Temporal convolutional detectors like DeepAnt and MSCRED capture short-term motifs and cross-channel signatures through dilated convolutions and attention over multiscale residual maps. Hybrid graph-temporal models, including GDN, MTAD-GAT, and THOC, leverage learned dependency structures to propagate anomaly evidence across sensors. Evaluations commonly rely on NAB, Yahoo S5, SMAP, and MSL, with later testbeds such as SWaT and WADI introducing multistage process faults and strict delay penalties. Scoring protocols report point-level F1, range-aware precision via tolerances, and early-detection area metrics under sliding windows and exclusion zones around injected events, with label noise handled by smoothing heuristics and delayed confirmation periods during evaluation phases. Despite gains on public benchmarks, many models rely on narrow threshold tuning that inflates F1 under fixed windows. Real deployments demand calibrated scores that transfer across sites and maintenance cycles. We adopt a probabilistic framing that separates detectability from alert costs through utility-aware training. The model incorporates temporal point processes to represent event persistence and cooldown. This structure encourages early yet stable alarms under shifting baselines. Our evaluation simulates operator feedback loops and delayed adjudication. We report coverage, time-to-detect, and net utility alongside traditional range-based metrics. Robustness analyses consider missing sensors, clock drift, and correlated disturbances. We also examine the effect of retraining cadence on stability and false positives. Case studies on power plant and water treatment datasets highlight domain-specific trade-offs. These findings underline deployment considerations beyond leaderboard performance.

Temporal convolutional detectors like DeepAnt and MSCRED capture short-term motifs and cross-channel signatures through dilated convolutions and attention over multiscale residual maps. Hybrid graph-temporal models, including GDN, MTAD-GAT, and THOC, leverage learned dependency structures to propagate anomaly evidence across sensors. document: Automated program repair seeks to generate patches that eliminate failures while preserving intended behavior. Traditional search-based repair explores mutation operators guided by test outcomes. Learning-based systems leverage neural models to propose edits conditioned on buggy context. Recently, large language models have amplified interest in data-driven repair at scale. However, correctness beyond unit tests and overfitting remain central obstacles. Datasets and benchmarks shape both methodology and claims in this area. We review corpora, models, and evaluation protocols that inform current practice. We then discuss gaps in reliability measurement under realistic developer workflows. Benchmark suites such as Defects4J, Bugs.jar, QuixBugs, and IntroClass provide failing tests and reference fixes across Java, Python, and C programs. Classical baselines include GenProg, RSRepair, and Nopol, which search patch spaces through genetic operators, random restarts, and constraint solving. Neural techniques such as SequenceR, CoCoNuT, and Tufano et al.’s Transformer model edits code by learning copy mechanisms, subtokenization, and context windows from large repositories. Recent approaches like TBar, PraPR, and AlphaRepair integrate template-based fix patterns, bytecode-level mutations, and retrieval-augmented prompts for patch generation. Evaluation typically measures test-suite adequacy, plausibility versus correctness labels via manual inspection, and generalization to unseen projects under cross-project splits. Complementary resources include ManySStuBs4J for small bug patterns, CodeRepairBench for unified pipelines, and Defects4J-Additions that expand tasks with flakiness annotations and multi-hunk changes alongside patch minimization and independent test replication for stronger claims of semantic preservation robustly. Despite rich tooling, overfitting to weak tests remains pervasive across datasets. Plausible but incorrect patches can satisfy unit suites while regressing hidden behaviors. We argue for counterfactual checks that validate behavioral equivalence beyond oracle leakage. Our protocol separates generation, validation, and confirmation via disjoint infrastructure. It couples patch scoring with dynamic slicing to measure affected footprints. We integrate language-model priors with repair templates through constrained decoding. The approach curbs degenerate insertions and spurious deletions without sacrificing diversity. Experiments evaluate transfer across projects and compiler toolchains. Human studies assess review burden and explainability of suggested edits. Results indicate safer generalization and lower reversion rates in continuous integration.
spans: Benchmark suites such as Defects4J, Bugs.jar, QuixBugs, and IntroClass provide failing tests and reference fixes across Java, Python, and C programs. Classical baselines include GenProg, RSRepair, and Nopol, which search patch spaces through genetic operators, random restarts, and constraint solving. Neural techniques such as SequenceR, CoCoNuT, and Tufano et al.’s Transformer model edits code by learning copy mechanisms, subtokenization, and context windows from large repositories. Recent approaches like TBar, PraPR, and AlphaRepair integrate template-based fix patterns, bytecode-level mutations, and retrieval-augmented prompts for patch generation. Evaluation typically measures test-suite adequacy, plausibility versus correctness labels via manual inspection, and generalization to unseen projects under cross-project splits. Complementary resources include ManySStuBs4J for small bug patterns, CodeRepairBench for unified pipelines, and Defects4J-Additions that expand tasks with flakiness annotations and multi-hunk changes alongside patch minimization and independent test replication for stronger claims of semantic preservation robustly.

document: Multimodal summarization targets concise digests from synchronized text, audio, and vision streams in large collections. Despite rapid progress in text summarization, handling cross-modal cues remains challenging in noisy conditions. Constraints on temporality, grounding, and redundancy differ across modalities and require tailored modeling. Prior work has explored alignment of speech segments to captions and shot boundaries to captions. Yet a systematic assessment of cross-modal evidence selection under domain shift is still limited. Early extractive approaches model content selection using graph ranking (Erkan and Radev, 2004) and maximal marginal relevance (Carbonell and Goldstein, 1998), while neural encoder-decoder models leverage attention (Rush et al., 2015; See et al., 2017). Multimodal extensions incorporate features from ResNet (He et al., 2016), detectors such as Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015), and datasets like TVSum (Song et al., 2015), SumMe (Gygli et al., 2014), and Newsroom (Grusky et al., 2018), with pretrained transformers including BART (Lewis et al., 2020) and T5 (Raffel et al., 2020). However, existing evaluations often emphasize single-domain news or instructional content and overlook conversational variability. We define a benchmark that stresses robustness by varying acoustic fidelity, camera motion, and discourse cohesion. Our approach separates selection from fusion by training a retrieval module before an abstractive generator. The design enables controllable trade-offs between coverage and fluency using lightweight knobs at inference. We additionally analyze failure cases to reveal modality-specific hallucinations and alignment drifts. Experiments compare constrained decoding with evidence dropout to estimate calibration under distribution shifts. Human studies measure factuality, focus, and cross-modal grounding using fine-grained rubrics. Together these results indicate that robust multimodal summarization benefits from explicit evidence selection and targeted evaluation.
spans: Early extractive approaches model content selection using graph ranking (Erkan and Radev, 2004) and maximal marginal relevance (Carbonell and Goldstein, 1998), while neural encoder-decoder models leverage attention (Rush et al., 2015; See et al., 2017). Multimodal extensions incorporate features from ResNet (He et al., 2016), detectors such as Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015), and datasets like TVSum (Song et al., 2015), SumMe (Gygli et al., 2014), and Newsroom (Grusky et al., 2018), with pretrained transformers including BART (Lewis et al., 2020) and T5 (Raffel et al., 2020).

document: Short-term traffic forecasting is essential for routing, congestion mitigation, and safety-critical response. Road networks exhibit strong spatial dependencies and heterogeneous temporal dynamics that motivate graph-based models. Yet deployments must cope with missing sensors, atypical events, and shifting travel demand across cities. Robust forecasting therefore requires architectures that generalize beyond fixed periodicity and narrow calibration windows. Existing comparisons often conflate model capacity with horizon-specific optimization and ignore structural uncertainty. We revisit generalization by decoupling spatiotemporal encoding from horizon-wise losses and by evaluating across unseen networks. Forecasting with spatial temporal graphs employs diffusion convolution and sequence models, exemplified by DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and MTGNN (Wu et al., 2020). Common benchmarks include METR-LA and PEMS-BAY (Li et al., 2018), as well as PEMS04 and PEMS08 (Zheng et al., 2020), with variants exploring temporal convolutions (Bai et al., 2018), attention based transformers such as Informer (Zhou et al., 2021) and Autoformer (Wu et al., 2021), and spatio temporal regularization through graph learning modules. Nevertheless, most pipelines still assume a static adjacency or rely on time-of-day encodings that fail under incidents. We study regime shifts created by weather, sporting events, and road closures to test adaptive reasoning. Our model couples a learned graph prior with a probabilistic state-space forecaster for multi-horizon predictions. Training uses multi-objective criteria that balance point accuracy, calibration, and change-point detection. We derive diagnostics for spillover effects that identify when topological errors dominate temporal noise. Cross-city splits and sensor cold-start experiments expose the brittleness of common design choices. The findings highlight where structural learning contributes to resilience without incurring excessive latency.
spans: Forecasting with spatial temporal graphs employs diffusion convolution and sequence models, exemplified by DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and MTGNN (Wu et al., 2020). Common benchmarks include METR-LA and PEMS-BAY (Li et al., 2018), as well as PEMS04 and PEMS08 (Zheng et al., 2020), with variants exploring temporal convolutions (Bai et al., 2018), attention based transformers such as Informer (Zhou et al., 2021) and Autoformer (Wu et al., 2021), and spatio temporal regularization through graph learning modules.

document: Ensuring fairness in clinical natural language processing is critical because model decisions can influence downstream care. Demographic and socioeconomic attributes often correlate with documentation practices, label availability, and health outcomes. These correlations can introduce spurious shortcuts that degrade performance for underrepresented patients and institutions. Auditing methods must therefore assess subgroup disparities alongside overall accuracy and calibration. Clinical language processing leverages electronic health records corpora such as MIMIC-III (Johnson et al., 2016) and i2b2 challenges (Uzuner et al., 2008), alongside task datasets like MedNLI (Romanov and Shivade, 2018) and CheXpert labels (Irvin et al., 2019). Pretrained encoders adapted to the biomedical domain include BioBERT (Lee et al., 2020), ClinicalBERT (Alsentzer et al., 2019), BlueBERT (Peng et al., 2019), and PubMedBERT (Gu et al., 2021), with evaluation protocols drawing on deidentification, concept extraction, relation extraction, and natural language inference benchmarks and mortality prediction for clinical narratives. Despite these resources, subgroup reliability remains poorly characterized due to label noise and site-specific charting styles. We formalize fairness as equalized calibration while allowing cost-sensitive operational thresholds across clinical pathways. Our estimator learns instance weights through counterfactual augmentation that balances observable covariates within strata. We release an auditing toolkit that reports bias metrics with uncertainty intervals and subgroup shift indicators. The empirical study spans intensive care, emergency department, and radiology note collections from multiple hospitals. We provide stratified evaluations by age, sex, and race proxies derived from structured metadata. Results reveal trade-offs between calibration parity and sensitivity that depend on triage prevalence and label delay. We discuss deployment safeguards including monitoring triggers and fallback policies for high-risk contexts. An ethics statement details governance, data protections, and limitations of the observational setting.
spans: Clinical language processing leverages electronic health records corpora such as MIMIC-III (Johnson et al., 2016) and i2b2 challenges (Uzuner et al., 2008), alongside task datasets like MedNLI (Romanov and Shivade, 2018) and CheXpert labels (Irvin et al., 2019). Pretrained encoders adapted to the biomedical domain include BioBERT (Lee et al., 2020), ClinicalBERT (Alsentzer et al., 2019), BlueBERT (Peng et al., 2019), and PubMedBERT (Gu et al., 2021), with evaluation protocols drawing on deidentification, concept extraction, relation extraction, and natural language inference benchmarks and mortality prediction for clinical narratives.

document: Neural machine translation excels with abundant parallel text but falters when supervision is scarce or noisy. In low-resource scenarios, practitioners must amplify signal from monolingual data and related languages. However, the interaction between augmentation quality and multilingual transfer is still not well understood. Typical evaluations emphasize single language families and mask fragility under domain shift and script variation. This paper examines curriculum design, noise shaping, and calibration to clarify trade-offs in low-resource pipelines. We analyze morphologically rich and distant language pairs to expose where transfer helps and where it hinders. Our study also considers lexicon entropy and morphology-aware tokenization to tune generation under limited data. Low resource translation has adopted data augmentation techniques such as back translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), self training (He et al., 2020), and dual learning with reinforcement signals (He et al., 2016), often combined with multilingual pretraining. Representative encoders and corpora include mBART (Liu et al., 2020), XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), multilingual BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), WMT shared tasks, TED Talks (Qi et al., 2018), and FLORES-101 (Goyal et al., 2021), alongside lexical constraints and vocabulary sharing strategies for cross lingual transfer in extremely small datasets. Yet a consolidated view of noise control across sampling, filtering, and objective design remains missing. We propose a quality-aware curriculum that schedules pseudo-parallel data by uncertainty and coverage. The approach integrates target-side perplexity, contrastive alignment, and bilingual lexicon confidence into selection. Experiments across twelve language pairs report accuracy, calibration, and translation churn under domain shifts. Human evaluation quantifies adequacy and fluency while probing literalness and over-copying failures. We release code, models, and standardized splits to support rigorous, reproducible comparisons.
spans: Low resource translation has adopted data augmentation techniques such as back translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), self training (He et al., 2020), and dual learning with reinforcement signals (He et al., 2016), often combined with multilingual pretraining. Representative encoders and corpora include mBART (Liu et al., 2020), XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), multilingual BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), WMT shared tasks, TED Talks (Qi et al., 2018), and FLORES-101 (Goyal et al., 2021), alongside lexical constraints and vocabulary sharing strategies for cross lingual transfer in extremely small datasets.

document: Converting natural language specifications into executable programs has matured quickly with advances in pretrained code models. Practical adoption nonetheless requires faithful execution, interpretable constraints, and robust generalization to unseen tasks. Benchmarks now test both synthesis from signatures and repair of buggy code under limited feedback. At the same time, prompt design, retrieval, and constrained decoding have become central levers for deployment. We focus on verifiable text to code generation with minimal unit tests and partial type annotations. Our study disentangles the roles of retrieval, constraint learning, and execution feedback across diverse tasks. We also analyze evaluation instability stemming from nondeterminism, test leakage, and multiple correct solutions. To situate our approach, we summarize prominent corpora and modeling directions in recent work. Research on text to code has examined semantic parsing and program synthesis across benchmarks including Django (Oda et al., 2015), CoNaLa (Yin et al., 2018), MBPP (Austin et al., 2021), HumanEval (Chen et al., 2021), and APPS (Hendrycks et al., 2021). Modeling approaches span sequence to sequence learners like PLBART (Ahmad et al., 2021) and CodeT5 (Wang et al., 2021), constrained decoding with grammars, retrieval augmented generation over code corpora, and large language models fine tuned on repositories such as GitHub using masked span objectives and causal continuation. Despite strong aggregate scores, many systems overfit to surface forms and brittle prompt patterns. We present a modular framework that pairs retrieval with type-guided decoding and counterexample-driven repair. The framework supports plug-in verifiers and unit test synthesis to tighten the execution loop. Our experiments evaluate compositional generalization, perturbation robustness, and test-time adaptation across languages. Findings indicate efficiency gains and fewer silent failures under constrained resources.
spans: Research on text to code has examined semantic parsing and program synthesis across benchmarks including Django (Oda et al., 2015), CoNaLa (Yin et al., 2018), MBPP (Austin et al., 2021), HumanEval (Chen et al., 2021), and APPS (Hendrycks et al., 2021). Modeling approaches span sequence to sequence learners like PLBART (Ahmad et al., 2021) and CodeT5 (Wang et al., 2021), constrained decoding with grammars, retrieval augmented generation over code corpora, and large language models fine tuned on repositories such as GitHub using masked span objectives and causal continuation.

document: Urban traffic forecasting underpins mobility planning, congestion mitigation, and real-time routing. Despite notable progress in spatiotemporal modeling, robustness under distribution shift remains limited. In practice, incidents, weather, and special events perturb demand patterns in ways that static models rarely anticipate. Moreover, sensor outages and topology changes introduce structural drift that complicates long-horizon prediction. We investigate how to represent exogenous disruptions while preserving graph inductive biases. Our goal is to learn transferable dynamics that adapt quickly to atypical conditions without extensive retraining. Prior studies on traffic forecasting span classical time-series filters, convolutional models, and graph learning approaches. ARIMA and VAR baselines are standard (Smith and Brown, 2003; Box and Jenkins, 2015). Early deep models include ST-ResNet and DeepST for grid-based city data (Zhang et al., 2017; Zheng et al., 2016). Graph-based methods such as DCRNN, STGCN, ASTGCN, and Graph WaveNet capture road network dependencies using diffusion processes, spectral filters, attention, or dilated temporal convolutions (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2019). Public datasets widely used for evaluation include METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03/04/07/08, and TaxiBJ (Jagdish et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2017). Evaluation typically reports MAE, RMSE, and MAPE across standard horizons. These advances motivate a formulation that unifies graph diffusion with event-driven context adaptation. We operationalize this idea through a modular architecture that conditions temporal kernels on learned exogenous codes. The approach incorporates uncertainty by aligning probabilistic forecasts with decision-oriented loss functions. To assess generalization under shift, we design perturbation regimes that simulate closures, storms, and demand spikes. Our empirical study quantifies resilience using counterfactual rollouts and recovery-time metrics beyond standard errors. The results highlight when and how context-aware graph models mitigate degradation while preserving efficiency.
spans: Prior studies on traffic forecasting span classical time-series filters, convolutional models, and graph learning approaches. ARIMA and VAR baselines are standard (Smith and Brown, 2003; Box and Jenkins, 2015). Early deep models include ST-ResNet and DeepST for grid-based city data (Zhang et al., 2017; Zheng et al., 2016). Graph-based methods such as DCRNN, STGCN, ASTGCN, and Graph WaveNet capture road network dependencies using diffusion processes, spectral filters, attention, or dilated temporal convolutions (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2019). Public datasets widely used for evaluation include METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03/04/07/08, and TaxiBJ (Jagdish et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2017). Evaluation typically reports MAE, RMSE, and MAPE across standard horizons.

document: Clinical named entity recognition is essential for structuring information from free-text notes and guidelines. However, privacy constraints and domain mismatch limit the utility of generic annotations. Models trained on newswire often overfit stylistic cues that do not transfer to clinical phrasing. Additionally, label scarcity and abbreviation drift hinder robust boundary detection. We study representation transfer that respects clinical terminology while maintaining generalization. Our approach views adaptation as a balance between lexical memorization and consistency under perturbations. We frame evaluation to reflect realistic deployment conditions with limited supervision. Named entity recognition has been studied across general and clinical domains with pretraining regimes and models. CRF and BiLSTM-CRF architectures remain standard baselines for span tagging (Lafferty et al., 2001; Huang et al., 2015). Contextual encoders such as ELMo, BERT, ClinicalBERT, and BioBERT provide strong lexical and syntactic signals for entities (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Alsentzer et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020). Common benchmarks include CoNLL03 for news, OntoNotes for mixed genres, BC5CDR and NCBI-Disease for biomedical text, and the i2b2 2010/2012 shared tasks for clinical notes (Sang and De Meulder, 2003; Pradhan et al., 2013; Li et al., 2016; Uzuner et al., 2011). Adaptation techniques leverage adversarial training, self-training, vocabulary alignment, and pseudo-labeling across unlabeled corpora. Building on these observations, we design a cross-domain training objective that decouples entity semantics from surface form. A constrained vocabulary interface mediates updates to reduce catastrophic forgetting. Beyond token-level F1, we quantify calibration and shift-robustness under controlled domain gaps. We further analyze abbreviation expansion and ontology linking as auxiliary tasks. An ablation study isolates the effects of representation tuning, data selection, and regularization. The resulting insights inform practical recipes for adapting NER systems in clinical settings.
spans: Named entity recognition has been studied across general and clinical domains with pretraining regimes and models. CRF and BiLSTM-CRF architectures remain standard baselines for span tagging (Lafferty et al., 2001; Huang et al., 2015). Contextual encoders such as ELMo, BERT, ClinicalBERT, and BioBERT provide strong lexical and syntactic signals for entities (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Alsentzer et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020). Common benchmarks include CoNLL03 for news, OntoNotes for mixed genres, BC5CDR and NCBI-Disease for biomedical text, and the i2b2 2010/2012 shared tasks for clinical notes (Sang and De Meulder, 2003; Pradhan et al., 2013; Li et al., 2016; Uzuner et al., 2011). Adaptation techniques leverage adversarial training, self-training, vocabulary alignment, and pseudo-labeling across unlabeled corpora.

document: Personalization at scale requires models that leverage scarce interactions without overfitting spurious regularities. Multimodal content is abundant but heterogeneously informative across users and items. Balancing collaborative signals with content requires careful inductive biases and training curricula. Moreover, exposure confounding and popularity skew complicate both optimization and evaluation. We study how modality-specific representations contribute to ranking gains under distribution shift. Our perspective is to disentangle user intent from item affordances via causal regularization. We also examine data efficiency when only a subset of items have rich modalities. Recommender systems ingest multimodal signals to capture item semantics and user intent. Matrix factorization and Bayesian personalized ranking constitute collaborative filtering baselines (Koren et al., 2009; Rendle et al., 2009). Visual and acoustic features from images and trailers are exploited by VBPR, DeepStyle, and MMGCN to enrich sparse interactions via joint embeddings or graph propagation (He and McAuley, 2016; Liu et al., 2017; Wei et al., 2019). Knowledge-aware models integrate side graphs using TransE-style link predictors and meta-path guided attention (Bordes et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2019). Public benchmarks include MovieLens, Amazon Product Data, Ali-CCP, and Pinterest with leave-one-out protocols (Harper and Konstan, 2015; McAuley et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2020; Geng et al., 2015). Common metrics comprise HR@K, NDCG@K. These observations call for architectures that adaptively weight modalities per-query while respecting graph structure. We instantiate this with a multi-view encoder and a slate-aware loss that corrects sampling bias. We derive training objectives that align counterfactual estimators with practical negative sampling. A suite of interventions probes robustness to missing or corrupted modalities. The analysis clarifies where multimodal signals help and when they can safely be ignored.
spans: Recommender systems ingest multimodal signals to capture item semantics and user intent. Matrix factorization and Bayesian personalized ranking constitute collaborative filtering baselines (Koren et al., 2009; Rendle et al., 2009). Visual and acoustic features from images and trailers are exploited by VBPR, DeepStyle, and MMGCN to enrich sparse interactions via joint embeddings or graph propagation (He and McAuley, 2016; Liu et al., 2017; Wei et al., 2019). Knowledge-aware models integrate side graphs using TransE-style link predictors and meta-path guided attention (Bordes et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2019). Public benchmarks include MovieLens, Amazon Product Data, Ali-CCP, and Pinterest with leave-one-out protocols (Harper and Konstan, 2015; McAuley et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2020; Geng et al., 2015). Common metrics comprise HR@K, NDCG@K.

document: Robust speech recognition in everyday environments remains a central goal for interactive systems. Background noise, reverberation, and device mismatch degrade both acoustic modeling and decoding. While data augmentation helps, its gains vary widely across domains and utterance lengths. Moreover, on-device constraints push models to trade accuracy for latency and memory. We focus on the interplay between self-supervised features and noise-aware training objectives. Our design targets stability under unseen noise types without sacrificing streaming constraints. To that end, we analyze how feature normalization and curriculum schedules affect convergence. Automatic speech recognition systems have progressed through hybrid, end-to-end, and self-supervised paradigms with noise robustness. HMM-DNN hybrids with fMLLR and i-vector adaptation remain on corpora (Gales, 1998; Saon et al., 2013). End-to-end CTC, attention encoder-decoder, and RNN-T architectures reduce decoding complexity leveraging units and objectives (Graves et al., 2006; Chan et al., 2016; He et al., 2019). Self-supervised front-ends such as wav2vec 2.0, HuBERT, and W2v-BERT improve performance (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Chung et al., 2021). Robustness techniques include beamforming, SpecAugment, and dereverberation (Vesperini et al., 2016; Park et al., 2019). Widely used datasets include LibriSpeech, WSJ, CHiME-4/6, and Aurora-4 with splits and tools (Panayotov et al., 2015; Lippmann, 1997; Vincent et al., 2017; Pearce and Hirsch, 2000). The resulting insights motivate an architecture that couples conformer encoders with adaptive denoising modules. A dual-path loss balances token alignment with segment-level consistency under perturbations. We evaluate streaming and offline variants to examine latency-accuracy trade-offs. Beyond word error rate, we report robustness profiles stratified by SNR and device class. This yields actionable guidance for deploying speech models in uncontrolled acoustic conditions.
spans: Automatic speech recognition systems have progressed through hybrid, end-to-end, and self-supervised paradigms with noise robustness. HMM-DNN hybrids with fMLLR and i-vector adaptation remain on corpora (Gales, 1998; Saon et al., 2013). End-to-end CTC, attention encoder-decoder, and RNN-T architectures reduce decoding complexity leveraging units and objectives (Graves et al., 2006; Chan et al., 2016; He et al., 2019). Self-supervised front-ends such as wav2vec 2.0, HuBERT, and W2v-BERT improve performance (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Chung et al., 2021). Robustness techniques include beamforming, SpecAugment, and dereverberation (Vesperini et al., 2016; Park et al., 2019). Widely used datasets include LibriSpeech, WSJ, CHiME-4/6, and Aurora-4 with splits and tools (Panayotov et al., 2015; Lippmann, 1997; Vincent et al., 2017; Pearce and Hirsch, 2000).

document: Online experiments increasingly operate on dense social graphs where spillovers are unavoidable. Standard estimators that assume isolation can be biased even when randomization is used. Product rollouts often combine user notifications, content feeds, and ad auctions that propagate exposure. Decision makers need estimates that separate direct effects from network-mediated effects. We study experimental designs and estimators that remain interpretable under realistic interference. Our perspective emphasizes transparency of estimands and the costs of simplifying assumptions. We also prioritize practicality for large-scale industrial deployments. Estimation of treatment effects under interference builds on foundations in causal inference and experimental design. The potential outcomes framework and SUTVA provide baselines without spillovers (Neyman, 1923; Rubin, 1974). Network and cluster interference have been modeled via exposure mappings, partial interference, and two-stage randomization (Aronow and Samii, 2017; Hudgens and Halloran, 2008; Tchetgen Tchetgen and VanderWeele, 2012). Randomization-based estimators, inverse probability weighting, and augmented estimators extend to graph settings with uncertainty quantification (Rosenbaum, 2007; Aronow et al., 2013). Observational strategies leverage propensity scores on networks, synthetic controls, and difference-in-differences with interference adjustments (Imbens and Rubin, 2015; Abadie et al., 2010; Forastiere et al., 2021). Common applications include vaccination campaigns, marketplaces, and information diffusion studies with metrics defined over ego networks and clusters. Against this backdrop, we formalize estimands that decompose exposure through predefined neighborhoods. We implement assignment mechanisms compatible with platform constraints and derive unbiased estimators. Scalable variance estimators enable sequential monitoring without inflating false positives. Simulations on replay logs complement field tests to assess sensitivity to graph misspecification. The framework clarifies trade-offs between precision, runtime, and policy relevance.
spans: Estimation of treatment effects under interference builds on foundations in causal inference and experimental design. The potential outcomes framework and SUTVA provide baselines without spillovers (Neyman, 1923; Rubin, 1974). Network and cluster interference have been modeled via exposure mappings, partial interference, and two-stage randomization (Aronow and Samii, 2017; Hudgens and Halloran, 2008; Tchetgen Tchetgen and VanderWeele, 2012). Randomization-based estimators, inverse probability weighting, and augmented estimators extend to graph settings with uncertainty quantification (Rosenbaum, 2007; Aronow et al., 2013). Observational strategies leverage propensity scores on networks, synthetic controls, and difference-in-differences with interference adjustments (Imbens and Rubin, 2015; Abadie et al., 2010; Forastiere et al., 2021). Common applications include vaccination campaigns, marketplaces, and information diffusion studies with metrics defined over ego networks and clusters.

document: Traffic forecasting underpins intelligent transportation and urban mobility planning. Accurate predictions help reduce congestion, emissions, and travel time variability. Recent advances in graph neural networks have improved spatiotemporal modeling capacities. Yet the interplay between road topology and demand heterogeneity remains difficult to capture. Classical statistical models rely on stationarity assumptions that rarely hold in practice. Pure sequence models overlook structural correlations among sensors and links. Hybrid approaches attempt to balance temporal recurrence with graph convolutions. Evaluation protocols vary widely across regions and data resolutions. Public datasets differ in sampling rates, sensor coverage, and missing data patterns. Forecasting horizons impose distinct biases on model design and training objectives. Robustness to distribution shifts is especially important during atypical events. Weather, incidents, and policy changes can all perturb baseline traffic dynamics. Transferability across cities also poses unresolved challenges for practitioners. Many works introduce adaptive adjacency or attention to encode dynamic connectivity. Others emphasize physics-inspired constraints to stabilize long-horizon predictions. Data augmentation and imputation strategies mitigate sparsity but may amplify bias. Benchmarks still lack standardized protocols for cross-city generalization. Realistic ablations should reflect lane closures, sensor outages, and seasonal shifts. We revisit modeling choices with a focus on controllable inductive biases. We also examine how uncertainty quantification interacts with routing objectives. We consolidate observations into practical recommendations for deployment. We compare models under consistent preprocessing and common training budgets. We analyze error decomposition to distinguish topology from demand contributions. We stress transparent reporting of evaluation metrics and hyperparameters. Despite reported gains, comparative fairness remains unclear across these settings. Prior studies frequently evaluate on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, PEMS08, and TaxiBJ, using architectures such as DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, MTGNN, GMAN, STSGCN, and ASTGCN, with implementations following standard published benchmarks and splits. Such diversity complicates claims about universal superiority of any single method. Our study isolates topology priors under controlled transfer protocols. We quantify benefits of dynamic adjacency relative to fixed graphs. We integrate exogenous features through calibrated temporal encoders. We release code, preprocessing scripts, and reproducible configurations. Ethical considerations include potential misuse in surveillance and inequitable routing. Limitations involve constrained sensor availability and unmodeled behavioral responses. We outline opportunities for integrating simulation and causal inference in future work.
spans: Prior studies frequently evaluate on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, PEMS08, and TaxiBJ, using architectures such as DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, MTGNN, GMAN, STSGCN, and ASTGCN, with implementations following standard published benchmarks and splits.

document: Multimodal medical image segmentation is critical for diagnosis and treatment planning. Combining MRI sequences or CT with PET can capture complementary tissue characteristics. However, heterogeneity in acquisition protocols hampers model generalization across sites. Annotation scarcity further limits scalability in rare pathologies and longitudinal cohorts. Self-supervision and domain adaptation have emerged as promising directions. Still, questions remain about reliability under distribution shifts and missing modalities. Architectural choices affect both sample efficiency and robustness to noise. Training pipelines must reconcile intensity normalization and anatomical priors. Evaluation standards vary widely across organs, modalities, and centers. Prominent datasets and baselines include BraTS, ISLES, MSD, and KiTS, with models such as U-Net, nnU-Net, DeepLabv3+, and SegFormer, plus MONAI augmentations, evaluated via Dice, Hausdorff distance, metrics, and stratified cross-validation protocols. Such diversity complicates fair comparison and clear attribution of performance gains. Prior work shows that data curation can rival architectural innovations in impact. Cross-site harmonization is essential when label noise correlates with scanner vendors. Modality dropout strategies seek to stabilize inference when sequences are unavailable. Attention mechanisms help the model balance global context with fine boundaries. Calibrated uncertainty is valuable for triaging manual review in clinical workflows. Test-time augmentation often improves stability but can inflate computation. Mixed precision accelerates training while preserving numerical stability on modern GPUs. We investigate how modality-aware normalization influences boundary adherence. We assess whether curriculum schedules mitigate optimization instabilities. We explore segmentation consistency under controlled modality ablations. We report calibration, robustness, and fairness metrics alongside Dice and Hausdorff. We analyze failure modes using uncertainty clusters and anatomy-aware stratification. We seek clinically interpretable error patterns for actionable model improvements. We unify preprocessing to minimize confounding in cross-dataset comparisons. We benchmark parameter counts against throughput to assess efficiency tradeoffs. We quantify sensitivity to annotation sparsity using progressive mask thinning. We study effects of scanner drift through time-aware evaluation splits. We release scripts enabling reproducible cross-site experiments and audits. Our ablations cover normalization, augmentation, and loss functions comprehensively. We discuss governance for dataset usage and patient privacy constraints. Limitations include access to only a subset of modalities in some cohorts. We summarize implications for deployment in resource-constrained clinical settings. Finally, we outline promising avenues for federated learning and causal data augmentation.
spans: Prominent datasets and baselines include BraTS, ISLES, MSD, and KiTS, with models such as U-Net, nnU-Net, DeepLabv3+, and SegFormer, plus MONAI augmentations, evaluated via Dice, Hausdorff distance, metrics, and stratified cross-validation protocols.

document: Reasoning over text with external knowledge remains a central challenge in NLP. Many tasks require integrating distributed evidence from documents and graphs. Latent connections often span multiple hops and modalities. Heuristics for retrieval can mislead downstream inference under spurious cues. Symbolic systems offer transparency but struggle with coverage. Neural systems scale well but can hallucinate unsupported facts. Hybrid approaches aim to achieve both breadth and rigor. Evaluation is complicated by annotation ambiguity and weak supervision. Existing datasets vary in scope, noise, and reasoning depth. Robust systems must balance precision with recall during evidence collection. Calibration of confidence is crucial for safe human oversight. Query reformulation can reduce retrieval brittleness across corpora. Graph construction choices strongly influence final answer quality. Explanations should align with the actual computation performed by the model. Causal analysis helps disentangle shortcuts from genuine reasoning competence. Reproducibility requires careful control over negative sampling and filtering. Common benchmarks and resources include FB15k-237, WN18RR, ConceptNet, ATOMIC, HotpotQA, WikiHop, OpenBookQA, and ARC, alongside methods like TransE, RotatE, ComplEx, and Graph Attention Networks used for multi-hop retrieval and inference tasks broadly. Simple accuracy obscures tradeoffs between faithfulness and coverage. Human studies highlight needs for verifiable, minimal explanations. Provenance tracking enables auditing of supporting evidence. We study retrieval policies conditioned on graph and text uncertainty. We compare symbolic constraints with differentiable relaxation schemes. We analyze error propagation between retrieval and reasoning modules. We incorporate abstention to handle insufficient evidence gracefully. We evaluate calibration using expected calibration error and risk coverage. We test robustness under adversarial distractors and counterfactual perturbations. We release standardized scripts and fixed seeds for replicability. We report compute budgets and dataset filters transparently. We ablate graph construction, negative sampling, and training curricula. We quantify gains from multi-hop query planning under budget constraints. Limitations include incomplete coverage of commonsense relations in graphs. Ethical considerations involve bias propagation through curated knowledge bases. We discuss implications for decision support and educational tools. Future work explores interactive retrieval with human feedback loops.
spans: Common benchmarks and resources include FB15k-237, WN18RR, ConceptNet, ATOMIC, HotpotQA, WikiHop, OpenBookQA, and ARC, alongside methods like TransE, RotatE, ComplEx, and Graph Attention Networks used for multi-hop retrieval and inference tasks broadly.

document: Edge deployment of deep models is constrained by latency, memory, and energy budgets. Battery-powered devices especially demand careful compute and bandwidth allocation. Accuracy alone is insufficient when models must meet real-time deadlines. Compression methods can reduce costs but may degrade robustness. Hardware heterogeneity further complicates algorithm design and portability. Toolchains vary widely in operator support and calibration fidelity. Kernel fusion and layout transformations significantly affect throughput. Workload characterization enables principled optimization across layers and tensors. Mixed-precision arithmetic trades numerical stability for speed and efficiency. Quantization schemes differ in granularity, calibration, and activation clipping. Pruning introduces sparsity patterns that interact with hardware schedulers. Distillation can transfer performance while shrinking models. Architecture search explores width, depth, and connectivity under constraints. Co-design aligns model structure with accelerator capabilities. Thermal throttling and memory bandwidth often dominate practical speedups. Batch size limitations require careful pipeline design for streaming tasks. Online adaptation may violate determinism required by safety standards. Evaluation should encompass accuracy, latency, power, and thermal headroom. Cross-hardware validation prevents overfitting to a single device. On-device training introduces new challenges for stability and privacy. Dataset selection influences repeatability and ecological validity of results. Reporting must include firmware versions and kernel configuration details. Representative approaches include quantization-aware training, post-training quantization, pruning, neural architecture search, and distillation, with frameworks such as TensorRT, TVM, OpenVINO, and TFLite, evaluated on ImageNet, CIFAR-10, and MLPerf Tiny benchmarking and suites. Despite many techniques, consistent measurement methodologies remain scarce. We examine portability across microcontrollers, NPUs, and mobile GPUs. We analyze accuracy efficiency frontiers under shared power budgets. We propose a profiling protocol that decouples model and runtime effects. We calibrate quantizers using percentile and entropy criteria systematically. We characterize sparsity patterns relevant to structured acceleration. We report energy per inference and latency distributions with confidence intervals. We examine privacy tradeoffs in federated on-device personalization. Limitations include restricted access to proprietary kernels on some platforms. We release scripts, logs, and board files to facilitate replication. Future work considers co-optimization with sensing and communication stacks.
spans: Representative approaches include quantization-aware training, post-training quantization, pruning, neural architecture search, and distillation, with frameworks such as TensorRT, TVM, OpenVINO, and TFLite, evaluated on ImageNet, CIFAR-10, and MLPerf Tiny benchmarking and suites.

document: Recommender systems influence exposure, opportunity, and cultural consumption at scale. Fairness concerns arise when algorithms systematically advantage certain users or items. Bias can originate from historical data, model inductive biases, or feedback loops. Popularity amplification often crowds out niche creators and minority viewpoints. Measurement remains challenging due to entangled utility and exposure goals. Stakeholders hold divergent notions of fairness across contexts and regulations. System designers must navigate legal requirements alongside business objectives. Transparency into data preprocessing is crucial for reproducible fairness claims. Exposure-aware objectives attempt to balance accuracy with equitable visibility. Reweighting and regularization can mitigate disparate impacts across groups. Counterfactual evaluation probes what would have happened under alternative policies. Causal inference clarifies confounding between preference and position bias. User studies reveal perception gaps between calibrated relevance and satisfaction. Auditing pipelines need to account for exploration in bandit settings. Feedback collection strategies directly influence long-term diversity outcomes. Privacy constraints limit access to sensitive attributes needed for assessment. Proxy groups can introduce new biases if not validated carefully. Post-processing adjustments allow operators to enforce exposure constraints. Multi-objective optimization frames fairness as tradeoffs with utility and risk. Longitudinal evaluation is necessary to detect delayed harms and benefits. Simulators enable safe pre-deployment testing of ranking interventions. Industry deployments require scalable and explainable compliance monitoring. We examine representation and exposure fairness under realistic traffic models. We analyze stability of disparate impact under exploration rates. We estimate group-wise calibration and cost of constraints on utility. We integrate counterfactual logging with exposure metrics for principled audits. We curate public benchmarks with clear splits for fair comparison. Common datasets and baselines include MovieLens 1M, MovieLens 20M, LastFM, Amazon Reviews, and RecSys Challenge data, alongside MF, NCF, LightGCN, and BPR, with metrics such as NDCG, Recall, and calibration error rates. Such variability in benchmarks can obscure incremental algorithmic improvements. Our framework aligns metric definitions with stakeholder preferences and harms. We report statistically rigorous interval estimates under nonstationary traffic. We detail limitations, including attribute uncertainty and shifting user populations. We discuss practical guidance for developers deploying fairness interventions. Future work includes richer stakeholder modeling and dynamic exposure controls.
spans: Common datasets and baselines include MovieLens 1M, MovieLens 20M, LastFM, Amazon Reviews, and RecSys Challenge data, alongside MF, NCF, LightGCN, and BPR, with metrics such as NDCG, Recall, and calibration error rates.

document: Detecting sarcasm online is essential for robust content moderation and sentiment-aware applications. Sarcasm often arises from incongruity between literal text and contextual cues, including imagery and user history. Prior computational work demonstrates that unimodal text models struggle when cues are distributed across modalities. To address this challenge, we investigate representations that tie local visual evidence to pragmatic textual markers under realistic data imbalance. Related research on textual sarcasm has traced signals in lexical polarity, intensifiers, and discourse structures learned by transformers. Studies also show that conversational turn-taking and author style provide discriminative features when aggregated over histories. Multimodal sarcasm research has introduced datasets and models spanning text, image, and user context. MUStARD (Castro et al., 2019) compiles audiovisual clips with dialogue transcripts, while MMSD (Cai et al., 2019) collects paired tweets and images annotated for sarcasm. Later efforts include the multimodal Twitter corpus of SARCOS (Schifanella et al., 2016), multimodal Reddit sarcasm subsets derived from SARC (Khodak et al., 2018), and the multimodal extension of SemEval Task 7 on humor and irony (Hossain et al., 2020). Modeling approaches range from late-fusion baselines using VGG-16 and GloVe embeddings, to cross-modal attention with ViLBERT (Lu et al., 2019), to contrastive alignment with CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), and to graph-based user modeling that leverages interaction networks on conversation threads across platforms. Despite these resources, disagreement in annotation guidelines and varying definitions of sarcasm complicate cross-dataset evaluation. Further, image-text pairs frequently encode world knowledge that standard pretrained encoders underrepresent. We therefore focus on grounding mechanisms that adjudicate clashes between depicted actions and captioned claims. Our approach links region-level objects and attributes to sentiment shifts using constrained cross-attention and sparse supervision. We evaluate on three benchmarks with consistent splits and report ablations on fusion depth and calibration. Finally, we discuss failure cases involving culture-specific jokes and the limits of visual commonsense.

MUStARD (Castro et al., 2019) compiles audiovisual clips with dialogue transcripts, while MMSD (Cai et al., 2019) collects paired tweets and images annotated for sarcasm.
