Modeling approaches range from late-fusion baselines using VGG-16 and GloVe embeddings, to cross-modal attention with ViLBERT (Lu et al., 2019), to contrastive alignment with CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), and to graph-based user modeling that leverages interaction networks on conversation threads across platforms.

document: Monitoring IoT deployments requires reliable detection of anomalous behavior in multivariate streams. False alarms and latency directly translate into operational costs in cyber-physical systems. Existing detectors vary in their modeling assumptions and training regimes, complicating downstream selection. We analyze the trade-off between sample efficiency, calibration, and interpretability under streaming constraints. Prior work highlights the brittleness of reconstruction losses under distribution shift and concept drift. Moreover, unsupervised scores can be difficult for operators to action without localized explanations. We therefore examine detectors through the lens of attribution faithfulness and retraining cost per drift event. Benchmarking for time-series anomaly detection commonly relies on public corpora and a mix of classical and neural models. The Numenta Anomaly Benchmark (Lavin and Ahmad, 2015) and Yahoo Webscope S5 (Laptev et al., 2015) offer labeled point and contextual anomalies, while the UCR Anomaly Archive (Tatbul et al., 2018) aggregates diverse univariate series. Model families include statistical baselines such as ARIMA and STL-ESD, reconstruction-based methods like LSTM-AD (Malhotra et al., 2015), Donut variational autoencoders (Xu et al., 2018), and USAD (Audibert et al., 2020), as well as density models like DeepSVDD (Ruff et al., 2018). Recent proposals extend to forecasting with DeepAR (Salinas et al., 2020), GAN methods like TadGAN (Geiger et al., 2020), and models e.g., OmniAnomaly (Su et al., 2019). While these benchmarks provide useful common ground, label quality and windowing conventions often differ substantially. This variability obscures meaningful comparisons of thresholds, decision delays, and maintenance overheads. We propose an evaluation protocol that decouples detection quality from forecast skill using fixed-lag scoring. Our study measures cost-aware metrics and derives guidelines for rolling retraining budgets in resource-limited nodes. The results indicate clear dependencies between granularity of attribution and operator response times.

Model families include statistical baselines such as ARIMA and STL-ESD, reconstruction-based methods like LSTM-AD (Malhotra et al., 2015), Donut variational autoencoders (Xu et al., 2018), and USAD (Audibert et al., 2020), as well as density models like DeepSVDD (Ruff et al., 2018). Recent proposals extend to forecasting with DeepAR (Salinas et al., 2020), GAN methods like TadGAN (Geiger et al., 2020), and models e.g., OmniAnomaly (Su et al., 2019).

document: Predicting molecular properties from structure underpins discovery in pharmaceuticals and materials. Graph representations align naturally with chemical graphs where atoms are nodes and bonds are edges. However, encoding long-range interactions and geometric priors remains a pressing challenge. We frame this study around data efficiency and robustness to scaffold splits. Prior analyses show that batch size, learning rate, and featurization choices exert strong effects on reported gains. Moreover, hyperparameter transfer across datasets is often inconsistent, complicating fair comparison. We therefore revisit architectural inductive biases with controlled training protocols and uncertainty estimates. Foundational datasets and architectures for molecular graphs have shaped recent benchmarks in property prediction. MoleculeNet collections such as ESOL, FreeSolv, and Lipophilicity (Wu et al., 2018) standardize regression and classification targets, while QM9 (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014) and ZINC subsets (Sterling and Irwin, 2015) support small-molecule studies with curated splits. Representative models include spectral GCNs (Kipf and Welling, 2016), message passing neural networks MPNN (Gilmer et al., 2017), and graph isomorphism networks GIN (Xu et al., 2019). Subsequent variants incorporate physics-motivated features, with SchNet (Schütt et al., 2018), DimeNet and DimeNet++ (Klicpera et al., 2020), and equivariant transformers like SE(3)-Transformer (Fuchs et al., 2020) and E3NN (Geiger and Smidt, 2020) emphasizing rotationally consistent interactions and GemNet (Gasteiger et al., 2021) for directionality. Beyond algorithmic design, evaluation protocols must prevent test-set leakage through scaffold overlaps. We adopt stratified scaffold splits and calibrate with conformal prediction for actionable uncertainty. Our experiments probe whether geometry-aware inductive biases help when labels are scarce or noisy. We analyze variance across seeds and ablate message scheduling, cutoff radii, and readout pooling. The study clarifies conditions under which additional geometric complexity is justified for practical screening.

document: Named entity recognition under multilingual settings is critical for information extraction at web scale. Low-resource languages often lack annotated corpora and language-specific tools. Transfer learning offers an appealing path but suffers from drift and domain noise. We study representation choices that balance cross-lingual generality with domain specificity. Prior work highlights the importance of tokenization coverage and subword regularization for robustness. We hypothesize that alignment fidelity at the sentence level governs zero-shot transfer more than lexicon size. To test this, we design controls for tokenizer frequency and alignment quality across families. Cross-lingual NER has been explored through standard corpora and multilingual encoders with varying supervision regimes. CoNLL 2002/2003 (Tjong Kim Sang, 2002; Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003) provides early benchmarks in Spanish, Dutch, English, and German, while WikiAnn (Pan et al., 2017) offers weakly supervised coverage for 282 languages. Methodologically, zero-shot transfer with mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020) serves as a strong baseline, alongside bilingual lexicon induction with MUSE (Lample et al., 2018) and sentence embeddings from LASER (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019). Projection-based approaches align labels across parallel corpora using word aligners like fast_align (Dyer et al., 2013), and recent shared tasks such as MultiCoNER (Malmasi et al., 2022) systematize evaluation for noisy web domains. Despite progress, evaluation settings often conflate label noise with distribution shift across domains. We introduce perturbed test suites that isolate transliteration drift and code-switching frequency. Our method regularizes alignments with sparsity constraints and calibrates confidence for entity boundaries. Experiments cover typologically diverse targets and include ablations over dictionary coverage. The analysis reveals conditions under which additional lexicon supervision is unnecessary.
spans: Cross-lingual NER has been explored through standard corpora and multilingual encoders with varying supervision regimes. CoNLL 2002/2003 (Tjong Kim Sang, 2002; Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003) provides early benchmarks in Spanish, Dutch, English, and German, while WikiAnn (Pan et al., 2017) offers weakly supervised coverage for 282 languages. Methodologically, zero-shot transfer with mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020) serves as a strong baseline, alongside bilingual lexicon induction with MUSE (Lample et al., 2018) and sentence embeddings from LASER (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019). Projection-based approaches align labels across parallel corpora using word aligners like fast_align (Dyer et al., 2013), and recent shared tasks such as MultiCoNER (Malmasi et al., 2022) systematize evaluation for noisy web domains.

document: Data augmentation is a practical tool for improving text classifiers in low-resource settings. However, augmentation can distort label semantics or distributional properties if applied indiscriminately. We investigate how semantic drift and calibration shift interact under class imbalance. Our analysis focuses on invariances that respect lexical polarity and compositional cues. Prior work has emphasized token-level edits and paraphrase generation using pretrained models. Yet systematic comparisons often conflate data volume with augmentation diversity. We provide a protocol that controls for training budget while varying policies and sources. A broad literature explores textual data augmentation across supervised and semi-supervised regimes. EDA with synonym replacement, insertion, swap, and deletion (Wei and Zou, 2019) offers simple baselines on SST-2 and TREC, while back-translation via neural machine translation systems (Sennrich et al., 2016) produces paraphrases for IMDB and Yelp. Contextual augmentation replaces tokens using bidirectional language models (Kobayashi, 2018), and UDA employs back-translation plus consistency training for semi-supervised tasks (Xie et al., 2020). Additional strategies include TF-IDF word dropout (Fadaee et al., 2017), paraphrase generation with PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020), and mixup-style interpolation in embedding space for text classification (Guo et al., 2019), with evaluations reported on AG News and other public benchmarks and multiple multilingual variants on XNLI and MLDoc datasets. Despite widespread adoption, many reports omit ablation of selection heuristics and quality filtering. We introduce a scoring mechanism that predicts utility before expensive model retraining. The method balances diversity with fidelity using constrained decoding and entailment filters. We evaluate across five tasks with consistent tokenization, reporting calibration in addition to accuracy. We release code and recipes to facilitate reproducible augmentation studies.
spans: A broad literature explores textual data augmentation across supervised and semi-supervised regimes. EDA with synonym replacement, insertion, swap, and deletion (Wei and Zou, 2019) offers simple baselines on SST-2 and TREC, while back-translation via neural machine translation systems (Sennrich et al., 2016) produces paraphrases for IMDB and Yelp. Contextual augmentation replaces tokens using bidirectional language models (Kobayashi, 2018), and UDA employs back-translation plus consistency training for semi-supervised tasks (Xie et al., 2020). Additional strategies include TF-IDF word dropout (Fadaee et al., 2017), paraphrase generation with PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020), and mixup-style interpolation in embedding space for text classification (Guo et al., 2019), with evaluations reported on AG News and other public benchmarks and multiple multilingual variants on XNLI and MLDoc datasets.

document: Multimodal summarization seeks to produce concise textual digests from inputs combining video, audio, and transcripts. Despite rapid progress in large language models, fusing heterogeneous evidence without hallucination remains challenging. Prior work highlights the sensitivity of content selection to temporal alignment and modality salience. Moreover, evaluation practices often conflate captioning and summarization, obscuring the nature of evidence attribution. We study instruction-tuned summarizers that incorporate timestamped cues and speech recognition confidence to reduce off-topic drift. Prior efforts span extractive and abstractive pipelines with pretrained encoders and cross-modal fusion. Representative models include BART and T5 for text, PEGASUS for summarization, and CLIP or ViLBERT for vision-language alignment. Benchmarking commonly uses CNN/DailyMail, XSum, MSR-VTT, and How2, alongside ROUGE, BLEU, and METEOR scores, with datasets such as YouCook2 and COCO Captions supporting video-to-text and caption grounding tasks and evaluation. These resources and tools illustrate the breadth of design choices but leave open how to couple granularity of evidence with controllable abstraction. Our perspective centers on controllability through lightweight planners that gate retrieval from a multimodal index. We frame the task as planning followed by drafting, enabling explicit separation of grounding and compression. The planner selects moments and modalities using a calibrated utility that balances coverage and redundancy. The drafter operates under constrained decoding with oracle-free guidance derived from planner rationales. This architecture supports transparent error analysis by exposing the planner’s selection decisions. We report results across domains and analyze failure modes related to speech disfluencies and visual ambiguity.
spans: Prior efforts span extractive and abstractive pipelines with pretrained encoders and cross-modal fusion. Representative models include BART and T5 for text, PEGASUS for summarization, and CLIP or ViLBERT for vision-language alignment. Benchmarking commonly uses CNN/DailyMail, XSum, MSR-VTT, and How2, alongside ROUGE, BLEU, and METEOR scores, with datasets such as YouCook2 and COCO Captions supporting video-to-text and caption grounding tasks and evaluation.

document: Traffic forecasting leverages spatiotemporal dependencies among sensor networks to predict future speeds and flows. Real-time accuracy is essential for routing, congestion mitigation, and safety-critical planning. Graph neural networks have emerged as a natural fit for modeling road topology and dynamic interactions. Yet, their performance can degrade under sensor failures, distribution shifts, and nonrecurrent events. We examine robust forecasting when edges and nodes exhibit time-varying reliability. Our framework introduces uncertainty-aware message passing that adapts topology using reliability scores. Canonical baselines include DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, and MTGNN, with variants using diffusion, Chebyshev filters, and adaptive adjacency. Common datasets are METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, and PEMS08, often preprocessed with Z-score normalization and fixed sensor graphs. Evaluation typically reports MAE, MAPE, and RMSE at 15, 30, and 60 minutes, with horizon-specific ablations and comparisons to ARIMA and LSTM baselines under unified protocols. The breadth of architectures and datasets highlights the diversity of modeling assumptions across the literature. However, comparatively little is known about how reliability priors interact with temporal attention in graph forecasting. We formalize a stochastic reliability process that modulates receptive fields during training and inference. This design exposes a controllable bias toward resilient subgraphs without sacrificing short-horizon fidelity. We further present a calibration scheme that aligns predictive intervals with reliability-aware losses. Extensive experiments quantify trade-offs between robustness and sharpness under simulated and real sensor outages.
spans: Canonical baselines include DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, and MTGNN, with variants using diffusion, Chebyshev filters, and adaptive adjacency. Common datasets are METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, and PEMS08, often preprocessed with Z-score normalization and fixed sensor graphs. Evaluation typically reports MAE, MAPE, and RMSE at 15, 30, and 60 minutes, with horizon-specific ablations and comparisons to ARIMA and LSTM baselines under unified protocols.

document: Named entity recognition under multilingual constraints aims to identify spans across languages with scarce labels. Transfer remains difficult when scripts, morphology, and capitalization diverge substantially. Moreover, domain variation in newswire, Wikipedia, and social media complicates distributional alignment. We revisit representation sharing by decoupling subword transfer from span-decoding supervision. Our method introduces adaptive lexicon prompts that condition the encoder on type-specific priors. Cross-lingual NER has been studied with multilingual encoders such as mBERT and XLM-R, and sentence representations like LASER and LaBSE. Evaluation commonly uses CoNLL-2002, CoNLL-2003, WikiAnn, and MasakhaNER, with zero-shot transfer and limited target supervision. Approaches include annotation projection via parallel corpora, translate-train and translate-test pipelines, character-level transfer, and CRF decoding layers with constraints, gazetteer integration and multilingual adversarial alignment techniques. These ingredients constitute a broad testbed that emphasizes resource and typological diversity. What remains unclear is how lexical priors interact with subword regularities across writing systems. We formulate a bilingual contrastive objective that calibrates token salience across aligned lexicon prompts. A lightweight adapter steers the encoder toward type-consistent gradients without catastrophic interference. The decoder combines constrained Viterbi inference with temperature-scaled confidence for OOV handling. We diagnose failures by tracking salience shifts under script mixing and diacritics. Results indicate marked robustness gains in low-resource settings with minimal computational overhead.
spans: Cross-lingual NER has been studied with multilingual encoders such as mBERT and XLM-R, and sentence representations like LASER and LaBSE. Evaluation commonly uses CoNLL-2002, CoNLL-2003, WikiAnn, and MasakhaNER, with zero-shot transfer and limited target supervision. Approaches include annotation projection via parallel corpora, translate-train and translate-test pipelines, character-level transfer, and CRF decoding layers with constraints, gazetteer integration and multilingual adversarial alignment techniques.

document: Federated learning enables training models across mobile devices without centralizing raw data. However, resource constraints and intermittent connectivity complicate optimization and deployment. Energy consumption is a primary bottleneck that limits sustained participation of edge clients. The interaction between computation, communication, and straggler mitigation drives practical efficiency. We target energy-aware training that respects device budgets while preserving accuracy. Our approach introduces schedulers that co-design local steps, quantization, and participation pacing. Standard federated algorithms include FedAvg, FedProx, SCAFFOLD, and FedNova, addressing client heterogeneity and objective inconsistency. Empirical studies often benchmark on FEMNIST, CIFAR-10, Shakespeare, and Tiny-ImageNet under non-IID partitions and varying client availability. Reporting typically covers accuracy, communication rounds, client participation rates, uplink bytes, and wall-clock time, with baselines such as LocalSGD and centralized training, energy usage, battery drain, and latency statistics. While these settings are widely adopted, they offer limited insight into per-device cost shaping. We formalize a budgeted objective that trades improvement per joule against fairness constraints. A knapsack-style coordinator selects clients using energy predictions learned from telemetry. Quantized updates with partial aggregation reduce uplink costs without destabilizing optimization. We provide an analysis of convergence under stochastic availability and budget constraints. Experiments detail end-to-end energy savings across heterogeneous Android hardware.
spans: Standard federated algorithms include FedAvg, FedProx, SCAFFOLD, and FedNova, addressing client heterogeneity and objective inconsistency. Empirical studies often benchmark on FEMNIST, CIFAR-10, Shakespeare, and Tiny-ImageNet under non-IID partitions and varying client availability. Reporting typically covers accuracy, communication rounds, client participation rates, uplink bytes, and wall-clock time, with baselines such as LocalSGD and centralized training, energy usage, battery drain, and latency statistics.

document: Robust automatic speech recognition remains difficult in real environments characterized by nonstationary noise and reverberation. Degradation arises from channel mismatch, competing speakers, and far-field microphones. Improvements require coordinated advances in front-end enhancement and sequence modeling. We revisit robustness through representations that expose signal quality estimates to the decoder. Our experiments focus on noise conditions encountered in everyday conversational settings. ASR systems are commonly built with CTC, RNN-T, or attention-based encoder–decoder models such as LAS, using filterbank or MFCC features. Benchmarks include LibriSpeech, CHiME-4, CHiME-6, and Aurora-4, with reported WER on clean and noisy splits. Regularization and robustness techniques span SpecAugment, multi-condition training, dereverberation front-ends, noise-aware training, and beamforming with microphone arrays, far-field recordings, domain mismatch, and reverberant speech conditions scenarios. These components define a typical experimental landscape for assessing performance under noise. Yet the mechanisms by which uncertainty propagates through acoustic and language models are not fully characterized. We couple a denoising front-end with an uncertainty-conditioned transducer to modulate blank emissions. The model exposes framewise confidence features to the predictor for adaptive hypothesis pruning. A training curriculum introduces noise profiles progressively to avoid brittle convergence. We analyze error types across SNR bands and reverberation times to isolate dominant failure modes. Results demonstrate gains under both additive and convolutive distortions with controlled computational overhead.
spans: ASR systems are commonly built with CTC, RNN-T, or attention-based encoder–decoder models such as LAS, using filterbank or MFCC features. Benchmarks include LibriSpeech, CHiME-4, CHiME-6, and Aurora-4, with reported WER on clean and noisy splits. Regularization and robustness techniques span SpecAugment, multi-condition training, dereverberation front-ends, noise-aware training, and beamforming with microphone arrays, far-field recordings, domain mismatch, and reverberant speech conditions scenarios.

document: Urban traffic forecasting is central to intelligent transportation and logistics planning across metropolitan regions. Practical systems must handle sparse sensors, missing data, and abrupt regime shifts caused by incidents and weather. Recent machine learning methods leverage spatial graphs and temporal dynamics to improve short-term predictions beyond historical baselines. However, existing evaluations often obscure model brittleness by averaging across heterogeneous rush and off-peak periods. In addition, many pipelines underutilize contextual signals such as event calendars, lane closures, and topology metadata available to agencies. Our study focuses on the interplay between representation capacity and data curation when training forecasting models in operational settings. We consolidate insights from prior work to motivate a systematic benchmark that aligns metrics with deployment constraints. Early approaches to networked traffic forecasting employed spatiotemporal convolutions and diffusion processes to model sensor interactions, exemplified by DCRNN (Li et al., 2018) and STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), with evaluations commonly conducted on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY benchmark datasets publicly. Subsequent studies introduced temporal convolutions and adaptive graph learning to capture long-range dependencies, including Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), MixHop variants (Abu-El-Haija et al., 2019), and AGCRN (Bai et al., 2020), tests on PEMS03, PEMS04, and PEMS07. Attention-based architectures expanded this line with temporal self-attention and receptive fields, as seen in ASTGCN (Guo et al., 2019), GMAN (Zheng et al., 2020), and STTN (Xu et al., 2020), frequently paired with positional encodings and residual connections. Parallel efforts explored physics-aware formulations, including variational graph autoencoders for uncertainty quantification (Kipf and Welling, 2016), physics-informed recurrent models (Raissi et al., 2019), and diffusion-based forecasters (Rangapuram et al., 2018), supported by ancillary sensor metadata and weather covariates. Transformer variants generalized sequence modeling with sparse attention, such as Informer (Zhou et al., 2021), LogTrans (Li et al., 2019), Autoformer (Wu et al., 2021), and FEDformer (Zhou et al., 2022), preprocessing with normalization of historical traffic signals. Despite the breadth of architectures, reported gains frequently correlate with dataset-specific preprocessing choices and training horizons rather than intrinsic modeling advances. Cross-dataset transfer experiments reveal substantial performance inversions, suggesting hidden confounders in graph construction and sensor alignment. We therefore standardize edge definitions, time indexing, and covariate availability to isolate the effect of temporal receptive fields. Furthermore, we examine failure modes under incident spikes by aligning predictions with external incident logs and precipitation records. Our analysis highlights how teacher forcing ratios and scheduled sampling influence stability during peak congestion periods. We introduce a stratified evaluation that disaggregates metrics by road class, speed regime, and missingness level to reflect operational priorities. To support reproducibility, we release data loaders, split scripts, and validation protocols compatible with common GNN and Transformer libraries. The resulting framework facilitates apples-to-apples comparisons and reveals when architectural complexity yields diminishing returns. Finally, we discuss implications for procurement processes in agencies that must balance accuracy, latency, and interpretability.
spans: Early approaches to networked traffic forecasting employed spatiotemporal convolutions and diffusion processes to model sensor interactions, exemplified by DCRNN (Li et al., 2018) and STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), with evaluations commonly conducted on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY benchmark datasets publicly. Subsequent studies introduced temporal convolutions and adaptive graph learning to capture long-range dependencies, including Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), MixHop variants (Abu-El-Haija et al., 2019), and AGCRN (Bai et al., 2020), tests on PEMS03, PEMS04, and PEMS07. Attention-based architectures expanded this line with temporal self-attention and receptive fields, as seen in ASTGCN (Guo et al., 2019), GMAN (Zheng et al., 2020), and STTN (Xu et al., 2020), frequently paired with positional encodings and residual connections. Parallel efforts explored physics-aware formulations, including variational graph autoencoders for uncertainty quantification (Kipf and Welling, 2016), physics-informed recurrent models (Raissi et al., 2019), and diffusion-based forecasters (Rangapuram et al., 2018), supported by ancillary sensor metadata and weather covariates. Transformer variants generalized sequence modeling with sparse attention, such as Informer (Zhou et al., 2021), LogTrans (Li et al., 2019), Autoformer (Wu et al., 2021), and FEDformer (Zhou et al., 2022), preprocessing with normalization of historical traffic signals.

document: Writing assessment at scale requires reliable, timely, and interpretable feedback for diverse learners. Automated essay scoring systems promise cost-effective assistance but risk reinforcing biases without careful validation. Prior literature highlights the importance of prompt effects, domain shift, and rater variability when modeling holistic quality. Moreover, the pedagogical utility of scores depends on transparent links to criteria and actionable suggestions. Existing benchmarks vary widely in genre, proficiency distribution, and annotation granularity, complicating fair comparisons. These inconsistencies motivate a closer examination of data splits, calibration, and generalization regimes for practical deployment. We contextualize current advances with attention to evaluation protocols that reflect educational priorities. Automated essay scoring has evolved from handcrafted feature bundles toward neural encoders, with classic systems like e-rater (Attali and Burstein, 2006) and newer CNN/LSTM encoders (Taghipour and Ng, 2016) evaluated on ASAP, TOEFL11, and FCE corpora under prompt-specific cross-prompt protocols. Subsequent transformer-based methods leveraged pretrained language models for features and prompt conditioning, including BERT fine-tuning (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa adaptations (Liu et al., 2019), and DeBERTa variants (He et al., 2021), integrating rubric keywords or discourse cues. Research on explainability emphasized feature importance and rationales, employing SHAP analyses (Lundberg and Lee, 2017), attention visualizations (Jain and Wallace, 2019), and example-based explanations with influence functions (Koh and Liang, 2017), compared against rater criteria or rubric annotations. Dataset development likewise broadened coverage and labels, with ICNALE essays and proficiency metadata (Ishikawa, 2013), FCE error-coded corpus (Yannakoudakis et al., 2011), the ASAP set, and BEA shared tasks (Yannakoudakis et al., 2019) standardizing splits and scoring scales. Related strands investigated grammatical error correction and quality estimation as signals, drawing on JFLEG and CoNLL datasets (Napoles et al., 2017; Ng et al., 2014), tagging architectures (Rei and Yannakoudakis, 2016), and objectives aligning rubric dimensions and proficiency. Despite strong leaderboard results, many models degrade when prompts change or when essays include code-switching and nonstandard orthography. Our analysis treats these phenomena as structured distribution shifts rather than mere noise. We design controlled train-test configurations to separate prompt-specific memorization from robust language proficiency modeling. In addition, we examine rater disagreement and employ uncertainty-aware metrics that penalize overconfident predictions on ambiguous responses. We compare calibration strategies, including temperature scaling and isotonic regression, for maintaining actionable score thresholds. To support instructional use, we couple global scores with criterion-aligned evidence extracted from model internals and shallow discourse features. We provide ablations that disentangle the contributions of pretraining, prompt tokens, and training objectives across datasets. The resulting picture clarifies which design choices carry across domains and which require prompt-specific adaptation. Finally, we discuss implications for formative assessment settings where fairness and transparency are paramount.

document: Federated learning enables collaborative model training without centralizing raw data across clients or organizations. Deploying such systems on resource-constrained devices raises intertwined concerns about energy, latency, and reliability. Practical workloads encounter non-IID data, intermittent connectivity, and variable compute budgets that complicate optimization choices. Moreover, privacy mechanisms like secure aggregation and differential privacy interact with communication schedules and update sparsity. Prior studies sometimes report throughput or accuracy in isolation, obscuring trade-offs relevant to edge deployment. We examine these trade-offs by aligning algorithmic choices with measured device costs and variability in participation. Our perspective emphasizes end-to-end efficiency metrics that reflect application service-level objectives. Core algorithms for federated optimization address data heterogeneity and communication constraints, beginning with FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017) and extending to FedProx (Li et al., 2020) and SCAFFOLD (Karimireddy et al., 2019), evaluated on FEMNIST, Shakespeare, and CIFAR-10 in non-IID. Optimization advances also include adaptive server optimizers and normalization, such as FedOpt (Reddi et al., 2021), FedAdam, FedYogi, and FedNova (Wang et al., 2020), which stabilize training under variable client participation, partial local epochs, and straggler effects significantly. Communication reduction has been pursued through gradient quantization and sparsification, leveraging QSGD (Alistarh et al., 2017), SignSGD with error-feedback (Bernstein et al., 2018; Karimireddy et al., 2019), and top-k selection (Stich et al., 2018) for bandwidth-limited links scenarios. Fairness and personalization have been explored via q-FFL (Li et al., 2020), clustered federated learning (Sattler et al., 2020), meta-learning initializations like Per-FedAvg (Fallah et al., 2020), and personalization layers, frequently measured with metrics and group disparity indicators. Benchmarking suites such as LEAF (Caldas et al., 2019) and FLUTE (Diao et al., 2021) provide standardized preprocessing, client partitions, and simulators, while production logs from mobile keyboard prediction and speech domains offer participation patterns and privacy constraints. Although the literature offers many ingredients, their combined impact on battery drain and responsiveness remains undercharacterized. We build a measurement harness that records energy, CPU cycles, memory, and wireless traffic during representative training rounds. Using controlled client pools and configurable network traces, we compare optimizers, compression, and aggregation schedules under equal-accuracy targets. We quantify the energy-accuracy frontier and identify regimes where additional local computation is cheaper than extra uplink communication. We further explore how adaptive client sampling improves both fairness and tail latency for slow devices. Our experiments integrate secure aggregation to assess overheads and feasibility within realistic duty cycles. We analyze sensitivity to participation volatility and propose robustness checks for production readiness. The study yields guidelines for selecting algorithms that meet energy budgets without sacrificing quality of service. Finally, we release traces, scripts, and dashboards to facilitate reproducible evaluations in edge environments.

document: Harmful content increasingly leverages combinations of imagery and text to evade detection and context filters. Moderation systems must therefore analyze cross-modal cues while accounting for platform dynamics and evolving vernacular. Beyond classification, practitioners require stable thresholds that minimize false positives without missing harmful variants. Existing work documents substantial shifts across communities, domains, and cultures that challenge transferability. Further, adversaries can exploit shallow correlations between visual styles and labels to trigger brittle behavior. These realities motivate evaluations that probe both multimodal semantics and the social context in which posts circulate. We review prior methods to situate the empirical choices that shape performance and reliability. Multimodal hate and toxicity detection has grown with datasets that pair images and text, including Hateful Memes (Kiela et al., 2020), MMHS150K (Gomez et al., 2020), and FB Multimodal Hate Speech (Founta et al., 2021), often extending earlier text-only corpora. Baseline methods explored late fusion of predictors, with FastText and BERT encoders for text (Joulin et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2019), and ResNet or EfficientNet backbones for images (He et al., 2016; Tan and Le, 2019) classifiers. Cross-modal transformers and pretraining expanded performance, using ViLBERT (Lu et al., 2019), VisualBERT (Li et al., 2019), UNITER (Chen et al., 2020), and CLIP-style alignment (Radford et al., 2021), with auxiliary tasks masked modeling, contrastive losses, and grounding. Context and user signals were incorporated through conversation threads, author histories, and social graphs, drawing on HateXplain rationales (Mathew et al., 2021), Gab and Twitter crawls, and image OCR pipelines that augment text with memes, captions, and overlays. Evaluation commonly adopts accuracy and AUROC alongside macro F1, with robustness checks for spurious correlations introduced by watermark cues, benign confounders, and template artifacts, and dataset revisions addressing annotator agreement, class imbalance, and multimodal contradiction cases and leakage. Despite steady progress, many models fail on memes where text negates or subverts the image content. We design tests that contrast matched pairs differing only in cross-modal contradiction signals. By controlling for typography, color palettes, and background textures, we isolate semantic reasoning from superficial cues. We also measure sensitivity to OCR noise and explore alternative pipelines for fusing extracted text with native captions. Our analysis includes subgroup metrics across dialects and image genres to surface uneven risks. We examine calibration under distribution shift to support defensible enforcement thresholds in practice. To aid transparency, we curate error sheets highlighting typical failure patterns and upstream data issues. The study offers recommendations for dataset construction and evaluation that reduce exploitable biases. Finally, we release reproducible code and checklists to help practitioners stress test multimodal classifiers.

document: Detecting anomalies in industrial telemetry is critical for safety, uptime, and predictive maintenance. Operators require timely alerts with low false positives to avoid alarm fatigue and unnecessary interventions. Time-series data present challenges including seasonality, regime changes, and heterogeneous sampling across sensors. Furthermore, ground-truth labels are scarce, delayed, and sometimes ambiguous due to multi-causal incidents. Traditional detectors can be brittle to distribution shifts, while overparameterized models may overfit subtle artifacts. A practical evaluation must integrate detection quality with diagnostic latency and actionability. We overview prominent paradigms before introducing an assessment protocol tailored to operational constraints. Classical time-series anomaly detection employs statistical and distance-based techniques such as ARIMA residual analysis (Box and Jenkins, 1970), seasonal decomposition with thresholds, Local Outlier Factor (Breunig et al., 2000), and Isolation Forest (Liu et al., 2008) across industrial settings globally. Neural reconstruction methods replace manual features with learned embeddings, exemplified by LSTM autoencoders (Malhotra et al., 2016), variational autoencoders for probabilistic scoring (Kingma and Welling, 2014), and temporal convolutional denoisers, trained with sliding windows and stopping heuristics criteria. Generative approaches model normal behavior distributions and flag deviations, including Donut with missing-data imputation (Xu et al., 2018), OmniAnomaly using stochastic recurrent flows (Su et al., 2019), and MAD-GAN ensembles (Li et al., 2019) for high-dimensional sensor streams. Multivariate correlation is captured by attention mechanisms and residual correlation maps, as in MSCRED (Zhang et al., 2019), DAGMM-style hybrids (Zong et al., 2018), graph temporal networks, and transformers adapted for irregular sampling with masking and positional encodings. Benchmark datasets and protocols include Yahoo and Numenta NAB streams (Lavin and Ahmad, 2015), NASA MSL and SMAP telemetry (Hundman et al., 2018), SMD machines, SWaT and WADI testbeds (Mathur and Tippenhauer, 2016), with precision-recall and latency-focused metrics. Despite numerous models, reported gains often diminish when evaluation emphasizes early detection and stability across shifts. We adopt consistent windowing, scoring, and delay penalties to avoid optimistic inflation from retrospective smoothing. In addition, we present cross-facility transfer tests that challenge robustness under diverse sensor layouts and processes. Our study analyzes alert aggregation and cooldown strategies that balance sensitivity and operator workload. We examine calibration under varying anomaly prevalence and propose thresholding methods that maintain interpretable risk categories. To support diagnostics, we pair probabilistic scores with ranked attributions over channels and time segments. We provide ablations quantifying the influence of reconstruction loss, context length, and sampling normalization. The resulting insights clarify how modeling choices map to maintenance objectives and downtime costs. Finally, we release scripts and baselines to facilitate reproducible comparisons across public and semi-public corpora.

document: Question answering systems increasingly operate across heterogeneous sources, combining retrieval, reasoning, and calibration to manage uncertainty. In production, errors carry asymmetric costs because ungrounded answers can erode trust or propagate misinformation. Reliable systems must therefore balance coverage with mechanisms for abstention when evidence is insufficient. Prior work demonstrates that modeling choices in retrieval and aggregation can dominate end-to-end quality. Yet it remains difficult to compare methods when training data, evidence granularity, and evaluation metrics vary widely across studies. We outline these challenges to motivate a careful examination of evidence selection and uncertainty handling in cross-domain settings. Our overview emphasizes the link between answerability and calibrated confidence in realistic deployments. There has been a sustained effort to build cross-domain machine reading comprehension resources and methods, including SQuAD-style span extraction (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), adversarially constructed questions (Jia and Liang, 2017), multi-hop reasoning benchmarks like HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018), and open-domain retrieval QA such as Natural Questions (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019). Neural architectures progressed from BiDAF and QANet (Seo et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2018) to large pretrained language models fine-tuned for answer selection and evidence identification (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Retrieval-augmented approaches integrate dense retrievers like DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) and sparse term-matchers like BM25, with re-ranking via cross-encoders and fusion-in-decoder strategies (Izacard and Grave, 2021). Robustness and generalization were studied with adversarial perturbations, out-of-domain evaluations, and contrast sets (Gardner et al., 2020), along with calibration metrics for abstention and selective prediction (Keskar et al., 2019). Datasets emphasizing explanation and support sentences, including FEVER (Thorne et al., 2018), QASC (Khot et al., 2020), and ELI5 (Fan et al., 2019), broadened evaluation to faithfulness, rationale coverage, and long-form answers. Despite substantial progress, disagreement remains on how to score systems that retrieve partial evidence or synthesize multi-document chains. We investigate evaluation schemes that couple answer correctness with evidence sufficiency and coverage. Concretely, we compare fixed-k retrieval against confidence-adaptive retrieval under identical compute budgets. We analyze how retriever recall interacts with reader calibration to influence abstention quality. We further test adversarial robustness using paraphrases and distractor passages that preserve answerability while degrading lexical overlap. Our experiments highlight conditions under which selective prediction reduces harmful errors without excessive deferral. We release a standardized evaluation harness with aligned splits, metrics, and cost accounting for retrieval. Finally, we discuss practical implications for product teams integrating question answering into search and assistance applications.

Neural architectures progressed from BiDAF and QANet (Seo et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2018) to large pretrained language models fine-tuned for answer selection and evidence identification (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Retrieval-augmented approaches integrate dense retrievers like DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) and sparse term-matchers like BM25, with re-ranking via cross-encoders and fusion-in-decoder strategies (Izacard and Grave, 2021). Datasets emphasizing explanation and support sentences, including FEVER (Thorne et al., 2018), QASC (Khot et al., 2020), and ELI5 (Fan et al., 2019), broadened evaluation to faithfulness, rationale coverage, and long-form answers.

document: Federated learning enables training across institutions without centralizing data, a property vital for clinical imaging workflows. Hospitals face strict privacy rules and heterogeneity in scanners, populations, and protocols that challenge generalization. This paper investigates robust aggregation under client shift for segmentation and classification tasks in real-world cohorts. We focus on balancing personalization with global performance when participation is intermittent and communication budgets are tight. Prior evaluations often assume synchronized clients and homogeneous label spaces, conditions rarely met in multi-center care. We therefore revisit optimization and systems choices from the perspective of reliability and clinical deployment timelines. Prior work on federated learning addresses aggregation and domain shift, communication constraints, and robustness, including FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), FedProx (Li et al., 2020), SCAFFOLD (Karimireddy et al., 2020), and personalization via pFedMe and Ditto (T D Li et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021), alongside medical imaging benchmarks such as BraTS and CheXpert (Menze et al., 2015; Irvin et al., 2019) and secure aggregation with differential privacy defenses (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Abadi et al., 2016) in practical deployments. Despite this progress, evidence about safety-critical failure modes under covariate and label shift remains sparse. Existing benchmarks underrepresent rare pathologies and scanner artifacts that routinely appear in tertiary centers. We frame these gaps as questions about calibration, out-of-distribution detection, and fairness across demographic subgroups. Our study examines failure-aware aggregation rules alongside lightweight personalization techniques that control for privacy budgets. We design stress tests that vary prevalence, scanner vendors, and missing modalities to probe stability. The experimental protocol emphasizes reproducibility, reporting run-to-run variability and sensitivity to hyperparameters. We further analyze communication trade-offs to understand when local adaptation outweighs global convergence. A cost model connects system constraints to clinical turnaround time, clarifying deployment feasibility. The resulting insights inform procurement and risk governance for institutions adopting federated systems.
spans: Prior work on federated learning addresses aggregation and domain shift, communication constraints, and robustness, including FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), FedProx (Li et al., 2020), SCAFFOLD (Karimireddy et al., 2020), and personalization via pFedMe and Ditto (T D Li et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021), alongside medical imaging benchmarks such as BraTS and CheXpert (Menze et al., 2015; Irvin et al., 2019) and secure aggregation with differential privacy defenses (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Abadi et al., 2016) in practical deployments.

document: Accurate traffic forecasting underpins routing, tolling, and congestion mitigation in urban networks. Graph neural networks model sensor connectivity to capture spatial correlations that vary with road topology. Temporal dynamics introduce long-range dependencies that challenge purely recurrent baselines. Equally important are data gaps, sensor failures, and distribution shifts caused by incidents and weather. We study architectures that retain performance under sparse supervision and delayed measurements. Our analysis revisits attention mechanisms and diffusion operators through the lens of stability. We also consider evaluation protocols that separate routine rush-hour patterns from rare disruptions. To ground the study, we curate event labels and missing patterns from a year of highway data. Spatiotemporal traffic forecasting commonly evaluates graph-based sequence models such as DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and ASTGCN (Guo et al., 2019), with recent Transformer variants including GMAN and ST-Transformer (Zheng et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2020), typically using the METR-LA and PEMS-BAY datasets (Li et al., 2018) and larger PEMS collections like PEMS03, PEMS04, and PEMS07 (Zheng et al., 2020), alongside auxiliary baselines ARIMA and LSTM for comparative evaluation. However, little is known about how these models behave when sensors drop out for extended periods. We formalize this as a stochastic masking process aligned with observed maintenance logs. The proposed benchmarks quantify both short-term accuracy and multi-horizon degradation during outages. We report calibration metrics and uncertainty intervals to assess decision risk. Ablations disentangle the roles of learned adjacency, static topology, and exogenous signals. Finally, we discuss deployment implications for agencies operating heterogeneous sensor fleets. These results inform robust forecasting strategies for incident-aware traffic management.
spans: Spatiotemporal traffic forecasting commonly evaluates graph-based sequence models such as DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and ASTGCN (Guo et al., 2019), with recent Transformer variants including GMAN and ST-Transformer (Zheng et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2020), typically using the METR-LA and PEMS-BAY datasets (Li et al., 2018) and larger PEMS collections like PEMS03, PEMS04, and PEMS07 (Zheng et al., 2020), alongside auxiliary baselines ARIMA and LSTM for comparative evaluation.

document: Clinical decision support requires models that are both accurate and interpretable to clinicians. Regulations and ethical commitments further necessitate justifications that can be audited. Yet explanations may distort or oversimplify the underlying computation. We investigate how explanation faithfulness varies with class imbalance and dataset shift. Our framework links explanation stability to patient safety metrics relevant to triage. We operationalize desiderata using counterfactual sensitivity and monotonicity constraints. The evaluation spans structured labs, radiology images, and clinical notes. Explainability in clinical machine learning has been explored through model-agnostic and gradient-based techniques, including LIME (Ribeiro et al., 2016), SHAP (Lundberg and Lee, 2017), Integrated Gradients (Sundararajan et al., 2017), Grad-CAM for imaging (Selvaraju et al., 2017), Anchors and counterfactual explanations (Ribeiro et al., 2018; Wachter et al., 2017), concept-based TCAV (Kim et al., 2018), and evaluations on MIMIC-III, eICU, and CheXpert datasets (Johnson et al., 2016; Pollard et al., 2018; Irvin et al., 2019) across tasks, modalities, and diverse populations. However, consensus on appropriate validation protocols for explanations remains elusive. Many prior studies rely on proxy tasks rather than ground-truth rationales. We therefore incorporate clinician-elicited rationales to benchmark plausibility without leaking labels. A calibration analysis examines whether explanations amplify model overconfidence on rare findings. We report compute-aware budgets to compare methods under realistic hospital constraints. The study includes ablations on smoothing, sparsity, and multi-modal fusion. Finally, we reflect on documentation obligations for explanation catalogs used in care pathways. The resulting guidance targets safe integration of explanations into routine workflows.
spans: Explainability in clinical machine learning has been explored through model-agnostic and gradient-based techniques, including LIME (Ribeiro et al., 2016), SHAP (Lundberg and Lee, 2017), Integrated Gradients (Sundararajan et al., 2017), Grad-CAM for imaging (Selvaraju et al., 2017), Anchors and counterfactual explanations (Ribeiro et al., 2018; Wachter et al., 2017), concept-based TCAV (Kim et al., 2018), and evaluations on MIMIC-III, eICU, and CheXpert datasets (Johnson et al., 2016; Pollard et al., 2018; Irvin et al., 2019) across tasks, modalities, and diverse populations.

document: Knowledge-augmented language models aim to answer questions beyond their parametric memory. Retrieval modules provide dynamic access to external sources that evolve over time. However, integrating retrieved evidence with generation remains challenging when documents conflict. We examine how indexing granularity and reader capacity affect compositional reasoning. Our analysis considers both single-hop factual queries and multi-hop synthesis tasks. Open-domain question answering leverages retrieval and generation across benchmarks such as Natural Questions, HotpotQA, and TriviaQA (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2018; Joshi et al., 2017), with systems including DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), Dense Passage Retrieval and REALM for learned indexing (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Guu et al., 2020), Retrieval-Augmented Generation and Fusion-in-Decoder for evidence aggregation (Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021), and large language models prompted for open-book answering (Brown et al., 2020) at scale. Despite strong benchmarks, discrepancies in evidence usage suggest hidden shortcuts. We propose diagnostics that measure robustness to distractors and paraphrases. The study isolates the role of retriever calibration from reader uncertainty. We explore training curricula that balance recall and precision under limited budgets. Efficiency experiments characterize latency under corpus growth and cache strategies. Human evaluation focuses on attribution quality and citation consistency. We also analyze failure cases when sources contain outdated or contradictory statements. A negative control corpus helps distinguish genuine reasoning from memorization. Finally, we discuss implications for building traceable knowledge services. The findings inform the design of accountable question answering systems.
spans: Open-domain question answering leverages retrieval and generation across benchmarks such as Natural Questions, HotpotQA, and TriviaQA (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2018; Joshi et al., 2017), with systems including DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), Dense Passage Retrieval and REALM for learned indexing (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Guu et al., 2020), Retrieval-Augmented Generation and Fusion-in-Decoder for evidence aggregation (Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021), and large language models prompted for open-book answering (Brown et al., 2020) at scale.

document: Detecting anomalies in industrial sensor networks is essential for reliability and safety. Multivariate streams exhibit complex dependencies that vary across operating modes. False alarms are costly, yet missed detections can trigger cascading failures. The challenge intensifies under nonstationarity and sparse ground truth labels. We focus on methods that remain resilient during regime shifts and maintenance events. Our perspective emphasizes interpretability for root-cause analysis in engineering workflows. We model shared and component-specific dynamics with structured latent variables. The training objective balances predictive likelihood and change-point sensitivity. A simulation harness generates controllable faults to stress-test algorithms. We complement simulations with case studies from a petrochemical plant. Evaluation covers early detection delay, precision at top ranks, and false discovery control. Research on multivariate time-series anomaly detection spans probabilistic and reconstruction-based methods including LSTM-VAE (Park et al., 2018), Donut and OmniAnomaly (Zong et al., 2018; Su et al., 2019), DeepSVDD and USAD (Ruff et al., 2018; Audibert et al., 2020), and sequence models like MSCRED and TadGAN (Zhang et al., 2019; Geiger et al., 2020), commonly evaluated on SMAP, MSL, SMD, SWaT, and WADI datasets (Hundman et al., 2018; Su et al., 2019; Mathur et al., 2016; Ahmed et al., 2017) benchmarks. Despite broad progress, few studies examine cross-site transfer with heterogeneous sensors. We introduce a split protocol that separates sites and seasons to avoid leakage. Cost curves illustrate trade-offs between inspection burden and downtime risk. Our results provide guidance for scalable monitoring across industrial fleets.
spans: Research on multivariate time-series anomaly detection spans probabilistic and reconstruction-based methods including LSTM-VAE (Park et al., 2018), Donut and OmniAnomaly (Zong et al., 2018; Su et al., 2019), DeepSVDD and USAD (Ruff et al., 2018; Audibert et al., 2020), and sequence models like MSCRED and TadGAN (Zhang et al., 2019; Geiger et al., 2020), commonly evaluated on SMAP, MSL, SMD, SWaT, and WADI datasets (Hundman et al., 2018; Su et al., 2019; Mathur et al., 2016; Ahmed et al., 2017) benchmarks.

document: Urban mobility systems increasingly rely on predictive analytics to mitigate congestion and improve reliability. Accurate short-term traffic forecasting is central to adaptive signal control, route guidance, and demand-responsive transit. Graph-based deep learning has emerged as a natural fit because road networks impose relational structure on sensor time series. Early spatiotemporal traffic forecasting models include DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and GMAN (Zheng et al., 2020), alongside later variants such as MTGNN (Wu et al., 2020) and StemGNN (Cao et al., 2020). Common evaluation benchmarks include METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PeMSD7, and TaxiBJ, with typical metrics such as MAE, RMSE, and MAPE reported across horizons, and training strategies ranging from teacher forcing and scheduled sampling to curriculum learning, while graph construction spans distance-based adjacency, adaptive attention, and learned embeddings, and implementations often build on PyTorch, DGL, or TensorFlow with optimizer choices like Adam or RMSProp and sequence-to-sequence decoders using GRU or Transformer layers. Datasets are publicly accessible through standard repositories online. Prior surveys categorize approaches by graph construction, temporal modeling, and training objectives. However, reported results differ widely due to dataset splits, horizon settings, and preprocessing choices. Clarifying these design factors is essential for interpreting comparative performance claims. Recent work also underscores the role of multimodal covariates such as weather, incidents, and calendar effects. Beyond base architectures, numerous extensions explore dynamic graphs, multi-resolution temporal encoders, and exogenous features, including ASTGCN (Guo et al., 2019), AGCRN (Bai et al., 2020), STG-NCDE (Kidger et al., 2020), and DGCRN (Li et al., 2021), with additional comparisons to T-GCN (Zhao et al., 2019) and Z-GCNETs (Chen et al., 2021). Data regimes cover loop-detector series, crowdsourced speed traces, and ride-hailing demand counts, while preprocessing includes z-score normalization, per-sensor detrending, temporal holiday flags, and road network pruning; augmentation employs random time warping, mixup on sequences, cutout on nodes, or downsampling, and baselines often list ARIMA, VAR, SVR, and LSTM alongside Prophet and Temporal Convolutional Networks. Open-source toolkits reference GluonTS, PyTorch Geometric Temporal, STGODE, and LibCity for reproducible pipelines and standardized configuration files. Despite maturity of benchmarks, deployment constraints often dictate architecture simplifications and memory budgets. Compression, pruning, and distillation are increasingly considered in operational settings with strict service levels. Robustness to missing data and topology changes remains a recurring concern in sensor networks. Transfer across cities poses additional challenges due to distributional shift and sparse supervision. Self-supervised pretraining on large unlabeled streams is gaining attention as a data-efficient alternative. Deployment considerations frequently note latency constraints, edge inference on NVIDIA Jetson, ONNX export, and quantization-aware training, as seen in benchmarks using Triton Inference Server, TensorRT, and TVM. Interpretability and diagnostics reference SHAP, Integrated Gradients, counterfactual evaluation on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY subsets, residual spectral analysis, and stress tests under sensor dropout, distribution shift, simulated incidents, and extreme weather, alongside standard ablations over adjacency schemes, horizon lengths, teacher forcing ratios, learning rates, and batch sizes. Reporting practices cite seed control, five-fold cross-validation, early stopping patience, and checkpoint averaging protocols for fair comparison. These practices form a backdrop for contemporary evaluations in traffic prediction research. This section outlines the landscape to situate subsequent technical material.

document: Large language models increasingly serve as general-purpose interfaces for knowledge work and automation. Understanding their behavior requires careful evaluation across tasks, prompts, and safety conditions. Related work spans performance, robustness, and alignment under varying supervision regimes. Evaluation of large language models frequently spans benchmarks such as SuperGLUE, BIG-bench, MMLU, HellaSwag, and ARC, with task types covering commonsense reasoning, multi-step math, reading comprehension, and closed-book factual recall. Prompting and decoding strategies include zero-shot and few-shot in-context learning, Chain-of-Thought prompting (Wei et al., 2022), Self-Consistency (Wang et al., 2023), ReAct (Yao et al., 2022), and Tree-of-Thoughts (Yao et al., 2023), as well as calibration methods like contextualized temperature scaling, logit biasing, and instruction tuning corpora such as FLAN, Super-NaturalInstructions, and Dolly. Retrieval-augmented generation variants utilize DPR, Contriever, or BM25 retrievers with FiD, Atlas, or RAG readers and evaluate on Natural Questions, TriviaQA, WebQuestions, and HotpotQA under exact match and F1 metrics. Datasets are released with standardized prompts and scripts. Open questions remain about how prompt formats interact with model scale and pretraining data. Work on interpretability, calibration, and uncertainty assessment provides diagnostic context for downstream use. We outline methodological trends before describing our experimental protocol. Safety evaluation draws on jailbreak suites, adversarial instructions, toxicity probes, and bias diagnostics from datasets such as RealToxicityPrompts, ToxiGen, BOLD, BBQ, and Winogender, while red teaming uses AdvBench, PromptInjectionBench, and JailbreakBench. Mitigation techniques include supervised instruction tuning on RLHF or DPO pipelines, safety-tuned checkpoints like Llama Guard, Detoxify-guided data filtering, PPLM and GeDi for controlled generation, constitutional AI with curated principles, and retrieval-based policy shaping using safety classifiers, combined with content filtering via Perspective API, OpenAI moderation, and HateCheck taxonomies. Evaluation protocols record refusal rates, safety score deltas, and attack success under paraphrase, roleplay, and multi-turn escalation, and systems are compared across AlpacaEval, MT-Bench, Vicuna evaluations, and LMSys Chatbot Arena with model backbones including GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Claude, PaLM, Llama, and MPT models. Guardrails and evaluation harnesses are increasingly bundled with SDKs to enforce policy at inference time. However, disparate metrics and heuristics complicate comparisons across studies and releases. Consensus on reporting standards is emerging but remains uneven across communities. Long-form and multi-turn assessments introduce additional sources of variance due to memory and state handling. Open-source initiatives have accelerated reproducibility through shared datasets, prompts, and scripts. Agentic tool-use references Toolformer, Gorilla, ReAct, MRKL, and HuggingGPT, with execution layers spanning LangChain, LlamaIndex, Guidance, and Semantic Kernel. Planning and memory components cite AutoGPT, BabyAGI, Voyager, and CAMEL, while evaluation covers WebArena, ScienceWorld, AlfWorld, and MiniWoB, and logging standards mention OpenTelemetry traces, JSONL transcripts, and interaction schemas for reproducibility, with baselines that include ReAct-only agents, plan-and-solve, and reflexion-style self-improvement loops. Reports also note prompt templates, tool catalogs, scratchpad formats, and guardrails configured via ensemble filters and finite-state policies. Experiments track latency, tool error rates, and recovery behaviors under load. We summarize these strands to contextualize empirical analyses in the following sections.

document: Automatic segmentation enables scalable analysis of medical images for diagnosis and treatment planning. Recent advances build on deep architectures tailored to volumetric data and limited annotations. Despite strong results, variability across sites and protocols challenges generalization and evaluation. Benchmarks for medical image segmentation commonly include BraTS, LiTS, KiTS, ACDC, PROMISE12, and the Medical Segmentation Decathlon, with modalities spanning MRI, CT, and ultrasound across organ and lesion targets. Representative architectures cover U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015), UNet++ (Zhou et al., 2018), Attention U-Net (Oktay et al., 2018), DeepLabv3+ (Chen et al., 2018), and nnU-Net (Isensee et al., 2021), along with transformer-based variants like TransUNet, Swin-UNet, and Swin-UNETR. Training pipelines often report Dice, Jaccard, Hausdorff, and surface distance metrics, employ data augmentations such as elastic deformation, intensity jitter, random cropping, and rotation, and use optimizers like AdamW with cosine decay and mixed precision on 3D patches. Preprocessing includes resampling, bias-field correction, z-score normalization, and skull stripping across cohorts with consistent spacing. Reported differences often derive from preprocessing, resampling, and sampling policies rather than core architecture design. Comparisons therefore require strict control of splits, augmentations, and evaluation scripts across cohorts. Multi-modal fusion explores complementary contrasts but complicates registration and harmonization. Labeling strategies range from voxel-wise expert contours and crowd annotations to weak supervision from scribbles, bounding boxes, and pseudo-labels derived from consistency training and temporal ensembling. Semi-supervised and self-supervised approaches reference Mean Teacher, FixMatch, Cross Pseudo Supervision, SimCLR, BYOL, and SwAV, while multi-domain adaptation leverages CycleGAN, AdaIN, MMD-based alignment, and style transfer to bridge scanner and protocol shifts between centers. Active learning pipelines compare uncertainty heuristics such as entropy, BALD, MC Dropout, and test-time augmentation against diversity-based sampling via k-Center, Core-set, and BADGE, and evaluate across BraTS, KiTS, and ACDC with budget-aware annotation rounds and Dice gains. Post-processing employs connected components, conditional random fields, largest-component selection, and Laplacian smoothing, and submissions follow challenge rules, dockerized bundles, and hidden-test servers for standardized leaderboards. Open-source codebases and pretrained weights have lowered entry barriers for challenge participation. Nevertheless, annotation scarcity remains an enduring bottleneck for new institutions and indications. Simulation and synthetic data are being explored to expand coverage without privacy exposure. Benchmark governance increasingly emphasizes transparent protocols and auditability. Clinical deployment reports mention DICOM integration, MONAI and nnU-Net pipelines, TorchScript or ONNX exports, and latency measurements on V100, A100, and T4 GPUs. Fairness and reliability audits cite subgroup analysis by age, sex, and site, evaluation under motion artifacts and metal implants, and stress tests with intensity shifts, resolution changes, and corrupted slices. Documentation references model cards, data sheets, and checklists for reporting preprocessing, augmentations, and hyperparameters, along with ablations on crop sizes, patch overlap, and sampling ratios. Licensing and governance note PHI handling, audit logs, and rollback procedures policies. These references frame the landscape for subsequent methodological description.

document: Recommendation systems increasingly operate in interactive settings with evolving user intent. Sequential signals and delayed outcomes motivate modeling choices beyond static supervised learning. Related research blends bandit inference, reinforcement learning, and evaluation protocols tailored to production constraints. Interactive recommendation has leveraged contextual bandits and reinforcement learning with environments such as RecSim (Ie et al., 2019) and RecoGym (Rohde et al., 2018), and datasets including MovieLens, KuaiRec, Yahoo! R6A, and MIND. Algorithms span LinUCB, Thompson Sampling, and UCB1 for bandits, and DQN, DDPG, SAC, and PPO for RL, with slates addressed by SlateQ, ListMLE, and GRU4Rec-based policies and sequence models like SASRec and BERT4Rec for user dynamics. Offline policy evaluation utilizes IPS, SNIPS, Doubly Robust, and Self-Normalized DR estimators, alongside direct models via matrix factorization and neural click predictors, and leaderboard-style comparisons report CTR, GMV, NDCG, and diversity under exposure constraints. Logging policies, propensities, and clipping thresholds are cataloged for reproducibility in batch counterfactual studies with public code releases. Context encoders exploit both short-term recency and long-term preferences across content types. Joint training of retrieval and re-ranking is widely explored for efficiency and relevance. Observation biases and confounding complicate causal interpretation of offline logs. User modeling approaches compare Markov Decision Processes, partially observed settings, and knowledge-aware states, incorporating long-term objectives such as retention, dwell time, and satisfaction proxies measured via surveys and re-engagement. Industrial-scale simulators reference RecSim NG, ReAgent Horizon, and TensorFlow Recommenders Addons, while reward shaping baselines include delayed credit assignment heuristics, eligibility traces, and slates-aware marginal gains with position bias correction using IPW, PBM, and DBN click models. Batch-constrained policy optimization methods mention BCQ, CQL, BRAC, and Conservative Q-Learning, and large-scale candidates are scored with approximate nearest neighbor retrieval such as FAISS, ScaNN, and Milvus before re-ranking via LambdaRank, ListNet, or pairwise hinge objectives. Serving stacks describe feature stores, online training, and shadow deployment, and public reports cite A/B, interleaving, and bandit testing protocols. Domain shift across seasons, events, and inventory churn demands continuous monitoring. Cold-start handling integrates metadata, content understanding, and hierarchical exploration policies. Multi-objective optimization balances engagement, satisfaction, and provider goals under constraints. Simulation complements field testing but remains sensitive to modeling assumptions and misspecification. Fairness considerations note exposure regularization, slate diversity, and user-group parity metrics, with methods such as MADR, xQuAD, and calibrated exposure constraints. Bias correction references counterfactual logging, inverse propensity weighting, drift detection, and deconfounding via front-door or back-door adjustments, and privacy aspects cite differential privacy SGD, federated learning, and secure aggregation across partners. Engineering reports cover feature freshness monitoring, delayed feedback ingestion, and canary rollouts, with dashboards tracking GMV lift, return rate, and session length alongside cost per serving and P99 latencies. Documentation mentions playbooks, runbooks, and reproducible offline pipelines artifacts. These references delineate the methodological space for subsequent discussion.

document: Automatic speech recognition across languages supports accessibility, search, and translation at global scale. Training data and modeling choices vary significantly across domains and resource levels. Prior work surveys architectures, datasets, and evaluation protocols for multilingual systems. Multilingual speech recognition research commonly evaluates on LibriSpeech, Common Voice, VoxPopuli, Multilingual LibriSpeech, and TED-LIUM, spanning read, conversational, and broadcast speech across numerous languages and domains. Representative encoders and acoustic front ends include wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020), HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021), XLS-R (Babu et al., 2021), Conformer (Gulati et al., 2020), and Whisper (Radford et al., 2022), with decoders employing CTC, transducers, or attention. Tokenization ranges from byte-pair encoding and unigram language models to byte-level and UTF-8 units, and reporting includes WER, CER, BLEU, chrF, and BEER, with beam search, length penalties, and shallow fusion tuned on dev sets. Pretraining often leverages multilingual corpora with SpecAugment, speed perturbation, and noise injections and vocabulary sharing across related languages. Language coverage and script diversity impose constraints on tokenization and lexicon design. Resource imbalance and domain shift motivate transfer and adaptation strategies. Streaming use cases require careful trade-offs between accuracy and latency. Adaptation techniques cover language-agnostic pretraining, adapters, and LoRA, with fine-tuning on domain corpora like MLS subsets, Fisher-Callhome, Switchboard, and AMI for conversational robustness and channel mismatch. Code-switching and multilingual transfer are assessed on SEAME, MUCS, and CSJ, while speech translation systems report BLEU and COMET on MuST-C, CoVoST, and Europarl-ST with cascaded ASR+MT and end-to-end ST baselines. Streaming and low-latency regimes benchmark chunked attention, transducer decoding, and online CTC with lookahead, using metrics like real-time factor and average lagging across Librispeech test-clean/test-other, VoxPopuli dev/test, and TED talks. Auxiliary tasks include punctuation and capitalization restoration, speaker diarization via x-vectors and VBx, and inverse text normalization, and toolchains reference ESPnet, Fairseq S2T, NeMo, and SpeechBrain with checkpoints and recipes for reproducible runs and baselines. Open-source toolkits have enabled standardized recipes and transparent comparisons across many settings. Nevertheless, audio quality, speaker accents, and recording conditions continue to limit performance. The interplay between speech recognition and translation tasks raises further evaluation considerations. Model compression and deployment architectures influence cost and energy efficiency. Deployment considerations list on-device inference with int8 and int4 quantization, VAD integration, and endpointing, along with server-side batching, speculative decoding, and waveform caching. Error analysis references per-language WER histograms, phoneme confusion matrices, accent grouping, and noise robustness tests under MUSAN and DEMAND augmentations, with ablations on beam width, LM weight, and CTC/attn interpolation. Ethical and governance discussions mention dataset consent, demographic coverage, language vitality, and label quality, plus documentation in model cards and data statements with release notes and versioned artifacts. Privacy policies cover PII scrubbing and audit logs retention. These references provide context for the empirical studies that follow.

document: Urban mobility systems rely on accurate traffic forecasting to manage congestion and deliver reliable travel times. Recent advances in deep learning have shifted the focus from univariate temporal models to spatiotemporal architectures that exploit road network structure. Graph neural networks provide a natural parameterization for sensor interactions mediated by the topology of transportation networks. Despite notable progress, performance varies widely across cities, sensor densities, and deployment constraints. Furthermore, the reproducibility of reported gains is challenged by heterogeneous preprocessing and unstandardized evaluation protocols. A clear view of data regimes and inductive biases is essential for making models dependable in practice. Representative traffic forecasting approaches include DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, ASTGCN, GMAN, DSTAGNN, AGCRN, and MTGNN, as well as transformer baselines adapted to graphs and temporal convolutions. Common benchmarks span METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03, PEMS04, PEMS07, and PEMS08, with evaluation typically reported via MAE, RMSE, and MAPE under fixed sensor splits, and with auxiliary metadata such as road distances, adjacency construction by k-nearest neighbors, and time-of-day encodings documented across public implementations and leaderboards from academic repositories and widely referenced benchmark papers and reports. However, the field lacks a principled analysis of how graph construction choices interact with horizon length and data sparsity. We investigate the sensitivity of forecasting accuracy to topology definitions, temporal aggregation, and exogenous features under a unified protocol. Our study quantifies the contribution of spatial and temporal components by ablations that hold evaluation metrics and data splits constant. We further examine robustness to missing sensors, corrupted streams, and shift between weekdays and weekends. To support transparent comparisons, we release preprocessing scripts that standardize scaling, masking, and horizon offsets. We also provide lightweight baselines that isolate recurrent, convolutional, and attention mechanisms without architecture-specific heuristics. The resulting insights inform deployment guidelines for agencies operating under compute, latency, and maintenance constraints. Together, these findings illuminate design tradeoffs that matter more than architectural novelty for reliable traffic prediction.
