document: Extracting entities and relations from clinical narratives is vital for cohort identification, pharmacovigilance, and outcome studies. Compared with general-domain text, clinical notes exhibit heavy abbreviation, telegraphic style, and institution-specific terminology. Privacy regulations and annotation burden constrain dataset size and availability, complicating model development. Domain-adaptive pretraining and distant supervision have partially mitigated data scarcity but often degrade calibration and portability. In particular, span boundary ambiguity and nested mentions remain challenging in production pipelines. A systematic comparison of modeling choices under realistic deployment constraints is therefore needed. Prominent clinical IE systems include BiLSTM-CRF pipelines, SciSpaCy entity recognizers, and transformer encoders such as BioBERT, BlueBERT, ClinicalBERT, and PubMedBERT trained on biomedical corpora. Widely used resources comprise the i2b2 2010 concept extraction dataset, the n2c2 2018 adverse drug event shared task, the ShAReCLEF disorder corpus, and MIMIC-III discharge summaries, often evaluated with token-level F1 and strict span F1 under de-identification constraints defined by dataset organizers. Additional benchmarks include MedNLI, BC5CDR, NCBI Disease, and CHEMPROT collections, with standard train dev splits. We focus on de-identified discharge summaries and problem lists to quantify tradeoffs between accuracy, latency, and maintainability. Our framework separates lexical normalization, section detection, and entity recognition to improve modularity and error analysis. We evaluate models under cross-site validation to expose brittleness to institution shifts and note formatting differences. To address label scarcity, we assess selective annotation strategies guided by uncertainty and section-specific sampling. We report calibration curves and threshold-independent metrics to clarify deployment risks beyond micro-averaged F1. We release reproducible preprocessing and inference scripts with unit tests that document assumptions. The results provide operational guidance for clinical IE pipelines in settings where retraining is infrequent and auditing is mandatory.
spans: Prominent clinical IE systems include BiLSTM-CRF pipelines, SciSpaCy entity recognizers, and transformer encoders such as BioBERT, BlueBERT, ClinicalBERT, and PubMedBERT trained on biomedical corpora. Widely used resources comprise the i2b2 2010 concept extraction dataset, the n2c2 2018 adverse drug event shared task, the ShAReCLEF disorder corpus, and MIMIC-III discharge summaries, often evaluated with token-level F1 and strict span F1 under de-identification constraints defined by dataset organizers. Additional benchmarks include MedNLI, BC5CDR, NCBI Disease, and CHEMPROT collections, with standard train dev splits.

document: Recommendation platforms increasingly seek explanations that justify suggested items to end users and auditors. Explanations can improve trust, enable debugging, and satisfy transparency regulations in high-stakes domains. Yet the link between recommendation accuracy and explanation usefulness remains underexplored. Prior work often evaluates with offline proxies that may not reflect user understanding or business goals. Moreover, explanation templates risk leaking recommendation signals and inflating perceived faithfulness. Canonical collaborative and content-aware recommenders encompass matrix factorization, BPR, NCF, NeuMF, NGCF, LightGCN, GATNE, and xDeepFM, along with post-hoc explainers based on attention weights and gradient attribution. Standard evaluation draws on MovieLens 1M, MovieLens 20M, Amazon Product Reviews, Yelp, and LastFM, reporting Hit@K, NDCG@K, MAP, and AUC over leave-one-out splits, while side information such as item descriptions, user reviews, and knowledge graph links is included in several widely used public baselines and benchmark leaderboards track explainability outputs with template-based rationales metrics. Despite the breadth of datasets and algorithms, there is little agreement on how to measure explanation faithfulness and plausibility jointly. We formulate a protocol that disentangles ranking performance from explanation evaluation through counterfactual masking and simulator studies. The protocol defines perturbations that target tokens or entities referenced by the explanation while preserving relevance features. We pair human judgments with automatic robustness metrics to triangulate faithfulness under controlled ablations. To reduce annotation burden, we design a small set of prompts and adversarial candidates that reveal inconsistency quickly. We also analyze calibration of explanation confidence and its correlation with downstream satisfaction proxies. Our experiments compare extractive, template, and generative explainers under identical ranking backbones. The findings surface tradeoffs between user-perceived helpfulness and causal impact on recommendations. We conclude with guidelines for reporting that align explanation quality with practical deployment constraints.
spans: Canonical collaborative and content-aware recommenders encompass matrix factorization, BPR, NCF, NeuMF, NGCF, LightGCN, GATNE, and xDeepFM, along with post-hoc explainers based on attention weights and gradient attribution. Standard evaluation draws on MovieLens 1M, MovieLens 20M, Amazon Product Reviews, Yelp, and LastFM, reporting Hit@K, NDCG@K, MAP, and AUC over leave-one-out splits, while side information such as item descriptions, user reviews, and knowledge graph links is included in several widely used public baselines and benchmark leaderboards track explainability outputs with template-based rationales metrics.

document: On-device speech recognition enables private, low-latency voice interfaces without continuous connectivity. Federated learning offers a means to train acoustic and language models across many devices without centralizing raw audio. However, user heterogeneity, intermittent connectivity, and hardware diversity complicate optimization stability and evaluation. Distribution shift across accents, background noise, and speaking styles further degrades aggregate performance. Standard centralized recipes do not readily translate to federated protocols due to communication and privacy constraints. There is a need to separate algorithmic advances from confounding factors introduced by data partitioning and deployment choices. We therefore examine benchmarks and formulations that isolate the dominant sources of variance in reported gains. End-to-end ASR architectures commonly include CTC, LAS, RNN-T, and Conformer models trained on corpora such as LibriSpeech, TED-LIUM, and Common Voice. In federated settings, optimization methods like FedAvg, FedProx, Scaffold, and Mime have been adopted alongside secure aggregation and differential privacy mechanisms, with evaluations reporting word error rate on held-out speakers and simulated device cohorts. Additional resources include AISHELL, Switchboard, Fisher, and VoxPopuli, with non-iid partitions, participation dropouts, and on-device quantization constraints documented in public baselines and open-source simulation toolkits widely. We introduce a simulator that reproduces client sampling, local computation limits, and drift patterns measured from real applications. The study compares partial fine-tuning strategies that adapt front ends or language models while freezing acoustic encoders. We analyze update clipping, adaptive optimizers, and client weighting schemes under identical privacy budgets. Our protocol emphasizes robust baselines with transparent hyperparameters and reproducible preprocessing. Results reveal tradeoffs between accuracy, uplink cost, and fairness across demographic groups. These insights inform practitioners deploying federated ASR under tight privacy and latency requirements.
spans: End-to-end ASR architectures commonly include CTC, LAS, RNN-T, and Conformer models trained on corpora such as LibriSpeech, TED-LIUM, and Common Voice. In federated settings, optimization methods like FedAvg, FedProx, Scaffold, and Mime have been adopted alongside secure aggregation and differential privacy mechanisms, with evaluations reporting word error rate on held-out speakers and simulated device cohorts. Additional resources include AISHELL, Switchboard, Fisher, and VoxPopuli, with non-iid partitions, participation dropouts, and on-device quantization constraints documented in public baselines and open-source simulation toolkits widely.

document: Models trained on one dataset often fail when deployed in environments with different textures, viewpoints, or devices. Domain generalization studies aim to learn features that transfer to unseen domains without access to target data. Beyond average accuracy, practitioners require reliability across sites and operating conditions. However, heterogeneous protocols and backbones make it difficult to attribute improvements to specific ideas. Research on domain generalization has explored invariance penalties such as IRM and VREx, distribution alignment via MMD and CORAL, augmentation strategies including Mixup and RandAugment, and meta-learning approaches like MLDG, MetaReg, and SWAD. Benchmarks commonly include PACS, VLCS, Office-Home, TerraIncognita, and DomainNet, with evaluation averaging accuracy across held-out domains under fixed backbones and training seeds documented by public codebases. Additional datasets feature Camelyon17, FMoW, and WILDS collections, while robustness metrics consider worst-domain accuracy, calibration error, and shift-induced degradation curves in practice. We present a controlled evaluation that isolates architectural, data, and optimization factors using a common training pipeline. The design ensures identical backbones, augmentations, and early stopping rules for all methods. We further probe the role of domain count, sample size imbalance, and label shift on generalization behavior. To assess reliability, we measure performance stratified by object frequency and scene attributes derived from interpretable tags. Our analysis highlights when invariance penalties help and when they conflict with class separation. We complement accuracy with calibration and abstention metrics relevant for risk-sensitive applications. Finally, we report compute-normalized results to contextualize gains against resource budgets. All code, logs, and random seeds are released to support independent verification. The study clarifies which components matter most for robust cross-domain recognition.
spans: Research on domain generalization has explored invariance penalties such as IRM and VREx, distribution alignment via MMD and CORAL, augmentation strategies including Mixup and RandAugment, and meta-learning approaches like MLDG, MetaReg, and SWAD. Benchmarks commonly include PACS, VLCS, Office-Home, TerraIncognita, and DomainNet, with evaluation averaging accuracy across held-out domains under fixed backbones and training seeds documented by public codebases. Additional datasets feature Camelyon17, FMoW, and WILDS collections, while robustness metrics consider worst-domain accuracy, calibration error, and shift-induced degradation curves in practice.

document: Federated learning enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data, an appealing property for sensitive domains such as healthcare. Nevertheless, real-world deployments must balance utility, privacy, and system efficiency under non-iid client behavior. Recent work on federated learning surveys privacy-preserving optimization, communication-efficient updates, and personalization across heterogeneous clients (McMahan et al., 2017; Konečný et al., 2016; Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020), plus robust aggregation and adversarial resilience (Blanchard et al., 2017; Yin et al., 2018) and benchmark suites such as LEAF and FedML (Caldas et al., 2018; He et al., 2020). Despite extensive benchmarking, questions remain about evaluation fidelity when clinical constraints distort participation and labeling. Prior results also reveal brittle behavior when client data distributions drift over time due to practice changes or sensor aging. We focus on cross-silo settings where institutions exhibit persistent statistical heterogeneity and intermittent connectivity. Considerations extend from optimization stability to governance, given strict audit requirements and shifting consent policies. In healthcare-focused deployments, data modalities and evaluation setups follow prior datasets and pipelines, drawing on brain tumor segmentation and pathology cohorts, sensing corpora, and cross-silo EMR benchmarks (Menze et al., 2015; Sheller et al., 2020; Rieke et al., 2020; Saeed et al., 2011). Methodologically, client-drift mitigation, proximal regularization, adaptive client weighting, and meta-learning variants recur across studies (Sahu et al., 2018; Li et al., 2020; Reddi et al., 2021; Fallah et al., 2020), while privacy techniques span secure aggregation, differential privacy, and homomorphic encryption (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Abadi et al., 2016; Aono et al., 2017). Systems work references aggregation, asynchronous updates, and scheduling on benchmarks such as LEAF, OARF, and FedML (Caldas et al., 2018; Nishio and Yonetani, 2019; He et al., 2020). These references demonstrate the breadth of tooling, but the interplay between methodological and operational axes remains underexplored in practice. Consequently, design choices should be examined under workload models that capture clinical cadence and resource envelopes. We thus formulate a study to stress-test training and privacy budgets under realistic participation regimes.

In healthcare-focused deployments, data modalities and evaluation setups follow prior datasets and pipelines, drawing on brain tumor segmentation and pathology cohorts, sensing corpora, and cross-silo EMR benchmarks (Menze et al., 2015; Sheller et al., 2020; Rieke et al., 2020; Saeed et al., 2011). Methodologically, client-drift mitigation, proximal regularization, adaptive client weighting, and meta-learning variants recur across studies (Sahu et al., 2018; Li et al., 2020; Reddi et al., 2021; Fallah et al., 2020), while privacy techniques span secure aggregation, differential privacy, and homomorphic encryption (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Abadi et al., 2016; Aono et al., 2017). Systems work references aggregation, asynchronous updates, and scheduling on benchmarks such as LEAF, OARF, and FedML (Caldas et al., 2018; Nishio and Yonetani, 2019; He et al., 2020).

document: Dynamic relations in real-world networks often entail time-varying connectivity and context-sensitive semantics. Graph neural networks include spectral and spatial message-passing variants for node, edge, and graph prediction (Kipf and Welling, 2017; Hamilton et al., 2017; Gilmer et al., 2017; Veličković et al., 2018), with pooling approaches (Ying et al., 2018; Lee et al., 2019) and evaluation on citation graphs including Cora, PubMed, Reddit, and OGB (Sen et al., 2008; Hu et al., 2020). Despite this breadth, temporal information introduces challenges in balancing expressivity and scalability for evolving graphs. Forecasting links or events requires handling censored histories and irregular observation schedules. Practical systems must also consider evaluation leakage when splitting streams by time. We center the discussion on temporal link prediction with inductive generalization across entities. Temporal and relational reasoning has been approached through dynamic graph embeddings, temporal point process models, and knowledge graph completion objectives, evaluated on benchmarks and datasets such as ICEWS, GDELT, WIKIDATA5M, and FB15k-237 (Trivedi et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2020; Jin et al., 2020; Toutanova and Chen, 2015). Architectures include recurrent or attention-based message passing with time encodings, decay mechanisms, and snapshot aggregations (Rossi et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2019; Kazemi and Goel, 2018), while negative sampling, adversarial training, and calibration strategies are common training heuristics (Sun et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019; He et al., 2018). Evaluation protocols vary across inductive versus transductive settings and micro- versus macro-temporal granularity, with metrics spanning MRR, Hits@k, and AUC and temporal coverage analysis. Prior findings highlight sensitivity to negative sampling choices and windowing policies during training. Reproducibility further depends on standardizing timestamp normalization and edge de-duplication procedures. Consequently, comparisons should be drawn under consistent filtering rules and time-aware baselines. The remainder of this paper outlines datasets, models, and protocols used in our study.

Temporal and relational reasoning has been approached through dynamic graph embeddings, temporal point process models, and knowledge graph completion objectives, evaluated on benchmarks and datasets such as ICEWS, GDELT, WIKIDATA5M, and FB15k-237 (Trivedi et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2020; Jin et al., 2020; Toutanova and Chen, 2015). document: Multimodal translation seeks to exploit auxiliary signals to disambiguate meaning and resolve referents across languages. Research at the intersection of speech, vision, and translation explores speech-to-text translation without transcripts, image-guided machine translation, and alignments (Berard et al., 2016; Weiss et al., 2017; Elliott et al., 2017; Caglayan et al., 2019), with datasets including MuST-C, How2, Multi30K, and Flickr30k Entities (Di Gangi et al., 2019; Sanabria et al., 2018; Elliott et al., 2016; Plummer et al., 2015). Such resources challenge models to learn grounded correspondences beyond purely textual supervision. However, modality gaps and alignment noise complicate optimization under scarce annotations. Practical systems must resource-balance encoders while meeting latency constraints for streaming inputs. We concentrate on data regimes that mix scripted and conversational domains with heterogeneous visuals. Additionally, we consider label sparsity in long-tail phenomena and underrepresented languages. Multimodal pre-training for translation leverages contrastive objectives, masked prediction, and alignment losses over speech, text, and images, building on frameworks such as CLIP, wav2vec, and M3P, and corpora like LAION, VoxPopuli, and CoVoST (Radford et al., 2021; Baevski et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2020; Schuhmann et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020). Methods span cross-attention fusion, gated adapters, and modality-specific encoders with shared multilingual subword vocabularies (Lu et al., 2019; Tsai et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2021), while training recipes report curriculum schedules, temperature scaling, and multilingual balancing heuristics (Conneau et al., 2020; Arivazhagan et al., 2019; Bapna and Firat, 2019). Evaluations use BLEU, chrF, and COMET on MuST-C, How2, and Multi30K in zero-shot conditions across languages globally. These ingredients frame design choices about representation sharing and capacity allocation across modalities. We emphasize comparisons under identical tokenization, audio feature extraction, and image normalization. The following section outlines datasets, baselines, and metrics used in our experiments.
spans: Research at the intersection of speech, vision, and translation explores speech-to-text translation without transcripts, image-guided machine translation, and alignments (Berard et al., 2016; Weiss et al., 2017; Elliott et al., 2017; Caglayan et al., 2019), with datasets including MuST-C, How2, Multi30K, and Flickr30k Entities (Di Gangi et al., 2019; Sanabria et al., 2018; Elliott et al., 2016; Plummer et al., 2015).

Multimodal pre-training for translation leverages contrastive objectives, masked prediction, and alignment losses over speech, text, and images, building on frameworks such as CLIP, wav2vec, and M3P, and corpora like LAION, VoxPopuli, and CoVoST (Radford et al., 2021; Baevski et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2020; Schuhmann et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020). Methods span cross-attention fusion, gated adapters, and modality-specific encoders with shared multilingual subword vocabularies (Lu et al., 2019; Tsai et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2021), while training recipes report curriculum schedules, temperature scaling, and multilingual balancing heuristics (Conneau et al., 2020; Arivazhagan et al., 2019; Bapna and Firat, 2019). Evaluations use BLEU, chrF, and COMET on MuST-C, How2, and Multi30K in zero-shot conditions across languages globally.

document: Software engineering tasks increasingly rely on learned representations to summarize and assess source code artifacts. Static and dynamic signals offer complementary evidence under diverse coding conventions and project histories. Neural code understanding spans sequence-to-sequence models, graph-based encoders, and pretrained models over source code, with applications to summarization, code search, and vulnerability detection (Alon et al., 2019; Hellendoorn et al., 2020; Ahmad et al., 2020; Feng et al., 2020), and evaluation on datasets such as CodeSearchNet, CodeXGLUE, and Defects4J (Husain et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2021; Just et al., 2014). Yet training data for security-oriented tasks often suffers from skewed labeling and duplication. Differences in build systems and language dialects further complicate preprocessing and normalization. This paper examines cross-project generalization for vulnerability detection and summary generation. We consider settings where only limited metadata is available for supervision and filtering. Classical program analysis contributes static taint tracking, abstract interpretation, and symbolic execution, supported by tools and frameworks including CodeQL, Infer, Frama-C, KLEE, and angr (Sui et al., 2016; Calcagno et al., 2015; Cousot and Cousot, 1977; Cadar et al., 2008; Shoshitaishvili et al., 2016). Empirical studies report benchmarks and datasets such as Juliet, SARD, Devign, and BigCloneBench with tasks covering vulnerability identification, clone detection, and bug localization (NIST, 2014; Zhou et al., 2019; Svajlenko et al., 2014). Hybrid approaches integrate mined patterns, program dependence graphs, and API usage models for richer features (Li et al., 2016; Krinke, 2001; LeClair et al., 2019), while evaluation protocols emphasize precision-recall curves, ROC-AUC, F1, and cost-effectiveness metrics over labeled functions and commits, and detailed repository-level change histories. These resources and techniques motivate a systematic look at evaluation design and artifact curation. We discuss protocols for temporal splits aligned to release cycles and dependency changes. Finally, we outline the experimental setup, baselines, and ablation criteria.

document: Recommendation platforms interact with users over time, turning ranking into a sequential decision process. Data shifts and exposure biases complicate learning and evaluation under bandit feedback. Interactive recommendation draws on contextual bandits, slate decision models, and reinforcement learning with feedback loops (Li et al., 2010; Swaminathan et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2019; Ie et al., 2019), coupled with training on public benchmark datasets like MovieLens, Criteo, RecSys Challenge, and KuaiRec (Harper and Konstan, 2016; Juan et al., 2016; Jannach et al., 2013; Gao et al., 2022). Real-world systems must contend with delayed rewards and confounded behavior logs. Reward shaping and diversity constraints introduce additional trade-offs between exploration and satisfaction. We study learning and evaluation protocols that avoid logging-policy overfitting. Environments also require safety mechanisms limiting harmful or low-quality exposures during learning. Off-policy evaluation in recommendation relies on estimators that correct for logging bias using propensity scores, importance sampling variants, and model-based value functions (Rosenbaum and Rubin, 1983; Horvitz and Thompson, 1952; Dudík et al., 2014; Jiang and Li, 2016). Methods include IPS, SNIPS, DR, DML, and fitted Q evaluation with doubly robust extensions, shrinkage, and self-normalization (Swaminathan and Joachims, 2015; Dudík et al., 2011; Feng et al., 2021; Le et al., 2019), with slate-specific corrections and position bias (Joachims et al., 2017; Ai et al., 2018). Experimental practice reports replay and simulators on datasets such as MovieLens, Yahoo! R6A, Criteo, and KuaiRec, using regret and off-policy accuracy as metrics (Li et al., 2011; Harper and Konstan, 2016; Chapelle et al., 2015; Gao et al., 2022). These ingredients motivate attention to confidence intervals and diagnostics when reporting policy gains. We discuss experimental designs that align with deployment constraints and audit requirements. The rest of the paper describes datasets, policies, and estimators used in our experiments.

Experimental practice reports replay and simulators on datasets such as MovieLens, Yahoo! R6A, Criteo, and KuaiRec, using regret and off-policy accuracy as metrics (Li et al., 2011; Harper and Konstan, 2016; Chapelle et al., 2015; Gao et al., 2022).

Federated learning has emerged as a compelling paradigm for training models across institutions without centralizing raw data. In clinical imaging, this promise intersects with strict privacy laws and fragmented infrastructures that complicate data sharing. Hospitals maintain heterogeneous scanners, labeling practices, and software stacks, which collectively challenge optimization and evaluation. Moreover, outcome shifts and demographic skews can bias site-specific models in ways that undermine equitable performance. These pressures motivate algorithms that can learn robustly from decentralized data while honoring regulatory and operational constraints. Yet methodological choices around communication, personalization, and privacy budgeting remain underexplored in domain-specific settings. Our study focuses on chest radiography classification across multiple health systems subject to diverse governance policies. We investigate how aggregation noise, client dropout, and label prevalence affect convergence and calibration.

Prior work has documented that non identically distributed data leads to slower training and degraded generalization. Instability often arises when gradient directions across sites diverge, amplifying drift under limited communication budgets. Personalization layers and mixture-of-experts style adapters have been proposed to bridge site idiosyncrasies while retaining a shared backbone. However, clinical deployments also confront auditing needs that require interpretable summaries of contribution and risk. Communication-efficient updates must also coexist with encryption protocols and intermittent network availability. In this context, careful benchmarking is essential to disentangle algorithmic benefits from infrastructure artifacts. We therefore align protocol design with measurement principles that emphasize fairness, uncertainty, and label provenance. We outline ablations that expose when reweighting, momentum schedules, or adaptive client selection drive improvements.

Early optimization strategies emphasize simple weight averaging and client sampling (McMahan et al., 2017; Konečný et al., 2016), while stabilization under heterogeneous devices is addressed by proximal regularization and adaptive aggregation (Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020). Privacy and security components include secure aggregation and differential privacy mechanisms (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Dwork et al., 2006), and evaluations rely on imaging benchmarks such as CheXpert, MIMIC-CXR, BraTS, and UK Biobank (Irvin et al., 2019; Johnson et al., 2019; Menze et al., 2015; Sudlow et al., 2015). 

While these ingredients provide building blocks, healthcare settings complicate assumptions about participation and stability. Many hospitals enforce maintenance windows that yield predictable but prolonged client unavailability. Labeling operations frequently lag imaging acquisition by weeks, introducing temporal feedback loops into training data. These practicalities motivate protocols that adaptively schedule clients while preserving privacy budgets. They also require calibration strategies that withstand sporadic updates and evolving case mixes. We evaluate sensitivity to client churn and site size through controlled drop patterns and synthetic resampling. For interpretability, we integrate counterfactual attention maps subject to site-specific constraints on gradient access. Our metrics include subgroup AUC gaps, expected calibration error, and privacy cost profiles.

The study design reflects three goals tied to external validity in clinical operations. First, we aim to decouple statistical heterogeneity from communication volatility in estimates of model improvement. Second, we seek to characterize how privacy mechanisms interact with calibration under shift. Third, we probe whether lightweight personalization can reduce disparities without widening performance variance. The resulting insights inform policy choices about participation thresholds and reporting obligations. They also suggest how to align engineering roadmaps with governance expectations for safety monitoring. We release code to encourage reproducibility under constrained networking assumptions. Institutional review approvals and data use agreements governed all data handling and experiment execution.
spans: Early optimization strategies emphasize simple weight averaging and client sampling (McMahan et al., 2017; Konečný et al., 2016), while stabilization under heterogeneous devices is addressed by proximal regularization and adaptive aggregation (Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020). Privacy and security components include secure aggregation and differential privacy mechanisms (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Dwork et al., 2006), and evaluations rely on imaging benchmarks such as CheXpert, MIMIC-CXR, BraTS, and UK Biobank (Irvin et al., 2019; Johnson et al., 2019; Menze et al., 2015; Sudlow et al., 2015).

Effective feedback accelerates learning by pinpointing misconceptions and highlighting actionable next steps. In large courses, providing timely individualized guidance strains instructor bandwidth and delays remediation. Natural language generation offers a scalable alternative but must balance accuracy, tone, and pedagogical value. Models that draft suggestions from student work risk hallucinations that can mislead or demotivate. Educational settings further demand transparency about evidence and alignment with rubrics and standards. These challenges motivate systems that are helpful yet cautious under real classroom constraints. We investigate feedback generation for short factual responses in introductory science courses. Our framework emphasizes faithfulness to source evidence and calibrated uncertainty communication.

Prior approaches fall into retrieval-augmented generation, classification with templated text, and edit-based rewriting. Retrieval locates relevant passages or exemplars that anchor claims to trusted content. Classification techniques map responses to rubric categories that drive structured messages. Edit models transform student answers toward reference solutions while preserving student voice. Despite progress, limited domain transfer and brittle error detection hinder adoption. Human raters also report that tone consistency and politeness remain uneven across cohorts. We accordingly adopt evaluation protocols that foreground both correctness and perceived helpfulness. Our data curation captures diverse phrasings, partial credit cases, and adversarial paraphrases.

For deployment realism, we restrict access to course materials and disallow internet retrieval. Uncertainty estimates drive abstention when evidence falls below calibrated thresholds. We benchmark ablations to quantify contributions from retrieval, calibration, and style conditioning. Pretrained sequence models and instruction-tuned variants underpin many approaches to automated feedback, including BERT, T5, and GPT-3 style decoders (Devlin et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020; Ouyang et al., 2022). Evaluation and benchmarking draw on question answering and educational platforms such as SQuAD, GLUE, EdNet, and ASSISTments, alongside rubric-annotated corpora for short answers and essays (Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2019; Choi et al., 2020; Heffernan and Heffernan, 2014). ASAP and BEA shared tasks are additionally used in several studies. 

Beyond task framing, feedback systems must navigate privacy obligations and student consent. Log retention policies and model updates should avoid leaking identifiable information. We implement privacy-preserving analytics that aggregate error types without storing raw answers. To ground claims, we integrate retrieval over instructor-authored notes with citation snippets. A style controller moderates verbosity and formality to respect course norms. We instruct models to defer to uncertainty rather than guess when knowledge is missing. The evaluation protocol blends automatic metrics with double-blind human judgments. We report inter-rater reliability and annotate disagreements to surface ambiguous cases. Error analyses categorize hallucination, omission, and misinterpretation across knowledge types. The findings inform rubrics and heuristics for better prompt designs and safeguards. These practices aim to strengthen trustworthiness and reduce instructor overhead without overpromising capability. All experiments complied with institutional policies on student data and informed consent.
spans: Pretrained sequence models and instruction-tuned variants underpin many approaches to automated feedback, including BERT, T5, and GPT-3 style decoders (Devlin et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020; Ouyang et al., 2022). Evaluation and benchmarking draw on question answering and educational platforms such as SQuAD, GLUE, EdNet, and ASSISTments, alongside rubric-annotated corpora for short answers and essays (Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2019; Choi et al., 2020; Heffernan and Heffernan, 2014). ASAP and BEA shared tasks are additionally used in several studies.

document:
Summarization enables readers to digest events quickly without scanning full articles. For international newsrooms, the need extends across languages with divergent discourse structures. Multilingual summarization systems must compress information while preserving salience and factual grounding. Low-resource languages amplify the challenge due to scarce high-quality training pairs. Additionally, stylistic norms about attribution and hedging vary across outlets and regions. These factors complicate evaluation because reference summaries capture only a slice of acceptable renderings. Our work studies cross-lingual abstractive summarization for global news feeds. We target scenarios where source articles and target summaries occupy different languages.

A central question is how to leverage translation signals without overfitting to literal phrasing. Naively translating sources and fine-tuning monolingual models often yields brittle paraphrases. We therefore examine architectures and data curation strategies that encourage language-agnostic content planning. The approach integrates contrastive supervision to penalize spurious copying across languages. Cross-lingual summarization draws on multilingual pretrained encoders and decoders such as mBART, mT5, and PEGASUS (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020). Public resources for training and evaluation include CNN/Daily Mail, XSum, XL-Sum, and WikiLingua, sometimes paired with machine-translated proxies for low-resource targets (Hermann et al., 2015; Narayan et al., 2018; Hasan et al., 2021; Ladhak et al., 2020). Additional baselines also include extractive heuristics and pointer-generator models with ranking components (Nallapati et al., 2016; See et al., 2017; Luo et al., 2019). 

Beyond modeling, we focus on calibration and verifiability under domain shift. We incorporate retrieval from multilingual wire services to ground claims. Evidence pointers are surfaced in the target language alongside segment-level uncertainty. Evaluators can click citations to inspect support for each salient statement. We train with a mixture of supervised and contrastive losses to balance fluency and faithfulness. A length controller allows editors to select headline, brief, or digest formats. We report performance on languages spanning three families and two scripts. Expert evaluation uses calibrated scales for accuracy, style, and geopolitical neutrality.

Automatic metrics include ROUGE variants and semantic similarity measures aligned with translation quality. We supplement these with entity-level consistency checks across languages. To stress test robustness, we introduce noisy machine-translated sources and domain-shifted topics. Ablations probe the contributions of retrieval, contrastive learning, and decoding constraints. We also audit for hallucinated entities and unsupported attributions. Results highlight trade-offs between compression rate and factual precision in low-resource settings. Case studies illustrate successes and failure modes across named entities and events. The analysis connects modeling choices with observed calibration behaviors. We discuss implications for news production workflows and editorial oversight. Resources and code will be released to facilitate reproducibility and shared evaluation. Ethical considerations cover anonymity of sources and amplification risks in sensitive coverage.

Public resources for training and evaluation include CNN/Daily Mail, XSum, XL-Sum, and WikiLingua, sometimes paired with machine-translated proxies for low-resource targets (Hermann et al., 2015; Narayan et al., 2018; Hasan et al., 2021; Ladhak et al., 2020). Additional baselines also include extractive heuristics and pointer-generator models with ranking components (Nallapati et al., 2016; See et al., 2017; Luo et al., 2019).

Urban mobility systems hinge on accurate forecasts of traffic speeds and volumes. Operators adjust signals and dispatch resources based on near-term predictions of flow. Long-range planning also depends on structural understanding of demand and bottlenecks. Graph neural networks encode roadway topology to capture spatial dependencies among sensors. Temporal dynamics are modeled through recurrence, convolution, or attention mechanisms. Nevertheless, deployments confront missing data, sensor outages, and distribution shift from events. In many cities, seasonal patterns interact with infrastructure projects in complex ways. Our work addresses reliability under perturbations in network connectivity and observation density.

We consider a scenario where sensors intermittently fail due to maintenance and weather. The model must interpolate missing streams while maintaining calibrated uncertainty. We propose training objectives that tie uncertainty to reconstruction difficulty. To emulate outages, we mask segments according to empirically observed failure durations. We additionally test robustness to topology errors from outdated maps. Performance is summarized at corridor and network levels to reflect operational needs. We provide a framework for stress testing different degradation regimes. This aligns evaluation with real constraints faced by transport agencies.

Prior work has investigated data imputation and robust training, but often in isolation. We connect these strands through joint objectives that penalize brittle feature use. Ablations reveal how each component contributes to accuracy and calibration under shift. Comparisons span model capacity, training budgets, and outage severity to permit fair assessment. Spatiotemporal forecasting models often build on graph recurrent and convolutional architectures, including DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, and ASTGCN (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2019). Common evaluation datasets encompass METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, and PEMS08, with error metrics such as MAE, RMSE, and MAPE standardized across studies (Jagadish et al., 2014; Li et al., 2018; Song et al., 2020). Benchmarks compare temporal attention, diffusion operators, and spectral filters across graphs and horizons (Zheng et al., 2020; Bai et al., 2020). 

Building on this context, we emphasize stress-tested estimates under partial observability. We calibrate interval forecasts to maintain coverage as sensors drop out. The training loss balances point accuracy with uncertainty sharpness and validity. We design metrics that expose overconfidence under bursty missingness patterns. Interpretability modules trace which neighbors drive predictions during outages. We analyze whether learned dependencies align with domain expectations about corridors. Case studies illustrate responses to stadium events and inclement weather. Findings inform maintenance scheduling and sensor redundancy decisions. The codebase supports reproducible ablations and standardized stress scenarios. Partnerships with agencies enable validation on archived incident logs. Ethical considerations address equity in service impacts from adaptive control.

Common evaluation datasets encompass METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, and PEMS08, with error metrics such as MAE, RMSE, and MAPE standardized across studies (Jagadish et al., 2014; Li et al., 2018; Song et al., 2020). Benchmarks compare temporal attention, diffusion operators, and spectral filters across graphs and horizons (Zheng et al., 2020; Bai et al., 2020).
