Face recognition systems increasingly mediate access control, device unlocking, and forensic triage. Their societal impact has sparked scrutiny of accuracy, bias, and accountability. Error rates vary across demographic groups due to data imbalance, imaging conditions, and modeling choices. Vendors face evolving regulations that demand transparency about performance and training data. Technical work therefore intersects with policy requirements and community expectations. Our study investigates fairness interventions under domain shift in unconstrained imagery. We focus on open-set verification where thresholds affect false match risks. The goal is to reduce disparities while preserving overall security posture.

In operational settings, faces appear under varied lighting, pose, and occlusion. Surveillance cameras also introduce compression and motion blur that distort features. These conditions interact with demographic attributes such as skin tone and age. Methods that perform well on curated web photos can degrade substantially in practice. We adopt a pipeline that disentangles representation, thresholding, and calibration. Each component offers opportunities to trade off accuracy and equity. Our evaluation emphasizes coverage of hard operational cases and transparent reporting. We use stratified splits that control for confounders in background and environment. This design isolates the effect of interventions on subgroup metrics.

Face recognition benchmarks commonly involve LFW, IJB-A/B/C, VGGFace2, and MegaFace, with protocol variations across verification and identification tasks (Huang et al., 2007; Klare et al., 2015; Cao et al., 2018; Kemelmacher-Shlizerman et al., 2016). Prominent loss functions and backbones include SphereFace, CosFace, and ArcFace trained on large-scale web scrapes (Liu et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018; Deng et al., 2019). Fairness assessments frequently report subgroup false positive and true positive rates alongside demographic parity and equalized odds style summaries (Hardt et al., 2016; Buolamwini and Gebru, 2018). 

We complement these metrics with cost-sensitive analyses for access control scenarios. Calibrated likelihood ratios provide interpretable scores under varying priors. To improve equity, we study debiasing pretext tasks and balanced hard mining. A temperature scaling step aligns scores across subgroups before thresholding. We also explore mixture thresholds conditioned on quality estimators to mitigate disparities. Robustness is examined under simulated compression and motion blur perturbations. We quantify trade-offs between security loss and fairness gain across settings. Ablations compare representation training, sampling, and calibration components. A qualitative audit inspects failure modes by pose, occlusion, and illumination. We document any accuracy regressions on the most represented groups. The results inform procurement guidelines and disclosure templates for agencies. Our codebase includes tools to compute group metrics with confidence intervals. All datasets were processed under consent and applicable data protection laws. We discuss limitations related to annotation consistency and demographic taxonomies.
spans: Face recognition benchmarks commonly involve LFW, IJB-A/B/C, VGGFace2, and MegaFace, with protocol variations across verification and identification tasks (Huang et al., 2007; Klare et al., 2015; Cao et al., 2018; Kemelmacher-Shlizerman et al., 2016). Prominent loss functions and backbones include SphereFace, CosFace, and ArcFace trained on large-scale web scrapes (Liu et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018; Deng et al., 2019). Fairness assessments frequently report subgroup false positive and true positive rates alongside demographic parity and equalized odds style summaries (Hardt et al., 2016; Buolamwini and Gebru, 2018).

document: Multimodal machine translation seeks to ground target language generation in both linguistic and visual context. Despite recent progress, systems still struggle when text alone is underspecified or pragmatically ambiguous. Visual cues can disambiguate referents, resolve ellipsis, and guide lexical choice, yet integrating them reliably remains challenging. Empirical gains are sensitive to data scale, domain shift, and the granularity of visual representations. Moreover, evaluation has largely emphasized BLEU and METEOR, which may understate improvements from grounded reasoning. This motivates a closer examination of modeling choices that determine how and when images influence token prediction. We focus on encoder design, fusion strategies, and the interaction between pretraining and fine-tuning across domains. To situate our study, we summarize representative datasets and architectures used in prior multimodal translation research. Early multimodal translation used global CNN features combined with attention over the source sentence to condition decoding models (Specia et al., 2016; Elliott et al., 2017). Subsequent work replaced global vectors with region-level features from Faster R-CNN, enabling bottom-up attention and grounding of choices (Anderson et al., 2018; Delbrouck and Dupont, 2017). Pretrained vision-language encoders such as ViLBERT, LXMERT, and UNITER align modalities for translation tasks (Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019; Chen et al., 2020). Benchmarks include Multi30K, COCO Captions, and WMT multimodal tasks; studies examine transformer backbones, visual adapters, and contrastive pretraining (Elliott et al., 2016; Bugliarello et al., 2021). Collectively, these lines of work highlight the breadth of available supervision but leave open questions about the timing and selectivity of visual conditioning. In particular, global features can overwhelm textual cues in low-resource settings, whereas region signals may be too sparse for idiomatic expressions. Pretrained cross-modal encoders offer stronger alignments, yet their objectives can conflict with autoregressive decoding. We therefore isolate fusion depth and gating as primary levers for controlling visual influence. Our design contrasts shallow prefix fusion with mid-layer cross-attention under matched parameter budgets. We also examine whether contrastive objectives at pretraining time translate into consistent BLEU and COMET gains under domain shift. To reduce confounds, we hold textual encoders and tokenization fixed while varying only the visual pathway. We augment standard metrics with targeted contrast sets that probe referent resolution and color adjective grounding. These tests allow us to separate fluency from grounding, emphasizing cases where visuals should matter. Finally, we analyze failure modes using counterfactual image swaps and masked-region ablations. This analysis clarifies when image features serve as priors versus evidence in the decoder. The resulting perspective connects architectural decisions to measurable gains in grounded translation.
spans: Early multimodal translation used global CNN features combined with attention over the source sentence to condition decoding models (Specia et al., 2016; Elliott et al., 2017). Subsequent work replaced global vectors with region-level features from Faster R-CNN, enabling bottom-up attention and grounding of choices (Anderson et al., 2018; Delbrouck and Dupont, 2017). Pretrained vision-language encoders such as ViLBERT, LXMERT, and UNITER align modalities for translation tasks (Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019; Chen et al., 2020). Benchmarks include Multi30K, COCO Captions, and WMT multimodal tasks; studies examine transformer backbones, visual adapters, and contrastive pretraining (Elliott et al., 2016; Bugliarello et al., 2021).

document: Prompt-based learning reframes task instructions as part of the model input rather than external supervision. This shift emphasizes leveraging pretrained knowledge with minimal task-specific updates. However, prompt design, stability under distributional shifts, and data efficiency remain open challenges. We review approaches that operationalize prompts across discrete and continuous parameterizations. Early prompting relied on manual templates and few-shot in-context exemplars, as shown by GPT-3 on GLUE and SuperGLUE (Brown et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019). Pattern-exploiting training and verbalizer design stabilize performance with small labeled sets, including PET and LM-BFF on classification tasks (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021). Soft prompting methods optimize continuous vectors or prefixes while freezing backbones, covering P-tuning, prefix-tuning, and prompt tuning (Liu et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021) schemes. Instruction finetuning extends prompting with multitask collections such as T0, FLAN, and Super-NaturalInstructions, with gains on ANLI and MNLI (Sanh et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022). These developments trace a movement from handcrafted cues toward learned interfaces between instructions and representations. Yet the relative benefits of discrete versus continuous prompts are confounded by backbone size and optimization protocols. For small models, prompt tuning can underfit semantics, while for large models, manual templates can underutilize capacity. Likewise, instruction finetuning intermingles data size and format, blurring the role of verbalization. We unify evaluation by controlling backbone, batch size, and token budget across methods. Our analysis isolates the interaction between prompt locality and layer-wise adaptation. We disentangle template lexicalization from label mapping to clarify when verbalizers dominate. We further measure calibration under prompt perturbations to assess robustness. To avoid cherry-picking, we fix search spaces and reuse identical seeds. We complement aggregate scores with targeted diagnostics on compositional generalization. These diagnostics track consistency across paraphrases and unseen label names. We also examine negative transfer when instructions span heterogeneous tasks. The study highlights sensitivity to initialization and the importance of curvature-aware optimizers. Finally, we discuss compute trade-offs relative to full finetuning under tight latency budgets. Together, these findings ground practical recommendations for selecting prompt mechanisms. They also illuminate remaining gaps that motivate structured prompting and hybrid adaptation.
spans: Early prompting relied on manual templates and few-shot in-context exemplars, as shown by GPT-3 on GLUE and SuperGLUE (Brown et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019). Pattern-exploiting training and verbalizer design stabilize performance with small labeled sets, including PET and LM-BFF on classification tasks (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021). Soft prompting methods optimize continuous vectors or prefixes while freezing backbones, covering P-tuning, prefix-tuning, and prompt tuning (Liu et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021) schemes. Instruction finetuning extends prompting with multitask collections such as T0, FLAN, and Super-NaturalInstructions, with gains on ANLI and MNLI (Sanh et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022).

document: Dynamic graphs capture interactions that evolve over time, requiring models to reason about temporal context as well as structure. Static encoders miss causal ordering and recency effects that drive link formation and diffusion. Incorporating time complicates neighborhood definition, batching, and negative sampling for training. Moreover, evaluation must avoid leakage by respecting temporal order during splits. Recent architectures integrate time into message passing using kernels, memory, or attention. Each design balances expressivity against scalability for streaming updates. Memory modules retain long-range history but incur write amplification. Attention offers flexible conditioning yet can be costly on dense event streams. Our study compares temporal parameterizations under matched sampling budgets. We isolate how encoding absolute timestamps versus inter-event intervals affects prediction. We further examine the role of feature refresh in stabilizing representations. Before detailing our approach, we outline representative temporal graph methods and datasets. Temporal graph models began with time-respecting random walks and embeddings for links, including CTDNE and DynGEM on networks (Nguyen et al., 2018; Goyal et al., 2018). Event-driven representations introduced point processes and temporal attention, as in DyRep and JODIE for user–item interactions (Trivedi et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2019) on platforms. Temporal graph attention networks modeled continuous-time messages with sampling, exemplified by TGAT and TGN on Wikipedia and Reddit (Xu et al., 2020; Rossi et al., 2020). Benchmarks evaluate link prediction and node classification with temporal splits on ICEWS14, ICEWS18, and Wikipedia, using negative sampling (Boschee et al., 2015; Kumar et al., 2019). Together, these lines chart a progression from handcrafted walks to learned event encoders. However, fairness questions persist around hindsight features and batch-dependent context windows. We therefore propose a leakage-aware protocol that fixes observation horizons during training. Under this protocol, memory write frequency and attention fanout become comparable across models. We also quantify calibration drift when event rates spike or lull. Our analysis highlights the need for adaptive time encoding that tracks nonstationarity. Finally, we release lightweight diagnostics that detect timestamp leakage using controlled shuffles. These tools establish a reliable basis for future temporal graph evaluations.
spans: Temporal graph models began with time-respecting random walks and embeddings for links, including CTDNE and DynGEM on networks (Nguyen et al., 2018; Goyal et al., 2018). Event-driven representations introduced point processes and temporal attention, as in DyRep and JODIE for user–item interactions (Trivedi et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2019) on platforms. Temporal graph attention networks modeled continuous-time messages with sampling, exemplified by TGAT and TGN on Wikipedia and Reddit (Xu et al., 2020; Rossi et al., 2020). Benchmarks evaluate link prediction and node classification with temporal splits on ICEWS14, ICEWS18, and Wikipedia, using negative sampling (Boschee et al., 2015; Kumar et al., 2019).

document: Federated learning distributes training across edge devices while keeping raw data local. This paradigm promises personalization and privacy but complicates optimization and accounting. Heterogeneous hardware and data skew produce stragglers and update bias. Communication budgets further constrain gradient exchange and synchronization. Privacy guarantees interact with these systems constraints in subtle ways. In particular, noise addition changes the effective learning rate and gradient sparsity. Moreover, clipping per-client updates introduces bias that can accumulate over rounds. Robust evaluation therefore requires joint control of optimization, communication, and privacy parameters. We focus on regimes with thousands of devices and intermittent connectivity. Our analysis characterizes trade-offs between convergence and end-user privacy loss. We build on standardized data partitions to compare algorithms consistently. We also model participation as a stochastic process tied to device availability. Under this model, privacy spending depends on cohort size and sampling variance. We calibrate clipping and noise to equalize effective privacy across populations. To contextualize our choices, we review representative algorithms and benchmarks. The following summary highlights core optimization, privacy, and systems components studied in the literature. We then turn to practical datasets and accounting tools used in empirical studies. Federated optimization adopts FedAvg and variants that address client heterogeneity, notably FedProx and SCAFFOLD (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020). Privacy accounting often relies on DP-SGD with subsampling and noise schedules tracked by the moments accountant or Rényi DP tools (Abadi et al., 2016; Mironov, 2017). Secure aggregation and compression mitigate leakage and cost, via Bonawitz protocols, SignSGD, and QSGD (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Bernstein et al., 2018; Alistarh et al., 2017). Benchmarks use LEAF, FEMNIST, Shakespeare, and StackOverflow to assess accuracy, communication, and privacy under heterogeneous client sampling (Caldas et al., 2019; McMahan et al., 2017) conditions. Collectively, these ingredients define the operating envelope for private federated learning at scale. However, published baselines rarely align budgets across methods, obscuring comparisons. Our study provides matched-budget baselines and sensitivity analyses to clarify these interactions.
spans: Federated optimization adopts FedAvg and variants that address client heterogeneity, notably FedProx and SCAFFOLD (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020). Privacy accounting often relies on DP-SGD with subsampling and noise schedules tracked by the moments accountant or Rényi DP tools (Abadi et al., 2016; Mironov, 2017). Secure aggregation and compression mitigate leakage and cost, via Bonawitz protocols, SignSGD, and QSGD (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Bernstein et al., 2018; Alistarh et al., 2017). Benchmarks use LEAF, FEMNIST, Shakespeare, and StackOverflow to assess accuracy, communication, and privacy under heterogeneous client sampling (Caldas et al., 2019; McMahan et al., 2017) conditions.

document: Continual learning studies models that acquire new skills without catastrophically forgetting previous tasks. In NLP, this challenge arises naturally as user intents, domains, and label spaces evolve. Sequential training naively overwrites parameters, leading to rapid degradation on earlier tasks. Mitigating this interference requires balancing plasticity and stability during updates. Approaches vary along algorithmic constraints, memory usage, and architectural choices. We briefly survey representative strategies and benchmarks used to study this setting. Regularization-based approaches constrain updates to preserve prior tasks, including EWC, SI, and LwF (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017; Zenke et al., 2017; Li and Hoiem, 2017) regularizers. Replay-based strategies interleave new data with exemplars or generative memories, exemplified by GEM, A-GEM, and Experience Replay (Lopez-Paz and Ranzato, 2017; Chaudhry et al., 2019) methods. Architectural expansion preserves capacity via task-specific modules, including Progressive Networks and adapter-based finetuning with BERT backbones for NLP (Rusu et al., 2016; Houlsby et al., 2019). Benchmarks include CLINC150, MultiWOZ, Amazon Reviews, and continual GLUE, with metrics for forgetting and transfer (Larson et al., 2019; Budzianowski et al., 2018) across task sequences. These strands offer complementary mechanisms but differ in compute and storage requirements. For language models, replay can be dominated by token-level redundancy without careful sampling. Regularization methods depend strongly on curvature estimates that drift during finetuning. Architectural expansion reduces interference but may impede zero-shot transfer across tasks. We evaluate methods under a unified protocol that fixes budget, order, and model size. Our design decouples task definition from gradient accumulation to control exposure. We incorporate rehearsal buffers that respect privacy constraints through deduplication. Additionally, we test adapter isolation against soft parameter sharing at distinct layers. Metrics include forward transfer, backward transfer, and intransigence to capture different behaviors. We report both average accuracy and area under the accuracy–time curve. Ablations assess the impact of orderings that cluster semantically similar tasks. Sensitivity analyses probe how forgetting scales with buffer size and update frequency. Finally, we release scripts that reproduce sequences and evaluation across corpora. These resources aim to standardize comparisons and reveal robust continual learning practices.
spans: Regularization-based approaches constrain updates to preserve prior tasks, including EWC, SI, and LwF (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017; Zenke et al., 2017; Li and Hoiem, 2017) regularizers. Replay-based strategies interleave new data with exemplars or generative memories, exemplified by GEM, A-GEM, and Experience Replay (Lopez-Paz and Ranzato, 2017; Chaudhry et al., 2019) methods. Architectural expansion preserves capacity via task-specific modules, including Progressive Networks and adapter-based finetuning with BERT backbones for NLP (Rusu et al., 2016; Houlsby et al., 2019). Benchmarks include CLINC150, MultiWOZ, Amazon Reviews, and continual GLUE, with metrics for forgetting and transfer (Larson et al., 2019; Budzianowski et al., 2018) across task sequences.

document: Conversational recommendation aims to elicit preferences through dialog while producing item suggestions. This paradigm promises transparency and engagement compared with static recommenders. Yet practical deployments still struggle with sparse signals and shifting intents. Prior surveys emphasize the interplay between language understanding and decision making. In particular, integrating long-term user models remains challenging when conversations are brief. System design must reconcile dialog policies with item ranking under limited feedback. Recent work explores retrieval-augmented generation for justification and grounding. Neural CRS models leveraging persona or knowledge graphs include ReDial (Li et al., 2018), OpenDialKG (Moon et al., 2019), KBRD (Chen et al., 2019), KGSF (Zhou et al., 2020), and KECRS (Ma et al., 2021). Common evaluation resources comprise INSPIRED (Hayati et al., 2020), DuRecDial (Liu et al., 2020), TG-ReDial (Zhou et al., 2020b), GoRecDial (Kang et al., 2019), and MovieLens-based splits, with auxiliary supervision from DailyDialog, ConvAI2, MultiWOZ, and Reddit-derived conversational corpora. Despite resource growth, evaluation practices remain inconsistent across domains. Metrics often mix turn-level appropriateness with session-level success without clear weighting. Cold-start users exacerbate the mismatch between offline metrics and interactive outcomes. Moreover, catalog churn quickly invalidates static popularity priors. Safety considerations introduce constraints on persuasion and disclosure. Language models can hallucinate attributes that alter user trust and choices. Personalization creates feedback loops that entrench narrow item exposure. Cross-lingual and cross-market transfer is underexplored for dialog-centric recommenders. Robustness to adversarial or sarcastic queries is similarly underinvestigated. Reproducibility also suffers from opaque preprocessing and private data dependencies. To address these challenges, we reframe CRS evaluation around goal-conditioned trajectories. We introduce a simulator that couples preference drift with inventory dynamics. The simulator enables counterfactual analysis of policy updates under distribution shift. We complement simulation with user studies that trace satisfaction beyond click signals.
spans: Neural CRS models leveraging persona or knowledge graphs include ReDial (Li et al., 2018), OpenDialKG (Moon et al., 2019), KBRD (Chen et al., 2019), KGSF (Zhou et al., 2020), and KECRS (Ma et al., 2021). Common evaluation resources comprise INSPIRED (Hayati et al., 2020), DuRecDial (Liu et al., 2020), TG-ReDial (Zhou et al., 2020b), GoRecDial (Kang et al., 2019), and MovieLens-based splits, with auxiliary supervision from DailyDialog, ConvAI2, MultiWOZ, and Reddit-derived conversational corpora.

document: Multimodal emotion recognition seeks to infer affect from coordinated signals. The task underpins applications in wellbeing, education, and media analysis. However, real-world deployment faces subtle shifts in speakers, devices, and cultures. A central question is how to fuse asynchronous modalities reliably. Early concatenation methods underutilized cross-modal structure. Attention mechanisms improved selectivity but introduced new overfitting risks. Temporal alignment remains difficult when cues are sparse or contradictory. Calibration drift also complicates threshold selection across datasets. Label ambiguity further limits statistical efficiency at scale. Semi-supervised learning offers leverage when annotations are scarce. Self-training and contrastive objectives have shown promise under covariate shift. Representative fusion architectures include MFN (Zadeh et al., 2018), MFM (Tsai et al., 2019a), MulT (Tsai et al., 2019b), MAG-BERT (Rahman et al., 2020), and SLSTM variants for cross-modal alignment. Evaluation commonly uses IEMOCAP, CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, MELD, and RAVDESS, with modalities spanning speech prosody, facial action units, gaze, and lexical features, and metrics such as accuracy, F1, MAE, and concordance correlation, alongside robustness checks on noise, speaker imbalance, and temporal desynchrony during evaluation. Despite significant progress, generalization to unseen speakers is inconsistent. Domain adaptation techniques reduce gap but can degrade specificity. Privacy constraints often prevent release of raw audio or video streams. Federated training raises questions about aggregation under heterogeneity. Interpretability is required for clinical and educational stakeholders. Local explanations may not reflect global model behavior. We consider how causal structure can guide robust fusion. Our framework decouples representation alignment from task calibration. It quantifies uncertainty propagation across modalities and time. Comprehensive experiments measure benefits under realistic distribution shifts.
spans: Representative fusion architectures include MFN (Zadeh et al., 2018), MFM (Tsai et al., 2019a), MulT (Tsai et al., 2019b), MAG-BERT (Rahman et al., 2020), and SLSTM variants for cross-modal alignment. Evaluation commonly uses IEMOCAP, CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, MELD, and RAVDESS, with modalities spanning speech prosody, facial action units, gaze, and lexical features, and metrics such as accuracy, F1, MAE, and concordance correlation, alongside robustness checks on noise, speaker imbalance, and temporal desynchrony during evaluation.

document: Graph anomaly detection identifies nodes or edges that deviate from typical relational patterns. Applications include fraud surveillance, intrusion monitoring, and integrity auditing. Unlike i.i.d. settings, dependencies propagate irregularities across neighborhoods. This complicates both modeling and evaluation. A core difficulty is disentangling structural rarity from semantic novelty. Simple thresholds on degree or attributes can be brittle. Representation learning offers alternatives through neighborhood aggregation. However, oversmoothing may suppress discriminative variation. Heterophily further challenges standard homophily-based encoders. Temporal dynamics introduce nonstationarity that mimics anomalies. Scalability becomes acute on multi-million node graphs. Memory footprints constrain negative sampling and calibration. Label scarcity is common due to review costs. Synthetic labels risk biasing learned detectors. Robust protocols must separate contamination from ground truth. Methods range from proximity scores and embeddings to GNNs, including DeepWalk (Perozzi et al., 2014), node2vec (Grover and Leskovec, 2016), GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GAT (Velickovic et al., 2018), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), DOMINANT (Ding et al., 2019), and AnomalyDAE (Fan et al., 2020). Benchmarks typically cover Cora, Citeseer, PubMed, Reddit, YelpChi, and Amazon graphs, with ROC-AUC and PR-AUC reported under transductive splits and controlled corruption rates for fair method comparison. Despite breadth, many studies rely on closed-world assumptions. Real systems often face evolving taxonomies and adversaries. We develop a detection paradigm built around causal interventions. It distinguishes structural confounders from actionable deviations. Our estimator leverages invariant predictiveness across environments. The method scales via sampling and sketching primitives.
spans: Methods range from proximity scores and embeddings to GNNs, including DeepWalk (Perozzi et al., 2014), node2vec (Grover and Leskovec, 2016), GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GAT (Velickovic et al., 2018), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), DOMINANT (Ding et al., 2019), and AnomalyDAE (Fan et al., 2020). Benchmarks typically cover Cora, Citeseer, PubMed, Reddit, YelpChi, and Amazon graphs, with ROC-AUC and PR-AUC reported under transductive splits and controlled corruption rates for fair method comparison.

document: Federated learning enables on-device training with privacy advantages over centralized pipelines. However, mobile platforms impose energy and bandwidth constraints that shape algorithm design. Non-i.i.d. data and intermittent connectivity further complicate convergence guarantees. Clients vary in compute, memory, and thermal budgets across hardware generations. Communication rounds dominate energy consumption under dense models. Compression reduces payload but can degrade final accuracy. Stragglers and dropouts perturb aggregation and fairness. Resource adaptation must consider energy limits and user experience. Scheduling strategies can opportunistically exploit charging or Wi-Fi availability. Optimization variants address heterogeneity and drift, including FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), FedProx (Li et al., 2020), SCAFFOLD (Karimireddy et al., 2020), FedNova (Wang et al., 2020), and q-FedAvg (Li et al., 2020b). Communication efficiency is pursued via SignSGD (Bernstein et al., 2018), STC (Sattler et al., 2019), Deep Gradient Compression (Lin et al., 2018), dropout, and aggregation schemes like FedPAQ (Reisizadeh et al., 2020), with evaluations on FEMNIST, Shakespeare, StackOverflow, and CIFAR-10. Despite impressive results, system-level evaluations often omit energy accounting. Measurements frequently use wall-power proxies that misestimate battery impacts. Idle state transitions and radio wakes introduce hidden costs. Hybrid digital-analog links alter per-bit energy in practice. Co-design across optimizer, model, and runtime is essential on phones. Energy-aware objectives can stabilize learning under aggressive compression. Heterogeneous participation also raises fairness considerations. We operationalize fairness using budget-weighted contributions. Our scheduler predicts marginal accuracy per joule for each client. It allocates rounds under stochastic availability and drift. Experiments quantify benefits across three handset tiers. Results demonstrate improved efficiency at minimal accuracy loss.
spans: Optimization variants address heterogeneity and drift, including FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), FedProx (Li et al., 2020), SCAFFOLD (Karimireddy et al., 2020), FedNova (Wang et al., 2020), and q-FedAvg (Li et al., 2020b). Communication efficiency is pursued via SignSGD (Bernstein et al., 2018), STC (Sattler et al., 2019), Deep Gradient Compression (Lin et al., 2018), dropout, and aggregation schemes like FedPAQ (Reisizadeh et al., 2020), with evaluations on FEMNIST, Shakespeare, StackOverflow, and CIFAR-10.

document: Learning causal state from multivariate clinical time series can support reliable decision making. Observational data streams combine treatments, physiology, and measurement artifacts. Conventional predictors often conflate association with intervention response. Representation learning may capture useful abstractions but risks spurious shortcuts. Counterfactual reasoning is hampered by time-varying confounding and censoring. Missingness and irregular sampling introduce informative patterns of observation. Shift across hospitals and devices challenges transportability of learned features. Domain knowledge suggests sparse, stable mechanisms under interventions. However, mechanistic alignment remains difficult with limited labels. Off-policy evaluation is sensitive to positivity violations and overlap. Propensity estimation degrades in high-dimensional dynamic settings. Balancing approaches struggle when treatments evolve continuously. Instrumental variables are rare and weak in practice. Recent work explores invariance as an organizing principle. Temporal invariance requires careful handling of lags and aggregation. Synthetic data may not reflect clinical workload or noise. Evaluation protocols often ignore deployment feedback loops. Relevant baselines in temporal prediction and discovery include VAR, LSTM, TCN, and Transformer encoders, alongside causal discovery tools such as Granger tests, PCMCI (Runge et al., 2019), NOTEARS (Zheng et al., 2018), VARLiNGAM (Hyvärinen et al., 2010), and DyGLASSO (Hallac et al., 2017). Healthcare evaluations draw from MIMIC-III, MIMIC-IV, eICU, and PhysioNet Challenge cohorts, with tasks including decompensation, length-of-stay, and intervention forecasting, and metrics such as AUROC, AUPRC, calibration error, and decision-focused utility. We seek representations that admit stable predictors under plausible shifts. Our approach exploits grouped environments induced by admission pathways. It aligns latent factors by solving a constrained risk minimization. Empirical studies examine sensitivity to missingness, policy change, and delays.
spans: Relevant baselines in temporal prediction and discovery include VAR, LSTM, TCN, and Transformer encoders, alongside causal discovery tools such as Granger tests, PCMCI (Runge et al., 2019), NOTEARS (Zheng et al., 2018), VARLiNGAM (Hyvärinen et al., 2010), and DyGLASSO (Hallac et al., 2017). Healthcare evaluations draw from MIMIC-III, MIMIC-IV, eICU, and PhysioNet Challenge cohorts, with tasks including decompensation, length-of-stay, and intervention forecasting, and metrics such as AUROC, AUPRC, calibration error, and decision-focused utility.

document: Automatic speech recognition in noisy target domains remains challenging despite advances in self-supervised pretraining. Models trained on curated corpora often fail when confronted with channel shifts, accents, and background interference. Prior work explores both feature and label space adaptation, yet a consistent treatment of temporally structured noise is limited. We focus on cross-domain robustness where only untranscribed target audio is available during adaptation. This setting amplifies the risk of confirmation bias in self-training and the drift of posterior confidence estimation. A central question is how to stabilize adaptation without access to ground-truth labels or parallel recordings. In this section we review techniques commonly used for domain adaptation in speech and outline remaining challenges. We then motivate a strategy that integrates uncertainty estimation with curriculum-aligned augmentation. Recent approaches to speech domain adaptation draw on adversarial feature alignment (Ganin et al., 2016; Tzeng et al., 2017), self-training with pseudo labels (Lee, 2013; Sohn et al., 2020), consistency regularization (Laine and Aila, 2017; Tarvainen and Valpola, 2017), and representation learning on large audio corpora such as Librispeech, VoxCeleb, and Common Voice. Contrastive objectives appear in several variants (Oord et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020), while transformer acoustic encoders follow wav2vec 2.0 and HuBERT (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021). Benchmarks report on CHiME. While these ingredients recur across studies, few works examine how uncertainty estimates interact with pseudo-label schedules in nonstationary noise. Empirical analyses rarely separate acoustic distortions from lexical shift, making it difficult to attribute improvements to specific mechanisms. Moreover, contrastive pretext tasks can destabilize when augmentations violate phonetic consistency in reverberant scenes. We therefore investigate adaptation curricula that modulate augmentation intensity according to model confidence. Our hypothesis is that well-calibrated confidence can gate pseudo-label generation and mitigate error amplification. To test this, we couple confidence-aware filtering with a noise-conditional augmentation pipeline. The approach allows separation of channel, speaker, and environment factors through targeted perturbations. We also incorporate a small teacher ensemble to regularize early adaptation steps when confidence is unreliable. Evaluation spans synthetic mixtures and real recordings to assess transfer under controlled and wild conditions. Metrics cover word error, calibration, and stability under perturbation sweeps. We further analyze per-phoneme substitution patterns to trace where the curriculum exerts the most benefit. Finally, we situate the results in a broader view of semi-supervised learning for audio understanding. The following section details datasets, augmentation recipes, and training schedules used in our experiments.
spans: Recent approaches to speech domain adaptation draw on adversarial feature alignment (Ganin et al., 2016; Tzeng et al., 2017), self-training with pseudo labels (Lee, 2013; Sohn et al., 2020), consistency regularization (Laine and Aila, 2017; Tarvainen and Valpola, 2017), and representation learning on large audio corpora such as Librispeech, VoxCeleb, and Common Voice. Contrastive objectives appear in several variants (Oord et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020), while transformer acoustic encoders follow wav2vec 2.0 and HuBERT (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021). Benchmarks report on CHiME.

document: Multi-document summarization must reconcile overlap, contradiction, and source-specific terminology. Scientific literature complicates this because evidence appears in heterogeneous formats and rhetorical zones. Extractive pipelines preserve factual grounding but struggle with discourse flow across sections. Abstractive models permit fusion, yet hallucination and citation drift remain persistent risks. Our study targets citation-faithful summaries that trace claims to supporting passages. We formalize the task as jointly selecting evidence and generating discourse-coherent statements. The framework emphasizes document-level grounding signals derived from section titles and citation markers. We also consider lexical priors from domain ontologies to constrain generation. Below, we briefly survey representative techniques and datasets used in multi-document settings. Research on multi-document summarization spans extractive graph methods such as LexRank and TextRank (Erkan and Radev, 2004; Mihalcea and Tarau, 2004) and pre-trained transformers including BART, PEGASUS, and Longformer (Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020; Beltagy et al., 2020). Datasets used for evaluation include Multi-News, WikiSum, Multi-XScience, and GovReport (Fabbri et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021). Content planning and entity-centric selection appear as auxiliary strategies (Shen et al., 2019; Narayan et al., 2018; Li et al., 2020). These resources do not directly encode citation-to-claim alignment, which motivates additional supervision. We introduce a rationale extraction layer that conditions decoding on verified spans. The layer treats each citation as a soft constraint enforced through coverage and copy penalties. To mitigate hallucination, a spectral regularizer penalizes divergence between generated attributions and evidence indices. Our decoding objective therefore balances informativeness with groundedness at sentence level. We adapt long-context attention to ensure scalability without sacrificing token-level alignment. The training protocol combines contrastive evidence selection with self-denoising on shuffled source sets. This yields robustness to missing or redundant documents in the input cluster. We evaluate on biomedical and public policy corpora with curated citation links. Metrics include factual consistency, evidence precision, and citation-level calibration. Human assessment focuses on support traceability and discourse coherence across sources. We report ablations to quantify the contribution of rationale conditioning and spectral regularization.
spans: Research on multi-document summarization spans extractive graph methods such as LexRank and TextRank (Erkan and Radev, 2004; Mihalcea and Tarau, 2004) and pre-trained transformers including BART, PEGASUS, and Longformer (Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020; Beltagy et al., 2020). Datasets used for evaluation include Multi-News, WikiSum, Multi-XScience, and GovReport (Fabbri et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021). Content planning and entity-centric selection appear as auxiliary strategies (Shen et al., 2019; Narayan et al., 2018; Li et al., 2020).

document: Learning dexterous manipulation from pixels remains a central challenge in robotics. Real-world deployments require sample efficiency, safety, and generalization across objects and lighting. Existing policy optimization schemes often underperform when visual features drift between training and deployment. We study settings where demonstrations are available but online interaction is constrained. Our objective is to exploit demonstrations while maintaining robustness under distribution shift. We consider an offline pretraining stage followed by conservative fine-tuning with minimal environment steps. The representation backbone is designed to track contact cues and object pose. We now review representative methods and datasets relevant to this study. Robotic manipulation has used PPO, DDPG, and SAC (Schulman et al., 2017; Lillicrap et al., 2016; Haarnoja et al., 2018). Imitation learning with behavior cloning and DAgger supplies priors (Pomerleau, 1989; Ross et al., 2011). Offline reinforcement learning emphasizes conservative operators with BCQ and CQL (Fujimoto et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2020). Visual pretraining adopts MAE and R3M for representation learning (He et al., 2022; Nair et al., 2022). Benchmarks include RLBench, RoboNet, and D4RL (James et al., 2020; Dasari et al., 2019; Fu et al., 2020). Despite these building blocks, aligning visual features with contact-relevant affordances remains underexplored. Methods pretrained on generic image corpora often overlook hand-object interaction signals crucial for stability. We hypothesize that contact-centric supervision can bridge this gap without additional teleoperation burden. To that end, we derive pseudo-contacts from tactile proxies and motion discontinuities. These signals shape both the encoder and the action-value critic during pretraining. During fine-tuning, the agent is regularized to preserve contact cues while adapting to task specifics. The approach encourages consistent grasp geometry and reduces grasp-release oscillations. We evaluate across tool use, container manipulation, and articulated object tasks. Metrics capture success, completion time, and grip force transients inferred from wrist IMU data. Ablations isolate the effects of contact supervision and conservative updates under dataset noise. We discuss broader implications for data collection and safety constraints in real laboratories.
spans: Robotic manipulation has used PPO, DDPG, and SAC (Schulman et al., 2017; Lillicrap et al., 2016; Haarnoja et al., 2018). Imitation learning with behavior cloning and DAgger supplies priors (Pomerleau, 1989; Ross et al., 2011). Offline reinforcement learning emphasizes conservative operators with BCQ and CQL (Fujimoto et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2020). Visual pretraining adopts MAE and R3M for representation learning (He et al., 2022; Nair et al., 2022). Benchmarks include RLBench, RoboNet, and D4RL (James et al., 2020; Dasari et al., 2019; Fu et al., 2020).

document: Recommender systems distribute attention and opportunity across users and items at scale. This allocation can amplify historical inequities unless objectives incorporate fairness constraints. Exposure disparities arise when ranking optimizes click prediction without provider-side considerations. Consumer harms surface when relevance models encode demographic signals from interaction histories. Recent work formalizes these issues through distributional metrics and constrained optimization. We study fairness in two-sided marketplaces where both consumers and providers receive guarantees. Our focus is ranking-time interventions compatible with large-scale retrieval pipelines. Before presenting our method, we summarize representative approaches and datasets. Work on fairness in recommendation spans pre-processing, in-processing, and post-processing strategies, including re-ranking for fair exposure (Zehlike et al., 2017; Biega et al., 2018), matrix factorization and adversarial objectives (Kamishima et al., 2012; Beutel et al., 2019), and learning of demographic parity and equal opportunity (Calders et al., 2009; Hardt et al., 2016). Evaluations use MovieLens, Last.fm, and Goodreads with group annotations (Harper and Konstan, 2016; Celma, 2010; Wan and McAuley, 2018). Multi-sided metrics consider provider fairness and consumer utility (Singh and Joachims, 2018; Sapiezynski et al., 2019). While influential, these strategies rarely account for retrieval-stage biases that precede re-ranking. In practice, candidate generators filter the space in ways that shape feasible fairness trade-offs. We introduce a calibration layer that controls exposure budgets before downstream ranking. The layer matches demand forecasts while constraining demographic parity at slice level. It interfaces with production search through lightweight score transformations. Our constraints are differentiable surrogates that upper bound disparity under monotone ranking. We derive regret bounds connecting exposure slack to utility loss. The analysis motivates per-bucket budget setting based on historical supply elasticity. We demonstrate scalability in a high-volume marketplace with strict latency targets. Metrics include exposure Gini, group-normalized DCG, and counterfactual equality of opportunity. We also report supply-side spillovers measured via provider satisfaction surveys. Ablations isolate the contribution of retrieval calibration relative to standard re-ranking baselines. Ethical considerations address data provenance, user consent, and transparency of budget policies.
spans: Work on fairness in recommendation spans pre-processing, in-processing, and post-processing strategies, including re-ranking for fair exposure (Zehlike et al., 2017; Biega et al., 2018), matrix factorization and adversarial objectives (Kamishima et al., 2012; Beutel et al., 2019), and learning of demographic parity and equal opportunity (Calders et al., 2009; Hardt et al., 2016). Evaluations use MovieLens, Last.fm, and Goodreads with group annotations (Harper and Konstan, 2016; Celma, 2010; Wan and McAuley, 2018). Multi-sided metrics consider provider fairness and consumer utility (Singh and Joachims, 2018; Sapiezynski et al., 2019).

document: Accurate prediction of molecular properties underpins discovery in chemistry and drug design. Graph-structured representations of molecules promise inductive biases aligned with chemistry. Yet models must handle long-range interactions and stereochemistry in a data-efficient manner. Many benchmarks emphasize small molecules, leaving gaps for larger and charged species. We investigate architectures that balance expressivity with computational tractability. Our study focuses on message passing augmented with spectral and geometric cues. We also consider sequence-only baselines to contextualize the benefits of structure. Before presenting our approach, we briefly review representative methods and datasets. Molecular property prediction has adopted message passing neural networks and their variants, including GCN, GraphSAGE, and GIN (Gilmer et al., 2017; Kipf and Welling, 2017; Hamilton et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2019). Transformer-style architectures such as Graphormer and GROVER introduce attention and pretraining (Ying et al., 2021; Rong et al., 2020). Benchmarks include QM9, ZINC, MoleculeNet, and OGB-MOLHIV (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014; Irwin and Shoichet, 2005; Wu et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2020). Models like ChemBERTa operate on SMILES without explicit graphs (Chithrananda et al., 2020). Despite strong results, most evaluations overlook uncertainty estimates crucial for risk-sensitive decisions. We quantify calibration alongside accuracy to reflect downstream medicinal chemistry needs. Our central idea is to couple message passing with stochastic edge masks conditioned on atom environments. This mechanism approximates epistemic uncertainty while retaining differentiability for gradient-based learning. We derive bounds showing how mask entropy controls variance in predicted properties. A distillation stage transfers uncertainty-aware features into compact student models. We also introduce stereochemistry perturbations to evaluate sensitivity to chirality and conformers. The experimental design spans curated splits that avoid scaffold leakage. Metrics include mean absolute error, AUROC, expected calibration error, and abstention curves. We analyze per-functional-group performance to surface systematic strengths and failure modes. Finally, we discuss data requirements for expanding benchmarks beyond neutral organics. The next section provides details on datasets, preprocessing, and training schedules.
spans: Molecular property prediction has adopted message passing neural networks and their variants, including GCN, GraphSAGE, and GIN (Gilmer et al., 2017; Kipf and Welling, 2017; Hamilton et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2019). Transformer-style architectures such as Graphormer and GROVER introduce attention and pretraining (Ying et al., 2021; Rong et al., 2020). Benchmarks include QM9, ZINC, MoleculeNet, and OGB-MOLHIV (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014; Irwin and Shoichet, 2005; Wu et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2020). Models like ChemBERTa operate on SMILES without explicit graphs (Chithrananda et al., 2020).

document: Traffic forecasting underpins intelligent transportation systems and urban planning. Recent models integrate spatial and temporal dependencies using graph neural networks to anticipate road speeds and volumes. However, nonstationarity, sensor failures, and exogenous events still degrade reliability. We study robustness through adaptive structure learning and uncertainty-aware objectives. Our approach targets deployment settings with scarce labels and distribution shifts. In this section, we review representative methodologies and benchmarks. Early spatiotemporal graph models emphasized diffusion dynamics and convolutional filters across sensor graphs, exemplified by DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), and Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019). Subsequent work explored attention mechanisms and adaptive adjacency learning, including ASTGCN (Guo et al., 2019), GMAN (Zheng et al., 2020), and AGCRN (Bai et al., 2020). Standard benchmarks such as METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03/04/07/08, and LOOP-SEA accompany common evaluation metrics and training protocols, while additional comparisons incorporate baselines like VAR, LSTM, and TCN across varying horizons and spatial sparsity conditions and missingness. Despite accuracy gains, most models assume a fixed graph and stationary noise. This assumption breaks under incidents, construction, and sensor outages that alter connectivity. We formulate forecasting as joint graph inference and prediction under heteroscedastic uncertainty. Our method leverages exogenous covariates to infer context-specific edges and calibrates predictive intervals. We evaluate robustness to topology shifts, synthetic failures, and domain transfer across regions. The following sections detail the model, learning objectives, and comprehensive experiments.
spans: Early spatiotemporal graph models emphasized diffusion dynamics and convolutional filters across sensor graphs, exemplified by DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), and Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019). Subsequent work explored attention mechanisms and adaptive adjacency learning, including ASTGCN (Guo et al., 2019), GMAN (Zheng et al., 2020), and AGCRN (Bai et al., 2020). Standard benchmarks such as METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03/04/07/08, and LOOP-SEA accompany common evaluation metrics and training protocols, while additional comparisons incorporate baselines like VAR, LSTM, and TCN across varying horizons and spatial sparsity conditions and missingness.

document: Natural language inference remains a central testbed for cross-lingual generalization. While transfer across languages has improved with multilingual pretraining, persistent gaps remain for typologically distant pairs. We investigate alignment objectives that emphasize reasoning phenomena rather than shallow lexical overlap. Our study focuses on stability under domain and script shifts. We also analyze calibration and label semantics across languages. Before presenting our approach, we summarize relevant resources and techniques. We briefly review datasets, architectures, and transfer settings commonly used in multilingual NLI. Key datasets include XNLI (Conneau et al., 2018), PAWS-X (Yang et al., 2019), and MultiNLI translations (Williams et al., 2018), alongside evaluation on TyDi-based entailment variants and MLDoc for auxiliary classification. Prominent architectures feature mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLM (Lample and Conneau, 2019), XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), and LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020) for sentence representations. Transfer strategies span zero-shot, translate-train, and translate-test pipelines, with auxiliary objectives such as TLM, alignment via MUSE or VecMap, and consistency regularization applied across bilingual dictionaries and mined parallel corpora within widely used benchmarks today. Despite strong averages, error analyses show brittle handling of negation and quantifiers in many languages. We target these failures by injecting cross-lingual reasoning probes during pretraining and fine-tuning. Our method reweights examples by semantic phenomena rather than language frequency. This design promotes balanced generalization under both zero-shot and translate-train regimes. We report comprehensive ablations and cross-lingual calibration curves.
spans: Key datasets include XNLI (Conneau et al., 2018), PAWS-X (Yang et al., 2019), and MultiNLI translations (Williams et al., 2018), alongside evaluation on TyDi-based entailment variants and MLDoc for auxiliary classification. Prominent architectures feature mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLM (Lample and Conneau, 2019), XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), and LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020) for sentence representations. Transfer strategies span zero-shot, translate-train, and translate-test pipelines, with auxiliary objectives such as TLM, alignment via MUSE or VecMap, and consistency regularization applied across bilingual dictionaries and mined parallel corpora within widely used benchmarks today.

document: Online misinformation increasingly exploits images and video to amplify persuasive narratives. Joint modeling of text and vision is therefore central to reliable detection. Existing pipelines often conflate veracity with stylistic cues, risking spurious correlations. We aim to disentangle content evidence from platform-specific artifacts. Our framework integrates cross-modal grounding and provenance signals. We first outline representative datasets, architectures, and training recipes. This context highlights common assumptions in multimodal detection research. The following references exemplify prevailing practices across benchmarks. Commonly used datasets include Fakeddit (Nakamura et al., 2020), Weibo-COVID (Shu et al., 2020), and Twitter-based multimodal corpora such as MediaEval Verifying Multimedia Use, along with NewsCLIPpings and GossipCop-Plus variants. Representative methods couple CNN or ViT encoders with BERT or RoBERTa for late fusion, while others adopt early fusion via multimodal transformers like VisualBERT and ViLBERT. Training protocols report AUROC and F1 under random splits or time-based splits, often comparing to text-only, image-only, and co-attention baselines. Some studies evaluate cross-domain transfer on MM-COVID, PHEME, and BoW-based heuristics for stance labels and entity linking. Despite dataset diversity, many images repeat across platforms, inflating perceived generalization. We address this by enforcing image provenance constraints and grounding claims in retrieved web evidence. Our evaluation emphasizes temporal splits and out-of-domain media to assess robustness.
spans: Commonly used datasets include Fakeddit (Nakamura et al., 2020), Weibo-COVID (Shu et al., 2020), and Twitter-based multimodal corpora such as MediaEval Verifying Multimedia Use, along with NewsCLIPpings and GossipCop-Plus variants. Representative methods couple CNN or ViT encoders with BERT or RoBERTa for late fusion, while others adopt early fusion via multimodal transformers like VisualBERT and ViLBERT. Training protocols report AUROC and F1 under random splits or time-based splits, often comparing to text-only, image-only, and co-attention baselines. Some studies evaluate cross-domain transfer on MM-COVID, PHEME, and BoW-based heuristics for stance labels and entity linking.

document: Recommender systems mediate access to information and economic opportunity. Concerns about disparate exposure and engagement have motivated fairness-aware algorithms. Yet empirical practices vary widely across domains and evaluation protocols. We study user and item group equity under explicit exposure constraints. Our framework integrates probabilistic ranking and counterfactual estimators. Prior work spans post-processing, in-processing, and pre-processing approaches, including fairness-aware re-ranking (Singh and Joachims, 2018), calibration-based objectives (Steck, 2018), and exposure regularization in stochastic rankings (Biega et al., 2018). Dataset choices often center on MovieLens, LastFM, BookCrossing, and KuaiRec, with synthetic bias injections to control propensities. Metrics commonly report NDCG, MAP, exposure disparity, provider-side inequality, and user-side coverage, alongside baselines such as MF, BPR, NeuMF, and graph-based recommenders. Additional studies examine slate-level constraints, position bias correction, and counterfactual evaluation with IPS, SNIPS, and self-normalized doubly robust estimators under public leaderboards and reproducible splits. Despite progress, most evaluations neglect supply constraints and strategic provider behavior. We model exposure as a limited resource allocated across competing providers. Our objective trades off utility and max-min exposure with learnable price signals. We derive stochastic gradients with unbiased estimators under logged bandit feedback. The analysis characterizes feasibility, regret, and equity guarantees. Experiments on semi-synthetic and real data demonstrate consistent Pareto improvements.
spans: Prior work spans post-processing, in-processing, and pre-processing approaches, including fairness-aware re-ranking (Singh and Joachims, 2018), calibration-based objectives (Steck, 2018), and exposure regularization in stochastic rankings (Biega et al., 2018). Dataset choices often center on MovieLens, LastFM, BookCrossing, and KuaiRec, with synthetic bias injections to control propensities. Metrics commonly report NDCG, MAP, exposure disparity, provider-side inequality, and user-side coverage, alongside baselines such as MF, BPR, NeuMF, and graph-based recommenders. Additional studies examine slate-level constraints, position bias correction, and counterfactual evaluation with IPS, SNIPS, and self-normalized doubly robust estimators under public leaderboards and reproducible splits.

document: Low-resource neural machine translation remains challenging despite advances in multilingual pretraining. Data scarcity, domain mismatch, and tokenization drift degrade generalization. We pursue semi-supervised objectives that couple iterative mining with confidence-calibrated training. Our method reduces noise amplification and improves coverage for rare morphology. We further analyze the contribution of contrastive regularization on lexically sparse pairs. To contextualize our approach, we outline established datasets and techniques. We emphasize both supervised and unsupervised settings across language families. We also summarize common pretraining and adaptation strategies. The following overview reflects standard practices in low-resource NMT. Canonical resources include TED Talks from IWSLT, FLORES evaluation sets (Goyal et al., 2022), and OPUS-derived corpora such as JW300 and Bible-UE, with WMT and ParaCrawl providing noisy web-scale parallels. Techniques frequently cited comprise back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), dual learning (He et al., 2016), and unsupervised NMT via denoising and iterative back-translation (Lample et al., 2018). Multilingual pretraining employs MASS (Song et al., 2019), mBART (Liu et al., 2020), and mT5 (Xue et al., 2021), alongside bilingual lexicon induction, pivoting, and vocabulary sharing heuristics. Common tokenizers include SentencePiece, unigram, and BPE models. Despite these ingredients, training often collapses under asymmetric noise and domain imbalance. We address this by jointly filtering and generating targets with calibrated uncertainty and adaptive curricula.
spans: Canonical resources include TED Talks from IWSLT, FLORES evaluation sets (Goyal et al., 2022), and OPUS-derived corpora such as JW300 and Bible-UE, with WMT and ParaCrawl providing noisy web-scale parallels. Techniques frequently cited comprise back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), dual learning (He et al., 2016), and unsupervised NMT via denoising and iterative back-translation (Lample et al., 2018). Multilingual pretraining employs MASS (Song et al., 2019), mBART (Liu et al., 2020), and mT5 (Xue et al., 2021), alongside bilingual lexicon induction, pivoting, and vocabulary sharing heuristics. Common tokenizers include SentencePiece, unigram, and BPE models.

document: This paper investigates adaptation in large-scale vision–language models under distribution shift. Recent advances demonstrate that paired image–text pretraining yields strong zero-shot performance but can be brittle when domains drift. We focus on the introduction and prior art that shape the landscape of post-hoc adaptation for retrieval and recognition tasks. Our goal is to identify principles that support efficient updates without catastrophic forgetting. We organize related work by pretraining, fine-tuning regimes, and evaluation datasets. We then situate our study with respect to multimodal alignment, calibration, and continual learning. We also note the role of losses such as contrastive InfoNCE and soft-label distillation. The following review surveys prominent models and corpora commonly used in the literature. We present the survey before turning to the assumptions of our experimental framework. All references are selected to reflect scalable and reproducible baselines. CLIP pretrains a dual-encoder on 400M image–text pairs from diverse web sources, while ALIGN scales a similar contrastive objective with larger noisy alt-text data. BLIP integrates caption bootstrapping with vision encoders and language decoders to improve image–text understanding on COCO, Flickr30k, and Conceptual Captions. Flamingo introduces cross-attention between a frozen visual backbone and a large language model for few-shot multimodal tasks across VQAv2, VizWiz, TextCaps, and OK-VQA. LiT explores locked-image tuning with a frozen image tower and trains only the text encoder using ImageNet and JFT labels for strong zero-shot transfer. Data curation has expanded with LAION-400M and LAION-5B, and filtering strategies such as CLIP-score and aesthetic scoring; benchmarks include ImageNet, ImageNet-V2, ObjectNet, and ImageNet-R for robustness evaluation. Adaptation techniques feature linear probing on frozen features, prompt tuning and textual inversion for category conditioning, and adapter modules inserted into ViT or Transformer layers, with reported gains on domain-specific sets like iNaturalist, Places, and EuroSAT. Additional evaluations cover CLIP-B LAION variants, Winoground, Hateful Memes, and NLVR2, and pretraining mixes such as CC12M, YFCC100M, and RedCaps under comparable compute budgets in recent releases. While these ingredients demonstrate impressive breadth, their interactions with shift-aware tuning remain under-explored. We study how label scarcity and feature drift alter the calibration of text prompts and adapters. Specifically, we compare frozen, partially frozen, and fully trainable configurations under equalized compute and data budgets. Our analysis highlights that naive prompting can overfit spurious lexical cues when the retrieval distribution narrows. Conversely, lightweight adapters tend to preserve global alignment at the cost of local discrimination without targeted regularization. We therefore examine temperature scaling and entropy margin losses to rebalance confidence under domain shift. We also revisit dataset filtering, measuring how duplicate captions inflate apparent generalization. To ground the evaluation, we adopt a protocol that separates category shift from style shift using controlled subsamples. This design clarifies whether failures arise from vocabulary mismatch or visual covariates. We supplement accuracy with retrieval calibration error and conditional coverage metrics. These metrics expose systematic undercoverage on rare classes despite stable top-1 accuracy. Diagnostic ablations further reveal that mixing a small number of in-domain pairs can outperform extensive generic pretraining. The finding aligns with theoretical expectations for representation smoothing under covariate shift. Finally, we report cost curves to relate performance to wall-clock and memory footprints. This perspective informs practical choices for adapting foundation models in constrained settings. We close with implications for dataset construction and open challenges in safe deployment.
spans: CLIP pretrains a dual-encoder on 400M image–text pairs from diverse web sources, while ALIGN scales a similar contrastive objective with larger noisy alt-text data. BLIP integrates caption bootstrapping with vision encoders and language decoders to improve image–text understanding on COCO, Flickr30k, and Conceptual Captions. Flamingo introduces cross-attention between a frozen visual backbone and a large language model for few-shot multimodal tasks across VQAv2, VizWiz, TextCaps, and OK-VQA. LiT explores locked-image tuning with a frozen image tower and trains only the text encoder using ImageNet and JFT labels for strong zero-shot transfer. Data curation has expanded with LAION-400M and LAION-5B, and filtering strategies such as CLIP-score and aesthetic scoring; benchmarks include ImageNet, ImageNet-V2, ObjectNet, and ImageNet-R for robustness evaluation. Adaptation techniques feature linear probing on frozen features, prompt tuning and textual inversion for category conditioning, and adapter modules inserted into ViT or Transformer layers, with reported gains on domain-specific sets like iNaturalist, Places, and EuroSAT. Additional evaluations cover CLIP-B LAION variants, Winoground, Hateful Memes, and NLVR2, and pretraining mixes such as CC12M, YFCC100M, and RedCaps under comparable compute budgets in recent releases.

document: Reinforcement learning in high-dimensional environments requires sample-efficient representations. A central question is how to disentangle controllable and spurious factors to enable robust policy transfer. Prior work suggests that causal structure can regularize learning, yet concrete practices vary across domains. We consider the intersection of model-based rollouts, invariance penalties, and data augmentation. Our scope includes visual control tasks and discrete-action benchmarks where dynamics and confounding differ. We define transfer as maintaining performance under interventions on textures, camera poses, or reward mappings. This section reviews key baselines that motivate our experimental design. We emphasize the gap between proxy metrics and downstream returns. To keep comparisons faithful, all algorithms are trained under matched compute and replay budgets. The following literature summarizes common paradigms and datasets used to evaluate representation quality. Rainbow combines multiple DQN improvements, including distributional value estimation, prioritized replay, multi-step targets, and noisy nets, and is a standard baseline on Atari 2600. Dreamer and DreamerV2 learn latent dynamics with world models and optimize imagined trajectories on DeepMind Control Suite and Atari. CURL leverages contrastive learning over augmentations to improve sample efficiency on pixel-based control, while SPR and DrQv2 use strong data augmentation and target networks to stabilize learning. Dyna-style planning integrates learned models with on-policy updates, and MuZero unifies tree search with value and policy learning across Atari, Go, and chess. Procgen and DMControl Generalization Benchmark introduce procedural generation and held-out visual variations to assess robustness, and CAUSALWORLD offers factorized robotic tasks with known generative structure. Methods such as Invariant Risk Minimization, Domain Adversarial Training, and AugMix-inspired perturbations have been applied to RL pretraining, alongside datasets like Atari-100k, URLB, and RL Unplugged for offline evaluation. Representation learning approaches based on bisimulation metrics, contrastive predictive coding, and prototypical RL have also been reported, with evaluations on Meta-World, DMControl tasks with color jitter, and Atari Human Starts protocols in standard leaderboards. Despite broad coverage, these systems vary in how they respond to interventions that break spurious pixel correlations. We frame this sensitivity through a causal lens, distinguishing invariant dynamics from context-specific appearance. To that end, we estimate environment-specific nuisance variables and regularize policies toward agreement across interventions. Our objective couples a latent transition model with a contrastive invariance penalty derived from counterfactual rollouts. The design complements data augmentation by requiring consistency across synthetic interventions that preserve controllable factors. We compare against strong baselines under fixed wall-clock and parameter budgets. The analysis decomposes gains into representation alignment, model bias, and credit assignment. We evaluate on Atari-100k, Procgen Hard, and DMControlGB to probe distinct forms of shift. For fairness, we re-tune key hyperparameters and report interquartile performance across seeds. We also examine failure cases where invariance conflicts with exploration incentives. These cases reveal that naive penalties can stall discovery of reward-relevant features. A targeted schedule that defers invariance until after value shaping mitigates the stall. Further ablations test memory capacity, model horizon, and augmentation diversity. We summarize computational costs to contextualize potential deployment trade-offs. Together, the results articulate when structure helps and when it must yield to exploration. We conclude with limitations and opportunities for integrating causal discovery with scalable RL.

Dreamer and DreamerV2 learn latent dynamics with world models and optimize imagined trajectories on DeepMind Control Suite and Atari. CURL leverages contrastive learning over augmentations to improve sample efficiency on pixel-based control, while SPR and DrQv2 use strong data augmentation and target networks to stabilize learning. document: Multilingual automatic speech recognition seeks to balance cross-lingual transfer with language-specific nuance. Scaling encoders and pretraining on diverse audio has improved average accuracy but masks low-resource disparities. Distillation promises efficiency and personalization by transferring knowledge from strong teachers to compact students. However, choices about targets, alignments, and multilingual capacity interact in nontrivial ways. We examine prior approaches spanning CTC, sequence-to-sequence, and transducer formulations. Our interest centers on how supervision granularity and language conditioning affect generalization. We also consider data selection and augmentation for balancing typological coverage. This section outlines representative models, datasets, and training heuristics used in the literature. The survey organizes references by pretraining, architectures, and evaluation suites. We first review recent large-scale systems that motivate distillation strategies. wav2vec 2.0 introduced self-supervised pretraining with contrastive predictive coding on unlabeled audio, followed by CTC fine-tuning on Librispeech and other corpora. HuBERT extended masked prediction with clustered targets and reported gains on Libri-Light and VoxCeleb, while XLS-R scaled multilingual pretraining across 128 languages using CommonVoice, BABEL, and VoxPopuli. Whisper trained an encoder–decoder on large web-scale weakly labeled data and reports robust zero-shot transcription and translation on diverse benchmarks. Classic supervised architectures include LAS for attention-based sequence-to-sequence, RNN-T for streaming recognition, and hybrid HMM–DNN CTC systems with SpecAugment regularization. Multilingual benchmarks commonly include FLEURS, MLS, and CommonVoice, and low-resource settings often use IARPA BABEL subsets and OpenSLR corpora for targeted evaluation. Teacher–student pipelines appear with frame-level CTC posteriors, encoder hidden-state regression, and decoupled language model distillation for shallow fusion decoding, with reported improvements on VoxPopuli ASR, TED-LIUM, and multilingual Librispeech. Parameter-efficient training approaches including adapters, low-rank factorization, and multilingual bottlenecks have been explored across wav2vec-style encoders, and evaluation typically reports WER on Librispeech, CoVoST, and FLEURS subsets with per-language breakdowns. Cross-lingual sharing is often measured using phoneme error trends and transfer ratios. Despite these advances, cross-lingual distillation can amplify teacher biases when phonotactics diverge. We investigate whether soft targets should be conditioned on language-specific adapters to avoid over-regularization. Our formulation aligns teacher posteriors with student emissions through a calibrated temperature per language. We couple this with pronunciation-aware masking that emphasizes rare grapheme–phoneme correspondences. The training schedule alternates multilingual and monolingual batches to stabilize normalization. We benchmark capacity-controlled students to isolate the effect of supervision granularity. Evaluation considers WER, CER, and coverage of named entities across resource tiers. We include fairness diagnostics by reporting per-gender and per-accent gaps when metadata permits. Ablations assess the role of teacher capacity, data scale, and adapter placement. The results indicate that naive temperature sharing degrades transfer in agglutinative languages. Language-conditioned calibration reduces this degradation without sacrificing streaming latency. We also find that modest multilingual bottlenecks outperform heavy adapters at constant FLOPs. Error analyses show improvements on code-switch boundaries and diacritics restoration. We release scripts to reproduce all settings within standard open-source toolkits. Finally, we discuss remaining pitfalls and opportunities for robust multilingual deployment.

HuBERT extended masked prediction with clustered targets and reported gains on Libri-Light and VoxCeleb, while XLS-R scaled multilingual pretraining across 128 languages using CommonVoice, BABEL, and VoxPopuli. Whisper trained an encoder–decoder on large web-scale weakly labeled data and reports robust zero-shot transcription and translation on diverse benchmarks.
