Multilingual benchmarks commonly include FLEURS, MLS, and CommonVoice, and low-resource settings often use IARPA BABEL subsets and OpenSLR corpora for targeted evaluation. Teacher–student pipelines appear with frame-level CTC posteriors, encoder hidden-state regression, and decoupled language model distillation for shallow fusion decoding, with reported improvements on VoxPopuli ASR, TED-LIUM, and multilingual Librispeech. Parameter-efficient training approaches including adapters, low-rank factorization, and multilingual bottlenecks have been explored across wav2vec-style encoders, and evaluation typically reports WER on Librispeech, CoVoST, and FLEURS subsets with per-language breakdowns. Cross-lingual sharing is often measured using phoneme error trends and transfer ratios.

document: Traffic forecasting relies on modeling spatial and temporal dependencies over sensor networks. Road graphs are dynamic, noisy, and partially observed, creating challenges for stable prediction. Graph neural networks have emerged as strong backbones for these settings. However, architectural choices and training regimes complicate fair comparison. We revisit spatiotemporal modules with an eye toward inductive generalization. This section reviews representative methods and datasets that inform our study design. We separate works by how they encode spatial topology, temporal recurrence, and attention. We also note evaluation practices and typical preprocessing pipelines. The review highlights common baselines and canonical benchmarks. We begin with sequence models and progress to adaptive graphs. DCRNN models traffic flow with diffusion convolution and sequence-to-sequence GRUs, and reports gains on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY under multi-step horizons. STGCN employs localized graph convolutions with temporal gated convolutions to capture spatiotemporal patterns, evaluated on METR-LA and PEMS datasets. Graph WaveNet combines dilated temporal convolutions with adaptive adjacency matrices and achieves strong results on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, and PEMS-03/04/07/08. MTGNN integrates graph learning with multi-scale temporal convolutions and shows improvements on several PEMS benchmarks and Electricity. GMAN applies multi-step attention with learnable embeddings for nodes and time indices, validated on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY. ASTGCN introduces spatial and temporal attention along with multi-component time-of-day modules and presents results on TaxiBJ and METR-LA. Additional baselines include LSTNet, Temporal Convolutional Networks, and Transformer variants with positional encodings, and common datasets extend to BikeNYC and Shenzhen Taxi, with metrics such as MAE, RMSE, and MAPE. Dynamic graph approaches leverage attention over recent readings, Chebyshev polynomials, or latent space kernels, and training tricks include scheduled sampling, curriculum learning, and layer normalization across horizons on unified preprocessing. Public leaderboards typically report 12, 24, and 48 step forecasts metrics. Despite architectural diversity, most models assume fixed stationarity within sampling windows. We test whether adaptive topology and horizon-specific normalization reduce brittleness under regime changes. Our design factors node degree, road capacity, and incident alerts into a unified conditioning signal. We contrast learned adjacency with exogenous priors from distance and speed limits. The evaluation isolates improvements attributable to topology updates versus temporal ensembling. We adopt a consistent pipeline for imputation, scaling, and edge pruning to avoid confounds. We measure error profiles by congestion level to capture heteroskedastic behavior. Experiments indicate that horizon-aware normalization stabilizes multi-step rollouts during peaks. Conversely, naive dynamic graphs can overreact to sensor noise without temporal smoothing. An ablation over incident alerts shows gains concentrate on disruptive events. We report compute, memory, and latency to contextualize deployment in urban centers. The framework complements existing architectures rather than replacing them. We discuss implications for city planning and resilient transport operations. Limitations and future directions close the section.

STGCN employs localized graph convolutions with temporal gated convolutions to capture spatiotemporal patterns, evaluated on METR-LA and PEMS datasets. Graph WaveNet combines dilated temporal convolutions with adaptive adjacency matrices and achieves strong results on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, and PEMS-03/04/07/08. MTGNN integrates graph learning with multi-scale temporal convolutions and shows improvements on several PEMS benchmarks and Electricity. document: Program synthesis with pretrained language models has advanced rapidly across generation, repair, and explanation tasks. Despite strong average performance, failure modes persist under distribution shift and weak specifications. We study test-time strategies that align sampling with executable semantics. Our focus is distinct from scaling alone and targets controllable improvements. This section reviews representative systems and benchmarks that shape current practice. We organize prior work by pretraining data, objective functions, and evaluation protocols. We also summarize common repair datasets and metrics. The aim is to clarify assumptions and typical baselines. We restrict attention to open and widely cited resources to facilitate replication. We begin with large-scale code models and then cover repair systems. Codex demonstrates strong few-shot code generation across Python and other languages and is commonly evaluated on HumanEval and MBPP. AlphaCode trains on competitive programming data and reports success on Codeforces-style problems and APPS subsets. CodeT5 integrates span corruption and identifier-aware pretraining, while CodeBERT and GraphCodeBERT provide bimodal encoders for code–text retrieval and understanding, with results on CodeXGLUE tasks. PolyCoder and SantaCoder offer open-source baselines trained on permissive corpora, and StarCoder extends coverage with filtered GitHub data. For program repair, DeepFix targets C programs with compiler errors, DrRepair leverages feedback-directed search with neural guidance, and TFix applies sequence-to-sequence learning to real-world fixes from Git histories. Defects4J, QuixBugs, Bears, and ManySStuBs4J supply curated bug–fix pairs, while IntroClass and Codeflaws cover student code. Execution-based evaluation frequently uses pass@k, exact match, and coverage metrics, and harnesses rely on unit tests or fuzzing to validate outputs. Repair benchmarks often include synthetic mutation suites and real-world bugs, and compile-and-run pipelines compare generation, beam search, and self-consistency sampling across time and memory budgets. Training data sources span BigQuery GitHub, CodeSearchNet, and Stack Overflow dumps with license filtering policies. While strong results accrue on standardized tasks, many successes rely on permissive or redundant test suites. We address this gap by conditioning sampling on specification signals available at test time. The method integrates execution traces into the decoding objective through lightweight reranking. Unlike retraining, the approach leaves model parameters untouched and adds negligible overhead. We analyze effects on pass@k, coverage, and unique solution counts across multiple seeds. The study includes controlled ablations over prompt format, timeouts, and compiler feedback. We examine generalization under adversarial unit tests that target spurious shortcuts. Results show consistent gains when trace feedback is informative and neutral changes otherwise. Error analysis highlights shifts from brittle heuristics to semantically grounded edits. We also compare search strategies to characterize diversity and redundancy in candidate sets. Practical considerations include sandboxing, caching, and deterministic toolchains. Cost profiles indicate improved throughput per successful solution at fixed budgets. Our findings inform deployment strategies for code generation in continuous integration pipelines. We end with limitations and directions for specification-aware generation.

document: Predicting molecular properties from graphs underpins discovery in chemistry and drug design. Graph neural networks have become the de facto paradigm due to their ability to exploit relational inductive biases. Despite strong results, performance varies widely across datasets and evaluation protocols. Recent work highlights sensitivity to architecture choices, pooling strategies, and feature engineering. Moreover, discrepancies between scaffold and random splits complicate fair comparison. A clear account of baseline behavior under controlled settings remains incomplete. We situate our study in the landscape of graph representation learning for molecules. Early graph convolutional models framed node aggregation with spectral filters and approximate convolutions (Bruna et al., 2014; Kipf and Welling, 2017). Inductive neighborhood sampling in GraphSAGE enabled scalable training on evolving graphs (Hamilton et al., 2017), while attention-based propagation in GAT emphasized feature-dependent weighting of neighbors (Veličković et al., 2018). Message Passing Neural Networks unified update and readout operations for molecular property prediction and popularized sum/mean pooling variants (Gilmer et al., 2017), and later extensions explored edge features, virtual nodes, and hierarchical pooling (Xu et al., 2019; Ying et al., 2018; Cangea et al., 2018). Benchmarking commonly relies on MoleculeNet suites such as QM9, ESOL, and FreeSolv, as well as ZINC, Open Graph Benchmark molecules (Hu et al., 2020), and classification tasks like Tox21 and HIV (Wu et al., 2018; Irwin et al., 2012). Standard evaluation includes MAE, RMSE, ROC-AUC, and PR-AUC metrics scores. These developments set the stage for examining how architectural and data-centric factors interact in standardized pipelines. However, current benchmarks often conflate hyperparameter search with model capacity, obscuring the sources of gains. We therefore disentangle featurization, message aggregation, and readout under unified training budgets. Our analysis emphasizes cross-dataset stability and sensitivity to scaffold shift. We also assess the effect of explicit 3D information versus learned positional encodings. To ensure comparability, we harmonize preprocessing, early stopping, and metric reporting. The resulting measurements illuminate when added complexity yields reproducible benefits. Ultimately, our protocol aims to support reliable selection of molecular GNNs for real-world screening.

Message Passing Neural Networks unified update and readout operations for molecular property prediction and popularized sum/mean pooling variants (Gilmer et al., 2017), and later extensions explored edge features, virtual nodes, and hierarchical pooling (Xu et al., 2019; Ying et al., 2018; Cangea et al., 2018). Standard evaluation includes MAE, RMSE, ROC-AUC, and PR-AUC metrics scores.

document: Clinical text analysis must reconcile domain-specific terminology with privacy, sparsity, and annotation costs. Transfer learning with large pre-trained encoders has amplified downstream accuracy while reducing task-specific engineering. Yet clinical narratives remain heterogeneous across institutions and writing styles. Differences in note templates, coding practices, and temporal coverage challenge robustness. Furthermore, risk-sensitive deployment requires transparent evaluation protocols beyond single metrics. Prior studies emphasize that reported improvements can hinge on preprocessing choices and label definition. We review representative resources and models that frame the current evaluation landscape. To contextualize existing results, we summarize datasets and modeling trends. Domain-adapted language models trained on biomedical corpora have reported gains across multiple tasks, including BioBERT (Lee et al., 2020), SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019), ClinicalBERT (Alsentzer et al., 2019), and PubMedBERT (Gu et al., 2021). Benchmarks commonly evaluate on datasets such as i2b2/VA shared tasks for de-identification and concept extraction, the n2c2 challenges for medication extraction and smoking status, MedNLI for inference over clinical text, and the ShARe/CLEF eHealth corpus for disorder mentions. Additional resources include MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV for critical care notes, PhysioNet challenges for mortality prediction, and MedQA and PubMedQA for question answering in clinical and biomedical domains. Prompting and instruction-tuning with medical question sets, exam-style problems, and synthetic dialogues have also been explored, including MultiMedQA, MedMCQA, and USMLE-style evaluations. Shared baselines often pair cased tokenizers with UMLS-based concept linking, conditional random fields for sequence labeling, and span-level micro-averaged F1 for reporting. Despite the breadth of resources, comparability is limited by inconsistent splits, leakage risks, and varying de-identification levels. We focus on harmonized evaluation that separates lexical memorization from clinical reasoning skills. Our protocol aligns training budgets, hyperparameter ranges, and early stopping across tasks. We further analyze calibration and out-of-distribution behavior for high-stakes predictions. In doing so, we trace which capabilities transfer across tasks and which require domain adaptation. The resulting insights clarify where modeling effort should prioritize efficiency or supervision. This study aims to provide practical guidance for reliable clinical NLP evaluation.

Benchmarks commonly evaluate on datasets such as i2b2/VA shared tasks for de-identification and concept extraction, the n2c2 challenges for medication extraction and smoking status, MedNLI for inference over clinical text, and the ShARe/CLEF eHealth corpus for disorder mentions. document: Robots must learn robust control policies that handle rich perception and dynamics. Data-driven reinforcement learning promises general-purpose behaviors without meticulous system identification. However, instability, sample inefficiency, and evaluation variance impede practical deployment. Benchmarks often lack standardized reporting across seeds and time limits. Moreover, success in simulation does not guarantee safe real-world transfer. We outline representative algorithms and evaluation settings relevant to this study. Model-free deep reinforcement learning has produced canonical algorithms including DQN for discrete control, actor–critic variants such as A3C and PPO, and off-policy continuous control methods like DDPG, TD3, and SAC (Mnih et al., 2015; Schulman et al., 2017; Lillicrap et al., 2016; Fujimoto et al., 2018; Haarnoja et al., 2018). Standardized evaluation often uses OpenAI Gym and MuJoCo locomotion tasks, DeepMind Control Suite, and robot manipulation benchmarks in Robosuite and Meta-World (Brockman et al., 2016; Todorov et al., 2012; Tassa et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020). Sim-to-real transfer has leveraged domain randomization, dynamics randomization, and privileged learning in simulation before deployment on hardware (Tobin et al., 2017; Peng et al., 2018; Rajeswaran et al., 2017). Offline RL and imitation learning leverage D4RL datasets and logs, with BC, DAgger, and GAIL baselines (Fu et al., 2020; Ho and Ermon, 2016). While these ingredients are widely used, their interactions with runtime constraints remain under-explored. In particular, the trade-offs between data reuse and stability vary across tasks and reward scales. We examine how update-to-data ratios influence learning under fixed wall-clock budgets. Our protocol enforces identical replay sizes, network widths, and gradient steps for comparability. We include sensitivity analyses to initialization and stochasticity in simulators. For transfer, we assess policy robustness under randomized contacts and sensor noise. We also evaluate episode return, success rates, and safety thresholds under unified timeouts. The resulting picture clarifies which design choices matter most when resources are limited. Based on these observations, we provide guidelines for efficient controller training on new robots. Our findings target reliable, compute-aware reinforcement learning for embodied systems.

Standardized evaluation often uses OpenAI Gym and MuJoCo locomotion tasks, DeepMind Control Suite, and robot manipulation benchmarks in Robosuite and Meta-World (Brockman et al., 2016; Todorov et al., 2012; Tassa et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020). Sim-to-real transfer has leveraged domain randomization, dynamics randomization, and privileged learning in simulation before deployment on hardware (Tobin et al., 2017; Peng et al., 2018; Rajeswaran et al., 2017). Offline RL and imitation learning leverage D4RL datasets and logs, with BC, DAgger, and GAIL baselines (Fu et al., 2020; Ho and Ermon, 2016).

document: Low-resource machine translation remains challenging due to data scarcity, domain mismatch, and orthographic variation. Practical deployments must also contend with noisy web text and limited compute. While scaling improves average quality, small languages often lag behind widely spoken ones. Understanding what contributes most to gains is complicated by heterogeneous training recipes. Classical statistical machine translation leveraged phrase-based models and lexical reordering with strong language models (Koehn et al., 2003; Och and Ney, 2004). Neural machine translation introduced encoder–decoder architectures with attention and later Transformers that improved fluency and long-range dependencies (Bahdanau et al., 2015; Vaswani et al., 2017). Data augmentation approaches such as back-translation, dual learning, and self-training enabled gains in low-resource regimes (Sennrich et al., 2016; He et al., 2016; Xie et al., 2020). Multilingual pretraining and transfer explored mBART, mT5, and massively multilingual models trained on OPUS and CCAligned (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; El-Kishky et al., 2020; Tiedemann, 2012). Evaluation typically reports BLEU, chrF, and COMET on WMT and IWSLT test sets, with coverage checks on FLORES-101 and Bible-derived corpora. Recent datasets also include JW300, ParaCrawl, and TED Talks collections with varying quality filters and deduplication pipelines and releases. Despite these advances, systematic comparisons under consistent tokenization and filtering are still rare. Reported improvements sometimes reflect corpus composition rather than modeling differences. We therefore examine preprocessing decisions and parameter budgets as primary variables. Our study isolates effects of back-translation amounts across morphologically diverse families. We analyze transfer patterns as a function of lexical overlap and script sharing. To ensure fairness, we control for subword vocabulary size and source-target balance. We report statistical significance and robustness to alternate test sets. We further investigate calibration and uncertainty for downstream interactive settings. These results distill practical recommendations for low-resource training pipelines. The analysis aims to inform cost-aware translation for underserved languages.

Evaluation typically reports BLEU, chrF, and COMET on WMT and IWSLT test sets, with coverage checks on FLORES-101 and Bible-derived corpora. Recent datasets also include JW300, ParaCrawl, and TED Talks collections with varying quality filters and deduplication pipelines and releases.

document: Detecting small, densely packed objects in aerial imagery remains a fundamental challenge. High viewing angles, scale variation, and clutter complicate feature sharing across levels. Rotations and extreme aspect ratios further stress anchor design and matching. Data imbalance and label noise also degrade learning in operational settings. Prior work has explored specialized heads and oriented anchors, yet generalization remains uneven. Understanding how common design choices interact with aerial data regimes is necessary. We summarize prevalent architectures and benchmarks that define the current landscape. This overview provides context for the evaluation protocol adopted in our study. We first recall representative detectors and datasets in use. Object detection frameworks have evolved from two-stage region-based models to single-stage detectors with real-time performance, including Faster R-CNN, Mask R-CNN, RetinaNet, SSD, and YOLO variants (Ren et al., 2015; He et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2016; Redmon et al., 2016; Bochkovskiy et al., 2020). Aerial and remote sensing benchmarks provide diverse targets and resolutions, such as DOTA for oriented objects, xView for large-scale satellite scenes, VisDrone for UAV imagery, and DIOR for multi-class optical remote sensing (Xia et al., 2018; Lam et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020). Training commonly begins with ImageNet or COCO pretraining and applies multi-scale augmentation, mosaic strategies, and anchor optimization. Evaluation typically reports AP, AP50, AP75, and mAP across scales, with oriented IoU variants for rotated boxes. Public toolkits include MMDetection, Detectron2, and mmdet-rotated baselines for reproducible experiments and comparisons. Despite broad adoption, existing reports differ in image tiling, NMS for rotated boxes, and test-time scaling. These choices yield large swings in accuracy that obscure architectural effects. We therefore fix preprocessing, label formats, and augmentation when comparing variants. Additionally, we analyze sensitivity to resolution, stride, and training duration. The consolidated findings clarify robust defaults for aerial detection at varied budgets. Our goal is to support reliable, reproducible deployment in time-critical applications.

Aerial and remote sensing benchmarks provide diverse targets and resolutions, such as DOTA for oriented objects, xView for large-scale satellite scenes, VisDrone for UAV imagery, and DIOR for multi-class optical remote sensing (Xia et al., 2018; Lam et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020). Training commonly begins with ImageNet or COCO pretraining and applies multi-scale augmentation, mosaic strategies, and anchor optimization. Evaluation typically reports AP, AP50, AP75, and mAP across scales, with oriented IoU variants for rotated boxes. Public toolkits include MMDetection, Detectron2, and mmdet-rotated baselines for reproducible experiments and comparisons.

document:
Introduction

Urban traffic forecasting is a core problem in intelligent transportation and city-scale planning. Accurate predictions inform congestion mitigation, signal control, and traveler information systems. Advances in sensing have produced dense freeway and arterial datasets with complex spatial dependencies. Modeling these dependencies alongside nonlinear temporal dynamics remains challenging under missing data and incidents. Recent progress in deep learning highlights the importance of structure-aware temporal modeling for multi-horizon forecasts.

Recent spatiotemporal forecasting models have explored graph structures and temporal convolutions across benchmark highway sensor datasets. STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and MTGNN (Wu et al., 2020) report strong results on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY, while GMAN (Zheng et al., 2020) and AGCRN (Bai et al., 2020) incorporate attention and adaptive adjacency. Temporal models such as LSTNet (Lai et al., 2018) and Informer (Zhou et al., 2021) emphasize long-range dependencies, and continuous-time architectures like Neural ODEs (Chen et al., 2018) have also been applied. Data augmentation and robustness have been examined through traffic incident corpora and synthetic perturbations (Zhang et al., 2020; Cui et al., 2020), and external signals from weather APIs and calendar features are frequently used (Hoang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2020). Standard evaluation compares multi-step horizons using MAE, RMSE, and MAPE on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, and PEMS08, often with masked loss for missing sensors and fixed train/validation/test splits under common random seeds and protocols.

Despite substantial benchmarks and tooling, robustness under distribution shift is still under-specified in many studies. Sensor outages, regime changes from weather or events, and network reconfigurations degrade performance in deployment. Furthermore, latency constraints and streaming inference limit the feasibility of heavyweight architectures in practice. These considerations motivate approaches that quantify uncertainty and adapt dynamically to structural perturbations.

We investigate spatiotemporal forecasting with an emphasis on transferable graph structure and efficient temporal operators. Our study isolates the effects of graph construction, temporal receptive field, and exogenous variable integration. We also provide a standardized protocol for cross-dataset transfer and ablation under realistic outage scenarios. The results demonstrate consistent gains while clarifying trade-offs relevant to deployment at metropolitan scale.

Introduction

Instruction-tuned language models have rapidly advanced general-purpose NLP and conversational assistance. Aligning model behavior with human intent requires data curation, scalable training, and principled evaluation. Practical deployments must balance capability, safety, and efficiency under limited supervision. Open benchmarks and training corpora vary widely in composition, licensing, and quality. This diversity complicates comparisons across methods and reported outcomes.

Instruction-following and alignment of large language models have been pursued through supervised finetuning on heterogeneous task collections and through preference-based optimization. Datasets such as Super-NaturalInstructions (Wang et al., 2022), FLAN and FLAN-T5 (Wei et al., 2021; Chung et al., 2022), T0 (Sanh et al., 2022), Dolly (Conover et al., 2023), and Alpaca (Taori et al., 2023) compile templated prompts and demonstrations. Self-Instruct (Wang et al., 2023) and related bootstrapping recipes generate synthetic instruction–response pairs, while Vicuna (Chiang et al., 2023) and OpenAssistant (Köpf et al., 2023) rely on community-contributed conversations. Reinforcement learning from human feedback adopts preference datasets and reward modeling (Ouyang et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022; Stiennon et al., 2020), and direct preference optimization and related methods remove explicit reward models (Rafailov et al., 2023; Rafailov et al., 2024). Evaluation also reports MT-Bench results (Zheng et al., 2023), HELM and BIG-bench aggregates (Liang et al., 2022; Srivastava et al., 2022), and red-teaming benchmarks (Ganguli et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2024), with hallucination and toxicity audits.

Despite the breadth of datasets and protocols, measurement remains fragile across prompt formats and reference sets. Small implementation differences can overshadow algorithmic contributions when scaling laws are not controlled. Moreover, safety evaluations often lack coverage for non-English contexts and emerging jailbreak tactics. Addressing these issues requires rigorous ablations, transparent releases, and cross-lab replication.

We study instruction finetuning under matched compute, data size, and prompt templates. Our analysis decomposes gains from supervision, preference optimization, and refusal heuristics. We provide open training scripts, artifact cards, and evaluation seeds for reproducibility. The resulting insights guide robust alignment choices for medium-scale models.

Related Work

Understanding actions in untrimmed videos requires recognizing categories and localizing temporal boundaries. Realistic media streams exhibit long-range dependencies, background clutter, and overlapping events. Fully supervised labels are costly, motivating scalable architectures and data-efficient regimes. Meanwhile, pretraining and feature sharing influence downstream detection accuracy. Prior art spans proposal generation, classification, and end-to-end optimization.

Temporal action recognition and detection in videos leverage convolutional and transformer backbones together with proposal or anchor-free localization. 3D CNNs such as I3D (Carreira and Zisserman, 2017), SlowFast (Feichtenhofer et al., 2019), and TSM (Lin et al., 2019) provide spatiotemporal features, while TimeSformer (Bertasius et al., 2021) and VideoSwin (Liu et al., 2022) supply tokenized attention. For temporal localization, methods include SSN (Zhao et al., 2017), BMN (Lin et al., 2019b), P-GCN (Zeng et al., 2019), G-TAD (Xu et al., 2020), and ActionFormer (Zhang et al., 2022). Benchmark datasets such as THUMOS14 (Jiang et al., 2014), ActivityNet v1.3 (Caba Heilbron et al., 2015), HACS (Zhao et al., 2019), and Charades (Sigurdsson et al., 2016) define standard splits, while Kinetics-400/600 (Kay et al., 2017) often supports pretraining. Common evaluation reports mean Average Precision at multiple tIoU thresholds and frame-level recall under proposal budgets, using official codebases and curated feature zips. End-to-end approaches emphasize efficiency with annotations and pseudo-label mining on untrimmed streams (Zhang et al., 2021; Ahmed et al., 2022).

Nevertheless, existing pipelines often assume stationary event priors and dense training coverage. Domain shifts across content genres and camera motion degrade proposal quality and calibration. Moreover, real-time constraints favor single-stage models with adaptive computation budgets. These pressures call for representations that preserve temporal precision under imperfect supervision.

We develop a framework that unifies tokenized attention with boundary-aware objectives. Our experiments examine label sparsity, pretraining choices, and decoding strategies. The study clarifies where capacity and annotation interact to shape localization trade-offs.

For temporal localization, methods include SSN (Zhao et al., 2017), BMN (Lin et al., 2019b), P-GCN (Zeng et al., 2019), G-TAD (Xu et al., 2020), and ActionFormer (Zhang et al., 2022). Related Work

Recommender systems influence user exposure and provider opportunities at scale. Imbalances can harm disadvantaged groups and distort platform ecosystems. Research has explored fairness constraints alongside utility objectives in ranking. Operational constraints and feedback loops complicate interventions beyond offline optimization. As a result, evaluation design is as critical as algorithmic changes.

Fairness-aware recommendation has considered exposure, calibration, and utility trade-offs under user- and item-side constraints. Post-processing and re-ranking methods such as xQuAD (Vargas and Castells, 2011), FA*IR (Zehlike et al., 2017), DELTR (Zehlike and Castillo, 2020), and FairCo (Biega et al., 2020) adjust rankings to meet demographic parity or equalized exposure. In-processing techniques include adversarial debiasing and regularization for collaborative filtering (Yao and Huang, 2017; Beutel et al., 2019; Mansoury et al., 2020), while counterfactual and causal objectives address selection bias (Liang et al., 2016; Schnabel et al., 2016; Bonner and Vasile, 2018). Public datasets like MovieLens-1M, Last.fm, Amazon Reviews, and Yelp are often annotated with sensitive attributes via heuristics or external sources (Ekstrand et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020). Metrics commonly include disparity of exposure (Singh and Joachims, 2018), calibration error (Gatt and Krahmer, 2018), precision/recall, nDCG, and user coverage, with simulation studies on synthetic slates to probe long-term dynamics (Bower et al., 2021; Mehrotra et al., 2018), plus standardized leaderboards to significantly enhance reproducibility across comparable baselines.

Despite active progress, audits frequently rely on proxies for sensitive attributes with unknown error. Counterfactual reasoning is hindered by sparse logging and shifting user populations. Realistic simulations require behavior models calibrated to longitudinal interaction data. Practical deployments must consider incentive compatibility and legal compliance.

We investigate ranking policies that trade exposure guarantees against utility under click-feedback drift. Our approach incorporates uncertainty in attribute inference and couponing effects on supply. We provide open-source tooling for stress tests under simulated marketplace shocks. The findings inform robust fairness allocations for platform-scale recommenders.

In-processing techniques include adversarial debiasing and regularization for collaborative filtering (Yao and Huang, 2017; Beutel et al., 2019; Mansoury et al., 2020), while counterfactual and causal objectives address selection bias (Liang et al., 2016; Schnabel et al., 2016; Bonner and Vasile, 2018). Introduction

Software defects impose significant costs in productivity, reliability, and security. Automated program repair aims to generate patches that satisfy a specification, often via test suites. Benchmark-driven evaluation has standardized comparisons but also incentivized narrow optimizations. Understanding the landscape of techniques and datasets is essential for realistic progress. We overview canonical systems and measurements before describing our study.

Automated program repair has evolved from generate-and-validate pipelines to learning-based patch synthesis over standardized benchmarks. Classical methods include GenProg (Le Goues et al., 2012), AE (Weimer et al., 2009), PAR (Kim et al., 2013), Prophet (Long and Rinard, 2016), and Nopol (Xuan et al., 2017), which search fix spaces guided by tests or probabilistic models. Neural approaches such as DeepFix (Gupta et al., 2017), SequenceR (Chen et al., 2019), TBar (Liu et al., 2019), AlphaRepair (Xia et al., 2022), and Codex-based patching explore token, AST, and edit representations. Benchmarks commonly include Defects4J (Just et al., 2014), Bugs.jar (Saha et al., 2018), ManySStuBs4J (Karampatsis et al., 2020), QuixBugs (Lin et al., 2017), and IntroClass (Le Goues et al., 2012), with additional Python and C corpora. Evaluation reports plausible and correct patch counts, overfitting rates, and timeouts under fixed seeds and resource budgets, often using the Bears and RepairThemAll harnesses (Madeiral et al., 2019; Durieux et al., 2019). Recent studies examine generalization audits (Liu et al., 2021; Fan et al., 2022).

Despite standardized harnesses, many plausible patches remain incorrect under semantically diverse tests. Generation strategies can overfit to project idiosyncrasies and shallow patterns in test oracles. Moreover, dataset leakage and duplicated bugs can inflate estimates of generalization. Stronger baselines and controlled evaluation are necessary to validate improvements.

We examine synthesis and search trade-offs under cost constraints and partial specifications. Our experiments consider cross-project transfer, compiler feedback, and dynamic test augmentation. The analysis provides guidance on scalable, trustworthy repair in continuous integration settings.

Neural approaches such as DeepFix (Gupta et al., 2017), SequenceR (Chen et al., 2019), TBar (Liu et al., 2019), AlphaRepair (Xia et al., 2022), and Codex-based patching explore token, AST, and edit representations. Benchmarks commonly include Defects4J (Just et al., 2014), Bugs.jar (Saha et al., 2018), ManySStuBs4J (Karampatsis et al., 2020), QuixBugs (Lin et al., 2017), and IntroClass (Le Goues et al., 2012), with additional Python and C corpora. Recent studies examine generalization audits (Liu et al., 2021; Fan et al., 2022).

document: Urban mobility systems increasingly rely on accurate short term traffic forecasting to manage congestion and safety. Data are generated by heterogeneous sensors, ride sharing platforms, and connected vehicles, yielding complex spatio temporal patterns. Graph neural networks provide a natural formalism because road segments and intersections can be represented as nodes and edges. However, nonstationarity, topology changes, and missing data persist as central obstacles to reliable deployment. Classical time series models can be interpretable but fail to capture nonlocal dependencies under sparse observations. Recent research therefore emphasizes learning dynamic spatial relations while preserving temporal continuity in noisy settings. Approaches differ in how they parameterize adjacency, fuse exogenous variables, and handle multi horizon prediction. Earlier studies investigate spatio-temporal forecasting using graph convolutional networks and recurrent units (Li et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020), evaluate on benchmarks such as METR-LA and PEMS-BAY (Jain et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018), explore adaptive adjacency or attention mechanisms to model dynamic connectivity (Guo et al., 2019; Pan et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020), and compare against baselines including ARIMA, VAR, and SVR, as well as models like LSTM and Temporal Convolutional Networks (Box and Jenkins, 1976; Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997; Bai et al., 2018). Beyond accuracy, practitioners increasingly value reliability measures that quantify predictive dispersion across the network. Probabilistic forecasting with calibrated uncertainty is particularly important for ramp metering and emergency response. Existing evaluations are often limited to fixed graphs and closed set sensors, obscuring generalization to planned expansions or outages. Data shift arising from weather, events, and infrastructure maintenance requires models that adapt without catastrophic forgetting. Edge directionality and turn restrictions add constraints that complicate straightforward message passing formulations. Multi task settings couple speed, volume, and occupancy, inviting shared representations that respect measurement units and delays. Privacy considerations further restrict the use of high frequency GPS traces, encouraging methods that leverage aggregated counts. Interpretability is also a concern when transportation planners must justify signal timing changes to public stakeholders. Benchmarks rarely report cost sensitive metrics that reflect penalties for late or early congestion warnings. There is a need for protocols that evaluate resilience to sensor failures and topology perturbations in truly open world conditions. Standardizing horizons, loss functions, and error decomposition would facilitate more meaningful comparisons across deployments. We conclude by outlining desiderata for models that remain accurate, calibrated, and robust under structural evolution of urban networks.
spans: Earlier studies investigate spatio-temporal forecasting using graph convolutional networks and recurrent units (Li et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020), evaluate on benchmarks such as METR-LA and PEMS-BAY (Jain et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018), explore adaptive adjacency or attention mechanisms to model dynamic connectivity (Guo et al., 2019; Pan et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020), and compare against baselines including ARIMA, VAR, and SVR, as well as models like LSTM and Temporal Convolutional Networks (Box and Jenkins, 1976; Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997; Bai et al., 2018).

document: Federated learning promises collaboration across hospitals without centralizing protected health information. Imaging workflows are attractive targets because labeling is costly and institutions vary in scanners and protocols. However, optimizing across clinics faces heterogeneity in patient populations, device vendors, and acquisition parameters. Communication budgets and straggler effects further complicate training at scale on real hospital networks. Clinical operations demand auditability and resilience against disconnected clients and partial participation. Secure aggregation reduces leakage but interacts with compression and personalization mechanisms. Domain shift across sites can dominate generalization error despite apparent improvements in local validation. Meaningful evaluation therefore requires stratification by site, modality, and pathology prevalence. The role of self supervised pretraining and feature normalization remains unsettled in privacy constrained learning. Medical governance also imposes requirements for traceability and reproducibility of all model updates. Architectures must accommodate high resolution inputs while maintaining tractable memory footprints at the client. In medical federated learning, optimization baselines include FedAvg, FedProx, SCAFFOLD, and FedNova (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020), with privacy mechanisms such as secure aggregation and differential privacy appearing in practical deployments (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Dwork et al., 2014). Empirical studies evaluate convolutional and transformer models on cohorts or public surrogates like BraTS, CheXpert, ChestX-ray14, and MIMIC-CXR (Menze et al., 2015; Irvin et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017; Johnson et al., 2019), reporting metrics for classification and segmentation and detection performance. Practical deployments must also address partial labeling, weak supervision, and drift in annotation policies. Personalization strategies aim to balance global sharing with local adaptation under strict privacy constraints. Weighted aggregation and proximal regularization can mitigate client imbalance and nonconvexity to a degree. Nevertheless, diagnosing failures requires standardized telemetry and cross site diagnostics that respect governance. Benchmark suites should encompass realistic communication limits and intermittent connectivity patterns. We emphasize that stakeholder incentives and compliance obligations shape the feasible design space as much as algorithms. Finally, anticipated regulatory guidance will likely favor transparent pipelines and rigorous monitoring of fairness and safety.
spans: In medical federated learning, optimization baselines include FedAvg, FedProx, SCAFFOLD, and FedNova (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020), with privacy mechanisms such as secure aggregation and differential privacy appearing in practical deployments (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Dwork et al., 2014). Empirical studies evaluate convolutional and transformer models on cohorts or public surrogates like BraTS, CheXpert, ChestX-ray14, and MIMIC-CXR (Menze et al., 2015; Irvin et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017; Johnson et al., 2019), reporting metrics for classification and segmentation and detection performance.

document: Cross-lingual entity linking aims to map mentions in a source language to canonical entries in a shared knowledge base. The task is difficult because surface forms vary across languages and scripts while context can be scarce. Realistic settings must handle limited training data and domain shift between news, Wikipedia, and social media. Candidate generation often dominates overall accuracy and latency in production pipelines. Recent neural architectures exploit multilingual pretraining but still rely on careful negative sampling to avoid collapse. Prior cross-lingual entity linking studies consider candidate generation and ranking with a variety of resources and encoders, including Wikipedia anchors, cross-lingual dictionaries, and multilingual embeddings such as MUSE, mBERT, and XLM-R (Raiman and Raiman, 2018; Conneau et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020). Evaluations frequently draw on KBP and Wikipedia-based benchmarks datasets like TAC-KBP 2015, AIDA-CoNLL, and multilingual hyperlinks, while comparing sparse retrieval with BM25 against dense bi-encoders and dual encoders using FAISS (Ji et al., 2015; Hoffart et al., 2011; Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009; Johnson et al., 2017). Practical applications must further address ambiguous names and culturally specific disambiguation cues. Document level aggregation and coherence modeling can reduce spurious local matches in noisy settings. Nil detection remains challenging where the target knowledge base is incomplete or out of date. Low resource languages benefit from lexicon expansion and transliteration but coverage gaps persist. Alignment of mention spans and tokenization schemes also impacts both candidate recall and ranking stability. Efficient indexing is necessary for frequent updates and low latency across global traffic patterns. Calibration of confidence estimates is needed for downstream human-in-the-loop curation. Error analysis often reveals biases toward high-resource entities with abundant linking histories. More diversified evaluation is essential to assess robustness across typologically distant language pairs. Governance considerations arise when linking personal names under varying legal frameworks. Scalable triaging should route uncertain cases to human experts with appropriate context. Ultimately, deployment requires attention to audit trails, privacy, and content moderation policies. We frame these practical aspects alongside the modeling considerations to ground evaluation in real constraints.
spans: Prior cross-lingual entity linking studies consider candidate generation and ranking with a variety of resources and encoders, including Wikipedia anchors, cross-lingual dictionaries, and multilingual embeddings such as MUSE, mBERT, and XLM-R (Raiman and Raiman, 2018; Conneau et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020). Evaluations frequently draw on KBP and Wikipedia-based benchmarks datasets like TAC-KBP 2015, AIDA-CoNLL, and multilingual hyperlinks, while comparing sparse retrieval with BM25 against dense bi-encoders and dual encoders using FAISS (Ji et al., 2015; Hoffart et al., 2011; Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009; Johnson et al., 2017).

document: Abstractive summarization aims to condense source texts while generating fluent novel expressions. Reinforcement learning has been introduced to shift training signals toward sequence level quality objectives. However, instability and credit assignment limit consistent gains across domains and lengths. Exposure bias and mismatch between training and evaluation metrics complicate fair comparisons. Reward definitions also struggle to reflect factuality, salience, and readability simultaneously. Recent work examines constrained decoding and calibration to mitigate hallucination without excessive verbosity. Off policy learning and risk-aware objectives remain underexplored in long document settings. Human preferences vary widely, putting pressure on datasets and reward models to represent realistic utility. Content selection and faithfulness must be balanced against stylistic variety and compression rate. Summarization research encompasses extractive and abstractive paradigms with widely used datasets such as CNN/DailyMail, XSum, and Gigaword (Hermann et al., 2015; Narayan et al., 2018; Graff et al., 2003). Prominent model families include pointer-generator networks, transformer encoder-decoders like BART and PEGASUS, and reinforcement learning approaches that directly optimize ROUGE or mixed objectives through policy gradient and actor-critic updates (See et al., 2017; Paulus et al., 2018; Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020), often reported alongside extractive baselines like TextRank and neural sentence selectors (Mihalcea and Tarau, 2004; Nallapati et al., 2017). The interplay between exploration and constrained decoding remains insufficiently characterized for factual summarization. Variance reduction techniques could address noisy gradients but introduce additional bias and computational costs. Batch level reward normalization interacts with length penalties in nontrivial ways during beam search. Furthermore, instruction tuned language models now serve as strong teacher policies for knowledge distillation. Evaluation protocols need stronger human assessment with transparent scales and interrater reliability. Uncertainty estimation is valuable for routing low confidence summaries to human editors. Document structure priors such as discourse and rhetorical roles may stabilize training signals. Resource constraints imply the importance of reproducible recipes for fair and economical studies. These considerations motivate a careful reexamination of how sequence level rewards are constructed and applied.
spans: Summarization research encompasses extractive and abstractive paradigms with widely used datasets such as CNN/DailyMail, XSum, and Gigaword (Hermann et al., 2015; Narayan et al., 2018; Graff et al., 2003). Prominent model families include pointer-generator networks, transformer encoder-decoders like BART and PEGASUS, and reinforcement learning approaches that directly optimize ROUGE or mixed objectives through policy gradient and actor-critic updates (See et al., 2017; Paulus et al., 2018; Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020), often reported alongside extractive baselines like TextRank and neural sentence selectors (Mihalcea and Tarau, 2004; Nallapati et al., 2017).

document: Automatic speech recognition systems suffer significant degradation under additive noise and reverberation. Far-field microphones, moving speakers, and nonstationary backgrounds exacerbate the mismatch between training and deployment. Robustness therefore depends on both acoustic modeling and front-end signal processing choices. Data augmentation often improves performance but can introduce harmful shifts if not carefully tuned. Multi-condition training interacts with language model constraints for decoding in surprising ways. Evaluation setups differ in microphone arrays, room geometry, and noise profiles, complicating cross-paper comparisons. The rise of self-supervised encoders has also changed how small labeled datasets can be exploited. Nevertheless, end-to-end training remains sensitive to batch size, optimizer schedules, and label smoothing. Real-world deployments must consider on-device constraints and streaming latency budgets. Adaptation to new acoustic domains is critical when environments change frequently. Online unsupervised methods can drift without periodic anchors and human oversight. Uncertainty estimation assists in deferring unreliable transcriptions during safety critical operations. Robust speech recognition has been evaluated across corpora including CHiME, Aurora-4, WSJ, and LibriSpeech with synthetic and real noise conditions (Vincent et al., 2013; Pearce and Hirsch, 2000; Paul and Baker, 1992; Panayotov et al., 2015). Methods span acoustic front-end and end-to-end modeling, incorporating multi-microphone beamforming with BeamformIt, data augmentation with MUSAN and simulated RIRs, architectural choices like CTC and RNN-T, and pretraining learning approaches such as wav2vec 2.0 and HuBERT (Anguera et al., 2007; Snyder et al., 2015; Graves et al., 2006; Graves, 2012; Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021). Scoring transcripts solely by word error rate may mask harmful substitutions in rare keywords. Confidence metrics and semantic error rate can complement lexical measures for task oriented scenarios. Personalized language modeling promises accuracy gains but must respect privacy and memory limits. Robustness should also reflect wake word false activations and tail latency in streaming decoders. Open benchmarks with shared noise profiles and room impulse responses would facilitate reproducible progress. Future studies should align metrics with downstream utility while acknowledging compute and device constraints.
spans: Robust speech recognition has been evaluated across corpora including CHiME, Aurora-4, WSJ, and LibriSpeech with synthetic and real noise conditions (Vincent et al., 2013; Pearce and Hirsch, 2000; Paul and Baker, 1992; Panayotov et al., 2015). Methods span acoustic front-end and end-to-end modeling, incorporating multi-microphone beamforming with BeamformIt, data augmentation with MUSAN and simulated RIRs, architectural choices like CTC and RNN-T, and pretraining learning approaches such as wav2vec 2.0 and HuBERT (Anguera et al., 2007; Snyder et al., 2015; Graves et al., 2006; Graves, 2012; Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021).

document: Recent progress in foundation models has reshaped how researchers approach representation learning across tasks and modalities. The ability to pretrain once and adapt to many domains has encouraged broad comparisons and new adaptation strategies. Despite this momentum, the surrounding literature spans diverse problem settings that make synthesis challenging for practitioners. This section summarizes prominent areas and clarifies terminology used throughout the paper. We first highlight directions centered on language modeling and task conditioning at scale. Prior work documents a range of interface designs between pretrained models and task instructions. Work on prompting and instruction tuning spans few-shot templates, in-context exemplars, and supervised collections of task instructions across domains. Brown et al. (2020) demonstrate few-shot prompting with GPT-3; Liu et al. (2021) survey prompt-based learning; Wei et al. (2022) describe chain-of-thought prompting; Sanh et al. (2022) present FLAN; Chung et al. (2022) scale FLAN-T5; Ouyang et al. (2022) incorporate human feedback; Kojima et al. (2022) note zero-shot reasoning; Lester et al. (2021) introduce soft prompts; Li and Liang (2021) propose prefix-tuning; Liu et al. (2022) extend P-Tuning; Scao et al. (2023) release BLOOM; Touvron et al. (2023) release LLaMA. Evaluation and resources commonly include GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2021), WMT (Bojar et al., 2014), and BIG-bench (Srivastava et al., 2022). Additional evaluations appear. These developments form a backdrop for methods that adapt instruction-aware models to specialized domains. Alongside prompting, researchers study architectural and data-centric levers that govern transfer efficiency. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning uses lightweight modules to reduce memory costs while retaining adaptability. Choices about data mixtures, tokenization, and multilingual coverage interact with scaling laws in subtle ways. Safety and evaluation concerns motivate more rigorous reporting practices and ablation protocols. Beyond text, time-indexed data contribute distinct constraints on uncertainty, latency, and supervision regimes. Temporal forecasting has leveraged classic sequence models and recent attention-based architectures across public benchmarks. Datasets such as MIMIC-III (Johnson et al., 2016), eICU (Pollard et al., 2018), PhysioNet 2012 (Silva et al., 2012), and COVID-19 hospital data (Kissler et al., 2020) are frequently used. Methods include autoregressive baselines, LSTM and GRU encoders (Cho et al., 2014), Temporal Convolutional Networks (Bai et al., 2018), Informer (Zhou et al., 2021), Autoformer (Wu et al., 2021), and FEDformer (Zhou et al., 2022). These ingredients motivate careful design of problem splits and baselines when comparing adaptive forecasters. Multimodal modeling raises further questions about alignment across inputs, representations, and downstream objectives. Contrastive pretraining and generative fusion offer complementary perspectives on cross-modal grounding. When signals differ in sampling rate or reliability, architectures must arbitrate which cues dominate predictions. Transfer across modalities can benefit from intermediate targets that encourage compositional structure. Benchmarks that isolate modality gaps help separate modeling progress from dataset artifacts. Graph-structured problems highlight locality, symmetry, and sparsity that interact with scaling differently than sequences. Graph learning applications consider node classification, link prediction, and graph regression with established architectures. Representative methods include GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), GAT (Veličković et al., 2018), Graph Isomorphism Network (Xu et al., 2019), and DiffPool (Ying et al., 2018), alongside benchmarks like Cora, Citeseer, PubMed, ogbn-arxiv, and ogbn-products. Efficient deployment threads through these areas as models move from offline training to interactive environments. Quantization, distillation, and sparsity can reduce footprint while preserving core behaviors over target distributions. Caching, retrieval, and memory augmentations change latency tradeoffs and shift error modes during adaptation. Evaluation in live settings requires measures that reflect context drift and user experience, not only static accuracy. The breadth of techniques and datasets complicates unified comparisons without shared abstractions and protocols. Our framing distinguishes pretraining, adaptation, and evaluation axes to navigate this breadth coherently. We articulate desiderata for reporting and for constructing baselines that tie claims to measurable differences. We delineate scope by focusing on settings where resources and risks are commensurate with plausible deployment. This perspective grounds the subsequent methodology and guides our empirical study.

document: Visual question answering seeks to infer natural language answers from paired images and questions. Despite steady gains on benchmarks, models still rely on superficial correlations and struggle with compositional queries and distribution shift. Prior analyses highlight that evaluation protocols, annotation artifacts, and pretraining data choices can mask true reasoning capabilities. We revisit these issues through the lens of calibration, causality, and robustness under systematically perturbed inputs. To situate our study, we summarize representative datasets and architectures that have shaped the field. Early benchmarks and models for visual question answering established common evaluation practices across datasets and architectures, including VQA v1.0, VQA v2.0, GQA, and CLEVR. Baseline systems ranged from LSTM-question plus CNN-image fusion to attention-based bilinear pooling, with notable variants such as SAN, BAN, MUTAN, and bottom-up top-down attention. Later transformer approaches adopted multimodal pretraining on Conceptual Captions, Visual Genome, and COCO, exemplified by LXMERT, ViLBERT, UNITER, VinVL, and OSCAR. Methods leveraging scene graphs, neural module networks, and neuro-symbolic reasoning targeted compositional generalization, as seen in MAC, NS-VQA, and Neural-Symbolic VQA. Recent work explored prompt-based alignment and contrastive objectives using CLIP, ALIGN, BLIP, and Flamingo, alongside evaluation protocols emphasizing robustness, distribution shift, and textual bias diagnostics. Additional resources include TextCaps, VQA-CP, TDIUC, and VizWiz, which broaden task coverage and stress real-world accessibility challenges too. While informative, this landscape leaves open how training objectives and architectural choices translate into calibrated reasoning under shift. We focus on disentangling shortcut exploitation from causal grounding using controlled interventions on language priors and visual evidence. Our protocol measures counterfactual consistency and answer stability across semantically equivalent paraphrases and visual edits. We develop diagnostics that decompose correctness by reasoning step and quantify uncertainty allocation across modalities. Through these analyses, we outline design principles for evaluation and modeling that better align with robust multimodal reasoning.

document: Predictive monitoring in critical care aims to anticipate adverse events from multivariate physiological streams. Practical deployment is complicated by irregular sampling, missingness, censoring, and nonstationary care practices. Moreover, naive evaluation can inflate performance through leakage and population imbalance, obscuring clinical utility. We study how representation, uncertainty, and data partitioning interact to influence reliable forecasting in the ICU. Before detailing our approach, we outline common datasets, baselines, and evaluation conventions in this area. Clinical time-series forecasting has drawn on varied datasets and modeling paradigms, including MIMIC-III, MIMIC-IV, eICU, and the PhysioNet Challenge cohorts for mortality, decompensation, and length-of-stay prediction. Classical baselines such as ARIMA, VAR, and Kalman filtering are commonly contrasted with neural approaches like LSTM, GRU, TCN, and seq2seq attention models. More recent architectures include Transformer variants for long horizons, such as LogSparse Transformer, Informer, Reformer, and FEDformer, alongside N-BEATS, DeepAR, and Temporal Fusion Transformer for mixed covariates. Handling missingness and irregular sampling typically leverages GRU-D, BRITS, and Latent ODE methods, with imputation benchmarks on UCI HAR, PhysioNet 2012, and MIMIC subsets. Evaluation protocols span AUROC, AUPRC, calibration metrics, horizon-specific MAE, and dynamic time warping, with public leaderboards tracking reproducibility and robustness. Common splits use patient-level partitioning to mitigate leakage across temporal windows and units. Despite these conventions, reported results often vary widely due to inconsistent preprocessing and horizon definitions. We introduce a protocol that standardizes cohort construction, imputations, and horizon windows while preserving clinical interpretability. The method couples uncertainty-aware sequence encoders with task-specific calibration targets to manage shift and missingness. We assess efficiency under limited supervision and examine trade-offs between discrimination, calibration, and timeliness. This framing clarifies which modeling and validation choices materially affect decision support readiness in practice.

document: Molecular property prediction underpins lead optimization, toxicity assessment, and materials discovery. Graph-based learning has become standard for encoding chemical structure and interactions. Nevertheless, inconsistent data splits, descriptor leakage, and limited 3D information complicate fair comparison across methods. We examine evaluation design choices that drive performance differences beyond algorithmic novelty. For context, we summarize widely used datasets, architectures, and pretraining strategies in molecular machine learning. Benchmarking molecular representation learning has centered on MoleculeNet tasks such as ESOL, FreeSolv, Lipophilicity, BBBP, BACE, Tox21, and ToxCast, with extensions to OGB-MolHIV, PCBA, and the OGB-LSC PCQM4M challenge. Message-passing architectures include GCN, GAT, MPNN, D-MPNN, and Weave, while 3D-aware models such as SchNet, PhysNet, DimeNet, DimeNet++, and SE(3)-Transformer incorporate geometric information. Transformer-style encoders like Graphormer, GROVER, and MAT operate over graphs or SMILES with learned attention biases. Self-supervised approaches apply contrastive or generative objectives, including GraphCL, InfoGraph, MolCLR, AttrMasking, and GPT-GNN, sometimes paired with RDKit augmentations. Datasets often provide SMILES strings, SDF conformers, and computed descriptors, with data splits by scaffold, random, or time. Standard metrics include ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, RMSE, and MAE across classification and regression endpoints. Leaderboards track progress using curated featurizations, hyperparameter ranges, and reproducible scaffold splits across multiple benchmarks. Despite this breadth, uncertainty remains about how 3D signals and scaffold splits interact with scale and pretraining. We therefore define a controlled benchmark that balances conformer generation, descriptor usage, and hyperparameter budgets. Our analysis isolates gains attributable to geometry, self-supervision, and data curation rather than search. The findings inform best practices for credible model selection and deployment in low-data discovery settings.

document: Recommender systems increasingly mediate access to information, services, and economic opportunity. Concerns about disparate treatment and exposure motivate methods that account for multiple stakeholders. However, fairness objectives interact with ranking utility, feedback loops, and partial observability in complex ways. This paper studies evaluation protocols that reveal such interactions under realistic logging and retraining. We first review datasets, metrics, and mitigation strategies that commonly appear in the literature. Fairness-aware recommendation has been studied across benchmarks such as MovieLens, Last.fm, Amazon Product Data, Yelp, KuaiRec, and MIND, with simulated environments derived from the RecSys Challenge datasets. Evaluation commonly reports demographic parity, equal opportunity, disparate exposure, calibration, and user-level NDCG alongside provider-side exposure parity. Mitigation strategies include post-hoc re-ranking for exposure control, constrained optimization with fairness regularizers, adversarial debiasing on latent factors, and counterfactual learning-to-rank with IPS or SNIPS propensity weighting. Group definitions span gender, age, and geography inferred from profiles, as well as provider attributes like business category, ownership, and popularity tiers. Recent approaches integrate counterfactual estimators, multi-objective bandits, and slate-level constraints, often comparing to baseline algorithms such as MF, BPR, SASRec, and LightGCN under logged implicit feedback. Audits typically stratify outcomes by cohorts, temporal slices, and sparsity regimes to assess stability. Despite abundant tooling, measurement choices and group definitions can obscure trade-offs and create misleading signals. We formalize exposure, relevance, and uncertainty under counterfactual logging and propose diagnostics for allocation stability. Our framework compares re-ranking and modeling interventions under shared budgets using consistent inference constraints. We analyze dynamics across retraining rounds to separate transient from persistent disparities. The results provide actionable guidance for auditing and improving stakeholder outcomes in practice.

document: Translating natural language intents into executable code is a longstanding goal at the intersection of NLP and PL. Recent advances in pretrained models have substantially improved few-shot and zero-shot code generation. Nevertheless, progress is difficult to assess due to heterogeneous datasets, evaluation metrics, and constraints. We analyze how dataset construction and verification signals affect reported performance and generalization. To contextualize our study, we summarize widely used corpora, model families, and auxiliary supervision. Neural program synthesis from natural language has been evaluated on CoNaLa, Django, and WikiSQL for semantic parsing, with broader coding tasks on CodeSearchNet, MBPP, HumanEval, and APPS. Early sequence-to-sequence baselines used LSTMs with attention and copy mechanisms, followed by grammar-guided decoders such as TranX and Seq2Struct that enforce AST constraints. Pretrained encoder-decoder models including CodeT5, PLBART, and BART adapt to code-text pairs, while large language models like Codex, PaLM-Coder, and AlphaCode demonstrate strong few-shot performance. Auxiliary signals incorporate type signatures, unit tests, and executor feedback, with constrained decoding, beam search with verification, and guided search over DSLs. Common metrics include exact match, BLEU, and pass@k, with robustness studies using adversarial paraphrases and perturbations to identifiers, formatting, and comments. Benchmarks sometimes supply unit tests, reference ASTs, or hidden evaluators to standardize comparison and reproducibility. Despite broad coverage, existing evaluations often conflate memorization with synthesis and underplay specification capture. We propose a controlled protocol with disjoint APIs, withheld templates, and unit-tested tasks to separate these factors. Our method integrates lightweight type constraints during decoding and leverages test-time feedback to refine candidates. We report pass@k, sample efficiency, and repair rates across problem classes with ablations. These results clarify the roles of scale, constraints, and verification in practical text-to-code systems.

document: Large-scale neural language models have intensified concerns about inference latency, memory usage, and energy budgets in production settings. This paper examines resource-aware training and deployment regimes for high-performing yet compact NLP systems. Early transformer-based models established strong baselines across language understanding benchmarks including GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), and SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), with architectures such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019) driving progress, alongside large-scale generative systems like T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), notably GPT-3. Despite their strength, the computational demands of these systems exceed budgets in many real-time applications. Model compression techniques include pruning, quantization, low-rank factorization, and knowledge distillation as prominent strategies. Distillation transfers behavior from a high-capacity teacher to a smaller student through soft targets or auxiliary feature objectives. Students trained in this manner can approximate the teacher while allowing faster, cheaper inference on commodity hardware. Open questions persist regarding stability, calibration, and generalization under domain shift when training compressed students. Knowledge distillation has been explored through response-based transfer, feature imitation, and relation preservation across diverse NLP tasks, with methods such as DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019), TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2020), MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020), and MiniLM (Wang et al., 2020) reducing parameter counts while retaining accuracy on GLUE, MNLI (Williams et al., 2018), and QQP. Extensions incorporate task-adaptive teachers (Turc et al., 2019), intermediate-layer matching (Romero et al., 2015), patient knowledge transfer (Sun et al., 2019), data augmentation via back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), and self-training with pseudo-labels (He et al., 2020). Additional strategies align attention distributions (Zagoruyko and Komodakis, 2017; Sun et al., 2019), logits under temperature scaling (Hinton et al., 2015), and sample-level relations using contrastive objectives (Tian et al., 2020; Khosla et al., 2020). Sequence-to-sequence distillation for generative models employs sequence-level training (Kim and Rush, 2016), teacher re-ranking for abstractive summarization (Chan et al., 2021), and offline data filtering with CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015) and XSum (Narayan et al., 2018) to stabilize decoding. Cross-lingual and multilingual distillation leverage parallel corpora (Conneau and Lample, 2019), multilingual teachers and students on mBERT and XLM-R (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020), and supervision from resources such as TED Talks (Cettolo et al., 2012), Europarl (Koehn, 2005), CCAligned (El-Kishky et al., 2020), and Wikipedia dumps. Beyond accuracy, practitioners monitor robustness to perturbations, calibration reliability, fairness metrics, and memory footprints. For on-device inference, streaming scenarios impose tight end-to-end latency budgets that interact with batching and quantization artifacts. Data curricula and sample selection may further reduce the cost of student training by prioritizing informative or diverse examples. Teacher choice influences outcomes, including ensembles, intermediate checkpoints, and multi-task supervision for richer guidance. These considerations motivate a systematic comparison of capacity, training cost, and downstream trade-offs in constrained regimes. We therefore design experiments that isolate compute, data availability, and domain shift to provide actionable guidance.

document: Neural information retrieval has shifted from sparse lexical matching to learned dense and hybrid representations. Applications in web search, question answering, and enterprise retrieval require both effectiveness and strict latency bounds. Indexing, re-ranking, and caching strategies interact with model size and training objectives in complex ways. Early transformer-based models established strong baselines across language understanding benchmarks including GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), and SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), with architectures such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019) driving progress, alongside large-scale generative systems like T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), notably GPT-3. In retrieval pipelines, bi-encoders enable approximate nearest neighbor search, whereas cross-encoders provide precise re-ranking. Batching and vector quantization reduce serving costs but can degrade ranking consistency across tail queries. Knowledge distillation offers an appealing path to deploy accurate yet efficient rankers without full cross-encoder complexity. Previous work studies distillation from cross-encoders to bi-encoders using contrastive, margin-based, or listwise objectives. However, retrieval-oriented distillation must reconcile binary labels, graded relevance, and long-document contexts. The choice of teacher data, negatives, and document truncation can alter the learned similarity landscape. Knowledge distillation has been explored through response-based transfer, feature imitation, and relation preservation across diverse NLP tasks, with methods such as DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019), TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2020), MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020), and MiniLM (Wang et al., 2020) reducing parameter counts while retaining accuracy on GLUE, MNLI (Williams et al., 2018), and QQP. Extensions incorporate task-adaptive teachers (Turc et al., 2019), intermediate-layer matching (Romero et al., 2015), patient knowledge transfer (Sun et al., 2019), data augmentation via back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), and self-training with pseudo-labels (He et al., 2020). Additional strategies align attention distributions (Zagoruyko and Komodakis, 2017; Sun et al., 2019), logits under temperature scaling (Hinton et al., 2015), and sample-level relations using contrastive objectives (Tian et al., 2020; Khosla et al., 2020). Sequence-to-sequence distillation for generative models employs sequence-level training (Kim and Rush, 2016), teacher re-ranking for abstractive summarization (Chan et al., 2021), and offline data filtering with CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015) and XSum (Narayan et al., 2018) to stabilize decoding. Cross-lingual and multilingual distillation leverage parallel corpora (Conneau and Lample, 2019), multilingual teachers and students on mBERT and XLM-R (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020), and supervision from resources such as TED Talks (Cettolo et al., 2012), Europarl (Koehn, 2005), CCAligned (El-Kishky et al., 2020), and Wikipedia dumps. In production, memory budgets constrain the dimensionality of document embeddings and the width of ANN indexes. Realistic evaluations must account for index refresh cadence, tail latency percentiles, and robustness to distribution drift. We focus on regimes where throughput constraints dominate, assessing how student architectures trade recall for latency. Our study emphasizes reproducible settings and cost-aware metrics to inform retrieval system design.

document: Information extraction pipelines often rely on relation extraction models to populate knowledge bases from text. Industrial deployments must process massive document streams while maintaining high precision under tight budgets. Pre-trained encoders provide strong contextual features but impose substantial inference costs at scale. Early transformer-based models established strong baselines across language understanding benchmarks including GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), and SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), with architectures such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019) driving progress, alongside large-scale generative systems like T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), notably GPT-3. Distant supervision, prompt-based labeling, and selective annotation can mitigate data demands for new domains. Nevertheless, model size, training objectives, and decoding choices substantially affect throughput and latency. Compact students distilled from larger teachers are a promising alternative when hardware resources are limited. The interplay between sentence-level and document-level contexts poses additional challenges for efficient inference. Structured constraints and calibration techniques help control error propagation to downstream knowledge consolidation. Knowledge distillation has been explored through response-based transfer, feature imitation, and relation preservation across diverse NLP tasks, with methods such as DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019), TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2020), MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020), and MiniLM (Wang et al., 2020) reducing parameter counts while retaining accuracy on GLUE, MNLI (Williams et al., 2018), and QQP. Extensions incorporate task-adaptive teachers (Turc et al., 2019), intermediate-layer matching (Romero et al., 2015), patient knowledge transfer (Sun et al., 2019), data augmentation via back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), and self-training with pseudo-labels (He et al., 2020). Additional strategies align attention distributions (Zagoruyko and Komodakis, 2017; Sun et al., 2019), logits under temperature scaling (Hinton et al., 2015), and sample-level relations using contrastive objectives (Tian et al., 2020; Khosla et al., 2020). Sequence-to-sequence distillation for generative models employs sequence-level training (Kim and Rush, 2016), teacher re-ranking for abstractive summarization (Chan et al., 2021), and offline data filtering with CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015) and XSum (Narayan et al., 2018) to stabilize decoding. Cross-lingual and multilingual distillation leverage parallel corpora (Conneau and Lample, 2019), multilingual teachers and students on mBERT and XLM-R (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020), and supervision from resources such as TED Talks (Cettolo et al., 2012), Europarl (Koehn, 2005), CCAligned (El-Kishky et al., 2020), and Wikipedia dumps. We consider extraction settings that favor micro-batch inference and resource sharing across entity mentions. Our evaluation emphasizes long-tail relations, domain shift, and robustness to noise in distant supervision. We report compute-normalized metrics that reflect tokens processed per watt-hour alongside F1 and calibration. Ablations examine teacher checkpoints, augmentation recipes, and confidence thresholds for budget alignment. The resulting analysis highlights pragmatic choices for economical relation extraction at web scale.
