document: Vision-language applications such as image captioning are attractive candidates for on-device inference. Edge deployment introduces strict constraints on memory, compute, and power while requiring robust generalization. Pretraining across image-text pairs has improved quality but increased model sizes to hundreds of millions of parameters. Early transformer-based models established strong baselines across language understanding benchmarks including GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), and SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), with architectures such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019) driving progress, alongside large-scale generative systems like T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), notably GPT-3. Captioning pipelines typically combine a visual encoder with an autoregressive language decoder or a unified transformer. Beam search, nucleus sampling, and constrained decoding affect latency and output diversity under resource budgets. Compression techniques can pare back parameters while preserving cross-modal alignment and generation fluency. Recent practice explores quantization-aware training and adapter-based fine-tuning for efficient transfer. Knowledge distillation has been explored through response-based transfer, feature imitation, and relation preservation across diverse NLP tasks, with methods such as DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019), TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2020), MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020), and MiniLM (Wang et al., 2020) reducing parameter counts while retaining accuracy on GLUE, MNLI (Williams et al., 2018), and QQP. Extensions incorporate task-adaptive teachers (Turc et al., 2019), intermediate-layer matching (Romero et al., 2015), patient knowledge transfer (Sun et al., 2019), data augmentation via back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), and self-training with pseudo-labels (He et al., 2020). Additional strategies align attention distributions (Zagoruyko and Komodakis, 2017; Sun et al., 2019), logits under temperature scaling (Hinton et al., 2015), and sample-level relations using contrastive objectives (Tian et al., 2020; Khosla et al., 2020). Sequence-to-sequence distillation for generative models employs sequence-level training (Kim and Rush, 2016), teacher re-ranking for abstractive summarization (Chan et al., 2021), and offline data filtering with CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015) and XSum (Narayan et al., 2018) to stabilize decoding. Cross-lingual and multilingual distillation leverage parallel corpora (Conneau and Lample, 2019), multilingual teachers and students on mBERT and XLM-R (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020), and supervision from resources such as TED Talks (Cettolo et al., 2012), Europarl (Koehn, 2005), CCAligned (El-Kishky et al., 2020), and Wikipedia dumps. We analyze memory-bandwidth interactions that arise when mixing vision backbones and text decoders under streaming. Our benchmarks reflect offline photos, bursty mobile uploads, and accessibility scenarios with low-light imagery. Evaluation considers caption quality, factual grounding, and latency variability across heterogeneous devices. We profile budget sensitivities to attention heads, layer sharing, and key-value cache reuse in decoding. These findings frame a systematic exploration of accuracy–efficiency trade-offs in multimodal captioning. We present empirical results that map device constraints to model choices and training recipes.

document: Conversational assistants increasingly operate on edge devices, mediating speech, intent classification, and response generation. Privacy, connectivity variability, and energy limits drive the need for compact and robust models. End-to-end transformers unify acoustic, lexical, and dialog context but are expensive to serve at scale. Early transformer-based models established strong baselines across language understanding benchmarks including GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), and SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), with architectures such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019) driving progress, alongside large-scale generative systems like T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), notably GPT-3. Pipeline designs often separate ASR, NLU, and dialog management, whereas unified models optimize joint objectives. Latency budgets span turn-taking thresholds, barge-in responsiveness, and on-device wake word detection. Compression methods aim to meet these budgets without sacrificing recognition accuracy or language understanding. Distillation naturally suits modular and end-to-end settings by transferring guidance from resource-rich teachers. Knowledge distillation has been explored through response-based transfer, feature imitation, and relation preservation across diverse NLP tasks, with methods such as DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019), TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2020), MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020), and MiniLM (Wang et al., 2020) reducing parameter counts while retaining accuracy on GLUE, MNLI (Williams et al., 2018), and QQP. Extensions incorporate task-adaptive teachers (Turc et al., 2019), intermediate-layer matching (Romero et al., 2015), patient knowledge transfer (Sun et al., 2019), data augmentation via back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), and self-training with pseudo-labels (He et al., 2020). Additional strategies align attention distributions (Zagoruyko and Komodakis, 2017; Sun et al., 2019), logits under temperature scaling (Hinton et al., 2015), and sample-level relations using contrastive objectives (Tian et al., 2020; Khosla et al., 2020). Sequence-to-sequence distillation for generative models employs sequence-level training (Kim and Rush, 2016), teacher re-ranking for abstractive summarization (Chan et al., 2021), and offline data filtering with CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015) and XSum (Narayan et al., 2018) to stabilize decoding. Cross-lingual and multilingual distillation leverage parallel corpora (Conneau and Lample, 2019), multilingual teachers and students on mBERT and XLM-R (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020), and supervision from resources such as TED Talks (Cettolo et al., 2012), Europarl (Koehn, 2005), CCAligned (El-Kishky et al., 2020), and Wikipedia dumps. We investigate dialog domains with diverse slot schemas, code-switching, and noisy far-field audio. Metrics cover word error rate, intent accuracy, end-to-end task success, and energy per utterance. We examine schedulers that adapt decoder steps to user patience and bandwidth conditions. Our analysis considers teacher choice, alignment losses, and semi-supervised data for constrained training. The study situates edge deployment practices within realistic product latencies and memory ceilings. Results are summarized to provide guidance on building efficient assistants under privacy and compute constraints.

document: Neural information retrieval has shifted from sparse lexical matching to learned dense and hybrid representations. Applications in web search, question answering, and enterprise retrieval require both effectiveness and strict latency bounds. Indexing, re-ranking, and caching strategies interact with model size and training objectives in complex ways. Early transformer-based models established strong baselines across language understanding benchmarks including GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), and SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), with architectures such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019) driving progress, alongside large-scale generative systems like T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), notably GPT-3. In retrieval pipelines, bi-encoders enable approximate nearest neighbor search, whereas cross-encoders provide precise re-ranking. Batching and vector quantization reduce serving costs but can degrade ranking consistency across tail queries. Knowledge distillation offers an appealing path to deploy accurate yet efficient rankers without full cross-encoder complexity. Previous work studies distillation from cross-encoders to bi-encoders using contrastive, margin-based, or listwise objectives. However, retrieval-oriented distillation must reconcile binary labels, graded relevance, and long-document contexts. The choice of teacher data, negatives, and document truncation can alter the learned similarity landscape. Knowledge distillation has been explored through response-based transfer, feature imitation, and relation preservation across diverse NLP tasks, with methods such as DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019), TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2020), MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020), and MiniLM (Wang et al., 2020) reducing parameter counts while retaining accuracy on GLUE, MNLI (Williams et al., 2018), and QQP. Extensions incorporate task-adaptive teachers (Turc et al., 2019), intermediate-layer matching (Romero et al., 2015), patient knowledge transfer (Sun et al., 2019), data augmentation via back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), and self-training with pseudo-labels (He et al., 2020). Additional strategies align attention distributions (Zagoruyko and Komodakis, 2017; Sun et al., 2019), logits under temperature scaling (Hinton et al., 2015), and sample-level relations using contrastive objectives (Tian et al., 2020; Khosla et al., 2020). Sequence-to-sequence distillation for generative models employs sequence-level training (Kim and Rush, 2016), teacher re-ranking for abstractive summarization (Chan et al., 2021), and offline data filtering with CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015) and XSum (Narayan et al., 2018) to stabilize decoding. Cross-lingual and multilingual distillation leverage parallel corpora (Conneau and Lample, 2019), multilingual teachers and students on mBERT and XLM-R (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020), and supervision from resources such as TED Talks (Cettolo et al., 2012), Europarl (Koehn, 2005), CCAligned (El-Kishky et al., 2020), and Wikipedia dumps. In production, memory budgets constrain the dimensionality of document embeddings and the width of ANN indexes. Realistic evaluations must account for index refresh cadence, tail latency percentiles, and robustness to distribution drift. We focus on regimes where throughput constraints dominate, assessing how student architectures trade recall for latency. Our study emphasizes reproducible settings and cost-aware metrics to inform retrieval system design.

document: Magnetic resonance image reconstruction increasingly relies on learned priors that balance artifact suppression with structural fidelity. Despite rapid progress, the field lacks clarity on how pretraining regimes transfer to clinical undersampling patterns. Prior work has explored both k-space models and image-domain refinement, yet the interaction between supervision levels remains uncertain. To contextualize our approach, we outline representative directions in modeling and evaluation. We focus on architectures, objectives, and datasets that define the current landscape for accelerated MRI. Within this landscape, supervised and self-supervised methods coexist with varied training signals. Recent advances in medical image reconstruction and analysis leverage supervised architectures and contrastive pretraining across diverse datasets. U-Net remains a strong baseline for segmentation and reconstruction post-processing (Ronneberger et al., 2015), while residual encoders and attention variants report gains on structural fidelity (He et al., 2016; Oktay et al., 2018; Woo et al., 2018). Unsupervised and self-supervised objectives have been explored with variational autoencoders and generative adversarial networks (Kingma and Welling, 2014; Goodfellow et al., 2014), and with instance discrimination methods such as MoCo, SimCLR, and BYOL (He et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020; Grill et al., 2020). Benchmarks include fastMRI for MRI undersampling (Zbontar et al., 2018), BraTS (Menze et al., 2015; Bakas et al., 2018), and the IXI and ACDC datasets collections (IXI, 2006; Bernard et al., 2018). These resources and methods motivate examining how representation quality interacts with sampling masks and coil configurations. We target the scenario where limited annotations coexist with abundant raw k-space, reflecting realistic hospital constraints. Our framework decomposes the problem into encoding, reconstruction, and calibration, each aligned with data availability. The evaluation emphasizes sensitivity to rare pathologies and stability under variable acceleration rates. We also report ablation tests isolating augmentation strength from contrastive temperature and queue size. Taken together, the study clarifies the role of pretraining signals in accelerating MRI without degrading clinically critical detail.

document: Conversational recommenders aim to elicit preferences interactively while minimizing user burden. Designing such agents requires balancing exploration, personalization, and natural language grounding. Static evaluation alone can obscure policy failures that emerge during multi-turn interactions. Moreover, sparse rewards and delayed feedback complicate credit assignment in dialog states. This section reviews directions relevant to learning policies and exploiting structured knowledge. We distinguish between policy learning, representation building, and evaluation protocols. Conversational recommendation has drawn on reinforcement learning and knowledge-aware modeling across open-domain and task-specific settings. Early systems optimize slot-filling or dialog policies with REINFORCE and policy gradient methods (Williams, 1992; Sutton et al., 2000), later extended with actor–critic and off-policy variants such as DQN, DDPG, and SAC (Mnih et al., 2015; Lillicrap et al., 2016; Haarnoja et al., 2018). Knowledge graphs and graph neural networks provide structured signals through neighborhood propagation or attention (Kipf and Welling, 2017; Velickovic et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019). Evaluation commonly relies on ReDial conversations for recommendation dialogs (Li et al., 2018), MovieLens ratings for offline metrics (Harper and Konstan, 2015), and large-scale review corpora from Amazon and Yelp to pretrain or distill user preferences (McAuley et al., 2015; Yelp, 2019) in various domains today. These ingredients shape how systems manage cold-start users and evolving intents over long sessions. In particular, reward design and off-policy correction must account for biased logging and exposure effects. We study policy objectives that align information gain with recommendation utility over dialog turns. The approach integrates uncertainty estimates to modulate question asking versus item suggesting. Our experiments examine robustness to annotation noise and popularity skew. Beyond offline metrics, we simulate adaptive feedback to measure conversation efficiency and user satisfaction.

Knowledge graphs and graph neural networks provide structured signals through neighborhood propagation or attention (Kipf and Welling, 2017; Velickovic et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019). document: Building machine translation for low-resource languages remains a central challenge in multilingual NLP. Data scarcity, domain drift, and script variation conspire to degrade generalization. Recent progress has come from scaling pretraining and refining data selection pipelines. However, the interplay between augmentation and domain adaptation is not fully understood. We summarize representative approaches and resources before outlining our study design. Our focus spans objectives for leveraging monolingual text and protocols for evaluating transfer. Cross-lingual transfer and low-resource translation have been studied through pretraining, data augmentation, and multilingual evaluation suites. Back-translation and dual learning establish strong baselines for exploiting monolingual text (Sennrich et al., 2016; He et al., 2016), while unsupervised and pivot-based approaches reduce reliance on parallel data (Lample et al., 2018; Firat et al., 2016). Pretrained sequence-to-sequence models such as mBART and mT5 provide multilingual priors for fine-tuning (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021), and sentence encoders like LASER enable corpus filtering and mining (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019). Common benchmarks include IWSLT for speech transcripts, WMT news tasks, OPUS collections, and FLORES for many-to-many evaluation (Cettolo et al., 2014; Bojar et al., 2018; Tiedemann, 2012; Goyal et al., 2021) covering typologically diverse pairs and varying data scales worldwide over multiple domains. These threads motivate a careful examination of how tokenization, sampling, and objective choice affect domain shift. We consider practical constraints such as noisy web text, limited dictionaries, and hardware budgets. The methodology factors in target-side fluency, adequacy, and calibration for downstream tasks. We explore curriculum strategies that couple bitext scarcity with synthetic data schedules. Experiments analyze sensitivity to script normalization and vocabulary granularity. The results highlight when cross-lingual pretraining helps and when tailored adaptation is required.

Back-translation and dual learning establish strong baselines for exploiting monolingual text (Sennrich et al., 2016; He et al., 2016), while unsupervised and pivot-based approaches reduce reliance on parallel data (Lample et al., 2018; Firat et al., 2016). Pretrained sequence-to-sequence models such as mBART and mT5 provide multilingual priors for fine-tuning (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021), and sentence encoders like LASER enable corpus filtering and mining (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019). Common benchmarks include IWSLT for speech transcripts, WMT news tasks, OPUS collections, and FLORES for many-to-many evaluation (Cettolo et al., 2014; Bojar et al., 2018; Tiedemann, 2012; Goyal et al., 2021) covering typologically diverse pairs and varying data scales worldwide over multiple domains.

document: Urban mobility planning depends on accurate forecasts of traffic states across complex sensor networks. Temporal dynamics are influenced by recurrent patterns and rare disruptions, both challenging for sequence models. Graph formulations capture topology, but stationarity assumptions can fail under dynamic routing. Recent architectures inject spatial priors into recurrent or convolutional modules to handle propagation effects. We review representative modeling choices and public datasets used in the literature. Our discussion distinguishes operator design, adaptivity, and benchmark protocol. Spatiotemporal traffic forecasting has been advanced by graph-based sequence models and dynamic connectivity mechanisms. Diffusion and convolutional operators on road networks underpin DCRNN, STGCN, and Graph WaveNet (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019), while adaptive adjacency and node-specific embeddings characterize AGCRN and related variants (Bai et al., 2020; Pan et al., 2021). Attention over spatial and temporal neighborhoods further refines long-range dependencies (Veličković et al., 2018; Zheng et al., 2020). Standard benchmarks include METR-LA and PEMS-BAY for speed and occupancy prediction, along with PEMS03, PEMS04, and PEMS08 for larger sensor grids (Li et al., 2018; Song et al., 2020; Zheng et al., 2020) with consistent data splits and horizon settings enabling comparable metrics across horizons and sensors under recurring congestion and weather perturbation conditions nationwide. Together, these ingredients create a testbed where architectural differences translate into measurable trade-offs. We focus on flexible operators that accommodate changing connectivity without expensive retraining. The training objective balances pointwise accuracy with calibration across forecast horizons. We assess efficiency under constrained latency budgets consistent with real-time deployment. Stress tests evaluate sensitivity to sensor dropouts and distributional shifts. The analysis informs when static graphs suffice and when dynamic relations are necessary.

document: Automated assessment promises timely feedback in classrooms where instructor time is limited. Short answers are particularly challenging due to lexical variability and sparse supervision. Practical deployment also requires transparency and robust domain transfer. Prior work spans feature engineering, metric learning, and pretrained encoders. We outline datasets and modeling patterns commonly adopted in this area. The review emphasizes input representations and supervision signals. Automated short-answer assessment has been explored with feature-based scoring, neural similarity models, and pretrained language encoders. Early systems relied on lexical overlap and handcrafted discourse features similar to e-rater components (Attali and Burstein, 2006; Yannakoudakis et al., 2011), while Siamese architectures and attention mechanisms modeled paraphrase-level correspondences (Mueller and Thyagarajan, 2016; Rocktäschel et al., 2016). Contextual encoders such as BERT, RoBERTa, and SBERT improved semantic matching for rubric-aligned predictions (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Reimers and Gurevych, 2019). Widely used datasets include ASAP-SAS from the Hewlett Foundation, SciEntsBank and BEETLE for student response analysis, and newer domain-specific sets in biology and physics classrooms (Kaggle, 2012; Dzikovska et al., 2013; Nielsen et al., 2019) covering multiple question types, varied prompts, and scoring schemes across grade levels and subjects worldwide. These references motivate disentangling semantic similarity from rubric-specific evidence aggregation. We investigate calibration strategies that align score distributions across prompts. The training setup balances pairwise contrastive learning with prompt-conditioned regression. We analyze bias with respect to demographic terms and content length. Our evaluation includes prompt transfer and unseen course generalization. We discuss implications for formative feedback and reliability in classroom settings.

Few-shot relation extraction seeks to identify semantic relations between entities when only a handful of labeled examples are available. The rise of pretrained language models has reframed the problem from metric learning to prompt-conditioned inference with constrained supervision. Despite accuracy gains, current systems remain sensitive to prompt wording, label verbalizers, and domain shifts. This section reviews the landscape and clarifies the technical assumptions that shape the evaluation regimes in this area.

Early metric-based methods adapted prototypical representations and episodic training to approximate class structure under data scarcity. Subsequent transformer encoders introduced stronger contextualization but still depended on brittle hand-crafted templates. Task design choices around entity markers, sentence cropping, and negative sampling often dominate reported improvements. Moreover, inconsistent train–test splits and cross-domain generalization protocols complicate direct comparison across papers. These observations motivate a sharper look at the roles of prompts, representations, and data curation in few-shot settings.

Early prompt-based strategies for few-shot relation extraction emphasize pattern verbalization and label mapping with masked language models, including PET (Schick and Schütze, 2021), LM-BFF (Gao et al., 2021), and ADAPET (Tam et al., 2021). Subsequent approaches introduce continuous prompts and lightweight adapters for task conditioning, such as P-tuning (Liu et al., 2021), Prefix-Tuning (Li and Liang, 2021), and Prompt Tuning for T5 (Lester et al., 2021). Data resources commonly used to benchmark these methods include FewRel 1.0 and 2.0 (Han et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2019), TACRED (Zhang et al., 2017), and SemEval-2010 Task 8 (Hendrickx et al., 2010). Recent instruction-driven methods leverage larger language models via in-context learning and templated rationales, drawing on GPT-3 style setups (Brown et al., 2020), T0 (Sanh et al., 2021), Flan-T5 (Chung et al., 2022), and LAMA-style probing suites (Petroni et al., 2019) across standard evaluation protocols.

Together, these components reveal a field where modeling choices are intertwined with annotation granularity and label semantics. Prompt engineering implicitly encodes task definitions, blurring the line between representation learning and dataset specification. Robust evaluation should disentangle improvements due to scaling from those attributable to calibration or better verbalizers. Furthermore, label space entropy and relation argument structure can confound in-context learners if weak priors dominate. We therefore examine how template design, demonstration selection, and entity marking interact under controlled ablations.

Our analysis emphasizes reproducible splits, standardized verbalizers, and calibrated decoding under constrained resources. We revisit cross-domain transfer from newswire to Wikipedia, enforcing constant budgets for demonstrations and gradient steps. The study isolates prompt sensitivity from pretraining advantages by aligning tokenization and marker conventions across baselines. Finally, we outline a measurement model that attributes variance in accuracy to specific prompt and data factors.

Predicting molecular properties from structure is central to drug discovery and materials design. Learning directly on graphs mitigates feature engineering and offers end-to-end adaptability to new endpoints. Yet performance often hinges on the alignment between molecular geometry, featurization choices, and the inductive biases of the encoder. We review techniques and evaluation protocols before presenting our study.

Early work emphasized two-dimensional graphs with handcrafted atom and bond attributes derived from cheminformatics toolkits. The emergence of message passing neural networks unified many variants under a shared computational template. Recent models incorporate distance-aware filters and angular terms to better capture local electronic effects. Data leakage due to scaffold overlap and salt-removal rules complicates fair assessment across public benchmarks. These considerations motivate principled data splits and careful normalization of input chemistry.

Foundational graph encoders for molecular property prediction include GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), and GIN (Xu et al., 2019), with benchmarks curated in MoleculeNet (Wu et al., 2018). Architectures exploiting geometric information extend this line with SchNet (Schütt et al., 2017), DimeNet (Klicpera et al., 2020), and SE(3)-Transformer (Fuchs et al., 2020). Widely used datasets span QM9 (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014), ZINC and ZINC-12k (Irwin et al., 2012), PCBA and Tox21 (Wu et al., 2018), and the OGB molecule suite including ogbg-molhiv and ogbg-molpcba (Hu et al., 2020). Pretraining and feature engineering practices commonly rely on masked attribute modeling, context prediction, and RDKit-derived descriptors, drawing on GraphCL (You et al., 2020), InfoGraph (Sun et al., 2020), GROVER (Rong et al., 2020), and attr-masking setups in OGB leaderboards under standard splits and evaluation metrics across benchmarks, commonly reported in literature.

Despite the breadth of models, reported gains can reflect differences in stereochemistry handling rather than representational capacity. Three-dimensional methods are particularly sensitive to conformer generation quality and partial charge assignment. Transfer learning across datasets benefits from consistent valence models and standardized canonicalization. We emphasize evaluation under scaffold and temporal splits to quantify generalization to novel chemotypes. Our analysis disentangles architectural effects from data preprocessing through controlled ablations.

The study presents a reference pipeline with reproducible featurization, geometry settings, and hyperparameter budgets. We benchmark multiple encoders under identical splits and revisit conclusions about the value of 3D cues. The results reveal how target noise and descriptor redundancy interact with model capacity in realistic settings. Finally, we discuss implications for active learning and prospective validation in medicinal chemistry campaigns.
spans: Foundational graph encoders for molecular property prediction include GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), and GIN (Xu et al., 2019), with benchmarks curated in MoleculeNet (Wu et al., 2018). Architectures exploiting geometric information extend this line with SchNet (Schütt et al., 2017), DimeNet (Klicpera et al., 2020), and SE(3)-Transformer (Fuchs et al., 2020). Widely used datasets span QM9 (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014), ZINC and ZINC-12k (Irwin et al., 2012), PCBA and Tox21 (Wu et al., 2018), and the OGB molecule suite including ogbg-molhiv and ogbg-molpcba (Hu et al., 2020). Pretraining and feature engineering practices commonly rely on masked attribute modeling, context prediction, and RDKit-derived descriptors, drawing on GraphCL (You et al., 2020), InfoGraph (Sun et al., 2020), GROVER (Rong et al., 2020), and attr-masking setups in OGB leaderboards under standard splits and evaluation metrics across benchmarks, commonly reported in literature.

Detecting anomalies in multivariate telemetry is essential for safe and reliable operation of cyber-physical systems. Industrial sensors exhibit nonstationarity, missing data, and asynchronous sampling that complicate modeling. Supervised labels are scarce and often delayed, motivating semi-supervised and unsupervised formulations. We survey representative approaches and identify evaluation pitfalls that mask true generalization.

Classical baselines rely on robust statistics, autoregressive filters, and isolation-based schemes that provide interpretable alerts. Neural methods leverage representation learning to capture cross-channel dependencies without manual crafting. However, heavy smoothing and windowing decisions can suppress short-lived but critical transients. Threshold calibration is further complicated by nonstationary operating modes and maintenance interventions. These characteristics motivate principled scoring and uncertainty quantification for deployment.

Prominent anomaly detection models for multivariate time series include LSTM-VAE (Park et al., 2018), DAGMM (Zong et al., 2018), OmniAnomaly (Su et al., 2019), MSCRED (Zhang et al., 2019), USAD (Audibert et al., 2020), GDN (Deng and Hooi, 2021), THOC (Shen et al., 2020), and Anomaly Transformer (Xu et al., 2021). Standard benchmarks encompass SMD and MSL/SMAP (Su et al., 2019; Hundman et al., 2018), SWaT and WADI (Mathur and Tippenhauer, 2016; Ahmed et al., 2017), Yahoo A1 (Laptev et al., 2015), TSB-UB (Montero-Manso et al., 2020), and the Numenta Anomaly Benchmark (Lavin and Ahmad, 2015). Evaluation practices frequently vary in windowing rules, point-level versus range-level scoring, and delay penalties, with implementations often derived from the Merlion toolkit (Bhatnagar et al., 2021), PyOD (Zhao et al., 2019), and Darts (Herzen et al., 2022). Reporting conventions also differ in anomaly window inflation and tolerance parameters.

Despite extensive benchmarking, reproducibility remains fragile due to hidden preprocessing and inconsistent resampling. Alarm economics also matter because false positives drive operator fatigue and costly rollbacks. We therefore frame anomaly detection as risk-sensitive estimation with explicit costs for delay and escalation. The analysis emphasizes calibration curves and post-hoc thresholding trained on frozen model scores. We examine shift-robustness under seasonal drift, missing blocks, and actuator saturations in controlled ablations.

Our empirical study standardizes window definitions, overlap rules, and look-ahead leakage prevention. We report range-aware metrics alongside point-wise precision and recall to balance localization and detection. The results clarify how dependence structure, window size, and training objectives trade off across regimes. Finally, we offer practical guidelines for operations teams adopting learning-based detectors in production.

Learning dexterous manipulation remains challenging due to contact-rich dynamics and partial observability. Reinforcement learning offers a unified objective but suffers from sample inefficiency and instability on real hardware. Imitation and offline learning mitigate risky exploration by exploiting demonstrations and logged experience. We consolidate relevant algorithms and datasets before detailing our methodology.

Policy learning with expressive function approximators requires careful regularization to avoid exploitation of simulator artifacts. Domain randomization and system identification reduce the gap between simulation and real robots but are rarely standardized. Visual observations introduce representation issues that entangle perception with control. Evaluation protocols also vary in success thresholds, resets, and action rate constraints across platforms. These factors complicate apples-to-apples comparisons of learning signals and convergence behavior.

Standard continuous-control baselines include DDPG (Lillicrap et al., 2016), PPO (Schulman et al., 2017), TD3 (Fujimoto et al., 2018), and SAC (Haarnoja et al., 2018), alongside behavior cloning and DAGGER for imitation (Ross et al., 2011). Offline and imitation-flavored algorithms widely used for manipulation comprise CQL (Kumar et al., 2020), AWAC (Nair et al., 2020), IQL (Kostrikov et al., 2021), BRAC (Wu et al., 2019), and DAPG for demonstrations (Rajeswaran et al., 2017). Benchmark suites frequently referenced include Meta-World (Yu et al., 2020), D4RL Locomotion and Adroit (Fu et al., 2020), Robomimic (Mandlekar et al., 2021), RLBench (James et al., 2020), and OpenAI Gym Robotics (Brockman et al., 2016). Pretraining and representation resources appear in R3M (Nair et al., 2022), VC-1 (Majumdar et al., 2023), and MVP (Xiao et al., 2022), with Franka Kitchen (Gupta et al., 2019) and ManiSkill (Mu et al., 2021).

Despite algorithmic diversity, improvements often stem from better initialization and data quality rather than new objectives. We highlight the importance of demonstrations that match hardware idiosyncrasies and reward shaping choices. A consistent benchmarking harness with fixed seeds and identical termination rules narrows variance significantly. Our study measures performance under limited resets and actuator delays that mimic deployment conditions. We also analyze safety filters and recovery behaviors that bound exploration risk.

The experimental design emphasizes reproducibility with shared assets and open logs for audit. We ablate representation, objective, and data coverage to attribute gains to specific components. Results clarify data efficiencies and robustness trends across visual and state-based policies. We conclude with recommendations for hardware-aware evaluation and reliable sim-to-real transfer.

Pretraining and representation resources appear in R3M (Nair et al., 2022), VC-1 (Majumdar et al., 2023), and MVP (Xiao et al., 2022), with Franka Kitchen (Gupta et al., 2019) and ManiSkill (Mu et al., 2021).

Data augmentation is a critical component for robust medical image segmentation under limited annotation budgets. Cross-institutional variability in scanners and protocols challenges models trained on single-site data. Augmentations can simulate plausible acquisition shifts but also risk violating anatomical realism. We review augmentation and pretraining practices in light of evaluation design.

Simple geometric transforms improve data coverage yet may distort organ topologies at high magnitudes. Intensity perturbations must respect modality-specific physics, particularly in MR sequences and CT Hounsfield units. Incorporating spatial priors through shape models can mitigate implausible deformations during training. Consistent preprocessing pipelines are necessary to isolate augmentation effects from resampling artifacts. These observations motivate principled ablations that separate regularization from genuine generalization.

Canonical segmentation backbones in medical imaging include U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015), U-Net++ (Zhou et al., 2018), and nnU-Net (Isensee et al., 2021), often paired with Dice and cross-entropy losses. Augmentation strategies widely reported comprise MixUp (Zhang et al., 2018), CutMix (Yun et al., 2019), RandAugment (Cubuk et al., 2020), and intensity- or geometry-based transforms offered by MONAI and TorchIO (Cardoso et al., 2022; Pérez-García et al., 2021). Self-supervised pretraining alternatives adopt contrastive and clustering objectives such as MoCo (He et al., 2020), SimCLR (Chen et al., 2020), BYOL (Grill et al., 2020), and SwAV (Caron et al., 2020). Representative benchmarks include BraTS (Menze et al., 2015; Bakas et al., 2018), KiTS (Heller et al., 2019), MSD tasks (Simpson et al., 2019), CheXpert and ChestX-ray14 (Irvin et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017), and ISIC (Codella et al., 2019) across modalities and clinical contexts.

Despite broad adoption, augmentation policies are often tuned on validation sets that leak task structure. Heterogeneous metrics and label conventions further obscure incremental gains across public leaderboards. We recommend reporting calibration curves and uncertainty maps to assess clinical safety. Our experiments vary augmentation strength while holding sampling and optimizer settings fixed. The analysis disentangles benefits from distribution coverage versus improved invariance.

We also examine transfer from self-supervised encoders to small labeled cohorts under strict splits. The study highlights trade-offs between photometric robustness and boundary accuracy for different organs. Results support practical settings that avoid extreme transforms and prioritize anatomy-aware constraints. Finally, we synthesize guidelines for reproducible, clinically grounded augmentation protocols.

document: Predicting quantum and pharmacological properties of small molecules is a central task in cheminformatics and drug discovery. Early supervised pipelines relied on expert descriptors and kernel methods, but scalability and transfer remained limited. Graph neural networks now dominate because they can exploit molecular topology and 3D cues with fewer manual features. However, reproducibility across datasets and the comparability of reported numbers are still open concerns in the community. Differences in featurization, splitting, and target curation can overshadow algorithmic advances and complicate evaluation. Consequently, a careful review of benchmarks, architectures, and training protocols is needed to contextualize existing results. Research on molecular property prediction has reported results on QM9, ZINC, MoleculeNet, PCBA, and curated ChEMBL subsets using canonical scaffold splits and standardized atom and bond features. Architectures commonly include graph convolutional networks (Kipf and Welling, 2017), message passing neural networks (Gilmer et al., 2017), graph attention networks (Velickovic et al., 2018), SchNet (Schutt et al., 2018), DimeNet and DimeNet++ (Klicpera et al., 2020), and equivariant transformers (Fuchs et al., 2020). Training and evaluation practices span RDKit featurization baselines, self-supervised pretraining on ZINC15 or PubChem, multi-task objectives on MoleculeNet panels such as BBBP, Tox21, and HIV, and leaderboard protocols adopted by Open Graph Benchmark PCQM4M and GEOM datasets. Benchmarking suites include ESOL, FreeSolv, Lipophilicity, QM7, and QM9-Torsion, while data augmentation strategies cover SMILES enumeration and conformer ensembles from ETKDG, CREST, or ANI, alongside hyperparameter sweeps with early stopping and decoy-aware splitting via DeepChem utilities. Despite the breadth of options, experimental setups rarely control for conformer generation, label imbalance, and leakage simultaneously. As a result, performance trends can invert across tasks when evaluation practice is harmonized under common preprocessing. Recent calls for standardized baselines underscore the importance of transparent hyperparameters and fixed public scripts. Addressing these concerns requires disentangling modeling capacity from data handling choices through controlled ablations. We frame our study within this agenda by aligning data pipelines and reporting statistically principled comparisons across families of GNNs.

document: Emotion recognition across speech, vision, and text has accelerated with advances in deep learning and multimodal fusion. Applications span mental health monitoring, human-computer interaction, and social robotics where robustness is essential. Yet inconsistencies in annotation schemes, label granularity, and protocol design hinder comparability. The literature reflects broad choices in features, temporal alignment, and uncertainty handling. To structure this landscape, we first summarize prominent datasets, method families, and evaluation standards. Benchmark datasets for multimodal emotion recognition widely include IEMOCAP, CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, MELD, and RAVDESS, covering acted and in-the-wild speech, video, and text modalities. Methodological families span early fusion of handcrafted prosody and facial action units, late fusion of unimodal classifiers, and aligned intermediate fusion using attention over time-synchronized streams. Recent architectures report gains with transformers and pre-trained encoders, such as MulT, MISA, MMIM, and MAG-BERT, alongside audio CNN front-ends on log-mel spectrograms, 3D CNNs for visual features, and contextualized language embeddings from BERT or RoBERTa. Evaluation is typically conducted with speaker-independent splits, cross-session protocols, and metrics like accuracy, F1, CCC, and MAE, with standard preprocessing including voice activity detection, face tracking via OpenFace, and forced alignment from Montreal Forced Aligner. Reported baselines often include SVMs on eGeMAPS, LSTMs over word sequences, HMMs for temporal smoothing, and decision-level stacking with calibration on development folds only. Despite common benchmarks, cross-corpus generalization and demographic coverage remain underexplored. The coupling of annotation noise and temporal segmentation further complicates fair assessment. Adopting principled splits and calibration-aware metrics can clarify model behavior under shift. Recent studies have highlighted the need for open protocols and robust statistical testing. We therefore focus on settings that decouple modality contributions while controlling confounds in training and evaluation.

Recent architectures report gains with transformers and pre-trained encoders, such as MulT, MISA, MMIM, and MAG-BERT, alongside audio CNN front-ends on log-mel spectrograms, 3D CNNs for visual features, and contextualized language embeddings from BERT or RoBERTa. Evaluation is typically conducted with speaker-independent splits, cross-session protocols, and metrics like accuracy, F1, CCC, and MAE, with standard preprocessing including voice activity detection, face tracking via OpenFace, and forced alignment from Montreal Forced Aligner. Reported baselines often include SVMs on eGeMAPS, LSTMs over word sequences, HMMs for temporal smoothing, and decision-level stacking with calibration on development folds only.

document: Collaborative model training across institutions is constrained by privacy regulations and data governance. Federated learning offers a way to leverage distributed datasets without centralizing protected health information. Medical imaging workloads add unique constraints due to large volumes, skewed labels, and scanner variability. Recent studies report strong results but also highlight sensitivity to client sampling and communication budgets. To situate these findings, we summarize representative datasets, algorithms, and evaluation practices in federated imaging. Federated learning for medical imaging has been studied across datasets such as BRATS, ISIC, CheXpert, ChestX-ray14, MIMIC-CXR, and HAM10000, using hospital-wise non-IID partitions and privacy-preserving protocols. Optimization algorithms frequently include FedAvg, FedProx, SCAFFOLD, FedNova, and MOON, with clients training CNNs or U-Nets under limited communication rounds and periodic model averaging. Security and privacy components typically adopt secure aggregation, differential privacy with Gaussian mechanisms, homomorphic encryption for gradient updates, and audit logging through blockchain-inspired append-only ledgers. Evaluation reports highlight Dice and Hausdorff for segmentation, AUROC and AUPRC for classification, communication cost per round, and convergence under stragglers, often implemented in Flower, TensorFlow Federated, PySyft, or FedML with simulated or real hospital nodes. Common baselines compare centralized training, local-only models, and hybrid personalization schemes like FedPer, FedBN, and pFedMe, alongside domain shifts induced by device heterogeneity, scanner vendors, and site-specific preprocessing pipelines and label noise patterns. Despite extensive experimentation, the impact of site-level covariates and annotation sparsity is not fully characterized. Moreover, existing protocols rarely quantify privacy-utility trade-offs in a harmonized manner. Reproducibility is further hampered by heterogeneous codebases and opaque preprocessing recipes. Establishing transparent baselines and stratified partitions can clarify the limits of current algorithms. We therefore present a study that standardizes clients, privacy budgets, and compute settings for fair comparison.

Security and privacy components typically adopt secure aggregation, differential privacy with Gaussian mechanisms, homomorphic encryption for gradient updates, and audit logging through blockchain-inspired append-only ledgers. Evaluation reports highlight Dice and Hausdorff for segmentation, AUROC and AUPRC for classification, communication cost per round, and convergence under stragglers, often implemented in Flower, TensorFlow Federated, PySyft, or FedML with simulated or real hospital nodes. Common baselines compare centralized training, local-only models, and hybrid personalization schemes like FedPer, FedBN, and pFedMe, alongside domain shifts induced by device heterogeneity, scanner vendors, and site-specific preprocessing pipelines and label noise patterns.

document: Urban congestion imposes economic and environmental costs that motivate adaptive traffic signal control. Reinforcement learning offers a data-driven framework for optimizing policies in complex, stochastic networks. However, reproducibility and transferability across cities remain challenging due to heterogeneous sensing and control constraints. The literature includes both classical control and RL, creating a broad landscape of baselines and configurations. Comparisons are further complicated by differences in simulators, network topologies, and demand models. We review experimental settings and algorithms commonly considered in recent studies. Traffic signal control has been benchmarked in simulators such as SUMO, CityFlow, Aimsun, and MATSim using grid, arterial, and real-network scenarios with fixed and variable demand patterns. Representative methods include rule-based Webster and Max-Pressure baselines, value-based DQN variants, policy-gradient agents like A2C and PPO, and specialized approaches such as PressLight, CoLight, FRAP, and MPLight with graph or attention modules. Common observation spaces incorporate lane occupancy, queue length, phase duration, and waiting time, while action spaces toggle phase selection, phase extension, or split ratios under signal safety constraints. Evaluation protocols typically report average travel time, throughput, queue length, and delay, drawing data from TSC benchmarks, Hangzhou and Jinan datasets, Peachtree corridor logs, and CityFlow-Benchmark configurations with public seeds. Baseline implementations often rely on Replay Buffer tuning, target-network updates, reward normalization, fixed yellow times, and demand scaling, and comparisons include actuated signals and coordinated fixed-time plans. Despite extensive benchmarks, reported improvements often diminish when phase safety and latency constraints are fully enforced. Cross-network generalization and sample efficiency also vary widely across implementations. Standardizing observation semantics and reward definitions can reduce confounds in reported gains. We adopt protocols that emphasize statistical power, ablation clarity, and deployment realism.

document: Code generation with large language models has advanced rapidly due to scale and dataset availability. Developers increasingly rely on assistants for scaffolding, completion, and repair across languages and frameworks. Despite progress, evaluation remains fraught with ambiguities in problem specification and testing rigor. Prompting strategies, adaptation methods, and decoding choices can dominate performance differences across studies. To characterize the landscape, we summarize common datasets, systems, and practices in recent literature. Large language models for code are evaluated on HumanEval, MBPP, CodeXGLUE, APPS, and CodeSearchNet, spanning function synthesis, completion, and multi-turn problem solving with unit tests. Systems include Codex, AlphaCode, InCoder, CodeGen, StarCoder, PolyCoder, and CodeT5 or PLBART variants, employing decoder-only or encoder-decoder architectures with byte-level tokenization and repository-scale pretraining. Prompting and adaptation techniques cover in-context learning with exemplars, instruction tuning on curated program datasets, prompt tuning and prefix-tuning, LoRA adapters, and retrieval-augmented generation with stack traces and API documentation. Decoding and evaluation practices report nucleus sampling, temperature scaling, beam search, constrained decoding with grammars, pass@k estimates, and test-time filtering using static analysis, lints, or sandboxed execution. Training corpora commonly aggregate permissively licensed GitHub repositories, competitive programming archives, Jupyter notebooks, and issue discussions, while pretraining objectives include masked span denoising, causal language modeling, and bimodal contrastive alignment for code and natural language and documentation links. Nonetheless, differences in license filtering, duplication handling, and evaluation harnesses substantially affect reported pass rates. Moreover, measuring security and maintainability of generated code is still an open challenge. Community efforts toward transparent leaderboards and reproducible pipelines are beginning to emerge. Better diagnostics of failure modes can inform safer deployment in integrated development environments. We outline evaluation settings that target robustness to prompt variation and test instability.

document: Recent advances in long-form summarization aim to condense scientific articles while preserving structure and factual grounding. The problem spans heterogeneous sections, technical nomenclature, and cross-referenced contributions that challenge standard sequence models. Prior work has studied data scaling, auxiliary objectives, and content selection under varying domain constraints. Evaluating output faithfulness remains difficult due to limited human references and evolving scholarly conventions. We outline the context of modeling choices, data sources, and evaluation regimes for research-article summarization. Early and contemporary approaches to summarization include TextRank (Mihalcea and Tarau, 2004), LexRank (Erkan and Radev, 2004), neural pointer-generator models (See et al., 2017), encoder-decoder Transformers with copy or coverage mechanisms (Gehrmann et al., 2018), BERTSUM for extractive and abstractive variants (Liu and Lapata, 2019), PEGASUS with gap-sentence objectives (Zhang et al., 2020), T5 unified text-to-text models (Raffel et al., 2020), and large-scale benchmarks such as CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015), Newsroom (Grusky et al., 2018), and XSum (Narayan et al., 2018), alongside domain datasets like arXiv and PubMed (Cohan et al., 2018), among others. Despite prolific progress, scientific discourse introduces tables, equations, and citations that require discourse-aware planning. Datasets differ in abstract length, section granularity, and citation density, complicating cross-benchmark comparison. Domain shifts across venues and subfields influence terminology and rhetorical moves in the target summaries. Models must handle long contexts, heterogeneous inputs, and selective compression with constrained decoding. Techniques for content planning range from extractive scaffolds to hierarchical encoders over sections and figures. Citation-aware modules attempt to track salient references and filter peripheral background statements. Discourse parsers can signal claims, evidence, and limitations to guide content prioritization. Retrieval-augmented generators incorporate external indexing to recover details not present in token-limited contexts. Constrained decoding methods attempt to enforce terminology, units, and section cues during generation. Pretraining on scholarly corpora promises better lexical coverage and technical style control. Curriculum schedules and auxiliary losses target factuality, salience, and redundancy through multi-task design. However, standardized evaluation across domains and paper structures remains open. Human-centered assessments report divergent judgments when criteria are underspecified and instructions are ambiguous. Common evaluation setups report ROUGE-N and ROUGE-L overlap (Lin, 2004), supplemented by BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002), METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005), and newer embedding-based metrics such as BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2019) and MoverScore (Zhao et al., 2019), while question-answering based measures like QAEval (Deutsch et al., 2021) and QuestEval (Scialom et al., 2021) probe content selection, with alignment-focused or entailment-oriented checks including SummaQA (Scialom et al., 2019), SummaC (Laban et al., 2022), and DAE-style factuality classifiers (Goyal and Durrett, 2021). Benchmarking resources for faithfulness and robustness include FRANK (Pagnoni et al., 2021), QAGS for validation (Wang et al., 2020), TRUE, a meta-evaluation suite (Honovich et al., 2022), FactCC and FactCCX for classification and explanation (Kryscinski et al., 2020), and domain-specific sets such as SciFact (Wadden et al., 2020), PubMedQA (Jin et al., 2019), and Evidence Inference (Lehman et al., 2019), alongside perturbation protocols like adversarial attacks on entities and numbers (Goodrich et al., 2019) and counterfactual edits that test calibration and consistency across variants. Current efforts also explore semi-structured inputs that combine text with citation graphs and tables. Such formats invite joint reasoning over nodes, edges, and captions beyond linear token streams. In-domain adaptation can be cost-effective with parameter-efficient tuning on small expert-curated corpora. Nonetheless, reproducibility varies with preprocessing pipelines that modify paragraph and equation boundaries. Clearer reporting of tokenization, truncation, and template prompts would aid fair comparisons. Our study examines long-context modeling and grounded evaluation under consistent preprocessing. We detail datasets, metrics, and protocols to isolate effects of retrieval, planning, and faithfulness constraints. Experimental design emphasizes robust ablations and power analyses over multiple seeds and institutions. We discuss implications for transparent benchmark construction and sustained scientific knowledge access.

Common evaluation setups report ROUGE-N and ROUGE-L overlap (Lin, 2004), supplemented by BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002), METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005), and newer embedding-based metrics such as BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2019) and MoverScore (Zhao et al., 2019), while question-answering based measures like QAEval (Deutsch et al., 2021) and QuestEval (Scialom et al., 2021) probe content selection, with alignment-focused or entailment-oriented checks including SummaQA (Scialom et al., 2019), SummaC (Laban et al., 2022), and DAE-style factuality classifiers (Goyal and Durrett, 2021). Benchmarking resources for faithfulness and robustness include FRANK (Pagnoni et al., 2021), QAGS for validation (Wang et al., 2020), TRUE, a meta-evaluation suite (Honovich et al., 2022), FactCC and FactCCX for classification and explanation (Kryscinski et al., 2020), and domain-specific sets such as SciFact (Wadden et al., 2020), PubMedQA (Jin et al., 2019), and Evidence Inference (Lehman et al., 2019), alongside perturbation protocols like adversarial attacks on entities and numbers (Goodrich et al., 2019) and counterfactual edits that test calibration and consistency across variants.

document: Molecular property prediction on graphs requires models that capture both local chemistry and long-range dependencies. Atoms and bonds must be represented with features reflecting valence, aromaticity, and electronic effects. Stereochemistry and conformational variability complicate the mapping from structure to activity or property. Generalization across scaffolds and assays is a central difficulty for machine learning in drug discovery. We situate modeling decisions within constraints posed by data size, noise, and benchmarking practice. Representative architectures for molecular graphs include Graph Convolutional Networks (Kipf and Welling, 2017), Graph Attention Networks (Velickovic et al., 2018), Message Passing Neural Networks (Gilmer et al., 2017), GraphSAGE for learning (Hamilton et al., 2017), Graph Isomorphism Networks (Xu et al., 2019), D-MPNN variants (Yang et al., 2019), and models such as SchNet (Schütt et al., 2018), with evaluations on MoleculeNet (Wu et al., 2018) benchmarks including QM9 (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014), ESOL and FreeSolv (Delaney, 2004; Mobley and Guthrie, 2014), PCBA and HIV (Ramsundar et al., 2013), and ZINC subsets (Sterling and Irwin, 2015). Aggregation choices determine how substructures influence molecular-level predictions. Bond-directed message passing affects chirality handling and functional group interactions. Readout mechanisms can bias toward heavy atoms or pharmacophoric fragments if not regularized. Uncertainty estimates are essential for safe decision making under limited or noisy labels. Calibration supports triage strategies and resource allocation in screening campaigns. Data augmentation mimics plausible chemistries via SMILES enumeration or 3D conformer sampling. Multi-task learning can exploit shared signals in overlapping endpoints and protocols. Scaffold split performance often contrasts with random split results due to leakage. Interpretability ranges from attention weights to substructure attribution and counterfactual edits. Practical deployment faces challenges in assay drift, lot effects, and proxy-label mismatch. Curating negatives and harmonizing units remain persistent issues in public datasets. External validity depends on synthesis feasibility and absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion. Benchmarks should address batch effects, duplicate molecules, and imbalanced classes through careful design. Pretraining and regularization strategies span supervised multi-task setups and self-supervised objectives, including InfoGraph for mutual information maximization (Sun et al., 2019), GraphCL augment-and-contrast pipelines (You et al., 2020), GROVER for large-scale graph Transformer pretraining (Rong et al., 2020), GCC for cross-scale contrast (Qiu et al., 2020), and node- or edge-masked prediction variants akin to MAMP (Hu et al., 2020a), with evaluation on OGB molecular benchmarks such as OGB-MOLHIV and OGB-MOLPCBA (Hu et al., 2020b) using ROC-AUC, while regression datasets emphasize MAE or RMSE. Common baselines and resources feature scaffold splits (Bemis and Murcko, 1996; Sheridan, 2013), molecular descriptors from RDKit (Landrum, 2016), Chemprop implementing directed message passing (Yang et al., 2019), and task suites from MoleculeNet including Tox21, ToxCast, BBBP, ClinTox, SIDER, and QM8 (Wu et al., 2018), alongside datasets like Lipophilicity and BACE curated from ChEMBL (Gaulton et al., 2017), with hyperparameter search spaces covering hidden sizes, message timesteps, dropout, and atom-bond featurizations commonly standardized by benchmarking toolkits such as DeepChem (Ramsundar et al., 2019). Integration with physics-based models permits constrained search and hybrid scoring. Conformer ensembles can align geometric and topological views of reactivity. Reaction-aware encoders extend representations beyond static molecular graphs. Cost-sensitive learning better reflects the economics of wet-lab experimentation. Label noise modeling can separate stochastic assay variation from systematic bias. Transfer across endpoints benefits from lightweight adapters and domain-specific tokenization. We report standardized preprocessing and faithful split protocols for fair assessment. Experiments evaluate data scale, supervision type, and calibration on diverse endpoints. Findings highlight reproducible gains under strict scaffold control and transparent reporting.

document: Personalization systems influence exposure, opportunities, and downstream economic outcomes. Fairness concerns arise among users, items, and groups with protected attributes. Relevance optimization often conflicts with parity goals under finite attention budgets. Feedback loops from recommenders can entrench historical imbalances and popularity bias. We examine fairness choices in metrics, modeling, and evaluation for large-scale platforms. Research on fairness in recommendation spans provider and consumer perspectives, with metrics and algorithms assessed on datasets such as MovieLens-1M and 20M (Harper and Konstan, 2015), Last.fm (Celma, 2010), Amazon Reviews (McAuley et al., 2015), and Yelp (Yelp Open Dataset, 2022), and methods including exposure-aware re-ranking like xQuAD and its variants (Vargas and Castells, 2011), FairCo and FairRec for calibrated coverage (Sonboli et al., 2020; Patro et al., 2020), pairwise debiasing via IPS and SNIPS (Schnabel et al., 2016), and causal corrections through propensity modeling (Wang et al., 2020), FA*IR constraints (Zehlike et al., 2017). Provider-side constraints include inventory limits and contractual delivery requirements. User-side constraints include diversity preferences and cognitive load. Platform policies typically combine business rules with learning objectives. Observational data exhibits selection bias due to previous ranking policies. Exposure fairness links position, clicks, and utility across heterogeneous items. Calibration aligns recommended distributions with desired coverage profiles. Multi-objective optimization introduces trade-offs between fairness and accuracy. Constraint handling can be performed at serving time or during training. Auditing requires careful cohort construction and confidence interval estimation. Longitudinal effects complicate static fairness measurements. Cold-start segments suffer from sparse signals and unstable estimates. Proxy labeling of sensitive attributes introduces measurement error. Robustness analysis should consider stress tests across demographics and geographies. Evaluation protocols reference statistical parity, disparate impact, equal opportunity, and exposure-based measures such as average rank and calibrated attention (Kamishima et al., 2012; Sapiezynski et al., 2019; Singh and Joachims, 2018), with offline estimators including IPS, SNIPS, and doubly robust corrections for logged bandit feedback (Swaminathan and Joachims, 2015), and benchmarked recommenders like MF, BPR, and NeuMF (Koren et al., 2009; Rendle et al., 2009; He et al., 2017) on MovieLens, Amazon, and Yelp subsets under popularity-based, time-based, and user-group splits commonly. Auditing and simulation toolkits include AIF360 and Fairlearn for metrics and interventions (Bellamy et al., 2019; Bird et al., 2020), RecSim for counterfactual user modeling (Ie et al., 2019), while contextual datasets such as KuaiRec, Gowalla, and Taobao (Guo et al., 2022; Ying et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2018) support policy-aware studies, and diversification or coverage-oriented re-ranking methods like xQuAD, MMR, and EILD (Vargas and Castells, 2011; Carbonell and Goldstein, 1998; Abdollahpouri et al., 2017) operationalize utility–fairness trade-offs in practical offline evaluation settings. Open problems include the interplay between fairness and strategic user behavior. Strategic suppliers may game exposure constraints through metadata manipulation. Confidence-aware ranking can reduce harm under uncertain relevance predictions. Causal inference clarifies pathways between policy levers and outcomes. Simulation frameworks enable safe exploration of alternative objectives and constraints. We systematize design choices and highlight reproducible baselines. Our experiments analyze exposure, calibration, and regret under multiple splits. Reporting focuses on parameter budgets and ablation-driven component attribution. We discuss policy implications and opportunities for practitioner tooling.

document: Generating radiology reports from images requires clinical accuracy and fine-grained grounding. Capturing uncertainty and negation is essential for safe interpretability. Cross-institution heterogeneity challenges generalization in multimodal settings. Domain shifts arise from scanners, protocols, and patient populations. We focus on training objectives, pretraining data, and evaluation protocols for this task. Foundational and domain-adapted multimodal approaches relevant to radiology include CLIP for contrastive image–text pretraining (Radford et al., 2021), ConVIRT for medical images and reports (Zhang et al., 2020), GLoRIA with global–local alignment (Huang et al., 2021), ALIGN for large-scale noisy pairs (Jia et al., 2021), and BioViL/ResLT for biomedical vision–language learning (Boecking et al., 2022), commonly trained or evaluated on MIMIC-CXR (Johnson et al., 2019), CheXpert (Irvin et al., 2019), ChestX-ray14 (Wang et al., 2017), IU X-Ray (Demner-Fushman et al., 2015), ROCO (Pelka et al., 2018), and PadChest (Bustos et al., 2020), and OpenI archives. Paired report–image corpora vary in label density and narrative style. Long-tail findings complicate coverage and calibration across rare conditions. Sentence plans and templates can stabilize medical terminology and structure. Segmentations and organ masks provide structural priors for downstream decoders. Report generation often benefits from retrieval-augmented contexts. Policy constraints limit the use of private annotations and images. Deidentification and audit trails are required for responsible deployment. Clinical taxonomies assist with normalized entities and relations. Errors propagate when labelers disagree on uncertain or conditional statements. Prevalence shifts alter precision–recall behavior across sites. Weak supervision can bootstrap scarce categories with noisy targets. Contrastive learning aligns visuals and text at multiple granularities. Evaluation should separate semantic correctness from stylistic fluency. Label extraction and evaluation resources include rule-based and neural tools such as NegBio (Peng et al., 2018), the CheXpert labeler (Irvin et al., 2019), CheXbert for entity-level agreement (Smit et al., 2020), and RadGraph for relation-centric grounding (Jain et al., 2021), with metrics spanning AUC, F1, precision–recall AUC, and RadGraph-based factuality, and auxiliary explanation techniques like Grad-CAM and integrated gradients (Selvaraju et al., 2017; Sundararajan et al., 2017) reported alongside retrieval-style measures in report-image alignment tasks and text metrics such as BLEU and ROUGE for legacy comparisons (Papineni et al., 2002; Lin, 2004). Related multimodal benchmarks cover VQA-RAD and SLAKE for visual question answering (Lau et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2021), PathVQA for pathology-focused QA (He et al., 2020), ROCO and ImageCLEFmedical for cross-modal retrieval (Pelka et al., 2018; Ionescu et al., 2020), MIMIC-CXR-JPG for classification (Johnson et al., 2019), IU X-Ray for generation (Demner-Fushman et al., 2015), and CheXpert for label robustness (Irvin et al., 2019), often with U-Net segmentations (Ronneberger et al., 2015). Calibration and selective generation can defer decisions to radiologists when uncertain. Continual learning is needed to adapt to new devices and practices. Robustness tests should include counterfactual negations and templated contradictions. Human factors research can assess cognitive load and trust calibration. Tools for error analysis help prioritize data collection and curation. We describe datasets, preprocessing, and metric choices used in our study. Implementation details include tokenization, image normalization, and loss composition. Experiments assess scaling, grounding, and uncertainty across multiple settings. We conclude with implications for reliable clinical text generation.

document: Multivariate time series forecasting supports planning, logistics, and resource allocation. Real-world systems demand accurate point and probabilistic predictions. Nonstationarity and regime shifts complicate model selection and tuning. Seasonality and special events introduce structured variation across horizons. We consider design choices spanning architecture, loss, and evaluation utilities. Classical and neural forecasting baselines include ARIMA and SARIMA (Box and Jenkins, 1970), ETS exponential smoothing (Hyndman et al., 2002), Prophet for trend and holidays (Taylor and Letham, 2018), DeepAR and DeepState for probabilistic sequences (Salinas et al., 2020; Rangapuram et al., 2018), N-BEATS for interpretable basis expansions (Oreshkin et al., 2020), Temporal Fusion Transformer (Lim et al., 2021), Informer and Autoformer for long horizons (Zhou et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2021), and PatchTST for channel-independent modeling (Nie et al., 2022), evaluated on M4, M5, ETT, Electricity, Exchange Rate, Traffic, Weather, and ILI benchmarks. Temporal covariates include holidays, promotions, and weather indicators. Static identifiers encode item or region attributes tied to dynamics. Calendar effects can be captured through engineered cyclical features. Missings and outliers require robust preprocessing and imputation. Varying sampling rates necessitate resampling and alignment procedures. Rolling evaluation enables stable estimates under serial correlation. Scalable training depends on batching schemes and window sampling. Parameter sharing trades off bias and variance across entities. Adaptive learning can track drift in evolving environments. Constraints tie forecasts to inventory or staffing capacities. Hierarchical aggregation links local and global consistency requirements. External regressors can improve short-term accuracy under shocks. Practical deployments must balance cost, accuracy, and latency. Evaluation across public benchmarks typically reports sMAPE, MASE, OWA for M4-style settings (Makridakis et al., 2018), MAE and RMSE for deterministic accuracy, and pinball loss or CRPS for probabilistic quality (Gneiting and Raftery, 2007), with rolling-origin splits, blocked cross-validation, and multi-horizon windows, while hierarchical retail assessments in M5 adopt WRMSSE and coherence constraints (Makridakis et al., 2020), and ETT, Electricity, Exchange Rate, Traffic, Weather, and ILI follow standardized train–validation–test partitions with lookback windows and calendar or covariate feature templates and fixed lags schemes. Baselines for comparison often include seasonal naïve and naïve-drift rules (Hyndman and Athanasopoulos, 2018), ETS and ARIMA pipelines tuned by auto.arima, gradient boosting over calendar regressors (Friedman, 2001), Prophet defaults, and neural toolkits such as GluonTS, darts, and PyTorch Forecasting (Alexandrov et al., 2020; Herold et al., 2021), with exogenous variables, static identifiers, and holiday effects documented per dataset card, and standardized data normalization, missing-value imputation, and resampling conventions specified to align batch construction and forecasting horizons across univariate and multivariate settings consistently. Ablations examine input lengths, decoder horizons, and covariate choices. We compare parameter counts and training time across models. Sensitivity analyses quantify robustness to hyperparameter variation. Confidence intervals guide risk-aware decision thresholds for operations. Backtesting pipelines align training, validation, and production rollouts. We report reproducible settings with fixed seeds and scripts. Datasets are standardized into uniform schemas with documented cards. Results are summarized across segments, horizons, and error regimes. We discuss implications for transparent, reliable forecasting at scale.

document: Recent advances in long-form summarization aim to condense scientific articles while preserving structure and factual grounding. The problem spans heterogeneous sections, technical nomenclature, and cross-referenced contributions that challenge standard sequence models. Prior work has studied data scaling, auxiliary objectives, and content selection under varying domain constraints. Evaluating output faithfulness remains difficult due to limited human references and evolving scholarly conventions. We outline the context of modeling choices, data sources, and evaluation regimes for research-article summarization. Early and contemporary approaches to summarization include TextRank (Mihalcea and Tarau, 2004), LexRank (Erkan and Radev, 2004), neural pointer-generator models (See et al., 2017), encoder-decoder Transformers with copy or coverage mechanisms (Gehrmann et al., 2018), BERTSUM for extractive and abstractive variants (Liu and Lapata, 2019), PEGASUS with gap-sentence objectives (Zhang et al., 2020), T5 unified text-to-text models (Raffel et al., 2020), and large-scale benchmarks such as CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015), Newsroom (Grusky et al., 2018), and XSum (Narayan et al., 2018), alongside domain datasets like arXiv and PubMed (Cohan et al., 2018), among others. Despite prolific progress, scientific discourse introduces tables, equations, and citations that require discourse-aware planning. Datasets differ in abstract length, section granularity, and citation density, complicating cross-benchmark comparison. Domain shifts across venues and subfields influence terminology and rhetorical moves in the target summaries. Models must handle long contexts, heterogeneous inputs, and selective compression with constrained decoding. Techniques for content planning range from extractive scaffolds to hierarchical encoders over sections and figures. Citation-aware modules attempt to track salient references and filter peripheral background statements. Discourse parsers can signal claims, evidence, and limitations to guide content prioritization. Retrieval-augmented generators incorporate external indexing to recover details not present in token-limited contexts. Constrained decoding methods attempt to enforce terminology, units, and section cues during generation. Pretraining on scholarly corpora promises better lexical coverage and technical style control. Curriculum schedules and auxiliary losses target factuality, salience, and redundancy through multi-task design. However, standardized evaluation across domains and paper structures remains open. Human-centered assessments report divergent judgments when criteria are underspecified and instructions are ambiguous. Common evaluation setups report ROUGE-N and ROUGE-L overlap (Lin, 2004), supplemented by BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002), METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005), and newer embedding-based metrics such as BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2019) and MoverScore (Zhao et al., 2019), while question-answering based measures like QAEval (Deutsch et al., 2021) and QuestEval (Scialom et al., 2021) probe content selection, with alignment-focused or entailment-oriented checks including SummaQA (Scialom et al., 2019), SummaC (Laban et al., 2022), and DAE-style factuality classifiers (Goyal and Durrett, 2021). Benchmarking resources for faithfulness and robustness include FRANK (Pagnoni et al., 2021), QAGS for validation (Wang et al., 2020), TRUE, a meta-evaluation suite (Honovich et al., 2022), FactCC and FactCCX for classification and explanation (Kryscinski et al., 2020), and domain-specific sets such as SciFact (Wadden et al., 2020), PubMedQA (Jin et al., 2019), and Evidence Inference (Lehman et al., 2019), alongside perturbation protocols like adversarial attacks on entities and numbers (Goodrich et al., 2019) and counterfactual edits that test calibration and consistency across variants. Current efforts also explore semi-structured inputs that combine text with citation graphs and tables. Such formats invite joint reasoning over nodes, edges, and captions beyond linear token streams. In-domain adaptation can be cost-effective with parameter-efficient tuning on small expert-curated corpora. Nonetheless, reproducibility varies with preprocessing pipelines that modify paragraph and equation boundaries. Clearer reporting of tokenization, truncation, and template prompts would aid fair comparisons. Our study examines long-context modeling and grounded evaluation under consistent preprocessing. We detail datasets, metrics, and protocols to isolate effects of retrieval, planning, and faithfulness constraints. Experimental design emphasizes robust ablations and power analyses over multiple seeds and institutions. We discuss implications for transparent benchmark construction and sustained scientific knowledge access.
