document: Graph representation learning has become central to computational chemistry and drug discovery. Molecular graphs encode atoms and bonds as nodes and edges, enabling neural models to reason over structure. Despite rapid progress, robustness across scaffold splits and rare chemotypes remains inconsistent. Data scarcity and label noise further complicate generalization, especially in low-resource assay families. Transfer learning across assays and tasks offers promise but its mechanisms are not fully characterized. We revisit pretraining, architecture depth, and equivariance under a unified evaluation protocol. Prior observations highlight interaction ranges and inductive biases as critical design choices. Early neural architectures for molecules include message passing networks and spectral convolutions, with representative methods such as MPNN (Gilmer et al., 2017), GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), and GAT (Veličković et al., 2018) widely adopted across benchmarks. Physically motivated models extend these ideas with directional or continuous-filter interactions, including SchNet (Schütt et al., 2018), DimeNet (Klicpera et al., 2020), and SphereNet (Liu et al., 2021), while pretraining strategies leverage MoleculeNet and OGB datasets. Benchmarks include QM9, ZINC, ESOL, FreeSolv, and Tox21, with ECFP and kernel baselines commonly reported in studies. Yet few studies interrogate how these design choices interact under mismatched train and test regimes. Our empirical analysis delineates confounding due to scaffold leakage and hyperparameter budgets. We also examine the stability of early stopping across correlated targets within multi-task settings. To isolate dataset artifacts, we stratify by size, label imbalance, and source assay diversity. This stratification allows controlled comparisons without conflating architecture benefits and curation effects. Finally, we report calibrated uncertainty metrics to contextualize improvements in ranking and regression. The resulting findings inform practical selections for screening pipelines and foundation models in chemistry. We release code and protocols to facilitate reproducibility and critical reassessment by the community.
spans: Early neural architectures for molecules include message passing networks and spectral convolutions, with representative methods such as MPNN (Gilmer et al., 2017), GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), and GAT (Veličković et al., 2018) widely adopted across benchmarks. Physically motivated models extend these ideas with directional or continuous-filter interactions, including SchNet (Schütt et al., 2018), DimeNet (Klicpera et al., 2020), and SphereNet (Liu et al., 2021), while pretraining strategies leverage MoleculeNet and OGB datasets. Benchmarks include QM9, ZINC, ESOL, FreeSolv, and Tox21, with ECFP and kernel baselines commonly reported in studies.

document: Recommender systems increasingly mediate access to information, services, and opportunities across domains. As deployments grow, concerns about disparate impacts on users and providers have intensified. Bias can arise from historical data, feedback loops, and platform objectives that prioritize engagement over equity. Measuring disparity requires disentangling errors, exposure allocations, and utility trade-offs among stakeholders. We formulate a multi-sided perspective that accounts for ranking, calibration, and opportunity redistribution. Research on fairness-aware recommendation spans provider-side constraints, consumer-side parity, and system-level exposure control, including calibrated ranking and regularization approaches by Burke, 2017; Celis et al., 2018; Singh and Joachims, 2018; Beutel et al., 2019; and Yao and Huang, 2017. Re-ranking methods adjust exposure post hoc using constrained optimization or greedy heuristics, while in-processing techniques incorporate fairness objectives during training via pairwise losses, adversarial critics, or multi-task setups exemplified by Biega et al., 2018; Narasimhan et al., 2020; and Li et al., 2021. Common datasets include MovieLens, Last.fm, KuaiRec, and MIND. However, empirical comparisons often conflate user-level and item-level fairness, blurring actionable conclusions. We provide a taxonomy linking metrics to failure modes observed in interactive settings. Building on this, we design interventions that target exposure while preserving relevance within tolerance thresholds. Our methodology introduces simulators that couple stochastic click models with creator supply constraints. This apparatus enables controlled stress tests over shift scenarios and strategic responses. We also evaluate the sensitivity of fairness gains to misspecified propensity estimates. Robustness analyses quantify degradation under retraining schedules and catalog churn. Beyond static datasets, we incorporate delayed impact through repeated user visits and inventory updates. The resulting insights guide principled deployment choices across marketplaces with heterogeneous goals. We release open-source tools to support transparent benchmarking and policy experimentation.
spans: Research on fairness-aware recommendation spans provider-side constraints, consumer-side parity, and system-level exposure control, including calibrated ranking and regularization approaches by Burke, 2017; Celis et al., 2018; Singh and Joachims, 2018; Beutel et al., 2019; and Yao and Huang, 2017. Re-ranking methods adjust exposure post hoc using constrained optimization or greedy heuristics, while in-processing techniques incorporate fairness objectives during training via pairwise losses, adversarial critics, or multi-task setups exemplified by Biega et al., 2018; Narasimhan et al., 2020; and Li et al., 2021. Common datasets include MovieLens, Last.fm, KuaiRec, and MIND.

document: Evaluating summarization quality remains challenging due to the multiplicity of valid outputs. Human judgments are costly and variable, motivating automatic metrics that correlate with expert assessments. For multilingual settings, additional issues arise from morphological richness, divergent word orders, and typological diversity. Proxy objectives that rely on English-centric resources may not transfer reliably. Recent models achieve impressive fluency but can hallucinate facts in low-resource languages. Therefore, metric design must capture both semantic adequacy and faithfulness across scripts and domains. We analyze metric behavior under perturbations that stress lexical choice and content selection. Our study covers news, procedural text, and conversational summaries spanning multiple families. Automatic evaluation has long relied on surface n-gram overlap metrics such as ROUGE (Lin, 2004) and BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002), with METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) introducing synonym matching and stemming for partial credit across variants. Embedding-based measures, including BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020), MoverScore (Zhao et al., 2019), and BLEURT (Sellam et al., 2020), seek semantic alignment via contextual encoders or learned regressors trained against human judgments on English benchmarks. Recent work explores reference-free quality estimation and question-answering metrics like QAEval, alongside datasets XSum, Multi-News, WikiLingua, and MLSum. Despite steady progress, correlations degrade for languages with sparse pretraining signals and complex morphology. We present a framework that isolates tokenization and alignment effects from model-induced variance. This framework quantifies cross-lingual robustness using bilingual pivots and transliteration baselines. We further introduce calibration plots to visualize metric uncertainty across content types. Experimental controls minimize confounds from length normalization and reference multiplicity. The results clarify where proxy metrics underperform and how reliability changes with data availability. Our recommendations inform practitioners selecting metrics for multilingual summarization pipelines.

document: Understanding 3D scenes from point clouds is fundamental for robotics, urban mapping, and AR. Unlike images, point sets are unordered, irregularly sampled, and often incomplete due to occlusions. These properties challenge convolutional inductive biases and memory budgets in large environments. Methods differ in how they encode local geometry, exploit sparsity, and fuse multi-scale context. Performance further depends on sensor characteristics and annotation granularity across datasets. We consider architectures and training schemes that balance accuracy, speed, and memory. Point-cloud segmentation has been approached by pointwise MLPs and local neighborhood aggregation, with representative methods including PointNet (Qi et al., 2017a), PointNet++ (Qi et al., 2017b), DGCNN (Wang et al., 2019), and KPConv (Thomas et al., 2019) establishing strong baselines. Sparse convolutional backbones such as MinkowskiNet (Choy et al., 2019) and voxel-hybrid designs like SPVNAS (Tang et al., 2020) enhance scalability, while memory-efficient decoders exemplified by RandLA-Net (Hu et al., 2020) maintain competitive accuracy on large scenes. Benchmarks commonly include ModelNet40, ShapeNet, S3DIS, ScanNet, and SemanticKITTI suites for comparable reporting. However, comparisons are hindered by varying voxel sizes, augmentations, and scene cropping strategies. We decouple these confounds by standardizing spatial resolutions and batch composition. Our pipeline measures amortized throughput and accuracy under controlled memory ceilings. We also probe sensitivity to LiDAR density and pose drift with synthetic perturbations. Cross-dataset transfer tests reveal overfitting to dataset-specific biases and sampling heuristics. To mitigate this, we introduce consistency regularizers that stabilize features across sparsity regimes. Finally, we examine error modes near object boundaries and long-range surfaces. The analysis yields guidance for deploying 3D perception on resource-limited platforms. Reproducible configurations and logs accompany the release to support future studies.

Benchmarks commonly include ModelNet40, ShapeNet, S3DIS, ScanNet, and SemanticKITTI suites for comparable reporting.

document: Transformer models have become the de facto backbone for language and vision tasks. Their deployment on edge devices remains constrained by memory, latency, and energy budgets. Hardware-aware training and model adaptation can bridge the gap between accuracy and efficiency. However, reported gains often depend on undocumented kernels and hand-tuned runtimes. Comparisons must therefore factor in operator availability, quantization support, and memory hierarchy effects. We study end-to-end inference pipelines across microcontrollers, mobile NPUs, and CPUs. Our focus is on practical configurations that preserve task utility under strict constraints. Model compression for transformers encompasses pruning, quantization, and distillation, with notable approaches including DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019), TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2020), MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020), and movement pruning (Sanh et al., 2020) targeting parameter and compute reductions. Low-precision inference has been explored via post-training and quantization-aware techniques, exemplified by Q8BERT, SmoothQuant, and bitsandbytes, while efficient attention variants such as Linformer, Performer, and Longformer reduce memory or sequence complexity with structured projections or kernel approximations. Evaluations frequently report GLUE, SQuAD, and WMT14 En-De throughput and accuracy under constraints. Prior studies rarely align hyperparameters and tolerances across tasks when contrasting methods. We propose a unified harness that normalizes batch sizes, sequence lengths, and decoding strategies. The harness integrates profiling hooks to attribute latency and energy to model components. We also calibrate numeric formats to prevent accuracy collapse under low-bit regimes. A device-agnostic cost model enables extrapolation to emerging accelerators and memory fabrics. Results highlight bottlenecks in attention kernels, embedding tables, and activation memory. These insights inform co-design opportunities spanning quantization policies and operator fusion. We release artifacts to facilitate standardized, reproducible evaluations on commodity hardware.

Low-precision inference has been explored via post-training and quantization-aware techniques, exemplified by Q8BERT, SmoothQuant, and bitsandbytes, while efficient attention variants such as Linformer, Performer, and Longformer reduce memory or sequence complexity with structured projections or kernel approximations. Graph-structured data increasingly underpins search, recommendation, and knowledge management in large-scale systems. Effective node and edge representations are central to downstream accuracy and data efficiency. While supervision can be costly or domain-specific, self-supervision offers a scalable alternative through structure- and attribute-level signals. However, heterogeneous graphs pose additional challenges due to multiple relation types and schema complexity. The robustness of learned embeddings under distribution shift and sparse labels remains an open concern. This paper examines self-supervised objectives that leverage heterogeneity without relying on extensive manual curation.

Prior work has adapted contrastive formulations to graphs by maximizing agreement between correlated views. Many of these views are induced by stochastic augmentations, but their semantics can break under typed nodes and relations. We therefore consider how metapath constraints and relation-aware perturbations might preserve schema information during pretraining. Furthermore, batch negatives derived from unrelated subgraphs can be misleading when cross-type proximity governs informativeness. A unified lens that links augmentation semantics with heterogeneity is needed to clarify design trade-offs. We position our study at the intersection of augmentation design and scalable pretraining for multi-relational networks.

Early studies on graph representation learning investigated shallow embeddings and random walks, including DeepWalk (Perozzi et al., 2014), LINE (Tang et al., 2015), and node2vec (Grover and Leskovec, 2016). Subsequent neural architectures introduced message passing and attention, such as GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), and GAT (Veličković et al., 2018). For self-supervised objectives, InfoGraph (Sun et al., 2020), DGI (Velickovic et al., 2019), GraphCL (You et al., 2020), and GCC (Qiu et al., 2020) explore contrastive signals across nodes, subgraphs, and augmented views. Work on heterogeneous and attributed graphs spans metapath2vec (Dong et al., 2017), HAN (Wang et al., 2019), HGT (Hu et al., 2020), and MAG240M as a large-scale benchmark (Hu et al., 2021). Pretraining with cross-network corpora involves methods like GPT-GNN (Hu et al., 2020b), GRACE (Zhu et al., 2020), BGRL (Thakoor et al., 2021), and GraphMAE (Hou et al., 2022). Evaluation practices typically include node classification on OGB datasets (Hu et al., 2020a), link prediction on OpenBioLink (Breit et al., 2020), and clustering with AMI and NMI scores under standard splits and protocols.

Despite steady progress, there is limited evidence on how contrastive signals should be routed across relation types in large benchmarks. We formalize this question by disentangling node, edge, and relation-level invariances in a single framework. Our analysis highlights when preserving metapath connectivity improves transfer versus when attribute masking is sufficient. We support these claims with controlled ablations and scale-sensitive evaluation protocols. The resulting perspective yields practical guidance for augmentation selection under heterogeneity and shift. We release code and reproducible pipelines to facilitate standardized comparisons.

Toxic language detection systems are increasingly deployed in content moderation and assistive writing tools. These systems must balance precision with harm minimization across sensitive demographic groups. Absent careful design, models can amplify stereotypes or produce disparate error rates. Variability in annotator perceptions and context length further complicates evaluation. Large pre-trained encoders offer strong baselines but inherit biases from training corpora. This section surveys datasets, metrics, and modeling practices that shape observed fairness outcomes.

A major challenge is separating offensiveness from identity mentions that are benign in context. Counterfactual and templated data augmentations attempt to control lexical confounding by swapping protected terms in matched sentences. While effective in isolation, these strategies can reduce coverage of dialectal variation and emergent slurs. Adversarial learning formulations target representation invariance but may conceal useful causal signals for moderation workflows. Practical deployments require calibration across thresholds to manage user-facing risk under traffic spikes. We thus consider evaluation regimes that combine distributional metrics with scenario-specific utility curves.

Work on toxicity detection has cataloged datasets and modeling choices across multiple domains, including Wikipedia Detox (Wulczyn et al., 2017), Civil Comments (Borkan et al., 2019), Jigsaw Unintended Bias in Toxicity Classification (Kaggle, 2019), and HateXplain (Mathew et al., 2021). Debiasing strategies span data balancing and counterfactual augmentation (Zhao et al., 2018; Garg et al., 2019), adversarial training against sensitive attributes (Zhang et al., 2018), and post-hoc calibration (Pleiss et al., 2017). Contextualized encoders such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), and DeBERTa (He et al., 2021) serve as standard backbones, while instruction-tuned models like T5 (Raffel et al., 2020) and FLAN (Wei et al., 2022) appear in recent studies. Measurement conventions include subgroup AUC, BPSN and BNSP AUC, equalized odds, demographic parity, and error rate balance on curated identity subsets (Borkan et al., 2019; Dixon et al., 2018). Research on stereotype probes and bias analyses includes StereoSet (Nadeem et al., 2021), CrowS-Pairs (Nangia et al., 2020), WinoBias (Zhao et al., 2018), and HolisticBias (Smith et al., 2022). Auditing tools cover AIF360 and Fairlearn in practice.

Despite diverse approaches, there is no consensus on the trade-offs between group fairness and contextual safety at scale. We study these tensions through stress tests that emulate platform shifts and adversarial paraphrases. Our analysis emphasizes the role of long-context modeling for disambiguating reclaimed terms and quoted speech. We introduce a protocol that disentangles thresholding from representation learning to isolate fairness effects. The findings point to practical configurations that maintain sensitivity without over-penalizing identity terms. We provide guidelines for auditing and documentation that align with policy and transparency requirements.

Recommender systems increasingly mediate user experiences across media, retail, and social platforms. Traditional supervised pipelines optimize static objectives on logged interactions, yet deployment dynamics are sequential. Reinforcement learning offers a principled framework to model long-term value and exploration. However, online experimentation is expensive and risky, motivating offline or batch RL approaches. Logged feedback is selective and policy-dependent, which complicates learning and evaluation. This section outlines background on methods and datasets relevant to sequential recommendation.

Classical collaborative filtering approaches include matrix factorization with implicit feedback (Hu et al., 2008), Bayesian personalized ranking (Rendle et al., 2009), and neural CF variants (He et al., 2017). Reinforcement learning paradigms introduce sequential decision modeling through DQN (Mnih et al., 2015), policy gradients (Sutton et al., 2000), actor–critic methods (Konda and Tsitsiklis, 2000), and Soft Actor-Critic (Haarnoja et al., 2018). Offline RL for recommendation has evaluated batch-constrained updates such as BCQ (Fujimoto et al., 2019), CQL (Kumar et al., 2020), and conservative PG methods (Kostrikov et al., 2021) on logged interaction datasets. Benchmarks frequently reference MovieLens (Harper and Konstan, 2015), KuaiRec (Gao et al., 2022), RecSim (Ie et al., 2019), and Open Bandit Dataset (Saito et al., 2021). Slate and ranking formulations consider ListNet (Cao et al., 2007), LambdaRank (Burges, 2010), and slate-Q learning (Ie et al., 2019). Off-policy evaluation techniques include IPS and doubly robust estimators (Bang and Robins, 2005), self-normalized variants (Swaminathan and Joachims, 2015), and model-based simulators for counterfactual testing (Bottou et al., 2013). Common metrics track CTR, cumulative reward, NDCG, and policy regret under offline constraints.

Offline training introduces extrapolation error when policies act beyond the support of historical logs. Conservative objectives aim to suppress overestimation but can underfit diverse user intents. Ranking and slate constraints further challenge temporal credit assignment across multiple displayed items. We analyze how action-space parameterization affects both policy expressivity and off-policy evaluation variance. Our study also examines simulator fidelity and its interaction with counterfactual estimators. Together, these considerations motivate design choices that balance safety with learning efficiency.

We develop a modular pipeline that isolates data curation, policy learning, and evaluation. The pipeline supports heterogeneous slates, delayed rewards, and partial observability. A set of ablations quantifies the trade-off between conservatism and responsiveness under shifts. We report findings on synthetic and real datasets with stress-tested OPE diagnostics. These results inform practical deployment strategies for sequential recommenders under offline constraints.

Automated segmentation underpins many clinical workflows by delineating organs and pathologies from scans. Accuracy and robustness are critical because downstream measurements drive diagnosis and planning. Annotated medical data are scarce and heterogeneous due to privacy and acquisition differences. Transfer and self-supervised learning can mitigate these limitations through task-agnostic signals. Yet domain shifts across scanners, protocols, and institutions remain a primary obstacle. We review architectural and data-centric advances relevant to scalable medical segmentation.

Convolutional designs offer strong inductive biases for locality but can struggle with long-range context. Hybrid architectures incorporate attention to bridge cross-organ dependencies in volumetric inputs. Pretraining from natural images is common, though modality gaps complicate representation reuse. Reconstruction and masking objectives exploit abundant unlabeled scans to warm-start segmentation heads. Robust evaluation requires standardized preprocessing and clear separation between tuning and test cohorts. We focus on reproducible practices that facilitate cross-institutional transfer and fair comparison.

Semantic segmentation in medical imaging has drawn on canonical CNN backbones such as U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015), V-Net (Milletari et al., 2016), and DeepLab variants (Chen et al., 2018). Transformer-based designs include TransUNet (Chen et al., 2021), Swin-UNet (Cao et al., 2021), and Segmenter adaptations for volumetric data (Strudel et al., 2021). Self-supervised pretraining leverages contrastive or reconstruction objectives, e.g., MoCo (He et al., 2020), BYOL (Grill et al., 2020), SimCLR (Chen et al., 2020), and masked autoencoding for medical scans (Zhou et al., 2021). Public datasets span BraTS for brain tumors (Menze et al., 2015), LiTS for liver lesions (Bilic et al., 2019), ACDC for cardiac MR (Bernard et al., 2018), and KiTS for kidney tumors (Heller et al., 2019). Multi-institutional benchmarks include MSD (Simpson et al., 2019), TCIA collections (Clark et al., 2013), and BTCV (Landman et al., 2015). Evaluation commonly reports Dice, Hausdorff distance, sensitivity, and average surface distance, with cross-validation protocols and preprocessing variations documented in leaderboards. Common toolkits include MONAI, nnU-Net, SimpleITK, and TorchIO for preprocessing, augmentation, training, and inference pipelines used across public challenges.

Despite broad progress, performance dispersion across acquisition protocols indicates unmet generalization needs. We examine normalization, patching, and sampling strategies that interact with anatomy variability. A disciplined benchmark suite clarifies when pretraining scales effectively with limited labels. We contribute ablations that isolate augmentation choices from architectural capacity. Finally, we release standardized pipelines to accelerate clinically meaningful comparisons.

Forecasting underpins planning in energy, logistics, and environmental monitoring. Practitioners must balance short-term accuracy with stability under distribution shift. Multivariate signals and exogenous drivers create complex dependency structures across scales. Long horizons exacerbate compounding error and calibration drift. Recent architectures target efficiency and context retention to address these challenges. We outline methodological foundations and benchmarking conventions for rigorous evaluation.

A key design choice is the granularity of temporal features and seasonal components. Sequence models capture nonlinear dynamics but can overfit when history windows are short. Decomposition techniques provide interpretable baselines that complement deep forecasting stacks. Probabilistic training turns point forecasts into calibrated intervals suited for risk-sensitive decisions. Data governance and leakage prevention are essential when using rolling-origin protocols. We emphasize transparency in hyperparameter search and validation alignment with deployment cadence.

Classical time-series models include ARIMA (Box and Jenkins, 1970), exponential smoothing (Hyndman et al., 2008), VAR (Lütkepohl, 2005), and state-space formulations via Kalman filtering (Kalman, 1960). Deep learning architectures cover LSTM and GRU encoders (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997; Cho et al., 2014), dilated temporal CNNs such as WaveNet (Oord et al., 2016), and attention-based transformers for long horizons (Vaswani et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2021). Probabilistic and distributional outputs appear in DeepAR (Salinas et al., 2020), TFT (Lim et al., 2021), N-BEATS (Oreshkin et al., 2020), and PatchTST (Nie et al., 2023). Benchmark suites and datasets include M4 and M5 competitions (Makridakis et al., 2018; Makridakis et al., 2020), Monash Time Series Forecasting Repository (Godahewa et al., 2021), Electricity and Traffic (Lai et al., 2018), and WeatherBench for global reanalysis (Rasp et al., 2020). Evaluation conventions adopt sMAPE, MASE, CRPS, pinball loss for quantiles, and capacity-weighted RMSE in energy settings. Data preprocessing often entails differencing, calendar features, holiday effects, z-scoring, and rolling-window splits across temporal folds. External regressors include weather, prices, mobility indices, and policy interventions for multi-horizon forecasting tasks.

Despite strong aggregate scores, many models degrade when covariate distributions shift unexpectedly. We investigate robust scaling, feature dropout, and ensembling to mitigate brittle behavior. Our study analyzes horizon-specific trade-offs between context length and parameter sharing. We report results with uncertainty diagnostics to assess operational fitness. The analysis yields practical guidance for selecting models under resource and reliability constraints.

document: Sarcasm detection remains challenging because signals are subtle and context dependent. Multimodal platforms introduce further ambiguity by mixing text, audio, and visual cues. Recent advances in self supervised learning encourage revisiting fusion strategies for sarcasm. However, disagreements across datasets complicate rigorous evaluation and comparison. Existing work reports large gains from pre training while fine grained cues remain elusive. In this study we situate sarcasm modeling within broader multimodal sentiment analysis. We review key modeling paradigms and identify sources of robustness failures in deployment. We also discuss annotation issues that confound generalization across communities and genres. Prior benchmarks illustrate the difficulty of measuring implicit intent and pragmatic meaning. Our discussion frames the empirical design choices that influence multimodal sarcasm outcomes. A careful accounting of resources and assumptions clarifies feasible progress within constraints. Multimodal sentiment research documents numerous architectures and datasets, including CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI for crowd annotated opinion intensity, CH-SIMS for Chinese audiovisual sentiment, and MUStARD for multimodal sarcasm in dialogues. Common text encoders use BERT or RoBERTa, while visual streams rely on ResNet or I3D and acoustic features on OpenSMILE or wav2vec. Fusion strategies range from early concatenation to tensor fusion networks, gated multimodal units, and co attention transformers. Cross modal pretrained models such as LXMERT, VisualBERT, and ViLBERT provide joint representations of language and vision. Recent studies adopt multimodal transformers with self attention, alignment losses, and auxiliary emotion labels. Annotator disagreement is tracked with reliability scores in MOSI and MOSEI, and word level timing is obtained via forced alignment. Standard evaluations report F1 and accuracy averages across folds, and robustness checks include train test shifts across benchmarks and adversarial noise perturbations. Despite promising tools the interplay of modalities still raises unresolved questions about context. We examine distributional shifts across platforms to delineate annotation drift and topical change. We evaluate aligners that synchronize words visemes and prosodic cues under realistic jitter. We define protocol decisions that affect label prevalence estimates in small data settings. We stress error decomposition by modality feature and sarcasm subtype to inform ablations. Our experiments incorporate masked channel evaluations to probe reliance on single modalities. We incorporate threshold calibration to account for prior shifts in deployment environments. We characterize uncertainty with label aware metrics suited to imbalanced sarcasm signals. We integrate confidence reporting to guide semi automated moderation workflows for creators. We augment analysis by examining cross corpus generalization patterns and annotator cohorts. We study metric sensitivity to class priors and temporal clustering by conversation.

document: Urban mobility systems depend on accurate traffic forecasts for safety and efficiency. Short term predictions inform routing, ramp metering, and transit control decisions. Neural models have improved accuracy by learning spatial and temporal dependencies. Yet deployment challenges arise from sensor outages and nonstationary demand. Structured priors can be beneficial when data are sparse or irregular. Graph representations encode road topology and help share information between sensors. Temporal modules capture recurrent patterns and special event deviations. The interplay of space and time remains central to modeling design choices. Recent work explores adaptive graphs to handle evolving correlations among nodes. Exogenous variables such as weather and incidents can further modulate predictions. Evaluation protocols must reflect operational horizons and reliability constraints. Comparisons often hinge on implementation details beyond nominal architecture names. Traffic forecasting literature enumerates canonical datasets such as METR-LA and PEMS-BAY with sensor graphs, PEMS04 and PEMS08 with countywide coverage, and TaxiBJ for citywide flows. Representative models include DCRNN with diffusion convolution over directed graphs, STGCN using Chebyshev polynomials and temporal convolutions, and Graph WaveNet combining adaptive adjacency with dilated TCNs. Attention based variants like ASTGCN and GMAN apply spatial temporal attention across learned neighborhoods. Baselines often compare against ARIMA, VAR, plain LSTM, and Seq2Seq with attention. Data preprocessing typically uses z score normalization, chronological splits, and horizon specific losses for 15, 30, and 60 minute steps. Common evaluation metrics are MAE, RMSE, and MAPE, sometimes augmented with masked losses for missing readings. Recent works incorporate multi graph fusion using road distance, dynamic correlation, and semantic similarity, and adopt curriculum sampling to stabilize training across long horizons and extreme event handling. Despite methodological diversity, consistent measurement practices remain crucial for credibility. We examine how graph construction affects equity across low volume districts. We study horizon specific losses that balance sharpness and bias under noise. We analyze calibration to align predictive intervals with service level targets. We benchmark data imputation pipelines for resilience during sensor downtimes. We investigate cold start behavior when new sensors join the network. We evaluate transfer across cities with mismatched topology and coverage. We report cost aware metrics that reflect control actuation constraints. We outline considerations for privacy when integrating probe vehicle data. We summarize implications for infrastructure planning and scalable traffic intelligence.

Representative models include DCRNN with diffusion convolution over directed graphs, STGCN using Chebyshev polynomials and temporal convolutions, and Graph WaveNet combining adaptive adjacency with dilated TCNs. Attention based variants like ASTGCN and GMAN apply spatial temporal attention across learned neighborhoods. Baselines often compare against ARIMA, VAR, plain LSTM, and Seq2Seq with attention. document: Automatic speech recognition degrades under acoustic and lexical shifts across domains. Background noise, microphone variability, and speaking style changes remain persistent challenges. Transfer learning and adaptation techniques aim to reduce annotation demands in practice. Recent pretraining methods learn robust representations from large scale unlabeled audio. However, downstream performance hinges on careful fine tuning and data selection. Mismatch between training and target distributions often produces brittle decoding behavior. Language model mismatch can amplify recognition errors in specialized vocabulary. Confidence estimation is essential for controlling self training loops at scale. Realistic deployment further imposes latency and memory constraints on streaming systems. Speech recognition studies frequently reference benchmark corpora such as LibriSpeech for read speech, TED-LIUM for talks, Switchboard for conversational telephone speech, and Common Voice for crowdsourced accents. Acoustic modeling architectures include Wav2Vec 2.0 pretraining, Conformer encoders, QuartzNet style temporal convolutions, and LSTM based CTC baselines. End to end objectives span CTC, attention encoder decoder, and RNN-T with joint networks. Adaptation techniques involve feature space transforms like fMLLR, speaker embeddings via i vectors or x vectors, and KLD regularization for fine tuning. Semi supervised pipelines rely on pseudo labeling, confidence filtering, and iterative self training from unlabeled audio. Data augmentation includes SpecAugment masks, speed perturbation, noise mixing from MUSAN, and room impulse responses from RIRS. Language modeling uses n gram rescoring, shallow fusion with Transformer LMs, and vocabulary normalization through grapheme or word piece units, and domain specific lexicon updates during adaptation. Despite abundant tooling, selecting strategies for specific mismatch types is nontrivial. We examine the relative benefits of lexicon updates versus acoustic adaptation. We analyze the interaction between language model fusion and CTC regularization. We study batching and curriculum choices that govern pseudo label quality. We present diagnostics for overfitting when adapting to small target sets. We measure calibration under temporal overlap and reverberant speech conditions. We investigate text normalization effects on medical and legal transcripts. We quantify the role of pronunciation modeling under emerging named entities. We report sensitivity to beam search parameters in streaming decoders. We evaluate latency accuracy tradeoffs using chunked attention configurations. We characterize robustness to channel drift in long term deployments. We integrate abstention policies to reduce critical transcription risk. We assess privacy constraints when incorporating user feedback for retraining.

End to end objectives span CTC, attention encoder decoder, and RNN-T with joint networks. Semi supervised pipelines rely on pseudo labeling, confidence filtering, and iterative self training from unlabeled audio. Language modeling uses n gram rescoring, shallow fusion with Transformer LMs, and vocabulary normalization through grapheme or word piece units, and domain specific lexicon updates during adaptation.

document: Recommender systems shape access to information and opportunities across digital platforms. Concerns about bias motivate techniques that align utility with fairness principles. Recommendation pipelines aggregate signals from implicit and explicit feedback sources. Historical data can encode societal disparities that propagate through models. Group level outcomes often diverge despite high aggregate accuracy. Interventions must consider utility tradeoffs and strategic behavior of stakeholders. Ranking settings complicate fairness by coupling item exposures within slates. Marketplace dynamics add provider concerns alongside user centric goals. Interpretability and transparency further influence perceptions of procedural justice. Governance requirements demand auditable metrics and replicable evaluation designs. Offline metrics may not capture welfare changes induced by re ranking. Deployment calls for monitoring, diagnosis, and updates under policy shifts. Sensitivity analyses highlight the risk of overfitting fairness to specific cohorts. Fairness aware recommendation studies employ datasets such as MovieLens 1M and 20M for ratings, Last.fm for music interactions, RecSys Challenge click logs, and KuaiRec for short video engagement. Metrics include demographic parity of exposure, equal opportunity on relevance, calibration within groups, and coverage diversity across items. Methods span post processing re ranking that adjusts exposure, constrained matrix factorization optimizing group objectives, adversarial representation learning to remove sensitive signals, and counterfactual evaluation using inverse propensity scoring. Some works model provider side fairness with exposure parity or minimum impression guarantees, while others address user side fairness through balanced relevance. Online approaches integrate contextual bandits with fairness constraints and slate optimization. Baselines include popularity, item kNN, Bayesian personalized ranking, and neural collaborative filtering. Evaluations report NDCG, MAP, and Recall alongside disparity indices across genders, age brackets, and protected attributes, and long tail exposure control. Despite metric variety, consistent reporting is necessary to compare interventions. We examine measurement error from preference confounding under selective exposure. We analyze target shift when demographics differ between training and serving. We study temporal drift in interests and its impact on fairness stability. We design audits that attribute disparities to ranking, feedback, or supply. We estimate opportunity costs imposed by fairness constraints under bounded budgets. We evaluate impact on providers with heterogeneous catalogs and capacities. We consider privacy implications when segmenting users into protected groups. We translate findings into operational guidance for equitable recommendation services.

Metrics include demographic parity of exposure, equal opportunity on relevance, calibration within groups, and coverage diversity across items. Methods span post processing re ranking that adjusts exposure, constrained matrix factorization optimizing group objectives, adversarial representation learning to remove sensitive signals, and counterfactual evaluation using inverse propensity scoring. Some works model provider side fairness with exposure parity or minimum impression guarantees, while others address user side fairness through balanced relevance. Online approaches integrate contextual bandits with fairness constraints and slate optimization. Baselines include popularity, item kNN, Bayesian personalized ranking, and neural collaborative filtering. Evaluations report NDCG, MAP, and Recall alongside disparity indices across genders, age brackets, and protected attributes, and long tail exposure control.

document: Knowledge graphs capture entities and relations that evolve over time. Temporal reasoning enables forecasting of future interactions and events. Applications include news analytics, recommendation, and risk monitoring. Modeling must respect temporal order and structural constraints jointly. Nonstationarity and sparsity challenge generalization across evolving contexts. Representation learning approaches vary in how time is encoded. Predictive accuracy depends on history selection and neighborhood scope. Efficient training is required for large dynamic graphs. Evaluation protocols should reflect both ranking and temporal validity. We outline typical assumptions about observation granularity and missingness. Benchmarks often conflate temporal prediction with static link inference. Temporal knowledge graph research commonly evaluates on ICEWS14, ICEWS05-15, GDELT subsets, and YAGO or WIKIDATA snapshots with timestamped triples. Static embedding baselines include TransE, DistMult, and ComplEx, while temporal extensions such as TTransE, HyTE, and DE-SimplE incorporate time via translation, hyperplanes, or decay. Tensor factorization approaches like TNTComplEx model periodic structure across time and relations. Sequence based models including Know-Evolve, DyRep, and RE-Net capture event dynamics with recurrent or point process components. Forecasting frameworks such as CyGNet and EvoKG use autoregressive histories and neighborhood aggregation. Negative sampling strategies vary between uniform, time aware, and adversarial distributions. Training commonly uses Adam with decoupled weight decay and gradient clipping. Evaluation reports filtered MRR, Hits@k for k in {1,3,10}, and time aware ranking variants that restrict candidate spaces. Implementations often rely on DGL-KE or PyTorch Geometric Temporal modules. With standardized entity and relation mappings. Despite progress, comparability suffers from inconsistent treatment of time windows. We examine history truncation effects on multi hop path exploitation. We study interval alignment strategies for heterogeneous timestamp resolutions. We analyze candidate generation schemes under memory constraints. We evaluate roll forward splits that prevent leakage across periods. We assess sensitivity to rare relation bursts and decay rates. We measure calibration for probabilistic event time predictions. We test robustness to delayed and backfilled updates in streams.

Static embedding baselines include TransE, DistMult, and ComplEx, while temporal extensions such as TTransE, HyTE, and DE-SimplE incorporate time via translation, hyperplanes, or decay. Tensor factorization approaches like TNTComplEx model periodic structure across time and relations. Sequence based models including Know-Evolve, DyRep, and RE-Net capture event dynamics with recurrent or point process components. Forecasting frameworks such as CyGNet and EvoKG use autoregressive histories and neighborhood aggregation. Negative sampling strategies vary between uniform, time aware, and adversarial distributions. Training commonly uses Adam with decoupled weight decay and gradient clipping. Evaluation reports filtered MRR, Hits@k for k in {1,3,10}, and time aware ranking variants that restrict candidate spaces. Implementations often rely on DGL-KE or PyTorch Geometric Temporal modules. With standardized entity and relation mappings.

document: Sentiment analysis models often degrade when applied to reviews from unseen domains due to covariate and label shift. Domain adaptation and active learning jointly offer a pathway to reduce annotation burden while maintaining robust transfer. Prior studies highlight that acquisition strategies can interact nontrivially with representation updates under shift. Nevertheless, existing evaluations rarely disentangle the roles of selection bias and alignment objectives. We focus on cross-domain review classification where label budgets are extremely constrained. Our goal is to characterize how acquisition signals shape representations that bridge domain gaps. We further examine calibration because decision confidence governs both selection and deployment risks. Prior work on sentiment domain adaptation spans feature alignment, instance reweighting, and representation learning. Classic pivots and SCL (Blitzer et al., 2006; Blitzer et al., 2007), autoencoder methods (Glorot et al., 2011), adversarial alignment (Ganin and Lempitsky, 2015; Shen et al., 2018), and contextualized pretraining with reviews such as Amazon, Yelp, and IMDB (McAuley et al., 2015; He and McAuley, 2016; Maas et al., 2011) are frequently leveraged, alongside benchmarks like Multi-Domain Sentiment, Amazon Reviews, and methods including MMD alignment (Tzeng et al., 2014), CORAL (Sun et al., 2016), and self-training with pseudo-labels (Lee, 2013). Despite the breadth of techniques, empirical comparisons often conflate pretraining corpora, model capacity, and labeling policies. As a result, it remains unclear which components actually drive gains under strict budgeted adaptation. We frame the problem through three interacting axes: feature alignment, uncertainty estimation, and coverage constraints. This framing enables ablations that isolate mechanisms rather than combining them opportunistically. We also revisit evaluation design to ensure that selected batches are not inadvertently leaked across folds. Beyond accuracy, we report calibration, label efficiency, and shifts in representation isotropy. To support reproducibility, all hyperparameters and seeds are fixed across acquisition strategies. We intentionally use the same encoder family to mitigate architecture confounds. In addition, we quantify the effect of domain-specific vocabulary through controlled synonym replacement. Our analysis reveals failure modes where uncertainty collapses around spurious artifacts. These insights motivate a simple modification to acquisition that penalizes redundancy while preserving uncertainty cues. The resulting protocol provides stable improvements across diverse review targets under tight budgets.

Classic pivots and SCL (Blitzer et al., 2006; Blitzer et al., 2007), autoencoder methods (Glorot et al., 2011), adversarial alignment (Ganin and Lempitsky, 2015; Shen et al., 2018), and contextualized pretraining with reviews such as Amazon, Yelp, and IMDB (McAuley et al., 2015; He and McAuley, 2016; Maas et al., 2011) are frequently leveraged, alongside benchmarks like Multi-Domain Sentiment, Amazon Reviews, and methods including MMD alignment (Tzeng et al., 2014), CORAL (Sun et al., 2016), and self-training with pseudo-labels (Lee, 2013).

document: Predicting molecular properties from structure underpins lead discovery, toxicity screening, and materials design. Graph neural networks offer inductive biases aligned with molecular graphs, but their generalization remains unstable across endpoints. Two factors complicate evaluation: scaffold shift and label noise from heterogeneous assays. Moreover, chemically plausible constraints are rarely enforced during model selection. We study how representation bottlenecks and regularization interact with data regimes spanning thousands to millions of compounds. In particular, we examine the role of expressivity versus calibration under scaffold splits. We also assess whether uncertainty estimates correlate with synthetic accessibility risk. Our analysis spans property prediction, reaction yield estimation, and constrained generation tasks. Canonical datasets and models include MoleculeNet collections such as QM7, QM8, QM9, ESOL, FreeSolv, and Lipophilicity (Wu et al., 2018), and ZINC subsets for constrained generation (Jin et al., 2018). Prominent architectures span GraphConv fingerprints (Duvenaud et al., 2015), message passing networks and D-MPNN variants (Gilmer et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2019), SchNet and PhysNet for quantum targets (Schütt et al., 2017; Unke and Meuwly, 2019), GIN and GAT baselines (Xu et al., 2019; Veličković et al., 2018), and transformer chemistries such as ChemBERTa and GROVER (Chithrananda et al., 2020; Rong et al., 2020). Despite substantial progress, cross-benchmark comparisons often conflate featurization, split strategy, and hyperparameter search. Consequently, it is difficult to attribute gains to architecture choices rather than training pipelines. We therefore adopt a standardized evaluation protocol that fixes descriptors, split seeds, and tuning budgets. To probe extrapolation, we include temporal splits derived from registration dates. We complement scalar metrics with error calibration and conformal coverage. For small data, we contrast pretraining and data augmentation against stronger regularization baselines. For larger data, we analyze capacity scaling and its impact on scaffold diversity. We further investigate how augmentations affect stereochemistry and valency preservation. The resulting study separates improvements due to representation power from those due to data curation. Our findings challenge several commonly held assumptions about model size and uncertainty. We release code, standardized splits, and trained weights to facilitate rigorous comparison.
spans: Canonical datasets and models include MoleculeNet collections such as QM7, QM8, QM9, ESOL, FreeSolv, and Lipophilicity (Wu et al., 2018), and ZINC subsets for constrained generation (Jin et al., 2018). Prominent architectures span GraphConv fingerprints (Duvenaud et al., 2015), message passing networks and D-MPNN variants (Gilmer et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2019), SchNet and PhysNet for quantum targets (Schütt et al., 2017; Unke and Meuwly, 2019), GIN and GAT baselines (Xu et al., 2019; Veličković et al., 2018), and transformer chemistries such as ChemBERTa and GROVER (Chithrananda et al., 2020; Rong et al., 2020).

document: Machine translation increasingly incorporates speech and visual modalities to handle real-world inputs. End-to-end speech translation reduces latency by bypassing intermediate transcripts but complicates training. A persistent challenge is label scarcity relative to automatic speech recognition and machine translation corpora. Cross-modal pretraining offers one remedy, yet alignment errors propagate across stages. We analyze how representation bottlenecks interact with multilingual vocabularies during fine-tuning. Further, we study whether auxiliary objectives mitigate overfitting on small speech–text corpora. Our evaluation emphasizes low-resource directions where data imbalance is severe. Multimodal and speech translation resources and baselines abound. Benchmarks such as MuST-C, How2, and CoVoST 2 provide large-scale parallel speech–text corpora (Di Gangi et al., 2019; Sanabria et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020), while IWSLT and TED-LIUM support supervised settings (Cettolo et al., 2015; Rousseau et al., 2014). End-to-end architectures include Transformer-based ST with CTC/attention (Karita et al., 2019; Inaguma et al., 2020), S2T Conformer models in Fairseq (Wang et al., 2021), ESPnet-ST pipelines (Inaguma et al., 2020), and pretraining via wav2vec 2.0 or HuBERT front-ends (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021). Despite the resources available, comparisons often blur differences in segmentation, vocabulary size, and decoding constraints. This obscures the source of gains and complicates system design for practical deployments. We therefore normalize tokenization, beam search, and data filters across all systems. To probe transfer, we incorporate frozen and partially updated encoders under identical schedules. We examine latency–quality trade-offs through constrained decoding and subsampling. We include robustness tests under noise and domain shifts typical of instructional media. Evaluation uses sacreBLEU with consistent references and casing policies. We also report ASR word error and MT adequacy to dissect cross-task influences. Ablations target the contribution of front-end pretraining and intermediate supervision. The results reveal systematic interactions between language typology and acoustic modeling. We discuss practical implications for streaming, adaptation, and curriculum design. All recipes and checkpoints will be released to encourage careful replication.
spans: Multimodal and speech translation resources and baselines abound. Benchmarks such as MuST-C, How2, and CoVoST 2 provide large-scale parallel speech–text corpora (Di Gangi et al., 2019; Sanabria et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020), while IWSLT and TED-LIUM support supervised settings (Cettolo et al., 2015; Rousseau et al., 2014). End-to-end architectures include Transformer-based ST with CTC/attention (Karita et al., 2019; Inaguma et al., 2020), S2T Conformer models in Fairseq (Wang et al., 2021), ESPnet-ST pipelines (Inaguma et al., 2020), and pretraining via wav2vec 2.0 or HuBERT front-ends (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021).

document: Recommender systems trained on logged interactions inherit selection bias from historical exposure policies. This bias distorts both evaluation and learning when naive empirical risk minimization is applied. Causal inference provides tools to correct for confounding and to assess counterfactual performance. Yet practical deployments face unstable estimators and scarce ground truth for unbiased outcomes. Moreover, item and user churn induce nonstationarity that invalidates common assumptions. We investigate learning objectives that trade off variance control and representation sufficiency. Our perspective emphasizes policy-aware embeddings that jointly model exposure and relevance. Prior work on counterfactual learning for recommenders spans off-policy evaluation and debiasing techniques. Inverse propensity scoring and its self-normalized variants are widely used (Rosenbaum and Rubin, 1983; Swaminathan and Joachims, 2015), with doubly robust objectives and DR-IPS extensions improving variance (Dudík et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2017). Matching and balancing approaches such as propensity score matching, entropy balancing, and covariate shift correction are (Rosenbaum and Rubin, 1985; Hainmueller, 2012; Shimodaira, 2000). Benchmarks include Yahoo! R3, Coat, MovieLens with propensities, and OpenBanditDataset (Chapelle and Chang, 2011; Schnabel et al., 2016; Takeda and Joachims, 2020). Despite the methodological variety, comparative studies often vary logging policies and feature sets simultaneously. This confounds attribution of improvements to estimators versus auxiliary modeling. We propose a controlled evaluation harness that fixes propensities and audits overlap. The harness includes diagnostics for extreme weights and support violations. We also examine how representation capacity influences covariate balance under learned propensities. To stabilize training, we introduce regularization that penalizes brittle exposure predictions. Our analysis spans short-head and long-tail items with realistic cold-start rates. Metrics report regret, calibration of estimated propensities, and effective sample size. We further quantify robustness to model misspecification through semi-synthetic stress tests. The study provides guidance for deploying counterfactual learning in large-scale recommenders.
spans: Prior work on counterfactual learning for recommenders spans off-policy evaluation and debiasing techniques. Inverse propensity scoring and its self-normalized variants are widely used (Rosenbaum and Rubin, 1983; Swaminathan and Joachims, 2015), with doubly robust objectives and DR-IPS extensions improving variance (Dudík et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2017). Matching and balancing approaches such as propensity score matching, entropy balancing, and covariate shift correction are (Rosenbaum and Rubin, 1985; Hainmueller, 2012; Shimodaira, 2000). Benchmarks include Yahoo! R3, Coat, MovieLens with propensities, and OpenBanditDataset (Chapelle and Chang, 2011; Schnabel et al., 2016; Takeda and Joachims, 2020).

document: Clinical segmentation systems must perform reliably under diverse scanners, protocols, and pathologies. Despite substantial progress, performance often deteriorates when deployment shifts away from curated benchmarks. Recent discourse emphasizes data-centric strategies that improve labels, sampling, and quality control. Yet systematic evaluations of such strategies across modalities remain limited. We study how dataset pruning and targeted relabeling interact with architecture-agnostic training recipes. Medical image segmentation has matured around standardized architectures and benchmarks. Widely used models include U-Net and variants, DeepLab families, and nnU-Net auto-configuration (Ronneberger et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2018; Isensee et al., 2021), with toolkits such as MONAI and nnU-Net pipelines facilitating reproducibility (Cardoso et al., 2022; Isensee et al., 2021). Representative datasets comprise BraTS for brain tumors, LiTS for liver lesions, KiTS for kidney tumors, MSD collections across organs, and PROMISE12 for prostate (Menze et al., 2015; Bilic et al., 2019; Heller et al., 2021; Simpson et al., 2019; Litjens et al., 2014). Much of the literature reports gains without isolating whether benefits arise from models or curation. Consequently, practitioners lack guidance on where to invest limited annotation budgets. We address this by holding model families fixed and varying only data operations. Our design measures returns on curation using matched compute and schedulers. We formalize label quality using probabilistic adjudication and traceable disagreement sets. We also analyze how augmentation and intensity normalization couple with revised labels. Beyond Dice, we report lesion-wise sensitivity, boundary Hausdorff, and calibration. Robustness is assessed under out-of-distribution scanners and adversarial corruptions. We incorporate reader studies to gauge clinical acceptability of improvements. Ablations highlight when simpler policies outperform sophisticated heuristics. The findings chart a principled path toward safer segmentation under realistic constraints. We provide tooling for audit trails, dataset versioning, and reproducible curation. All code, configuration files, and de-identified metadata will be released publicly.
spans: Medical image segmentation has matured around standardized architectures and benchmarks. Widely used models include U-Net and variants, DeepLab families, and nnU-Net auto-configuration (Ronneberger et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2018; Isensee et al., 2021), with toolkits such as MONAI and nnU-Net pipelines facilitating reproducibility (Cardoso et al., 2022; Isensee et al., 2021). Representative datasets comprise BraTS for brain tumors, LiTS for liver lesions, KiTS for kidney tumors, MSD collections across organs, and PROMISE12 for prostate (Menze et al., 2015; Bilic et al., 2019; Heller et al., 2021; Simpson et al., 2019; Litjens et al., 2014).

document: Time series anomaly detection in sensor networks faces the dual challenge of extreme class imbalance and nonstationary distributions. Prior work has examined reconstruction, density estimation, and classification heuristics under varying levels of supervision. Realistic deployments also impose constraints on latency, memory footprint, and calibration under drift. Recent meta-learning approaches promise data efficiency but can be brittle when anomalies shift in semantics. Evaluation practices differ significantly across telemetry sources and preprocessing pipelines. Prior studies examine few-shot anomaly detection via meta-learning and reconstruction criteria (Zhao et al., 2020; Ruff et al., 2018; Li et al., 2021), contrastive representation learning (Chen et al., 2020; Sohn, 2016; He et al., 2020), probabilistic forecasting baselines (Salinas et al., 2020; Rangapuram et al., 2018), and benchmarks including MSL, SMAP, NAB, and SWaT (Lavin and Ahmad, 2015; Goh et al., 2016). Comparisons include TSFresh, ROCKET, OmniAnomaly, DeepSVDD in telemetry and weblogs. Despite broad coverage of modeling techniques, comparisons rarely report confidence reliability or post-deployment adaptation cost. Many industrial traces exhibit regime switches that complicate model reuse across assets. Transfer strategies often presume stationarity in normal behavior that is violated during maintenance windows. Furthermore, labeling policies vary from incident-based to interval-based annotations. This heterogeneity complicates threshold selection and alert aggregation. Few studies investigate the interaction between detector sensitivity and operator triage workload. We position our study within this operational context to emphasize actionable evaluation. The following sections detail data, metrics, and protocols used in our experiments.
spans: Prior studies examine few-shot anomaly detection via meta-learning and reconstruction criteria (Zhao et al., 2020; Ruff et al., 2018; Li et al., 2021), contrastive representation learning (Chen et al., 2020; Sohn, 2016; He et al., 2020), probabilistic forecasting baselines (Salinas et al., 2020; Rangapuram et al., 2018), and benchmarks including MSL, SMAP, NAB, and SWaT (Lavin and Ahmad, 2015; Goh et al., 2016). Comparisons include TSFresh, ROCKET, OmniAnomaly, DeepSVDD in telemetry and weblogs.

document: Recommender systems at web scale consume substantial compute during training and serving. Growing demand for personalization has intensified interest in efficiency without sacrificing relevance. Latency constraints are particularly severe for ads and media streaming scenarios. Hardware heterogeneity and data center energy variability further complicate deployment. Prior studies examine accuracy, but environmental impact and cost remain underreported. Transparent measurement frameworks are beginning to emerge in machine learning more broadly. Existing studies address recommendation efficiency through model compression and distillation (Hinton et al., 2015; Sun et al., 2019), nearest neighbor retrieval (Johnson et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2020), quantization and sparsification (Gong et al., 2014; Jacob et al., 2018), and carbon-aware evaluation with MLCO2, CodeCarbon, and Experiment Impact Tracker (Lacoste et al., 2019; Henderson et al., 2020). Benchmarks include MovieLens, Criteo, Amazon Reviews, and Alibaba Ads datasets for accuracy, throughput, and latency. These lines of work highlight the prevalence of accuracy-centric reporting in industrial contexts. However, variability in feature pipelines often masks algorithmic contributions to efficiency. Differences in negative sampling, logging policy, and caching can dominate observed gains. Moreover, load spikes and diurnal patterns stress test retrieval components. Understanding these interactions is important for practical system design. We therefore contextualize efficiency within a lifecycle that includes data collection, training, serving, and monitoring. Our study reports unified cost and utility metrics to inform sustainable deployment.
spans: Existing studies address recommendation efficiency through model compression and distillation (Hinton et al., 2015; Sun et al., 2019), nearest neighbor retrieval (Johnson et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2020), quantization and sparsification (Gong et al., 2014; Jacob et al., 2018), and carbon-aware evaluation with MLCO2, CodeCarbon, and Experiment Impact Tracker (Lacoste et al., 2019; Henderson et al., 2020). Benchmarks include MovieLens, Criteo, Amazon Reviews, and Alibaba Ads datasets for accuracy, throughput, and latency.

document: Automatic generation of natural language summaries from source code supports maintenance and onboarding. The task demands reasoning over control flow, data flow, and naming conventions. Existing models must generalize across languages and repositories with diverse idioms. Pretraining on large code corpora has improved robustness but evaluation remains inconsistent. Reproducibility is challenged by differences in tokenization and filtering of noisy comments. Research on code summarization and comment generation spans sequence-to-sequence models with AST encoders (Iyer et al., 2016; Alon et al., 2019), pretrained language models adapted to code such as CodeBERT, GraphCodeBERT, and PLBART (Feng et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2021; Ahmad et al., 2021), and datasets including CodeSearchNet, FunCom, JavaDoc, and CONCODE (Husain et al., 2019; LeClair and McMillan, 2019; Iyer et al., 2018). Evaluation commonly reports BLEU, ROUGE-L, METEOR, and CodeBLEU. Despite widespread use of text-only metrics, human studies reveal gaps in factuality and usefulness. Summary quality is also affected by project-specific style guides and documentation norms. Length control mechanisms can trade completeness for precision in subtle ways. Some settings require bilingual or cross-lingual training to serve multinational teams. Error analysis frequently points to unresolved identifiers and library semantics. Benchmarks constructed from issue trackers remain underexplored relative to code-context corpora. We frame the task with stronger controls on repository sampling and deduplication. Our study outlines protocols for meaningful, replicable assessment under limited computational budgets.
spans: Research on code summarization and comment generation spans sequence-to-sequence models with AST encoders (Iyer et al., 2016; Alon et al., 2019), pretrained language models adapted to code such as CodeBERT, GraphCodeBERT, and PLBART (Feng et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2021; Ahmad et al., 2021), and datasets including CodeSearchNet, FunCom, JavaDoc, and CONCODE (Husain et al., 2019; LeClair and McMillan, 2019; Iyer et al., 2018). Evaluation commonly reports BLEU, ROUGE-L, METEOR, and CodeBLEU.

document: Situated dialogue with robots requires grounding language in perception, state, and action. Users expect fluid turn-taking, corrections, and clarification under real-world noise. Embodied settings introduce occlusions, latency, and device constraints that degrade language understanding. Tracking discourse entities over time remains a key difficulty. Prior research spans symbolic, probabilistic, and neural pipelines for conversational control. Shared tasks and simulations have accelerated progress while highlighting generalization gaps. Dialogue grounding in human-robot interaction has been explored with alignment and reference resolution (Kennington and Schlangen, 2015; Chai et al., 2018; Whitney et al., 2017), neural pipelines RASA, DIET, and Transformer trackers (Bocklisch et al., 2017; Rastogi et al., 2020; Henderson et al., 2019), simulation benchmarks TEACh, ALFRED, CerealBar, and SIMMC (Padmakumar et al., 2021; Shridhar et al., 2020; Kottur et al., 2021), and datasets of speech including Talk the Walk and PersuasionForGood. While these resources are valuable, transferring to hardware-constrained platforms often exposes brittleness. Sensing failures and grounding errors compound to produce dialogue breakdowns. Conversely, overconfident models may ignore safety-critical requests for clarification. The lack of unified evaluation across tasks obscures true progress. Moreover, privacy and consent requirements restrict data collection in domestic environments. Auditing model behavior thus benefits from interpretable state tracking and retriable policies. We focus on scenario design that supports reliable recovery from ambiguity. The next sections present system assumptions, datasets, and evaluation setups.
spans: Dialogue grounding in human-robot interaction has been explored with alignment and reference resolution (Kennington and Schlangen, 2015; Chai et al., 2018; Whitney et al., 2017), neural pipelines RASA, DIET, and Transformer trackers (Bocklisch et al., 2017; Rastogi et al., 2020; Henderson et al., 2019), simulation benchmarks TEACh, ALFRED, CerealBar, and SIMMC (Padmakumar et al., 2021; Shridhar et al., 2020; Kottur et al., 2021), and datasets of speech including Talk the Walk and PersuasionForGood.

document: Label scarcity in clinical imaging has motivated self-supervised learning to leverage unlabeled scans. These methods aim to learn transferable representations that respect anatomical structure and acquisition artifacts. Heterogeneity across devices and protocols complicates both pretraining and evaluation. Distribution shift is pronounced when transferring between hospitals or patient populations. Downstream tasks often involve detection, classification, and segmentation under strict safety constraints. Comparisons are frequently confounded by inconsistent preprocessing and label definitions. Self-supervised learning for medical imaging has included contrastive pretraining on Radiograph, ChestX-ray14, and CheXpert (Wang et al., 2017; Irvin et al., 2019), masked autoencoding and inpainting (He et al., 2022; Pathak et al., 2016), jigsaw and rotation prediction (Noroozi and Favaro, 2016; Gidaris et al., 2018), and multi-instance, multi-label objectives for pathology slides (Campanella et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2021). Evaluation reports AUC and F1 on NIH, RSNA Pneumonia, and SIIM-ACR datasets. Despite progress, pretext-task choice can bias attention toward textures rather than pathologies. Patch-level supervision may fail to capture long-range context necessary for clinical reading. Evaluation on temporal cohorts is rare but critical for monitoring model aging. Regulations also limit the dissemination of test sets and annotations. Reporting compute, memory, and calibration should accompany AUC to enable fair comparisons. We concentrate on label-efficient pipelines that reduce tuning instability in practice. Subsequent sections describe datasets, protocols, and metrics used in our study.
spans: Self-supervised learning for medical imaging has included contrastive pretraining on Radiograph, ChestX-ray14, and CheXpert (Wang et al., 2017; Irvin et al., 2019), masked autoencoding and inpainting (He et al., 2022; Pathak et al., 2016), jigsaw and rotation prediction (Noroozi and Favaro, 2016; Gidaris et al., 2018), and multi-instance, multi-label objectives for pathology slides (Campanella et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2021). Evaluation reports AUC and F1 on NIH, RSNA Pneumonia, and SIIM-ACR datasets.

document: Temporal link prediction in dynamic graphs aims to anticipate future interactions among entities. Applications range from recommendation to knowledge base completion. Prior introductions emphasize both representation learning and event modeling. We review foundations and recent advances to contextualize the task. Static embeddings treat observed edges as exchangeable across time. Dynamic models incorporate temporal ordering and varying intensities. Evaluation protocols complicate comparisons due to leakage risks. Split strategies must respect causality and inductiveness. Training losses vary from margin ranking to likelihood estimation. Negative sampling choices affect calibration and efficiency. Temporal granularity further shapes the modeling assumptions. Benchmarks differ in density, stationarity, and node churn. Prior studies span embedding, graph, and temporal models alongside widely used datasets, including TransE (Bordes et al., 2013), DistMult (Yang et al., 2015), ComplEx (Trouillon et al., 2016), RotatE (Sun et al., 2019), DeepWalk (Perozzi et al., 2014), node2vec (Grover and Leskovec, 2016), GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GAT (Veličković et al., 2018), R-GCN (Schlichtkrull et al., 2018), Know-Evolve (Trivedi et al., 2017), DyRep (Trivedi et al., 2019), TGAT (Xu et al., 2020), TGN (Rossi et al., 2020), JODIE (Kumar et al., 2019), FB15k (Bordes et al., 2013), WN18RR (Dettmers et al., 2018), ICEWS14 (Trivedi et al., 2017), GDELT (Leetaru and Schrodt, 2013), YAGO (Suchanek et al., 2007), and OGB datasets (Hu et al., 2020) for evaluation and comparison across settings. Despite diverse tooling, temporal generalization remains elusive. Heterogeneity across domains challenges one-size-fits-all designs. Scalability constraints inhibit deployment on web-scale graphs. Memory mechanisms trade expressivity for cost. Streaming settings demand bounded state updates. Event-driven formulations capture fine-grained dynamics. Discrete-time snapshots emphasize simplicity and batching. Node and relation features modulate performance significantly. Cold-start nodes are especially problematic in sparse regimes. Attention mechanisms help focus on informative neighborhoods. Regularization alleviates overfitting on short-lived bursts. Calibration remains underexplored for temporal scores. Uncertainty estimation is valuable for decision-making. Evaluation should consider horizon sensitivity and drift. Reproducibility requires transparent preprocessing and seeds. Hyperparameter search impacts conclusions in subtle ways. Public leaderboards often mask dataset idiosyncrasies. Interpretability can aid auditing and fairness. Privacy considerations arise with sensitive interaction logs. These aspects frame ongoing challenges in the literature.

document: Multimodal sentiment analysis integrates language, vision, and acoustics. Its goal is to infer attitudes and emotions from human communication. Early work focused on handcrafted features and late fusion. Deep learning enabled end-to-end cross-modal modeling. Challenges include alignment, noise, and domain shift. Benchmark practices vary substantially across papers. Reproducibility and fair comparison remain pressing concerns. Evaluation must consider topic, speaker, and setting biases. The role of pretraining has grown rapidly. Transformer backbones dominate recent progress. Robustness to missing modalities is another frontier. Interpretability is important for human-centered applications. Dataset scale directly impacts generalization. Privacy and consent constraints limit data collection. Annotation protocols introduce label uncertainty. Transfer across cultures is still difficult. Cross-modal synchronization often degrades in the wild. Existing multimodal sentiment and emotion studies reference canonical datasets and architectures, including CMU-MOSI (Zadeh et al., 2016), CMU-MOSEI (Zadeh et al., 2018), IEMOCAP (Busso et al., 2008), and MELD (Poria et al., 2019); fusion models such as TFN (Zadeh et al., 2017), LMF (Liu et al., 2018), MFN (Zadeh et al., 2018), and MISA (Hazarika et al., 2020); cross-modal attention approaches like MulT (Tsai et al., 2019) and MAG-BERT (Rahman et al., 2020); unimodal encoders from BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019); and auxiliary resources including glove embeddings (Pennington et al., 2014), COVAREP acoustic features (Degottex et al., 2014), and OpenFace facial landmarks (Baltrušaitis et al., 2016) for preprocessing and evaluation. Many studies differ in feature normalization. Sequence truncation can discard affective cues. Latency constraints affect real-time deployment. Calibration enhances downstream decision making. Semi-supervised learning offers gains with less annotation. Multimodal distillation reduces inference cost. Continual learning addresses temporal drift. Evaluation on unseen speakers is essential. Adversarial training can improve invariance. Standardized splits prevent subtle leakage. Shared codebases facilitate ablation parity. Reporting compute budgets aids fairness. Ethical risks motivate careful release processes. Considerations extend to accessibility and inclusion. These themes motivate a rigorous comparative framework.

document: Healthcare predictive modeling increasingly requires collaboration across institutions. Regulations and ethics restrict centralized data sharing. Federated learning offers a decentralized alternative. It enables model training without raw data exchange. Practical deployments face heterogeneity across sites. Communication efficiency is a core barrier. Privacy accounting must be explicit and auditable. Secure protocols mitigate inference risks. Clinical datasets exhibit shift and label noise. Robustness to missing codes and modalities is vital. Evaluation must respect temporal causality. External validation is a primary requirement. Personalization balances global and local objectives. Fairness across subpopulations remains challenging. Work on federated and privacy-preserving healthcare modeling references widely used algorithms, protections, and benchmarks, including FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), FedProx (Li et al., 2020), SCAFFOLD (Karimireddy et al., 2020), and FedNova (Wang et al., 2020); differential privacy with DP-SGD (Abadi et al., 2016) and Rényi accounting (Mironov, 2017); secure aggregation protocols (Bonawitz et al., 2017) and homomorphic encryption toolkits (Acar et al., 2018); datasets such as MIMIC-III (Johnson et al., 2016), eICU (Pollard et al., 2018), PhysioNet Challenge cohorts (Goldberger et al., 2000), and UK Biobank imaging (Bycroft et al., 2018); model families from logistic regression to transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017); and open-source frameworks like TensorFlow Federated (Ingerman and Ostrowski, 2019) and Flower (Beutel et al., 2020) used experimentally. Reporting standards vary widely in the literature. Cohort definitions can make comparisons misleading. Outcome prevalence influences apparent gains. Hyperparameters interact with aggregation frequency. Client sampling affects convergence stability. Compression reduces bandwidth but may harm utility. Asynchronous updates complicate optimization analysis. Heterogeneous privacy budgets require careful tuning. Missingness patterns reveal hidden selection effects. Clinician interpretability strengthens trust in models. Monitoring guards against silent training failures. Governance structures shape collaboration dynamics. Documentation improves reproducibility across partners. Common metrics include AUROC and AUPRC. Confidence intervals should accompany all reports. Practical constraints define realistic threat models. Prospective studies remain relatively rare. These observations frame the current research landscape.
