document: Labeled medical images are expensive to curate. Self-supervised learning promises to reduce annotation needs. Visual pretraining can transfer across anatomical domains. Clinical deployment imposes strict reliability constraints. Distribution shift across scanners complicates generalization. Pretext tasks determine inductive biases in practice. Contrastive methods rely on suitable augmentations. Distillation approaches emphasize invariance without negatives. Multi-instance sampling combats class imbalance. Patch-level modeling helps with gigapixel slides. Scaling laws provide guidance on data size. Evaluation metrics vary across modalities. Segmentation and classification present distinct challenges. Label-efficient fine-tuning remains a key goal. Data curation pipelines affect noise and coverage. Governance and consent shape available datasets. Cross-institution transfer is an important stress test. Reproducibility benefits from standardized code and splits. Prior work in self-supervised visual representation learning and medical imaging employs a spectrum of algorithms and benchmarks, including SimCLR (Chen et al., 2020), MoCo (He et al., 2020), BYOL (Grill et al., 2020), SwAV (Caron et al., 2020), and DINO (Caron et al., 2021); medical datasets such as ChestX-ray14 (Wang et al., 2017), CheXpert (Irvin et al., 2019), MIMIC-CXR (Johnson et al., 2019), ISIC skin lesions (Codella et al., 2019), and CAMELYON16 (Bejnordi et al., 2017); transformer and convolutional backbones from ResNet-50 (He et al., 2016) to ViT-B (Dosovitskiy et al., 2021); augmentation policies like RandAugment (Cubuk et al., 2020) and Mixup (Zhang et al., 2018); and evaluation protocols covering linear probing, fine-tuning, and transfer across institutions and patient-level generalization studies. Compute budgets influence feasible pretraining schedules. Mixed precision is common for throughput gains. Memory banks trade speed for staleness. Teacher momentum stabilizes representation updates. Projection heads alter downstream linear accuracy. Temperature parameters tune similarity sharpness. Multi-view sampling enriches local-global features. Curriculum strategies schedule augmentations over time. Domain adaptation techniques can complement pretraining. Calibration under shift is underexplored. Uncertainty estimates assist triage decisions. Human factors affect annotation consistency. Clinical validation demands stringent protocols. These considerations contextualize current methodological choices.
spans: Prior work in self-supervised visual representation learning and medical imaging employs a spectrum of algorithms and benchmarks, including SimCLR (Chen et al., 2020), MoCo (He et al., 2020), BYOL (Grill et al., 2020), SwAV (Caron et al., 2020), and DINO (Caron et al., 2021); medical datasets such as ChestX-ray14 (Wang et al., 2017), CheXpert (Irvin et al., 2019), MIMIC-CXR (Johnson et al., 2019), ISIC skin lesions (Codella et al., 2019), and CAMELYON16 (Bejnordi et al., 2017); transformer and convolutional backbones from ResNet-50 (He et al., 2016) to ViT-B (Dosovitskiy et al., 2021); augmentation policies like RandAugment (Cubuk et al., 2020) and Mixup (Zhang et al., 2018); and evaluation protocols covering linear probing, fine-tuning, and transfer across institutions and patient-level generalization studies.

document: Urban intersections create bottlenecks that amplify delay. Adaptive signal control aims to reduce congestion dynamically. Reinforcement learning has emerged as a promising paradigm. Agents interact with simulators to learn control policies. The state space aggregates local and global information. Rewards encode efficiency and safety objectives. Credit assignment complicates multi-intersection coordination. Sample efficiency remains a core limitation. Exploration must avoid unsafe behaviors. Generalization across layouts is still difficult. Transfer learning can warm start policies. Model-based variants exploit predictive structure. Graph representations capture topology explicitly. Communication constraints limit cooperative gains. Partial observability degrades performance under noise. Weather and events perturb traffic demand. Evaluation protocols frequently vary by scenario. Realistic arrival processes are essential for testing. Scalability determines practical deployment potential. Interpretability improves operator acceptance. Research on adaptive traffic signal control frequently cites reinforcement learning algorithms, simulators, and benchmarks, including DQN (Mnih et al., 2015), Double DQN (Van Hasselt et al., 2016), DDPG (Lillicrap et al., 2016), A2C (Mnih et al., 2016), and PPO (Schulman et al., 2017); multi-agent formulations such as QMIX (Rashid et al., 2018) and MADDPG (Lowe et al., 2017); environments like SUMO (Krajzewicz et al., 2012), CityFlow (Zhang et al., 2019), and Aimsun (Barceló, 2010); datasets including Hangzhou and Jinan traffic logs (Zhang et al., 2019), PeMS loops (Chen et al., 2001), and METR-LA (Li et al., 2018); and reward or state designs leveraging queue lengths, delays, and phase pressure for standardized evaluation across intersections under varying demand patterns and coordination settings. Offline datasets provide opportunities for policy learning. Counterfactual evaluation reduces testing risk. Safety shields can bound worst-case costs. Transfer across cities requires domain randomization. Reward shaping influences stability and fairness. Multi-objective trade-offs remain largely unexplored. Carbon-aware metrics are increasingly relevant. Field trials face sensing and actuation delays. Open interfaces can accelerate benchmarking. Reporting seeds and splits aids reproducibility. Human oversight remains important in deployment. These factors outline the current landscape.
